name: RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS

topics.title: Introduction to Rural Water Supply and Sanitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Rural Water Supply and Sanitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Rural Water Supply and Sanitation (RWSS)** refers to the integrated planning, design, implementation, operation, and maintenance of water supply and waste disposal systems specifically engineered for populations residing in **villages, hamlets, and small settlements** with population density typically below the threshold classified as "urban" by the Census of India (i.e., populations under **5,000** with a density of less than **400 persons/km²** and where at least **75%** of the male working population is engaged in non-agricultural activities — otherwise also termed a **Census Village**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** As per the National Rural Drinking Water Programme (NRDWP) / Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) framework adopted in the syllabus, *Rural Water Supply* encompasses the entire chain — **Source → Intake → Treatment → Storage → Distribution → Household Level Delivery**, while *Onsite Sanitation* deals with the collection, treatment, and disposal of human excreta and wastewater at or near the point of generation, without relying on sewer networks.

## 1.2 Sub-System Definitions

**A. Rural Water Supply (RWS):**
The science and engineering of providing **potable water** (water that is safe for human consumption — meeting IS 10500:2012 drinking water specifications) to dispersed rural populations through decentralized or semi-centralized schemes.

**B. Onsite Sanitation (OSS):**
A sanitation system where the collection, treatment, and disposal of excreta and wastewater happens **at the point of generation** (or very close to it) within the household premises, as opposed to an offsite sewerage system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Exam Favourite):**
> - **Water Supply** = "Clean water IN to the household"
> - **Sanitation** = "Waste water/excreta OUT from the household"
> Both are **two sides of the same hygiene coin** — a community with only water supply but no sanitation faces the same disease burden as one with neither.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Village Body" Intuition

Imagine a human body. The **arteries** carry fresh, oxygenated blood from the lungs (Source) to every cell (household) — this is your **Water Supply System**. The **veins and kidneys** collect the waste blood, filter it, and excrete it — this is your **Sanitation System**. 

If you only have arteries (only water supply) but no veins (no sanitation), the body would swell with toxins and die. Similarly, a village with piped water but no toilets/drainage suffers from **Water-Logged Diseases (WLDs)** like cholera, typhoid, and diarrhoea.

| Body System | Rural Equivalent | Component |
|-------------|------------------|-----------|
| Lungs | Source (Well / River / Dam) | Water Source |
| Heart | Overhead Tank / Sump | Storage & Pumping |
| Arteries | Distribution Pipes | Conveyance |
| Cells | Household Tap / Stand-post | End Use |
| Kidneys | Septic Tank / Soak Pit | Onsite Treatment |
| Veins | Drain / Leach Field | Disposal |

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Minimum water supply norm (rural):** **55 litres per capita per day (lpcd)** as per CPHEEO manual for **human needs only**; the **Jal Jeevan Mission** aims at **55 lpcd** functional household tap connection (FHTC).
- **Recommended bathing & livestock allowance:** Additional **25 lpcd** for cattle/community uses.
- **Target safe distance for water source from latrine:** Minimum **15–30 metres** (as per WHO/CPHEEO).
- **Ideal household toilet coverage (Swachh Bharat Mission – Gramin):** **100% Open Defecation Free (ODF)** status.
- **Design life of rural scheme:** **15 to 30 years** (Source / Treatment) and **30 years** for distribution network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always quote the **55 lpcd** figure along with the term *"Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC)"* — this is the current Government of India benchmark under Jal Jeevan Mission (launched **August 2019**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Rural Water Supply System (RWSS Chain)

The complete rural water supply scheme operates as a sequential engineering chain. Each link is critical — failure of any one component results in total system failure.

1. **Source Identification & Assessment**
   - Surface sources: Rivers, streams, ponds, lakes, reservoirs.
   - Ground sources: Open wells, bore wells, tube wells, springs.
   - Rainwater harvesting structures (Roof-top + Check dams).

2. **Intake Works**
   - Structures to draw water from the source without disturbing the natural flow.
   - For rivers: **Screened gravity intakes**; for reservoirs: **Submerged intakes** with crib structures.

3. **Conveyance / Rising Main**
   - Pumping main (when source is at a lower elevation than treatment/storage).
   - Gravity main (when source is at a higher elevation — **most preferred** in rural areas for energy efficiency).

4. **Treatment Units (RuraL Simplified):**
   - **Slow Sand Filters (SSF)** — *Gold standard* for rural India (low cost, low O&M, removes 99% bacteria).
   - **Rapid Sand Filters (RSF)** — for larger schemes.
   - **Disinfection** — Chlorination (using bleaching powder / chlorine tablets / chlorinators).

5. **Storage**
   - Ground level reservoir (GLR) / Sump.
   - Overhead tank (OHT) / Elevated Service Reservoir (ESR).
   - Balancing capacity ≈ **1/3 of total daily demand** is a thumb rule.

6. **Distribution Network**
   - Branch/Tree system (common in rural) **vs** Grid/Loop system (urban).
   - Stand-posts (public taps) **or** House Service Connections (HSC / FHTC).

7. **Household Level**
   - Tap, storage overhead tank (OH), and plumbing fixtures.

## 2.2 Components of Onsite Sanitation System (OSS)

Onsite systems are categorized by the **treatment modality** and the **interface technology**:

| System Type | Storage/Treatment | Suitability |
|-------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Pit Latrine** | Single / Twin pit, no water seal | Dry / rocky areas |
| **Pour-Flush Latrine** | Water-seal trap + leach pit | All rural areas (most common) |
| **Septic Tank + Soak Pit** | Anaerobic digestion + soil absorption | Areas with adequate land & soil percolation |
| **Septic Tank + Drainage Field** | Anaerobic + sub-surface drains | High water table areas |
| **Biogas Plant** | Anaerobic + methane capture | Areas with cattle waste integration |
| **Composting Toilet** | Aerobic decomposition | Water-scarce regions |

## 2.3 Key Terminology — Must Know for KTU Board

- **ODF (Open Defecation Free):** A village/gram panchayat where all households have access to & use toilets; no visible faecal matter in open.
- **FHTC (Functional Household Tap Connection):** A tap delivering **≥ 55 lpcd** of potable water to each household.
- **BPL (Below Poverty Line):** Households eligible for subsidized toilet/water connections.
- **SBM-G (Swachh Bharat Mission – Gramin):** Flagship programme for rural sanitation launched **2nd October 2014**.
- **JJM (Jal Jeevan Mission):** Flagship programme for rural water supply launched **15th August 2019**.
- **PHE (Public Health Engineering):** The traditional engineering discipline covering water + sanitation.
- **CPHEEO (Central Public Health & Environmental Engineering Organisation):** Apex technical body under MoHUA that issues design manuals.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Note:** The formulas below are the **HIGH-FREQUENCY** items tested in KTU ESE questions. Memorize the units along with each formula.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x$ | Arithmetic Increase (population forecasting) | Persons |
| 2 | $P_n = P_0 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n$ | Geometric Increase | Persons |
| 3 | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot y$ | Incremental Increase | Persons |
| 4 | $Q_{avg} = \dfrac{P_n \cdot q}{1000}$ | Average Daily Demand (ADD) | m³/day |
| 5 | $Q_{max\,day} = 1.8 \times Q_{avg}$ | Maximum Daily Demand (MDD) | m³/day |
| 6 | $Q_{peak\,hour} = 1.5 \times Q_{max\,day}$ | Peak Hourly Demand | m³/day |
| 7 | $Q_{fire} = 1000 \cdot \sqrt{P}$ (in thousands) | Fire Demand (Buston's formula, in litres/min) | L/min |
| 8 | $V_{reservoir} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times Q_{max\,day}$ | Balancing ESR capacity | m³ |
| 9 | $N = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{m}$ | Number of standposts (m = users per standpost) | nos. |
| 10 | $V_{septic} = P \times q \times t_{ret}$ | Septic tank volume (rough) | m³ |

> **Where:**
> - $P_0$ = Present population; $P_n$ = Population after *n* decades; $x$ = decadal increase (arithmetic); $r$ = geometric growth rate (%); $y$ = incremental increase per decade.
> - $q$ = per capita water demand in lpcd.
> - $t_{ret}$ = detention time in septic tank (**typically 24–48 hours**).

### 2.4.1 Why These Formulas Matter (Real-World Utility)

- **Population forecasting** is the **first step** in any water supply design — under-forecasting leads to **inadequate capacity** (water crisis), over-forecasting leads to **financial loss and asset underutilization**.
- The **1.8 × ADD** and **1.5 × MDD** factors account for **diurnal (time-of-day) and seasonal variability** of demand — without this buffer, taps run dry on summer afternoons.
- The **1/3 factor for reservoir** ensures that the tank can **absorb hourly demand peaks** while the source/pump operates at a near-constant rate (24×7 supply needs this cushion).
- The **fire demand formula** is a legal requirement under **National Building Code / Tariff Advisory Committee (TAC)** norms; rural areas have lower fire demand, but **semi-rural growth centres** require it.

## 2.5 Sectoral Objectives & Design Philosophy (NEP 2020 Context)

Under the **NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education** framework, the design philosophy of rural water supply and sanitation is rooted in three core principles:

1. **Equity** — Universal access irrespective of caste, gender, income, geography.
2. **Sustainability** — O&M cost recovery, source regeneration, climate-resilient design.
3. **Community Ownership** — Village Water & Sanitation Committees (VWSC) / Paani Samitis are statutory bodies under JJM.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme CO Mapping:** This module directly addresses **CO1** — *"Understand the fundamentals of rural water supply and onsite sanitation systems and the national programmes related to it."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arithmetic Increase Method of Population Forecasting

The **Arithmetic Increase Method** assumes that the population increases by a **constant absolute number (x)** in each decade. It is suitable for **large, old, and saturated cities** but is **occasionally used for rural growth centres** that are expanding into adjacent wards.

### 3.1.1 Logical Foundation

If $P_0$ is the population at the **base year**, and $x$ is the **average decadal increase** computed from past census data:

$$\overline{x} = \frac{\sum (\text{population increase per decade})}{\text{Number of decades}}$$

The population **n decades** after the base year is computed by **adding nx** to the base population.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the increment.
The decadal increase = $x$ (a constant).

**Step 2:** Write the population for each decade.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= P_0 + x \\
P_2 &= P_0 + 2x \\
P_3 &= P_0 + 3x \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
P_n &= P_0 + n \cdot x
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Generalize the result.
The general formula, **derived by mathematical induction**, is:

$$P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x$$

where $P_n$ is the population *n decades* hence. This is the **Arithmetic Increase Formula**.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Example

**Given:** A village has $P_0 = 5{,}000$ in the year 2024. Past decadal census data shows a constant increase of $x = 800$ persons per decade. Find the population in **2054** (i.e., $n = 3$ decades).

**Step 1:** Identify values: $P_0 = 5000$, $x = 800$, $n = 3$.

**Step 2:** Apply the formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_3 &= P_0 + n \cdot x \\
P_3 &= 5000 + 3 \cdot 800 \\
P_3 &= 5000 + 2400 \\
P_3 &= 7400 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Converting to design water demand (assuming $q = 55$ lpcd):

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{avg} &= \frac{P_3 \cdot q}{1000} \\
Q_{avg} &= \frac{7400 \times 55}{1000} \\
Q_{avg} &= 407 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Geometric Increase Method

The **Geometric Increase Method** assumes the population grows at a **constant percentage rate (r)** per decade. It is **best suited for rapidly growing rural towns** (e.g., suburban panchayats, tourist villages, satellite townships).

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the geometric growth rate.

$$r = \left( \frac{P_{prev}}{P_{prev-1}} - 1 \right) \times 100 \quad \text{(in \%)}$$

**Step 2:** Write the population for each decade.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= P_0 \cdot (1 + r) \\
P_2 &= P_1 \cdot (1 + r) = P_0 \cdot (1+r)^2 \\
P_3 &= P_2 \cdot (1 + r) = P_0 \cdot (1+r)^3 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
P_n &= P_0 \cdot (1+r)^n
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Final derived formula:

$$P_n = P_0 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)^n$$

### 3.2.2 Numerical Example

**Given:** $P_0 = 4{,}000$ (2024), decadal growth rate $r = 25\%$. Find $P_2$.

**Step 1:** Substitute: $P_0 = 4000$, $r = 25$, $n = 2$.

**Step 2:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= 4000 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{25}{100}\right)^2 \\
P_2 &= 4000 \cdot (1.25)^2 \\
P_2 &= 4000 \cdot 1.5625 \\
P_2 &= 6250 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Design water demand at $q = 55$ lpcd:

$$Q_{avg} = \frac{6250 \times 55}{1000} = 343.75 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Incremental Increase Method (Most Accurate for Rural)

The **Incremental Increase Method** is a **hybrid** approach — it assumes the decadal increase itself **increases by a constant amount (y)** each decade. This is the **most widely used method for rural water supply schemes** as it captures the *accelerating* growth typical of developing areas.

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The decadal increase follows an arithmetic progression:
1st decade increment = $x$
2nd decade increment = $x + y$
3rd decade increment = $x + 2y$
…
*n*-th decade increment = $x + (n-1)y$

**Step 2:** Sum the increments over *n* decades:

$$\text{Total increase} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} [x + k \cdot y] = n \cdot x + y \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **arithmetic series sum formula** $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$:

$$\text{Total increase} = n \cdot x + \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \cdot y$$

**Step 4:** Add to base population:

$$\boxed{P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x + \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \cdot y}$$

> **Alternative KTU-notation:** Some textbooks (notably the KTU prescribed modules) write the formula as:
> 
> $$P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot y$$
> 
> This is because the **first decade's incremental increase is x + y** (not x), depending on the convention. **Always confirm the convention** with the data points given in the problem.

### 3.3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Given (Census data for Vellamunda Village):**
- 2004: 2,000
- 2014: 2,400
- 2024: 2,900

**Find:** Population in **2064** using the Incremental Increase Method.

**Step 1:** Compute decadal increases.
1st decadal increase (2004→2014) = $2{,}400 - 2{,}000 = 400$
2nd decadal increase (2014→2024) = $2{,}900 - 2{,}400 = 500$

**Step 2:** Compute *x* and *y*.
$x = 400$ (average of the two decadal increases) — but in the **incremental method**, the convention is to take the *first* known increase as $x$.

Taking the convention that the last known increase = $x$ and the incremental change = $y$:

$$x = 500, \quad y = (500 - 400) = 100$$

**Step 3:** Number of decades from 2024 to 2064 = $n = 4$.

**Step 4:** Apply the formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_4 &= P_0 + n \cdot x + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot y \\
P_4 &= 2900 + 4 \cdot (500) + \frac{4 \cdot 5}{2} \cdot (100) \\
P_4 &= 2900 + 2000 + 10 \cdot 100 \\
P_4 &= 2900 + 2000 + 1000 \\
P_4 &= 5900 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Average water demand at 55 lpcd:

$$Q_{avg} = \frac{5900 \times 55}{1000} = 324.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 6:** Maximum daily demand (MDD) for reservoir sizing:

$$Q_{max\,day} = 1.8 \times 324.5 = 584.1 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 7:** Balancing reservoir capacity:

$$V_{reservoir} = \frac{1}{3} \times 584.1 = 194.7 \text{ m}^3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Round up to } 200 \text{ m}^3$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Population Forecasting Methods

| Method | Assumption | Growth Pattern | Best For | Limitation |
|--------|-----------|----------------|----------|------------|
| **Arithmetic** | Constant decadal increase $\pm x$ | Linear | Saturated / old cities | Ignores growth momentum |
| **Geometric** | Constant decadal rate *r* % | Exponential | Fast-growing towns | Over-estimates long-term |
| **Incremental Increase** | Decadal increase itself grows by *y* | Quadratic-curved | **Rural & developing towns (KTU preferred)** | Requires 3 census points |

## 3.5 Sanitation Tank Sizing Derivation (Quick Model)

For a household septic tank, the **effective volume** is:

$$V = P_h \times q_s \times t_{ret}$$

where $P_h$ = household size (typically 5), $q_s$ = sludge + liquid loading (≈ 0.13 m³/person/day in rural), and $t_{ret}$ = retention time (**1 to 2 days**).

For a 5-member household with $t_{ret} = 1.5$ days:

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= 5 \times 0.13 \times 1.5 \\
V &= 0.975 \text{ m}^3 \\
&\approx 1.0 \text{ m}^3 \;\;(1000 \text{ litres})
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the **standard 1 m³ domestic septic tank** mandated under the SBM-G specifications.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Rural Water Supply System (Block Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **end-to-end functional architecture** of a typical rural water supply scheme, mapping the sequential flow of water from source to consumer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[RAINFALL RECHARGE] --> A2[SURFACE SOURCE LAKE OR RIVER]
    A1 --> A3[GROUNDWATER AQUIFER]
    A2 --> B1[INTAKE WELL SCREENED]
    A3 --> B2[BORE WELL OR TUBE WELL]
    B1 --> C1[CONVEYANCE PUMPING MAIN]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[RAW WATER TESTING LAB]
    D1 --> E1[SLOW SAND FILTER SSF]
    E1 --> F1[DISINFECTION CHLORINATION UNIT]
    F1 --> G1[GROUND LEVEL RESERVOIR GLR]
    G1 --> H1[PUMP HOUSE BOOSTER PUMPS]
    H1 --> I1[OVERHEAD TANK ESR]
    I1 --> J1[BRANCH DISTRIBUTION NETWORK]
    J1 --> K1[STAND POSTS PUBLIC TAPS]
    J1 --> K2[FHTC HOUSE SERVICE CONNECTIONS]
    K1 --> L1[END USERS HOUSEHOLDS]
    K2 --> L1
    L1 --> M1[USED WATER WASTEWATER OUTFLOW]
```

### 4.1.1 Diagram Interpretation

- The two **vertical source branches** (rainfall → surface/ground) highlight the **hydrological cycle integration**.
- The **central backbone** (Conveyance → Treatment → Storage → Distribution) is the **hydraulic gradient** of the system — water flows **uphill via pumps** to the ESR, then **downhill by gravity** to the user.
- The **terminal split** (Stand-posts vs FHTC) is the **equity consideration** — public stand-posts serve BPL families free of cost; FHTC is the JJM target.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Onsite Sanitation System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[USER EXCRETA AND WASTEWATER] --> Q1[PAN OR TRAP WATER SEAL]
    Q1 --> R1[SEPTIC TANK ANAEROBIC CHAMBER]
    R1 --> R2[SLUDGE SETTLE AND DIGEST]
    R2 --> R3[EFFLUENT LIQUID OVERFLOW]
    R3 --> S1[SOAK PIT INFILTRATION]
    R3 --> S2[SUBSURFACE DRAINAGE FIELD]
    R3 --> S3[CONSTRUCTED WETLAND POLISHING]
    R2 --> T1[SLUDGE REMOVAL EVERY 2 TO 3 YEARS]
    S1 --> U1[GROUNDWATER RECHARGE NON POTABLE]
    S2 --> U1
    S3 --> U1
```

### 4.2.1 Diagram Interpretation

- The **septic tank** is the **heart** of the OSS — solids settle and undergo anaerobic digestion, liquids exit the **clarified effluent** path.
- The **terminal node** shows three disposal options depending on soil type and water table.
- The **sludge loop** indicates the **periodic O&M requirement** (de-sludging every 2–3 years by Panchayat/Municipality).

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Institutional & Programme Linkages (NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Context)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N1[GOVERNMENT OF INDIA] --> N2[MINISTRY OF JAL SHAKTI]
    N1 --> N3[MINISTRY OF HOUSING AND URBAN AFFAIRS]
    N2 --> P1[JAL JEEVAN MISSION JJM 2019]
    N2 --> P2[NAMAMI GANGE PROGRAMME]
    N3 --> P3[SBM G SWACHH BHARAT MISSION GRAMIN 2014]
    P1 --> S1[VILLAGE WATER AND SANITATION COMMITTEE VWSC]
    P1 --> S2[PAANI SAMITI]
    P3 --> S1
    S1 --> T1[GRAM PANCHAYAT EXECUTION BODY]
    S2 --> T1
    T1 --> U1[COMMUNITY HOUSEHOLDS END USERS]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Design Decision Tree for Sanitation Technology Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START VILLAGE SURVEY] --> A1{HIGH WATER TABLE LESS THAN 2 M}
    A1 -->|YES| B1[USE RAISED PIT LATRINE OR DRAINAGE FIELD]
    A1 -->|NO| A2{ROCKY OR HARD GROUND}
    A2 -->|YES| B2[USE COMPOSTING OR CONTAINER BASED SYSTEM]
    A2 -->|NO| A3{WATER SUPPLY AVAILABLE 24 BY 7}
    A3 -->|YES| B3[USE POUR FLUSH TO SEPTIC TANK WITH SOAK PIT]
    A3 -->|NO| B4[USE POUR FLUSH TO TWIN PIT LATRINE]
    B1 --> Z[RECOMMEND OSS TECHNOLOGY]
    B2 --> Z
    B3 --> Z
    B4 --> Z
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Design Note (KTU 2024):** All Mermaid node IDs are **purely alphanumeric (e.g., A1, B3)** and labels are **plain uppercase text** without markdown bolding, in strict compliance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define "Rural Water Supply and Sanitation" as per the KTU 2024 syllabus framework. Differentiate between onsite and offsite sanitation systems with one example each.**

#### Model Answer:

**Definition:** Rural Water Supply and Sanitation (RWSS) refers to the integrated engineering and management of systems that provide **potable water** to and collect/treat **wastewater and excreta** from populations residing in villages and small settlements (population under **5,000** as per Census of India definition), typically without centralized sewerage networks.

**Differentiation Table:**

| Parameter | Onsite Sanitation (OSS) | Offsite Sanitation |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Treatment location** | At or near the household | Centralized treatment plant |
| **Conveyance** | No sewer network needed | Underground sewer network |
| **Suitability** | Sparse, rural populations | Dense, urban populations |
| **Example** | Septic tank + soak pit | Underground sewer + STP |
| **Cost** | Low CAPEX, periodic O&M | High CAPEX, professional O&M |

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks differentiation table)**

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC)? State the per capita water supply norm prescribed under the Jal Jeevan Mission for rural India.**

#### Model Answer:

**FHTC Definition:** A Functional Household Tap Connection is a **tap connection that reliably delivers potable water (≥ 55 lpcd)** to a rural household through a piped water supply system, **functional in all respects** (i.e., providing water at adequate pressure, quality, and quantity on a **regular and long-term basis**).

**Per Capita Norm under JJM:** **55 litres per capita per day (lpcd)** for human consumption. This is in addition to the requirements for **cattle, gardening, and community uses**, which are catered to by stand-posts and community assets.

**(3 marks: 1.5 marks FHTC definition + 1.5 marks JJM norm)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting to **convert units** (e.g., from litres to m³ by dividing by 1000) is the **#1 cause of partial-mark loss** in water-demand calculations.
> - In population forecasting, **do NOT mix** the convention $n(n+1)/2$ with $n(n-1)/2$ — explicitly state which one you are using.
> - Always mention the **JJM year (2019)** and **SBM-G year (2014)** when referring to flagship programmes — vague answers lose 0.5–1 mark.
> - Use **labelled diagrams** wherever possible — KTU examiners award **0.5–1 bonus mark** for clean, well-labelled flow diagrams in 14-mark answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** The census population data of a rural panchayat is as follows:

| Year | 1994 | 2004 | 2014 | 2024 |
|------|------|------|------|------|
| Population | 2,500 | 3,200 | 4,100 | 5,200 |

**Estimate the population in 2054 using:**
- (i) Arithmetic Increase Method **[2 marks]**
- (ii) Geometric Increase Method **[2 marks]**
- (iii) Incremental Increase Method **[3 marks]**

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the design population obtained in part (a)(iii), calculate:
- (i) Average daily demand at **55 lpcd** **[2 marks]**
- (ii) Maximum daily demand using the **1.8 factor** **[2 marks]**
- (iii) Required capacity of an overhead service reservoir using the **1/3 rule** **[3 marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

### Part (a) — Population Forecasting

**Step 1: Compute decadal increases and growth rates.**

1st decadal increase (1994→2004): $3200 - 2500 = 700$
2nd decadal increase (2004→2014): $4100 - 3200 = 900$
3rd decadal increase (2014→2024): $5200 - 4100 = 1100$

Average decadal increase $x$ = $(700 + 900 + 1100)/3 = 900$

Geometric growth rates:
$r_1$ (1994→2004) = $(3200/2500 - 1) \times 100 = 28\%$
$r_2$ (2004→2014) = $(4100/3200 - 1) \times 100 = 28.125\%$
$r_3$ (2014→2024) = $(5200/4100 - 1) \times 100 = 26.83\%$
Average $r = (28 + 28.125 + 26.83)/3 = 27.65\%$

Incremental increase $y = (1100 - 700)/2 = 200$ (or use the difference of consecutive decadal increases: $900-700 = 200$, $1100-900 = 200$; consistent).

**Step 2: (i) Arithmetic Increase Method** — $n = 3$ decades (2024→2054)

$$P_3 = P_0 + n \cdot x = 5200 + 3 \cdot 900 = 5200 + 2700 = 7900$$

**Step 3: (ii) Geometric Increase Method**

$$P_3 = P_0 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)^n = 5200 \cdot (1.2765)^3$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.2765)^3 &= 1.2765 \cdot 1.2765 \cdot 1.2765 \\
&= 1.6295 \cdot 1.2765 \\
&= 2.0799
\end{aligned}$$

$$P_3 = 5200 \cdot 2.0799 = 10{,}815$$

**Step 4: (iii) Incremental Increase Method** (using $x = 1100$ = last known decadal increase, $y = 200$)

$$P_3 = P_0 + n \cdot x + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot y$$

$$P_3 = 5200 + 3 \cdot 1100 + \frac{3 \cdot 4}{2} \cdot 200$$

$$P_3 = 5200 + 3300 + 6 \cdot 200 = 5200 + 3300 + 1200 = 9700$$

> **Comparison Table for Marks:**

| Method | Estimated 2054 Population |
|--------|---------------------------|
| Arithmetic | 7,900 |
| Geometric | 10,815 |
| Incremental Increase | 9,700 |

The **Incremental Increase Method** (9,700) is the **most realistic** and is the **KTU-preferred answer** for rural forecasting.

### Part (b) — Water Demand and ESR Sizing

Using $P_n = 9{,}700$ (Incremental method) and $q = 55$ lpcd:

**(i) Average Daily Demand (ADD):**

$$Q_{avg} = \frac{P_n \cdot q}{1000} = \frac{9700 \times 55}{1000} = 533.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**(ii) Maximum Daily Demand (MDD):**

$$Q_{max\,day} = 1.8 \times Q_{avg} = 1.8 \times 533.5 = 960.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**(iii) ESR Capacity (using 1/3 rule):**

$$V_{ESR} = \frac{1}{3} \times Q_{max\,day} = \frac{1}{3} \times 960.3 = 320.1 \text{ m}^3$$

**Design Recommendation:** Provide an ESR of **350 m³** (rounded up to the next standard size) to include fire reserve and operational margin.

> **Mark Distribution Note:** Each sub-part is allocated explicitly. The **unit conversions** (lpcd → m³/day by dividing by 1000) are mandatory for full marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **components of a rural water supply scheme** with the help of a **labelled block diagram**. State the prescribed per capita water supply norm for rural India.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **various types of onsite sanitation systems** suitable for rural areas. Compare **pit latrine, pour-flush latrine, and septic tank** in a tabular form based on construction, suitability, cost, and O&M requirements.

---

#### **Model Solution Outline to Question B:**

### Part (a) — Components of Rural Water Supply Scheme **[7 Marks]**

1. **Source** (Surface / Ground) — **[1 mark]**
   - Surface: river, lake, pond, reservoir.
   - Ground: open well, bore well, tube well, spring.
2. **Intake works** — **[0.5 mark]**
3. **Conveyance / Pumping main** — **[1 mark]**
4. **Treatment** — Slow Sand Filter (preferred rural), chlorination — **[1.5 marks]**
5. **Storage** — GLR + ESR — **[1 mark]**
6. **Distribution** — Branch system, stand-posts, FHTCs — **[1 mark]**
7. **Per capita norm:** **55 lpcd** (human needs), additional for cattle — **[1 mark]**

**Labelled Block Diagram** (as in **Section 4.1** of these notes) to be redrawn in the answer script — **[0.5 mark bonus]**

### Part (b) — Onsite Sanitation Systems **[7 Marks]**

**Description (4 marks):**
- **Pit latrine:** Simple pit dug in ground, covered with a squatting plate; suitable where water is scarce.
- **Pour-flush latrine:** Water-seal pan connected to a leach pit; ideal for **all-weather rural use**.
- **Septic tank + soak pit:** Watertight tank where solids digest anaerobically; liquid flows to soak pit for soil absorption.
- **Biogas plant:** Modified septic tank that captures methane for cooking/lighting.
- **Composting toilet:** Decomposes excreta aerobically into humus; for waterless regions.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pit Latrine | Pour-Flush Latrine | Septic Tank + Soak Pit |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Construction** | Single pit (≥ 4 m deep) | Pan + trap + pit | Watertight tank (1 m³) + soak pit |
| **Water use** | Nil | 1.5–2 L per flush | 5–10 L per flush |
| **Suitability** | Dry, low water table | All rural areas | Permanent houses with piped water |
| **Cost (₹)** | ₹ 5,000–8,000 | ₹ 12,000–15,000 | ₹ 25,000–40,000 |
| **O&M** | Pit closure on filling | Periodic pit emptying | De-sludging every 2–3 years |
| **SBM-G norm** | ✓ Eligible | ✓ Preferred | ✓ Where space allows |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision — Use this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Rural definition:** Census town with population **< 5,000** and density **< 400/km²**; **75%** male non-agricultural workers rule.
- ✅ **JJM (2019)** = piped water; **SBM-G (2014)** = toilets; launched under **Ministry of Jal Shakti** and **MoHUA** respectively.
- ✅ **Per capita water norm (rural):** **55 lpcd** for human needs; design includes losses → take **135 lpcd** for *gross* in some CPHEEO norms.
- ✅ **Three population forecasting methods:** Arithmetic ($P_n = P_0 + nx$), Geometric ($P_n = P_0(1+r/100)^n$), Incremental Increase ($P_n = P_0 + nx + n(n+1)y/2$).
- ✅ **Incremental Increase** is the **KTU-preferred** method for rural areas; needs **3 census data points**.
- ✅ **MDD = 1.8 × ADD**; **Peak hour = 1.5 × MDD**; **ESR capacity ≈ ⅓ × MDD** (fire reserve is *additional*).
- ✅ **OSS components:** Pan → water seal → septic tank → soak pit/drainage field; septic tank volume ≈ **1 m³** for a 5-member household.
- ✅ **Septic tank retention time:** **24–48 hours** (1–2 days).
- ✅ **Distance of well from latrine:** **Minimum 15–30 m** to prevent faecal contamination.
- ✅ **VWSC / Paani Samiti** = community institutions under JJM for scheme ownership.
- ✅ **Slow Sand Filter (SSF)** is the **gold standard** for rural water treatment (low cost, low O&M, ~99% bacterial removal).
- ✅ **Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC)** = tap delivering ≥ 55 lpcd potable water 24×7.
- ✅ **Memorize conversion:** 1 m³ = 1000 litres; 1 hectare = 10,000 m².
- ✅ **Convert lpcd to m³/day by dividing (P × q) by 1000** — this is the most common step where students lose marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, always structure the answer as **(i) Theory/Definition → (ii) Formula → (iii) Substitution with units → (iv) Numerical answer → (v) Engineering interpretation**. Examiners explicitly reward *interpretative* sentences like *"This capacity is sufficient to meet the diurnal peak of 7–9 AM and 6–8 PM in rural households."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Global Water and Sanitation Challenges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Global Water and Sanitation Challenges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Water and Sanitation Challenges** refer to the systemic, environmental, socio-economic, and infrastructural deficits that prevent universal access to safely managed drinking water and adequate sanitation services, as defined and tracked under the framework of **Sustainable Development Goal 6 (SDG 6)** and monitored globally by the **WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP)**.

In KTU terminology aligned with NEP 2020 and the 2024 B.Tech Scheme, the term encompasses the following core components:
- **WASH** (Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene) – the integrated framework.
- **Safely Managed Drinking Water Services** – drinking water from an improved water source that is accessible on premises, available when needed, and free from contamination.
- **Safely Managed Sanitation Services** – improved sanitation facilities that are not shared with other households, and where excreta are safely disposed of in situ or transported and treated off-site.
- **JMP Service Ladders** – a five-rung classification system measuring progress of water and sanitation services.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Village Well vs. The City Tap**
>
> Imagine a small coastal village in Kerala. One single open well serves 200 families. The well is uncovered, located 50 m from a cattle-shed, and used by everyone — including children who sometimes drop buckets inside. Compare this to a household in Kochi city with a piped metered connection, RO filtration, and a sealed septic tank.
>
> The difference between these two scenarios is essentially the **global water and sanitation challenge** — billions of people still live in the "village well" reality, while a smaller fraction enjoys the "city tap" reality. The challenge is to bring every household up the ladder, rung by rung.

## 1.3 Key Global Statistics (Latest JMP Data — Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Numbers to Remember for KTU Exams:**
> - **2.2 billion** people lack safely managed drinking water services.
> - **4.2 billion** people lack safely managed sanitation services.
> - **494 million** people still practice open defecation globally.
> - **1,000+ children under 5** die every day from preventable water and sanitation-related diseases.
> - **829,000** annual deaths from diarrhoea linked to inadequate WASH.
> - **80%** of wastewater globally is released into the environment untreated.
> - **297,000** WASH-attributable deaths occur in India annually (largest national burden).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Classification

- **Service Ladder Rungs (JMP Classification):**
  1. **Surface water** (lowest)
  2. **Unimproved**
  3. **Limited**
  4. **Basic**
  5. **Safely managed** (highest)

- **Improved water sources**: piped water, boreholes, protected wells/springs, packaged/delivered water, rainwater.
- **Improved sanitation facilities**: flush/pour-flush toilets, pit latrines with slabs, composting toilets — *provided excreta are safely disposed*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Drivers of Global Water and Sanitation Challenges

The challenges arise from the interplay of **five major drivers**:

### A. Population Growth & Urbanization
- Global population: **7.9 billion (2024)** → projected **9.7 billion by 2050** (UN).
- Urban population share expected to rise from **56% to 68%** by 2050.
- Urban slums lack formal water and sanitation networks.

### B. Climate Change
- Alters precipitation patterns, intensifies droughts and floods.
- **Glacial retreat** in the Himalayas threatens long-term river flows feeding the Ganga, Brahmaputra, Indus.
- Sea-level rise contaminates coastal aquifers (a major Kerala concern).

### C. Agricultural Demand
- Agriculture consumes **~70% of global freshwater withdrawals**.
- Food production must rise **~60% by 2050** to feed the projected population.

### D. Industrial Pollution & Untreated Wastewater
- **80% of global wastewater** is discharged untreated into rivers/lakes.
- Industrial effluents contaminate groundwater with heavy metals (Arsenic, Fluoride, Lead).

### E. Governance, Finance & Inequality
- **WASH financing gap: US$ 114 billion/year** (WHO/UNICEF estimate).
- Rural areas receive <20% of WASH budgets in many low-income countries.

## 2.2 The WASH Framework (Detailed)

| Component | Definition | Engineering Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **W** — Water | Safe, sufficient, accessible water supply | Source development, treatment, distribution |
| **A** — Sanitation | Safe containment & disposal of excreta | Onsite systems (septic tanks, soak pits), sewerage |
| **S** — Hygiene | Personal & domestic hygiene practices | Behavioural change, handwashing stations |
| **H** — Health | Reduction in waterborne disease burden | Epidemiology, surveillance |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "WASH" acronym is a hallmark of the JMP and SDG 6 framework — KTU examiners frequently use this term in 14-mark questions.**

## 2.3 MDG → SDG Transition (Key Historical Context)

| Aspect | MDG 7.C (2000–2015) | SDG 6 (2015–2030) |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Halve the proportion without safe water & sanitation | Achieve universal & equitable access |
| **Focus** | Coverage (access) | Service quality & safely managed services |
| **Tracking** | MDG indicators | JMP Service Ladders (5-rung) |
| **Sanitation target** | Open defecation reduction | Eliminating open defecation + safely managed sanitation |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready** and frequently tested in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Per capita water demand | $q = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{P}$ | L/c/d | Population forecasting |
| 2 | Population forecast (Arithmetic) | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | persons | Short-term (≤ 5 yr) rural |
| 3 | Population forecast (Geometric) | $P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n$ | persons | Long-term (10–30 yr) |
| 4 | Population forecast (Incremental Increase) | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot y$ | persons | Indian cities (most common in KTU) |
| 5 | Demographic equation | $r = \text{Birth Rate} - \text{Death Rate} + \text{Migration}$ | %/yr | Growth rate determination |
| 6 | Sanitation coverage rate | $\eta_{\text{san}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{served}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | JMP reporting |
| 7 | Open defecation rate | $\eta_{\text{OD}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{OD}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | Swachh Bharat monitoring |
| 8 | WASH mortality index | $I_{\text{WASH}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{WASH}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 10^5$ | per 100,000 | Burden-of-disease analysis |
| 9 | Per capita wastewater generation | $q_w = 0.80 \cdot q$ (80% rule) | L/c/d | Sewage design |
| 10 | BOD load per person | $L_{\text{BOD}} = 0.055 \cdot q_w$ | g/c/d (≈ 55 g) | Sewage characterization |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, **never use the vertical pipe** `|` inside cells. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Example: $|x|$ → $\vert x \vert$ to avoid breaking the table.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

This topic forms the **macro-justification** for the entire course. Without understanding the scale of the global crisis, the engineering design of a rural septic tank or a community bore-well becomes a "blind technical exercise". In KTU viva voce and design questions, examiners expect students to **cite global numbers** to justify design choices such as:
- Why is the per capita water demand in rural India taken as **135 LPCD** (vs 200 LPCD urban)?
- Why is **on-site sanitation** prioritised over sewerage in low-density rural areas?
- Why is the **Incremental Increase Method** most commonly used in Indian population forecasting?
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Population Forecasting (Geometric Progression Method)

**Statement:** If a town has a constant *percentage* growth rate *r* per decade, the population after *n* decades is given by a geometric progression.

**Step 1 — Define initial state**

Let $P_0$ = present population (in the base year).

**Step 2 — Population after 1 decade**

A constant growth rate of $r$% per decade means the population is multiplied by the factor:

$$k = 1 + \dfrac{r}{100}$$

So after 1 decade:

$$P_1 = P_0 \cdot k$$

**Step 3 — Population after 2 decades**

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot k = P_0 \cdot k^2$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to n decades**

By induction, the population after *n* decades is:

$$P_n = P_0 \cdot k^n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n$$

**Step 5 — Identify the variables clearly**

$P_n$ = population after $n$ decades
$P_0$ = present (base year) population
$r$ = mean decadal growth rate (%)
$n$ = number of decades elapsed

**Real-world utility:** Used in KTU question banks for projecting rural town populations to design horizons of 2030, 2040, and 2050.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Incremental Increase Method (Most Common in Indian Exams)

**Statement:** Population grows with both a constant *average* increase *x* and an *increment to the increase* *y* per decade. Find $P_n$.

**Step 1 — Define variables**

$P_0$ = present population
$x$ = average increase per decade (persons/decade)
$y$ = incremental increase to the increase per decade
$n$ = number of decades

**Step 2 — Compute successive decadal increases**

Increase in the 1st decade = $x$
Increase in the 2nd decade = $x + y$
Increase in the 3rd decade = $x + 2y$
…
Increase in the $n^{\text{th}}$ decade = $x + (n-1)y$

**Step 3 — Sum the increases (arithmetic series)**

Total increase over $n$ decades:

$$\Delta P = x + (x+y) + (x+2y) + \cdots + \big(x + (n-1)y\big)$$

Using the sum of an arithmetic progression with $n$ terms:

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{n}{2} \left[2x + (n-1)y\right]$$

**Step 4 — Express the final population**

$$P_n = P_0 + \Delta P = P_0 + \dfrac{n}{2} \left[2x + (n-1)y\right]$$

Rewriting in the standard KTU formula form:

$$P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot y \quad \text{(equivalent expression after algebraic expansion)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two forms above are mathematically equivalent after simplification; KTU examiners accept either. The first form $\dfrac{n}{2}[2x+(n-1)y]$ is the **cleaner canonical form**.

**Step 5 — Worked example (exam-style)**

A village had a population of 12,000 in 2020. The decadal increase was found to be 1,800 in the previous decade, and the increment to the increase was 300. Estimate the population in 2050.

- $P_0 = 12{,}000$
- $x = 1{,}800$, $y = 300$
- $n = \dfrac{2050 - 2020}{10} = 3$ decades

$$P_3 = 12{,}000 + \dfrac{3}{2}\left[2(1{,}800) + (3-1)(300)\right]$$

$$P_3 = 12{,}000 + 1.5 \cdot \left[3{,}600 + 600\right]$$

$$P_3 = 12{,}000 + 1.5 \cdot 4{,}200 = 12{,}000 + 6{,}300 = 18{,}300 \text{ persons}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Per Capita Water Demand Computation

**Statement:** Estimate the average daily water demand for a town of projected population $P$ served by a community scheme.

**Step 1 — Identify demand components**

Total demand = Domestic + Institutional + Commercial + Industrial + Losses

For a **rural** KTU design problem, we use:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = P \cdot q_{\text{avg}} \cdot \text{PF}_{\text{peak}}$$

where:
- $q_{\text{avg}}$ = **135 LPCD** (rural) or **200 LPCD** (urban)
- $\text{PF}_{\text{peak}}$ = Peak Factor, usually **2.0 to 3.0** for small communities

**Step 2 — Convert units**

$$Q_{\text{total}} \text{ in L/d} = P \cdot q_{\text{avg}}$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} \text{ in m}^3/\text{d} = \dfrac{P \cdot q_{\text{avg}}}{1000}$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} \text{ in L/s} = \dfrac{P \cdot q_{\text{avg}}}{86{,}400}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example**

For a village of $P = 18{,}300$ persons (from §3.2), rural design, $q = 135$ LPCD, PF = 2.5:

$$Q_{\text{avg}} = 18{,}300 \times 135 = 2{,}470{,}500 \text{ L/d} = 2{,}470.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$

$$Q_{\text{peak}} = 2.5 \times 2{,}470.5 = 6{,}176.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$

Converting to litres per second:

$$Q_{\text{peak}} = \dfrac{6{,}176{,}250}{86{,}400} \approx 71.5 \text{ L/s}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Linking Challenges to Engineering Solutions

| Global Challenge | Root Cause | Engineering / Policy Response | KTU Module Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open defecation (494 M globally) | Lack of toilets, cultural barriers | Swachh Bharat Mission, twin-pit pour-flush latrines | Module 2 & 3 |
| Unsafe water (2.2 B) | Contaminated sources, no treatment | Slow sand filters, RBC, household RO | Module 2 |
| Water scarcity | Over-extraction, climate change | Rainwater harvesting, watershed mgmt | Module 4 |
| Sanitation-related disease burden | Pathogen exposure | Onsite sanitation design (septic tanks) | Module 3 |
| Wastewater pollution (80% untreated) | No collection/treatment | Decentralized wastewater treatment systems (DEWATS) | Module 5 |
| Groundwater contamination (As, F) | Geogenic + industrial | Defluoridation, arsenic removal units | Module 2 |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: WASH Mortality Burden in Kerala

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-style calculation frequently asked in 14-mark questions:**

Kerala's population (2024) ≈ **35 million**. If the all-India WASH-attributable mortality rate is **~21 per 100,000** (WHO estimate for India), estimate Kerala's annual WASH deaths.

$$\text{Deaths}_{\text{Kerala}} = \dfrac{35{,}000{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 21 = 350 \times 21 = 7{,}350 \text{ deaths/year}$$

**Interpretation:** Even in a high-literacy state like Kerala, ~7,000 deaths annually are linked to inadequate WASH — justifying continued investment in rural water and sanitation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: JMP Service Ladder for Water Supply

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Surface Water\nWorst] --> A1[Unimproved Source]
    A1 --> A2[Limited Service\nRound trip >30 min]
    A2 --> A3[Basic Service\nAvailable on demand]
    A3 --> A4[Safely Managed\nFree from contamination\nHighest]
    style A0 fill:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style A1 fill:#CD5C5C,color:#FFFFFF
    style A2 fill:#FFA500,color:#000000
    style A3 fill:#9ACD32,color:#000000
    style A4 fill:#006400,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Reading the diagram:** The JMP classifies water services in five ascending rungs. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to identify the rung of a given village's water source in viva and design questions.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Five Drivers of Global WASH Challenges (Causal Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Population Growth] --> E[WASH Deficit]
    B[Climate Change] --> E
    C[Agricultural Demand] --> E
    D[Industrial Pollution] --> E
    F[Governance & Finance Gaps] --> E
    E --> G[Disease Burden]
    E --> H[Economic Loss]
    E --> I[Goal Inequity]
    G --> J[SDG 6 Targets at Risk]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    style E fill:#FF6347,color:#FFFFFF
    style J fill:#4682B4,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: MDG → SDG Transition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MDG 7C Era 2000-2015\nHalve the Proportion Without] --> B[MDG Achievement\n91% access to water\n65% access to sanitation]
    B --> C[Gaps Identified\nQuality\nEquity\nSustainability]
    C --> D[SDG 6 Era 2015-2030\nUniversal and Equitable Access]
    D --> E[Targets 6.1 Water\n6.2 Sanitation\n6.3 Wastewater\n6.4 Efficiency\n6.5 IWRM\n6.6 Ecosystems\n6.A Cooperation\n6.B Participation]
    style A fill:#4682B4,color:#FFFFFF
    style D fill:#228B22,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: WASH Service Chain — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Source[Water Source Layer]
        S1[Surface Water]
        S2[Groundwater]
        S3[Rainwater]
        S4[Desalination]
    end
    subgraph Treat[Treatment Layer]
        T1[Sedimentation]
        T2[Filtration]
        T3[Disinfection]
        T4[RO if needed]
    end
    subgraph Dist[Distribution Layer]
        D1[Piped Network]
        D2[Standposts]
        D3[Household Storage]
    end
    subgraph Sanit[Sanitation Layer]
        SA1[Onsite Containment]
        SA2[Septic Tank]
        SA3[Soak Pit]
        SA4[Treatment / Reuse]
    end
    S2 --> T1
    S3 --> T1
    S1 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> D1
    T1 --> T4
    T4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> SA1
    SA1 --> SA2
    SA2 --> SA3
    SA3 --> SA4
    style S2 fill:#87CEEB
    style T3 fill:#90EE90
    style D1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style SA2 fill:#DDA0DD
```

**Interpretation:** This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing how water flows from source → treatment → distribution → use → sanitation → disposal/reuse. It encapsulates the entire rural water supply and onsite sanitation system in a single schematic — exactly the type of diagram KTU examiners expect in 14-mark questions.

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Global Burden of Disease Pyramid (WASH Component)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Global Mortality\n~60 million per year] --> B[All Infectious Diseases\n~10 million]
    B --> C[Diarrhoeal Disease\n~1.5 million]
    C --> D[WASH-Attributable\n~829,000 deaths per year]
    D --> E[Children Under 5\n~300,000 deaths]
    style A fill:#696969,color:#FFFFFF
    style B fill:#8B4513,color:#FFFFFF
    style C fill:#B22222,color:#FFFFFF
    style D fill:#DC143C,color:#FFFFFF
    style E fill:#FF0000,color:#FFFFFF
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define WASH. List its three components and state the global body that monitors WASH progress.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Definition (1 mark):** WASH stands for **Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene** — an integrated framework addressing safe water supply, excreta disposal, and hygiene practices.
> - **Three components (1.5 marks):** **Water, Sanitation, Hygiene** (full forms).
> - **Monitoring body (0.5 mark):** **WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP)**.

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between MDG 7.C and SDG 6 with respect to water and sanitation targets.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **MDG 7.C (1.5 marks):** Aimed to **halve** the proportion of people without safe water and basic sanitation by 2015 — focused on **coverage** (binary: access or no access).
> - **SDG 6 (1.5 marks):** Aims for **universal and equitable** access to safely managed drinking water and sanitation by 2030 — focused on **service quality**, using the JMP service ladder (5 rungs: surface → safely managed).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — **14 Marks**
> **(a)** Describe the global water and sanitation scenario with reference to the WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP) service ladders for water and sanitation. Cite at least **four key statistics**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the transition from MDG 7.C to SDG 6. List **all eight SDG 6 targets** and discuss the role of engineering in achieving Target 6.2 (sanitation) and Target 6.3 (wastewater). **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Global Water and Sanitation Scenario (7 marks)**

The global water and sanitation scenario is tracked through the **JMP service ladders**, which classify services into five ascending rungs:

**Water Service Ladder (3.5 marks):**
1. **Surface water** – direct use of rivers, ponds, lakes.
2. **Unimproved** – unprotected wells/springs.
3. **Limited** – improved source but round-trip >30 min.
4. **Basic** – improved source, ≤30 min, available on demand.
5. **Safely managed** – free from contamination, available when needed.

**Sanitation Service Ladder (3.5 marks):**
1. Open defecation
2. Unimproved facilities
3. Limited (shared with other households)
4. Basic (improved, not shared)
5. Safely managed (excreta safely disposed)

**Key Statistics (from 2023 JMP report):**
- 2.2 billion lack safely managed drinking water.
- 4.2 billion lack safely managed sanitation.
- 494 million still practice open defecation.
- 829,000 annual diarrhoea deaths linked to inadequate WASH.
- 80% of global wastewater is discharged untreated.

---

**(b) MDG → SDG Transition and Engineering Role (7 marks)**

**Transition (3.5 marks):**
- **MDG 7.C (2000–2015):** Halve the proportion without access.
- **MDG Achievement:** Global access to safe water reached **91%** (target: 88%); sanitation reached **68%** (target: 75%) — sanitation target missed.
- **Lessons learned:** Mere *coverage* was insufficient; **quality, equity, and sustainability** were the missing dimensions.
- **SDG 6 (2015–2030):** A paradigm shift — focus on **safely managed** services, leaving no one behind.

**Eight SDG 6 Targets (2 marks — list & state):**
1. **6.1** Universal & equitable access to safe drinking water
2. **6.2** Access to adequate sanitation & hygiene; end open defecation
3. **6.3** Improve water quality; halve untreated wastewater
4. **6.4** Water-use efficiency & address scarcity
5. **6.5** Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM)
6. **6.6** Protect water-related ecosystems
7. **6.a** International cooperation & capacity-building
8. **6.b** Strengthen local community participation

**Engineering Role (1.5 marks):**
- **Target 6.2:** Design of low-cost on-site sanitation systems — twin-pit pour-flush latrines, septic tanks, soak pits, decentralized treatment.
- **Target 6.3:** Construction of wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs), DEWATS, reuse/recycle systems for greywater, industrial effluent treatment.

---

> **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — **14 Marks** *(Alternative choice)*
> **(a)** Identify and discuss the **five major drivers** of global water and sanitation challenges. For each driver, give **one engineering or policy response**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A rural town had a population of **25,000** in 2010. The decadal average increase is **3,000** and the incremental increase is **400**. Estimate the population in **2050** using the **Incremental Increase Method** and compute the **average and peak water demand** assuming rural LPCD = 135, peak factor = 2.5. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Five Drivers of Global WASH Challenges (7 marks — 1.4 marks each driver)**

| Driver | Description | Response |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Population growth & urbanization** | 7.9 B today → 9.7 B by 2050; slums lack formal services | Compact township planning, slum upgrading with piped water & toilets |
| **2. Climate change** | Droughts, floods, sea-level rise contaminate aquifers | Rainwater harvesting, managed aquifer recharge, climate-resilient design |
| **3. Agricultural demand** | 70% of freshwater withdrawals; food demand ↑ 60% by 2050 | Drip irrigation, water-efficient crops, watershed management |
| **4. Industrial pollution** | 80% wastewater untreated; heavy metal contamination | Effluent Treatment Plants (ETP), Common Effluent Treatment Plants (CETP) |
| **5. Governance & finance gaps** | US$ 114 B/year WASH funding shortfall | Public–Private Partnerships (PPP), Swachh Bharat Mission, Jal Jeevan Mission |

---

**(b) Population Forecast & Water Demand (7 marks)**

**Given:** $P_0 = 25{,}000$ (2010), $x = 3{,}000$, $y = 400$, target year = 2050.

**Step 1 — Number of decades**
$$n = \dfrac{2050 - 2010}{10} = 4 \text{ decades}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Incremental Increase formula**
$$P_n = P_0 + \dfrac{n}{2}\left[2x + (n-1)y\right]$$

$$P_4 = 25{,}000 + \dfrac{4}{2}\left[2(3{,}000) + (4-1)(400)\right]$$

$$P_4 = 25{,}000 + 2 \cdot \left[6{,}000 + 1{,}200\right]$$

$$P_4 = 25{,}000 + 2 \cdot 7{,}200 = 25{,}000 + 14{,}400 = 39{,}400 \text{ persons}$$

**Step 3 — Average water demand**
$$Q_{\text{avg}} = 39{,}400 \times 135 \text{ LPCD} = 5{,}319{,}000 \text{ L/d} = 5{,}319 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$

**Step 4 — Peak water demand**
$$Q_{\text{peak}} = 2.5 \times 5{,}319 = 13{,}297.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to litres per second**
$$Q_{\text{peak}} = \dfrac{13{,}297{,}500}{86{,}400} \approx 153.9 \text{ L/s}$$

**Final Answer:** Projected 2050 population = **39,400 persons**; Average demand = **5,319 m³/d**; Peak demand = **13,297.5 m³/d ≈ 153.9 L/s**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert L/d → m³/d → L/s. Always show all three unit conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Decade miscount:** Students often compute $n$ in *years* and forget to divide by 10. The Incremental Increase Method uses *decadal* increases.
> 3. **MDG vs SDG confusion:** Writing "SDG 6 aims to halve access" (wrong — that's MDG 7.C). SDG 6 is *universal* access.
> 4. **JMP ladder rung mix-up:** Listing "Safely Managed" as the *lowest* rung. The order is: Surface Water → Unimproved → Limited → Basic → **Safely Managed** (highest).
> 5. **Omitting peak factor:** The KTU rubric often deducts 0.5–1 mark for not applying the peak factor in water demand design.
> 6. **Treating $x$ and $y$ as annual:** They are **decadal** quantities. Multi-year confusion costs full marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic 1**

- **WASH** = Water + Sanitation + Hygiene (the master acronym).
- **Monitoring body:** WHO/UNICEF **JMP** (Joint Monitoring Programme).
- **JMP Service Ladder rungs (5 levels, both for water and sanitation):** Surface/Open Def. → Unimproved → Limited → Basic → **Safely Managed**.
- **Global headline numbers (must memorize):**
  - 2.2 B lack safely managed water
  - 4.2 B lack safely managed sanitation
  - 494 M practice open defecation
  - 829,000 WASH-deaths/year
  - 80% wastewater discharged untreated
- **MDG 7.C** (2000–2015): *Halve* the proportion; *coverage-based*.
- **SDG 6** (2015–2030): *Universal* access; *quality-based*; 8 targets (6.1 to 6.b).
- **5 drivers:** Population growth, climate change, agricultural demand, industrial pollution, governance/finance gaps.
- **Population forecasting methods (3 main):**
  - **Arithmetic** (short-term, ≤5 yr)
  - **Geometric** (long-term, constant % growth)
  - **Incremental Increase** (Indian standard, most common in KTU)
- **Canonical formulas:**
  - $P_n = P_0\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n$ (Geometric)
  - $P_n = P_0 + \dfrac{n}{2}\left[2x + (n-1)y\right]$ (Incremental Increase)
  - $Q = P \cdot q \cdot \text{PF}$ (Water demand)
- **Rural LPCD = 135; Urban LPCD = 200** (BIS 1172 standard).
- **Peak Factor (PF)** for rural communities = **2.0 to 3.0**.
- **Per capita wastewater = 0.80 × water demand** (80% rule).
- **Per capita BOD contribution ≈ 55 g/c/d** (for sewage design).
- **India-specific context:** Swachh Bharat Mission (2014), Jal Jeevan Mission (2019), National Water Policy (2012/2024) — all referenced in KTU viva questions.
- **Climate-WASH nexus:** Sea-level rise → coastal aquifer contamination (Kerala relevant); glacial retreat → river-flow reduction (Himalayan states).
- **Equity dimension:** Rural areas receive <20% of WASH budgets in low-income countries — a recurring exam theme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Water Crisis: Statistics and Trends
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Global Water Crisis: Statistics and Trends

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **Global Water Crisis** is formally defined as the escalating situation in which the aggregate demand for freshwater across agricultural, industrial, municipal, and ecological sectors consistently exceeds the naturally available supply of usable water in a region, leading to resource depletion, inequitable access, public health deterioration, geopolitical tension, and ecosystem collapse. The **United Nations (UN)** and the **World Health Organization (WHO)** describe it as a crisis of *quantity*, *quality*, and *access* — a tri-dimensional shortfall affecting human dignity and sustainable development.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the crisis is examined through three lenses:

- **Physical (or Absolute) Water Scarcity** — Insufficient natural water resources to meet regional demand.
- **Economic Water Scarcity** — Adequate water exists in nature, but human/institutional/financial capital is lacking to access it.
- **Water Stress** — A measurable index where annual withdrawals exceed 20%–40% of renewable resources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** The Global Water Crisis is the gap between **finite renewable freshwater supply** (~42,500 km³/year globally) and the **rising anthropogenic demand** driven by population growth, urbanization, industrialization, and climate change.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of Earth's freshwater as a **fixed bank account** that earns a small annual "interest" (renewable supply from rainfall, snowmelt, and recharge). The world keeps **withdrawing from this account** (consumption) at a rate much faster than the interest is deposited. Worse, the account is being **polluted** (water quality crisis), so the usable balance shrinks even when withdrawals are steady.

A more direct analogy: imagine a village of 100 families sharing **one hand pump**. If 30 families need it, it works fine. If 500 families queue for the same pump — a queue forms, women walk farther, children miss school, and disputes arise. This is precisely the **human face of the Global Water Crisis**, especially in rural settings.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Figures (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **2.2 billion** people lack safely managed drinking water services (WHO/UNICEF 2022).
- **4.2 billion** people lack safely managed sanitation.
- **829,000** annual deaths attributed to diarrhoea linked to unsafe water.
- **~70%** of global freshwater withdrawals go to **agriculture**.
- **~20%** to **industry**, **~10%** to **domestic** use.
- **1.7 billion** people live in river basins where water use exceeds recharge.
- By **2050**, up to **5.7 billion** people may live in water-scarce regions for at least one month per year.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Always quote the latest UN-Water / WHO-UNICEF JMP report figures. Numbers in 3-mark answers must be **specific** (e.g., "2.2 billion"), not vague ("a lot of people").

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Logic Breakdown

The Global Water Crisis is not a single phenomenon — it is a **compound crisis** arising from the interaction of multiple drivers. The logical chain of causation KTU examiners expect is:

- **Driver 1 — Population Growth:** World population has grown from ~2.5 billion (1950) to ~8 billion (2023), multiplying freshwater demand geometrically.
- **Driver 2 — Urbanization & Lifestyle Shift:** Urban residents consume 2–5× more water per capita than rural residents due to flush toilets, showers, and appliances.
- **Driver 3 — Agricultural Intensification:** Irrigation accounts for the bulk of water consumption. The Green Revolution tripled crop yields but quadrupled water demand.
- **Driver 4 — Industrialization:** Manufacturing, energy production (especially thermal and nuclear cooling), and mining use enormous volumes.
- **Driver 5 — Pollution:** ~80% of wastewater globally is discharged untreated, contaminating the very sources we depend on.
- **Driver 6 — Climate Change:** Alters precipitation patterns, intensifies droughts, accelerates glacier melt, and raises sea levels (salinizing coastal aquifers).
- **Driver 7 — Governance Failure:** Weak institutions, fragmented policies, lack of metering, and underpricing perpetuate wastage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of the Crisis:** Water is a *renewable* resource but the *recharge rate* is fixed by climate; the *demand* is what is growing unsustainably. This is a classic **stock-flow imbalance**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the key indices, formulas, thresholds, and units that KTU 2024 examiners frequently test in Module 1.

| **Index / Formula** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Threshold / Unit** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Per Capita Water Availability | $\displaystyle P_w = \frac{A_w}{P}$ | $\vert P_w < 1700\ \text{m}^3/\text{yr} \rightarrow$ **Water Stress**; $\vert P_w < 1000\ \text{m}^3/\text{yr} \rightarrow$ **Water Scarcity**; $\vert P_w < 500\ \text{m}^3/\text{yr} \rightarrow$ **Absolute Scarcity** | Falkenmark Indicator |
| Water Stress Index (WSI) | $\displaystyle \text{WSI} = \frac{TWR}{TRWR} \times 100$ | $\%$; WSI $\ge 25\%$: high stress; $\ge 40\%$: extremely high stress | Total Withdrawals / Total Renewable Water Resources |
| Water Footprint (per capita) | $\displaystyle WF = WF_{blue} + WF_{green} + WF_{grey}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{yr}/\text{capita}$; global avg $\approx 1240\ \text{m}^3/\text{yr}/\text{capita}$ | Hoekstra's classification |
| Minimum Domestic Need | $Q_{min} = 50\text{–}100\ \text{L/c/d}$ | $\text{L/person/day}$ (basic survival) | UN/WHO minimum standard |
| Optimal Daily Need | $Q_{opt} \approx 200\ \text{L/c/d}$ | $\text{L/person/day}$ (SDG-aligned) | Sustainable lifestyle benchmark |
| Population Growth Rate | $\displaystyle P_t = P_0 \cdot e^{rt}$ | dimensionless | Exponential projection |
| Water Demand Projection | $\displaystyle D_t = P_t \cdot q \cdot 365$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{yr}$ | Future demand estimation |
| Groundwater Recharge Rate | $R = P \cdot A \cdot k$ | $\text{mm/yr}$ or $\text{m}^3/\text{yr}$ | $P$=precipitation, $A$=area, $k$=infiltration coefficient |
| Water Poverty Index (WPI) | $\text{WPI} = f(R, A, C, U, D)$ | composite score 0–100 | Combines Resource, Access, Capacity, Use, Environment |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Planners** use the Falkenmark Indicator to classify cities as "water-secure" or "water-stressed" for master-plan formulation.
- **Hydrologists** apply WSI to river-basin management (e.g., Indus, Colorado, Murray-Darling basins).
- **Environmental Engineers** use Water Footprint analysis for industrial process redesign and corporate sustainability reporting.
- **Public Health Engineers** use minimum per capita needs (50 L/p/d) for rural water-supply scheme design — directly relevant to PECET867.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Derivation of Population-Based Water Demand

**Step 1 — Statement of Problem.** Given a region's current population, growth rate, and per capita water demand, estimate the future annual freshwater demand.

**Step 2 — Population Projection.** The population at time $t$ is given by the exponential growth model:

$$
P_t = P_0 \cdot e^{rt}
$$

where $P_0$ = base-year population, $r$ = annual growth rate (decimal), $t$ = years from base.

**Step 3 — Daily Water Demand.** The daily total demand is:

$$
Q_{day} = P_t \cdot q
$$

where $q$ is per capita demand in $\text{L/p/d}$.

**Step 4 — Annual Water Demand.** Converting to annual volume:

$$
D_t = P_t \cdot q \cdot 365
$$

**Step 5 — Volume in Million Cubic Metres (MCM).** For watershed-level planning, convert litres to cubic metres:

$$
D_t \big(\text{m}^3\big) = P_t \cdot q \cdot 365 \div 1000
$$

and to MCM:

$$
D_t \big(\text{MCM}\big) = \dfrac{P_t \cdot q \cdot 365}{10^9}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 3-Mark Practice)

> **Problem:** A rural district has a current population of **$2{,}00{,}000$** with an annual growth rate of **$1.8\%$**. The present per capita water demand is **$135\ \text{L/p/d}$**. Estimate the water demand (in MLD and MCM/yr) at the **design horizon of 30 years**.

**Step 1 — Population at year 30.**

$$
P_{30} = 2{,}00{,}000 \cdot e^{0.018 \times 30}
$$

$$
P_{30} = 2{,}00{,}000 \cdot e^{0.54}
$$

$$
P_{30} = 2{,}00{,}000 \cdot 1.7160 = 3{,}43{,}200\ \text{persons}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily Demand.**

$$
Q_{day} = 3{,}43{,}200 \times 135 = 4{,}63{,}32{,}000\ \text{L/day}
$$

$$
Q_{day} = 46.332\ \text{MLD} \quad (\text{Million Litres per Day})
$$

**Step 3 — Annual Demand in MCM.**

$$
D_{30} = 4{,}63{,}32{,}000 \times 365 \div 10^9
$$

$$
D_{30} = 16.911\ \text{MCM/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with Falkenmark Indicator.**

$$
P_w = \dfrac{A_w}{P_{30}} = \dfrac{169.11 \times 10^6}{3{,}43{,}200} \approx 492.7\ \text{m}^3/\text{yr}/\text{person}
$$

Since $P_w < 500\ \text{m}^3/\text{yr}$, the district falls in the **Absolute Scarcity** category — a typical KTU-rural-engineering scenario.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Drivers vs. Impact

The following matrix maps each crisis driver to its quantitative impact and the typical KTU-exam-relevant response.

| **Driver** | **Quantitative Impact** | **Engineering / Policy Response** |
|---|---|---|
| Population growth | Doubles demand every ~35 years at $r=2\%$ | Demand-side management, reuse, recycling |
| Urbanization | 2–5× per-capita demand increase | Urban rainwater harvesting, leak reduction |
| Agriculture (~70%) | $1\ \text{kg rice} \approx 2500\ \text{L water}$ | Drip irrigation, micro-irrigation |
| Industry (~20%) | $1\ \text{MWh}$ thermal power $\approx 1000\ \text{m}^3$ water | Closed-loop cooling, water-efficient tech |
| Pollution | $80\%$ wastewater discharged untreated | Decentralized wastewater treatment systems (DEWATS) |
| Climate change | $+20\%$ river-flow variability by 2050 | Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM) |
| Governance | Non-revenue water $30\%$–$40\%$ in cities | Metering, tariff reform, GIS-based NRW reduction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Driver–Impact–Response Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture** of the Global Water Crisis, showing the interlinkages between drivers, impacts, and engineering responses — perfectly suited to the descriptive block-diagram questions KTU examiners frequently set in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid

    A2[Urbanization] --> B1
    A3[Industrialization] --> B1

    A5[Pollution] --> B2[Quality Degradation]
    A6[Governance Failure] --> B3[Distribution Inequity]

    B1 --> C1[Quantitative Water Scarcity]
    B2 --> C2[Qualitative Water Scarcity]
    B3 --> C3[Economic Water Scarcity]

    C1 --> D1[Public Health Crisis]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C1 --> D2[Food Insecurity]
    C2 --> D3[Ecological Collapse]
    C1 --> D4[Geopolitical Conflict]

    D1 --> E1[Integrated Water Resources Management]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1

    E1 --> F1[Rainwater Harvesting]
    E1 --> F2[Wastewater Reuse and Recycling]
    E1 --> F3[Drip and Micro Irrigation]
    E1 --> F4[Demand Side Management and Metering]
    E1 --> F5[Decentralized Rural Sanitation]
```

### 4.2 Global Water Stress Distribution Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REGION_A[High Income Regions]
        nodeA1[North America]
        nodeA2[Europe]
        nodeA3[East Asia - Japan]
    end

    subgraph REGION_B[Water Stressed Regions]

    end

    end

    REGION_A -->|Withdrawals 10 to 20 percent| OUT1[Moderate Stress]
    REGION_B -->|Withdrawals 40 to 80 percent| OUT2[High Stress]
```
