
### 4.3 SDG 6 — Global Target Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SDG6[Sustainable Development Goal 6 - Clean Water and Sanitation] --> T1[6.1 Universal Safe Drinking Water by 2030]
    SDG6 --> T2[6.2 Universal Sanitation and Hygiene]
    SDG6 --> T3[6.3 Improve Water Quality and Reduce Pollution]
    SDG6 --> T4[6.4 Water Use Efficiency and Address Scarcity]
    SDG6 --> T5[6.5 Integrated Water Resources Management]
    SDG6 --> T6[6.6 Protect and Restore Water Related Ecosystems]
    SDG6 --> T7[6.A International Cooperation and Capacity Building]
    SDG6 --> T8[6.B Strengthen Local Community Participation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid is Used:** Mermaid renders natively inside most modern viewers (GitHub, VS Code, Obsidian, and KTU's recommended e-learning platforms). The diagrams are **text-based**, so they are easy to edit, version-control, and inspect for examiners and students alike.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question]**
Define the term **Global Water Crisis**. List any **four major drivers** responsible for the current crisis.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Global Water Crisis refers to the escalating situation where the **demand for freshwater** across agriculture, industry, and domestic sectors exceeds the **available supply** in terms of quantity, quality, and accessibility, threatening human health, ecosystems, and sustainable development.

Four major drivers are:

1. **Population growth** — rising from 2.5 billion in 1950 to ~8 billion in 2023.
2. **Climate change** — alters precipitation, intensifies droughts, and causes glacier retreat.
3. **Untreated wastewater discharge** — pollutes ~80% of global wastewater, shrinking usable supply.
4. **Agricultural intensification** — irrigation alone consumes ~70% of global freshwater.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question]**
Explain the **Falkenmark Indicator** for water scarcity. State the per-capita thresholds for *water stress*, *water scarcity*, and *absolute scarcity*.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Falkenmark Indicator**, developed by Malin Falkenmark (1989), is the **per capita annual water availability** $P_w$ expressed in $\text{m}^3/\text{person/year}$, calculated as:

$$
P_w = \frac{A_w}{P}
$$

where $A_w$ is the annual renewable freshwater and $P$ is the population.

The thresholds are:

- $P_w < 1700\ \text{m}^3/\text{yr}$ → **Water Stress**
- $P_w < 1000\ \text{m}^3/\text{yr}$ → **Water Scarcity**
- $P_w < 500\ \text{m}^3/\text{yr}$ → **Absolute Scarcity**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often quote the thresholds in the wrong unit (e.g., L/day) or mix up the order. **Memory aid:** *1700, 1000, 500* — memorize as descending, each ~half of the previous.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Q3. (A) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Model Question — 14 Marks]**

> *(a) Describe in detail the **statistics and global trends** of the water crisis with reference to WHO/UNICEF and UN-Water reports. Support with suitable data on water scarcity, sanitation, and health impact. (7 Marks)*
>
> *(b) Discuss the **major drivers** of the global water crisis and explain how climate change is amplifying each of them. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Statistics & Trends (7 Marks):**

According to the **WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP) 2022** and **UN-Water 2023 reports**:

- **2.2 billion** people lack safely managed drinking water services; **703 million** lack even basic drinking water.
- **4.2 billion** people lack safely managed sanitation; **494 million** still practice open defecation.
- **2 billion** people live in water-stressed countries, projected to rise to **5.7 billion by 2050**.
- Water-related diseases cause **~829,000 deaths annually**, of which ~297,000 are children under five.
- The **SDG 6 targets** are severely off-track: at current pace, achieving universal safely managed water by 2030 will require a **6× acceleration** in implementation.
- Groundwater depletion: India, China, USA, Iran, and Mexico account for ~70% of global groundwater loss; the North China Plain aquifer has fallen by ~40 m in 30 years.
- **80% of wastewater** is released into the environment without adequate treatment (UN-Water, 2023).
- **$260 billion/year** is the estimated economic cost of inadequate water and sanitation — a ~1.5% drag on global GDP.
- The **World Economic Forum's Global Risks Report** consistently ranks "water crisis" in the **top 5 global risks by impact**.

**Part (b) — Drivers & Climate Amplification (7 Marks):**

The principal drivers are:

1. **Demographic** — Population growth (8 billion, 2023) and urbanization (>56% urban by 2030).
2. **Economic** — Industrial expansion and rising per-capita consumption.
3. **Agricultural** — Irrigation for 70% of withdrawals, especially in Asia.
4. **Environmental** — Pollution, land-use change, and ecosystem degradation.
5. **Climatic** — Altered precipitation, glacier retreat, sea-level rise.
6. **Institutional** — Weak governance, fragmented institutions, underpricing.

**Climate change amplifies each:**

- It **increases variability** of rainfall, lengthening droughts and intensifying floods.
- It **accelerates glacier loss** in the Hindu Kush-Himalaya, threatening Indus, Ganges, and Brahmaputra flows.
- It **raises sea levels**, salinizing coastal aquifers in Bangladesh, Egypt, Vietnam, and small island nations.
- It **increases crop water demand** by 5–20% in arid regions due to higher evapotranspiration.
- It **reduces safe sanitation** windows by intensifying floods that contaminate water sources.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (a):** Do **not** just list statistics. Tie each number to a **trend** (rising/falling) and a **geography** (region-specific). KTU rewards answers that *interpret* data, not merely *state* it.

---

**Q3. (B) [Alternative Choice — KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern — 14 Marks]**

> *(a) With the help of suitable **formulas and worked examples**, explain the **Falkenmark Water Stress Index** and the **Water Poverty Index**. Discuss their limitations. (7 Marks)*
>
> *(b) Outline the **engineering and policy responses** to the global water crisis in rural and urban contexts, with specific reference to rainwater harvesting, drip irrigation, and decentralized sanitation. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Falkenmark & WPI (7 Marks):**

The **Falkenmark Water Stress Index (WSI)** measures the ratio of total freshwater withdrawals to renewable resources:

$$
\text{WSI} = \frac{TWR}{TRWR} \times 100
$$

**Worked Example:** If $TWR = 80\ \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$ and $TRWR = 200\ \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$,

$$
\text{WSI} = \frac{80}{200} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

A WSI of 40% signals **high water stress**, requiring urgent management interventions.

The **Water Poverty Index (WPI)** is a composite indicator (Sullivan, 2002) that combines five sub-indices:

$$
\text{WPI} = f(R, A, C, U, D)
$$

where $R$ = Resources, $A$ = Access, $C$ = Capacity, $U$ = Use, $D$ = Environment. Each is scored 0–100; higher WPI = better water security.

**Limitations:**

- WSI ignores **quality** and **seasonal variability**.
- WPI is data-intensive and may be inconsistent across regions.
- Neither captures **transboundary conflict** or **ecological flow requirements**.

**Part (b) — Engineering & Policy Responses (7 Marks):**

| **Context** | **Intervention** | **Benefit** |
|---|---|---|
| Rural | **Roof-top Rainwater Harvesting (RWH)** | Augments supply by 30–50% in 750 mm rainfall zones |
| Rural | **Drip Irrigation** | Saves 30–50% water; raises yield 20–40% |
| Rural | **DEWATS** (Decentralized Wastewater Treatment Systems) | Treats 90% BOD; recharges groundwater |
| Urban | **Smart Metering & SCADA** | Reduces Non-Revenue Water (NRW) from 40% to <15% |
| Urban | **Greywater Recycling** | Recovers 50–80 L/p/d for non-potable use |
| Policy | **Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM)** | Coordinates stakeholders across basin |
| Policy | **Water Tariff Reform** | Reduces wastage; subsidizes the poor via Lifeline Block Tariff |
| Policy | **National Water Mission (India)** | Targets 20% water savings through pricing & audits |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (a):** Students often write the **formula** but skip the **worked numerical example**. KTU 2024 pattern requires that every index/formula be backed by at least one **calculation**. Also, **limitations** carry 1–2 marks — never skip them.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- The **Global Water Crisis** is a *tri-dimensional* problem: **quantity, quality, access**.
- **Falkenmark thresholds:** $1700$, $1000$, $500\ \text{m}^3/\text{yr}$ per capita.
- **Withdrawals split:** Agriculture 70%, Industry 20%, Domestic 10%.
- **JMP 2022 headline figures:** 2.2 billion lack safe water; 4.2 billion lack safe sanitation.
- **Annual deaths:** ~829,000 from water-related diarrhoea.
- **SDG 6** is the United Nations' framework for water — targets 6.1 to 6.6 plus means-of-implementation 6.A and 6.B.
- **WSI formula:** $TWR \div TRWR \times 100$; thresholds 25% and 40%.
- **Minimum per capita domestic need:** **50–100 L/p/d**; optimum ~**200 L/p/d**.
- **Water Footprint** = Blue + Green + Grey.
- **Climate change** is the *force multiplier* of every other driver.
- **IWRM (Integrated Water Resources Management)** is the internationally accepted umbrella response strategy.
- **RWH, Drip Irrigation, DEWATS, Smart Metering, Greywater Reuse** are the key engineering levers.
- For 14-mark KTU answers, **always include**: definition → statistics → drivers → engineering response → policy → limitations.
- **Unit-conversion sanity check:** $1\ \text{MLD} = 10^6\ \text{L/day} = 1000\ \text{m}^3/\text{day} = 0.365\ \text{MCM/yr}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner's Mantra:** *Define crisply. Quote recent numbers. Show one worked calculation. End with at least one engineering/policy solution.* This structure consistently secures 12+ out of 14 marks in PECET867 Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Water and Sanitation in Rural Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Importance of Water and Sanitation in Rural Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Rural Water Supply and Sanitation (RWSS)** is the integrated provision of safe, adequate, and accessible drinking water, along with the safe management of human excreta, wastewater, and solid waste, in villages and rural settlements (typically defined by population $\leq$ 20,000 as per the **2011 Census of India** rural classification).

According to the **National Water Policy (2012, Ministry of Jal Shakti, Government of India)**, rural water supply aims to provide:

- A minimum of **55 litres per capita per day (lpcd)** for domestic needs in rural areas.
- Potable water within a walking distance of $\leq$ 50 metres in plains and $\leq$ 100 metres in hilly areas.
- Latrine coverage for every household under the **Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM-G Phase II)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECET867, Module 1):**
> The syllabus mandates understanding that rural water and sanitation are *not stand-alone engineering works* but are foundational **drivers of socio-economic development**. The 2024 scheme emphasizes Outcome-Based Education (OBE), and this topic is mapped to **CO1**: *“Describe the importance of safe water supply and sanitation in the holistic development of rural communities.”*

### 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — "The Village as a Living Body"

Imagine a rural village as a **human body**:
- **Drinking water** is the **bloodstream** that carries oxygen (nutrition, hygiene, economic productivity) to every cell (household).
- **Sanitation systems** are the **kidneys and excretory system** that filter out toxins (disease, contamination) and maintain internal purity.
- When either system fails, the body (village) falls sick — leading to disease outbreaks, school absenteeism, reduced agricultural output, and economic stagnation.

In this analogy, the **engineer (you, the future B.Tech graduate)** acts as the **physician** — diagnosing infrastructure deficiencies, prescribing sustainable systems, and monitoring long-term health of the community.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Global Statistics (as per WHO/UNICEF JMP, 2023):**
> - **703 million** people globally still lack access to safe water.
> - **2.4 billion** people lack adequate sanitation facilities.
> - Diarrhoeal diseases claim approximately **1,000 children under 5 every day** globally — over **80%** of which are attributable to unsafe water, poor sanitation, and inadequate hygiene (WASH).
> - In India, ~**16.6%** of rural households lacked tap water connections as of 2019 (pre-Jal Jeevan Mission baseline).

### 1.3 Core Linkages in Rural Development

| Pillar of Development | Role of Safe Water | Role of Sanitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Public Health** | Reduces waterborne diseases (cholera, typhoid) | Breaks faecal-oral transmission route |
| **Education** | WASH in schools → 10–15% rise in girls' enrolment | Reduces school absenteeism by ~**25%** |
| **Gender Equity** | Reduces women's time fetching water (often 6 hrs/day) | Provides dignity and safety for women/girls |
| **Economic Productivity** | Healthy workforce → higher farm output | Saves ~**$1.6 billion/year** (India) in healthcare costs |
| **Environmental Sustainability** | Protects aquifers from over-extraction | Prevents groundwater contamination (e.g., nitrate leaching) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Multi-Dimensional Framework: Why WASH is Foundational

Rural water and sanitation is best understood as a **pyramid of interdependent layers**, often called the **WASH Sustainability Framework** (WHO/UNDP). Each layer depends on the one below:

- **Layer 1 — Health & Survival (Base):** Without water, survival itself is threatened. The human body can survive weeks without food but only 3–5 days without water.
- **Layer 2 — Livelihoods & Productivity:** Time saved from water-fetching translates directly into productive economic activity.
- **Layer 3 — Education & Gender Equity:** WASH in schools is a precondition for quality learning, especially for adolescent girls.
- **Layer 4 — Social Dignity & Inclusion:** Sanitation restores dignity, especially to women, elderly, and persons with disabilities.
- **Layer 5 — Environmental Sustainability (Apex):** Long-term water source protection ensures intergenerational equity.

### 2.2 The Five-Point Justification (KTU Board Favourite)

Examiners in KTU 2024 scheme frequently ask: *"Why is rural water supply and sanitation important?"* Use this **5-point technical structure** to score full marks:

1. **Public Health Imperative:** Waterborne diseases account for ~**80%** of communicable disease burden in rural India (Ministry of Health, 2017).
2. **Economic Imperative:** Every **₹1** invested in water and sanitation yields a return of **₹4–₹34** (WHO 2012 study, updated 2023 estimates).
3. **Gender Equity Imperative:** Women and girls spend **~6 hours/day** collecting water in water-stressed regions (UN Women data).
4. **Educational Imperative:** Sanitation in schools reduces drop-out rates among adolescent girls by up to **30%** (UNESCO 2018).
5. **Environmental Imperative:** Onsite sanitation (e.g., septic tanks, soak pits) prevents **F-diagram pathway** contamination: Fluids, Fields, Flies, Fingers, Food.

### 2.3 The F-Diagram: Critical for KTU 2024 Examinations

The **F-diagram** (often used in Community Health Engineering) is a high-yield topic. It describes the **faecal-oral transmission route** of pathogens. Sanitation works by **breaking** as many F-pathways as possible:

- **F1 — Faeces** (safe disposal = improved sanitation)
- **F2 — Fingers** (handwashing with soap)
- **F3 — Flies** (proper latrine covers)
- **F4 — Fields / Floors** (preventing open defecation)
- **F5 — Fluids / Food** (safe water + clean food preparation)

A correctly designed sanitation system **interrupts a minimum of 3 of the 5 F-pathways**, dramatically reducing disease transmission.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the **core quantitative metrics** you must memorize for the ESE (End Semester Examination) of PECET867.

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Unit | Remarks / KTU Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rural per capita water demand | $Q = P \times q$ | lpcd | $P$ = population, $q$ = per capita demand (≥ **55 lpcd** as per Jal Jeevan Mission) |
| 2 | Total village water requirement | $Q_{total} = 1.10 \times Q$ | litres/day | Includes 10% allowance for cattle/waste |
| 3 | Population forecast (Arithmetic method) | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | persons | $n$ = design period in decades; $\bar{x}$ = decadal growth |
| 4 | Population forecast (Geometric method) | $P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{n}$ | persons | $r$ = growth rate (%) |
| 5 | Sanitation coverage gap | $Gap = \dfrac{HH_{no\text{-}latrine}}{HH_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Used in SBM-G progress reports |
| 6 | Cost–Benefit Ratio (CBR) | $CBR = \dfrac{\text{Present Worth of Benefits}}{\text{Present Worth of Costs}}$ | dimensionless | Acceptable if $CBR \geq 1.0$ |
| 7 | Time saved per household | $\Delta T = T_{before} - T_{after}$ | hrs/day | Used to estimate economic uplift |
| 8 | Disease incidence reduction | $R = \dfrac{D_{baseline} - D_{after}}{D_{baseline}} \times 100$ | % | Sanitation impact assessment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In the KTU 2024 valuation key, the examiner will deduct marks if you use **urban water supply norms (135 lpcd)** for a rural problem statement. Always default to **55 lpcd** unless explicitly stated otherwise (e.g., institutional demand in schools/hospitals may be additional).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

This conceptual framework is the **bedrock** for:
- Designing rural piped water supply schemes (Jal Jeevan Mission).
- Planning village-level Septic Tank + Soak Pit (STS) units.
- Conducting Baseline Surveys under SBM-G Phase II.
- Preparing Detailed Project Reports (DPR) for MGNREGA/State Plan schemes.
- Environmental Impact Assessments (EIA) for rural water resource projects.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Arithmetic Population Forecast for a Rural Scheme

The **Arithmetic Increase Method** is a foundational forecasting tool. It assumes the population grows by a **constant numerical increase** between successive decades.

**Step 1 — Statement of the Model**

Let $P_0$ be the population at the **base year (census year)**. Let $\bar{x}$ be the **average decadal increase** observed over the past three census decades. The population at the **end of design period $n$ decades** is denoted $P_n$.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Expression**

$$
P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example (KTU Typical Style)**

A rural panchayat had the following census populations:

| Census Year | Population |
|---|---|
| 1991 | 8,000 |
| 2001 | 9,500 |
| 2011 | 11,000 |

Design the water supply scheme for the year **2041** (i.e., 3 decades after 2011). Compute $P_{2041}$.

**Step 4 — Calculate Average Decadal Increase**

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{(9{,}500 - 8{,}000) + (11{,}000 - 9{,}500)}{2}
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{1{,}500 + 1{,}500}{2} = 1{,}500 \text{ persons/decade}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Formula**

$$
P_{2041} = P_{2011} + n \cdot \bar{x} = 11{,}000 + 3 \times 1{,}500
$$

$$
P_{2041} = 11{,}000 + 4{,}500 = 15{,}500 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Total Water Demand**

Using the rural norm $q = 55$ lpcd:

$$
Q = P_{2041} \times q = 15{,}500 \times 55
$$

$$
Q = 8{,}52{,}500 \text{ litres/day} = 852.5 \text{ kL/day}
$$

This value is the **design demand** for sizing the Overhead Tank (OHT), pipe diameters, and source yield requirements.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cost–Benefit Logic for Sanitation Investment

For a KTU 14-mark question, you may be asked to justify a sanitation scheme economically. Use the **Net Present Value (NPV) framework**:

**Step 1 — Define Annual Benefit**

$$
B = B_{health} + B_{time} + B_{education}
$$

where:
- $B_{health}$ = savings in medical expenditure due to reduced waterborne disease
- $B_{time}$ = economic value of time saved (especially by women)
- $B_{education}$ = long-term productivity from improved school attendance

**Step 2 — Annualized Cost**

$$
C = \dfrac{(C_{cap} \times CRF) + C_{O\&M}}{1}
$$

where $CRF$ = Capital Recovery Factor:

$$
CRF = \dfrac{i (1+i)^{N}}{(1+i)^{N} - 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision Rule**

$$
\boxed{\text{Accept the project if } NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{(B_t - C_t)}{(1+i)^t} \geq 0}
$$

For rural WASH schemes, $N$ is typically **15–30 years** (project life of soak pits, septic tanks, pipelines) and $i$ is the social discount rate, usually **5–8%** in India.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Development Indicators Before vs. After WASH Intervention

| Indicator | Pre-Intervention (Typical Rural India) | Post-Intervention (Target) | Source / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open Defecation rate | ~ **15–25%** (pre-SBM era) | **0%** | SBM-G Phase I goal |
| Water-fetching time (women) | **30–120 min/trip** | $\leq$ **15 min** | Jal Jeevan Mission goal |
| Diarrhoeal incidence (under-5) | **~13.2 episodes/child-year** | Reduction by $\geq$ **40%** | WHO WASH impact study |
| School attendance (girls, adolescent) | Drops ~**20%** during menstruation | Sustained **95%+** | UNESCO 2018 |
| Piped water coverage | **17%** (2019) | **100%** by 2024 | JJM target |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Interconnection of WASH with Rural Development Pillars

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates how water and sanitation act as **foundational enablers** cascading into all other rural development outcomes. This type of diagram is highly valued in KTU answer sheets.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Safe Water Supply]:::coreNode
    B[Improved Sanitation]:::coreNode
    C[Hygiene Promotion]:::coreNode

    D[Public Health Improvement]:::outNode
    E[Reduced Water-Borne Disease]:::outNode
    F[Lower Infant Mortality]:::outNode

    G[Gender Equity]:::outNode
    H[Time Saved for Women and Girls]:::outNode
    I[Increased Female Workforce Participation]:::outNode

    J[Educational Outcomes]:::outNode
    K[Higher School Enrolment]:::outNode
    L[Lower Drop-out Rates]:::outNode

    M[Economic Productivity]:::outNode
    N[Healthy Agricultural Workforce]:::outNode
    O[Reduced Healthcare Expenditure]:::outNode

    P[Environmental Sustainability]:::outNode
    Q[Protected Groundwater Aquifers]:::outNode
    R[Reduced Open Defecation Pollution]:::outNode

    A --> D
    A --> G
    A --> J
    A --> M
    A --> P

    B --> D
    B --> G
    B --> J
    B --> M
    B --> P

    C --> D
    C --> G
    C --> J
    C --> M
    C --> P

    D --> E
    D --> F

    G --> H
    H --> I

    J --> K
    J --> L

    M --> N
    M --> O

    P --> Q
    P --> R

    classDef coreNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outNode fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The three "core nodes" (Water, Sanitation, Hygiene) collectively form the **WASH triad** — they jointly trigger every downstream development outcome. Notice that **Hygiene Promotion** is often overlooked in engineering curricula but is rated as the **most cost-effective WASH intervention** by WHO.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Rural Water Supply Chain (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source: Borewell or Surface Reservoir]:::sourceNode
    pump[Pump Set: Submersible or Centrifugal]:::pumpNode
    st[Storage Tank: Sumps and Break Pressure Tanks]:::storageNode
    treat[Water Treatment: Filtration and Chlorination]:::treatNode
    oht[Overhead Tank: Distribution Reservoir]:::ohtNode
    net[Distribution Network: PVC or HDPE Mains]:::netNode
    hh[Rural Households: Tap Connections]:::hhNode
    drain[Drainage of Wastewater: Soak Pits]:::drainNode

    src --> pump
    pump --> st
    st --> treat
    treat --> oht
    oht --> net
    net --> hh
    hh --> drain

    classDef sourceNode fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef pumpNode fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef storageNode fill:#0277bd,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef treatNode fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef ohtNode fill:#00838f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef netNode fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef hhNode fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef drainNode fill:#5d4037,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — The Sanitation Ladder (Adapted from WHO/UNICEF JMP)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Open Defecation: Unimproved Practice]:::lowNode
    L2[Service Level 1: Unimproved Facility]:::lowNode
    L3[Service Level 2: Basic Pit Latrine]:::midNode
    L4[Service Level 3: Twin Pit Pour Flush]:::midNode
    L5[Service Level 4: Septic Tank with Soak Pit]:::highNode
    L6[Service Level 5: Community Sewerage with STP]:::highNode

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6

    classDef lowNode fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef midNode fill:#f57c00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef highNode fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Context:** India’s SBM-G Phase II promotes progression from **Level 3 → Level 4/5**. The KTU examiner expects students to know that the "Sanitation Ladder" is the **standard assessment tool** used in MHRD and WHO reports for evaluating rural sanitation status.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, Dec 2024 (Expected)]**
*Define the term "Rural Water Supply" as per the National Water Policy. What is the minimum per capita water supply norm prescribed for rural areas in India?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Rural water supply is the provision of safe, adequate, and accessible drinking water to villages and rural settlements for domestic, livestock, and small-scale institutional needs. As per the **National Water Policy (2012)** and the operational guidelines of the **Jal Jeevan Mission (2019)**, the minimum prescribed per capita norm for rural domestic water supply is **55 litres per capita per day (lpcd)**, with the goal of providing Functional Household Tap Connections (FHTC) to every rural household.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mentioning Jal Jeevan Mission, 1 mark for the 55 lpcd norm.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
*Explain the "F-diagram" in the context of rural sanitation. Why is it important in public health engineering?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **F-diagram** is a conceptual model used to describe the **faecal-oral transmission route** of pathogens. It identifies five pathways by which faecal matter contaminates a new host: **Fluids, Fields (Floors), Fingers, Flies, and Food**. Improved sanitation and hygiene interventions are designed to "break" these F-pathways — for instance, handwashing breaks the **Finger** route, while latrine use breaks the **Faeces** route. It is a fundamental public health tool because it provides a **systematic engineering and behaviour-change framework** for prioritizing low-cost interventions in rural communities, ensuring that infrastructure investments yield maximum disease-reduction impact per rupee spent.

*(Award: 1 mark for naming 5 F-routes, 1 mark for the "breaking the chain" logic, 1 mark for public health relevance.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Choice)

**Q. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, ESE Module 1]**
*(Choose either A or B)*

---

#### **Option A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **importance of safe water supply and sanitation for rural development** in India. Your answer must cover at least **five dimensions** with supporting data. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rural panchayat had census populations of 4,800 in 2001 and 5,250 in 2011. Using the **Arithmetic Increase Method**, estimate the population in the year **2041** and compute the total design water demand in litres per day using the rural norm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

The importance of safe water and sanitation in rural development can be understood through five interconnected dimensions:

**(1) Public Health Dimension (2 marks):**
Waterborne diseases such as cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A, and diarrhoea account for nearly **80% of the communicable disease burden** in rural India. Safe water supply combined with improved sanitation breaks the **faecal-oral transmission route (F-diagram)**, reducing infant and child mortality significantly. The WHO estimates that every **$1 invested in WASH yields $4 in healthcare savings**.

**(2) Economic Dimension (1.5 marks):**
Poor health due to unsafe water reduces agricultural productivity (rural workforce is largely agrarian). Studies (World Bank, 2016) show that improved WASH raises labour productivity by **1–2%** and increases household income by up to **10%** over a decade.

**(3) Gender Equity Dimension (1.5 marks):**
In water-stressed rural areas, women and girls spend an average of **30 minutes to 6 hours per day** fetching water. This robs them of education and economic opportunities. Tap water at the doorstep (Jal Jeevan Mission) **restores dignity, safety, and time** — directly empowering women.

**(4) Educational Dimension (1 mark):**
Lack of separate toilets for girls in schools is a primary reason for **drop-out** at the adolescent level. UNESCO data (2018) confirms that providing gender-sensitive WASH facilities in schools **reduces absenteeism by 25–30%**, especially among girls.

**(5) Environmental Dimension (1 mark):**
Onsite sanitation systems like septic tanks and soak pits, when properly designed, **prevent nitrate contamination of groundwater** and reduce surface water pollution from open defecation, ensuring **sustainable water resources for future generations**.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $P_{2001} = 4{,}800$ persons
- $P_{2011} = 5{,}250$ persons
- Design year = 2041, so $n = 3$ decades after 2011
- Rural per capita norm $q = 55$ lpcd

**Step 1 — Compute Average Decadal Increase**

$$
\bar{x} = P_{2011} - P_{2001} = 5{,}250 - 4{,}800 = 450 \text{ persons/decade}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Arithmetic Increase Formula**

$$
P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}
$$

$$
P_{2041} = 5{,}250 + 3 \times 450
$$

$$
P_{2041} = 5{,}250 + 1{,}350 = 6{,}600 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Design Water Demand**

$$
Q = P_{2041} \times q
$$

$$
Q = 6{,}600 \times 55 = 3{,}63{,}000 \text{ litres/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Add 10% Allowance for Cattle and Wastage**

$$
Q_{design} = 1.10 \times 3{,}63{,}000 = 3{,}99{,}300 \text{ litres/day} \approx 399.3 \text{ kL/day}
$$

**Step 5 — State Engineering Implications**
This value will be used to size the **Overhead Tank (OHT) capacity, distribution main diameter, source yield, and treatment unit capacity**.

---

#### **Option B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **F-diagram** in detail. With a neat diagram, describe how improved sanitation **interrupts each of the five F-pathways**. Why is the F-diagram considered a cornerstone of rural public health engineering? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Briefly outline the **Salient Features of the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** and the **Swachh Bharat Mission – Gramin (SBM-G) Phase II**. Explain how these two flagship programmes are interdependent in driving holistic rural development. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The F-diagram is a public health model that traces the **faecal-oral transmission cycle** of pathogens. The "F"s stand for **Faeces, Fluids, Fields/Floors, Fingers, Flies, and Food**. (Note: Often the 6th F is "Fomites" but KTU 2024 syllabus expects the 5-F classic model.)

**Five F-Pathways and Interventions (4 marks):**

| Pathway | Description | Sanitation/Hygiene Intervention |
|---|---|---|
| **Faeces** | Open defecation pollutes environment | Use of **Twin-Pit Pour-Flush latrines** |
| **Fluids** | Contaminated drinking water | **Safe piped water supply** with chlorination |
| **Fields/Floors** | Soil contamination near homes | **Sanitary disposal of child faeces**, soak pits |
| **Fingers** | Hand-to-mouth contamination | **Handwashing with soap** at critical times |
| **Flies** | Insect vectors on exposed faeces | **Covered latrines**, screened ventilation |
| **Food** | Contaminated food preparation | **Safe water for cooking**, hygienic practices |

**Why Cornerstone (1.5 marks):**
The F-diagram is considered a **cornerstone** because it provides:
- A **visual, low-literacy-friendly** tool for community health workers to use during IEC (Information, Education, Communication) campaigns.
- A **cost-prioritization framework** — engineers can identify which "F" to break first for maximum impact (typically sanitation + safe water = 3 of 5 broken).
- A **measurable evaluation metric** — SBM-G Phase I outcome evaluation used F-pathway indicators.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) — Key Features (3 marks):**
- **Launch:** August 15, 2019 by Government of India.
- **Vision:** Provide **Functional Household Tap Connections (FHTC)** to every rural household by **2024** with 55 lpcd of potable water.
- **Budget:** ₹3.60 lakh crore (estimated).
- **Institutional mechanism:** Village Water & Sanitation Committees (VWSC) under each Gram Panchayat.
- **Technology focus:** Use of IoT-based monitoring, solar-powered pumps, and greywater management.

**SBM-G Phase II — Key Features (2 marks):**
- **Launch:** February 2020 (Phase I was Oct 2014 – Oct 2019; declared India ODF on Oct 2, 2019).
- **Vision:** Sustain ODF (Open Defecation Free) status and convert all **dump sites to sanitary landfills**; ensure **biodegradable waste management** at village level.
- **Components:** Greywater management, plastic waste management, faecal sludge management.

**Interdependence (2 marks):**
JJM provides **safe water input** (drinking, handwashing, food preparation), while SBM-G Phase II provides the **safe containment and disposal output** (sanitary latrines, sludge management). Together they form a **closed-loop WASH system** — water enters the household clean, and waste exits the household safely without contaminating the **same source** of water. This circular logic is the **engineering essence of the National Rural Drinking Water Programme (NRDWP)**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Using urban water norms (135 lpcd) in rural problems** — Automatic 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing SBM (Swachh Bharat Mission) with SBM-G** — SBM has Urban (SBM-U) and Gramin (SBM-G) components. Specify the rural version.
> 3. **Forgetting the 10% cattle/wastage allowance** in demand calculations — Examiner's key explicitly lists this.
> 4. **Listing development benefits without data** — Every "importance" question in KTU 2024 expects at least **2 numerical data points** (e.g., $1 → $4 ROI, 55 lpcd, 6 hours water-fetching).
> 5. **Sketching the F-diagram poorly** — If the question asks for a diagram (7 marks), a missing/poor sketch can cost 2–3 marks instantly.
> 6. **Mixing Jal Jeevan Mission and NRDWP** — JJM is the **2019 successor** to NRDWP; do not use the older acronym unless the question specifies the pre-2019 era.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Rural water norm = 55 lpcd** (not 135 lpcd, which is urban).
- **Three flagship programmes** to remember: **JJM (2019), SBM-G Phase II (2020), NRDWP** (predecessor of JJM).
- **F-diagram** has 5 (or 6) F's — always list at least 3 in your answer.
- **Sanitation Ladder** has 5 levels — India is moving from Level 3 to Levels 4/5 under SBM-G II.
- **ROI of WASH investment** = ₹4 to ₹34 per ₹1 spent (WHO 2012).
- **Water-fetching time saved** = real, measurable economic empowerment for women.
- **Arithmetic method** is used when growth is **linear/constant**; **Geometric method** for **exponential growth**; **Incremental Increase method** for **changing rates**.
- **Closed-loop WASH logic**: Safe Water Input (JJM) + Safe Sanitation Output (SBM-G) = Sustainable Rural Health.
- **F-Diagram** is the **most frequently asked diagram** in KTU Module 1 — practice drawing it with clean labels.
- **CO mapping:** This topic covers **CO1** of PECET867 — *"Describe the importance of water supply and sanitation in rural development."*
- **Cognitive level (Bloom's Taxonomy):** Primarily **Understand (L2)** and **Apply (L3)** for 14-mark questions; **Remember (L1)** for 3-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) Related to Water and Sanitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) Related to Water and Sanitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS (SDGs) RELATED TO WATER AND SANITATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** are a universal call to action adopted by all United Nations Member States in 2015, as part of the **2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development**. The SDGs comprise **17 interconnected global goals** designed to be a "blueprint to achieve a better and more sustainable future for all." For the discipline of Civil and Environmental Engineering, particularly Rural Water Supply and Onsite Sanitation, the most directly relevant goal is **SDG 6: "Ensure availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SDG 6 – Clean Water and Sanitation (Official UN Definition):**
> "Ensure availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all" — adopted by the UN General Assembly in Resolution A/RES/70/1, transforming the earlier **Millennium Development Goals (MDGs)** into a broader, more inclusive 2030 framework. It has **8 targets** and **11 indicators** as of the 2024 revision.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Village Well" Story**
> Imagine a village of 500 families. For centuries, they all drew water from a single community well. Then pollution entered the well, and the village split into two groups — one wanted to purify the water (engineers), one wanted to build a new well far upstream (planners), and one wanted to ignore the problem (a few residents). The **SDGs** are like a master agreement signed by *everyone* in the village saying: "We will all do our part — the engineers will purify, the planners will build, the residents will stop polluting, and the government will fund it." The deadline is **2030**, and progress is measured every year.

- The **17 SDGs** are like 17 chapters in this agreement.
- **SDG 6 (Water)** and **SDG 3 (Health)** are the "engineer" chapters for our course.
- The targets are the **measurable promises**, and the indicators are the **meters** that track success.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Highlighted)

- **2030 Agenda** — the 15-year global action plan adopted in **September 2015**.
- **17 Goals, 169 Targets, 231 Indicators** (as per the 2024 UN Statistical Commission update).
- **MDGs (2000–2015)** — predecessors; had only 8 goals with a narrower focus.
- **JMP (Joint Monitoring Programme)** — WHO/UNICEF body that publishes the official global figures for water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH).
- **Indicator 6.1.1** — *Proportion of population using safely managed drinking water services.*
- **Indicator 6.2.1** — *Proportion of population using (a) safely managed sanitation services and (b) a hand-washing facility with soap and water.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 17 SDGs at a Glance (Engineering-Relevant Subset)

For our course (PECET867), the following goals have direct or indirect bearing on rural water supply and onsite sanitation systems:

| SDG | Title | Direct Link to Water/Sanitation |
|-----|-------|----------------------------------|
| **SDG 1** | No Poverty | Affordability of WASH services |
| **SDG 2** | Zero Hunger | Water for irrigation & food security |
| **SDG 3** | Good Health & Well-Being | Reduction in waterborne diseases |
| **SDG 5** | Gender Equality | Women/girls spend less time fetching water |
| **SDG 6** | Clean Water & Sanitation | **Primary goal** — entire scope |
| **SDG 9** | Industry, Innovation & Infrastructure | Piped networks, treatment plants |
| **SDG 11** | Sustainable Cities & Communities | Urban WASH, slum upgrading |
| **SDG 12** | Responsible Consumption & Production | Water reuse, recycling |
| **SDG 13** | Climate Action | Resilience of WASH systems to floods/droughts |
| **SDG 14** | Life Below Water | Pollution control, marine outfalls |
| **SDG 15** | Life on Land | Watershed protection, groundwater recharge |
| **SDG 17** | Partnerships | Funding, technology transfer for rural WASH |

### 2.2 Detailed Targets of SDG 6 (High-Yield for Examinations)

SDG 6 has **8 targets**, conventionally split into:
- **6.1 – 6.6**: Outcome targets (technical/operational)
- **6.A – 6.B**: Means-of-implementation targets (finance, capacity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8 Targets of SDG 6 (Memorize These for KTU):**
> 1. **6.1** — By 2030, achieve universal and equitable access to safe and affordable drinking water for all.
> 2. **6.2** — By 2030, achieve access to adequate and equitable sanitation and hygiene for all and end open defecation, with special attention to the needs of women and girls and those in vulnerable situations.
> 3. **6.3** — By 2030, improve water quality by reducing pollution, eliminating dumping and minimizing release of hazardous chemicals and materials, halving the proportion of untreated wastewater and substantially increasing recycling and safe reuse globally.
> 4. **6.4** — By 2030, substantially increase water-use efficiency across all sectors and ensure sustainable withdrawals and supply of freshwater to address water scarcity and substantially reduce the number of people suffering from water scarcity.
> 5. **6.5** — By 2030, implement integrated water resources management (IWRM) at all levels, including through transboundary cooperation.
> 6. **6.6** — By 2020, protect and restore water-related ecosystems, including mountains, forests, wetlands, rivers, aquifers and lakes.
> 7. **6.A** — By 2030, expand international cooperation, capacity-building support and technology transfer to developing countries in water- and sanitation-related activities and programmes, including water harvesting, desalination, water efficiency, wastewater treatment, recycling and reuse technologies.
> 8. **6.B** — Support and strengthen the participation of local communities in improving water and sanitation management.

### 2.3 The "Service Ladders" — A Core Concept

The JMP classifies WASH services into a graduated "ladder" of improvement. **This is a frequently tested concept.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Drinking Water Ladder (5 Rungs):**
> 1. **Safely Managed** — On premises, available when needed, free from contamination.
> 2. **Basic** — From an improved source, ≤ 30 min round trip.
> 3. **Limited** — From an improved source, > 30 min round trip.
> 4. **Unimproved** — From an unprotected well or spring.
> 5. **Surface Water** — Directly from rivers, ponds, canals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanitation Service Ladder (5 Rungs):**
> 1. **Safely Managed** — Improved facility not shared, excreta safely disposed *in situ* or off-site.
> 2. **Basic** — Improved facility not shared (e.g., household pour-flush latrine).
> 3. **Limited** — Improved facility **shared** between two or more households.
> 4. **Unimproved** — Pit latrine without slab, hanging latrines, bucket latrines.
> 5. **Open Defecation** — Disposal of human faeces in open fields, forests, water bodies.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Definition / Formula | Unit | KTU Relevance |
|---|---------|----------------------|------|---------------|
| 1 | % Population using safely managed water | $P_w = \dfrac{N_{safely}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Indicator 6.1.1 |
| 2 | % Population using safely managed sanitation | $P_s = \dfrac{N_{safely\_san}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Indicator 6.2.1a |
| 3 | Per capita water availability | $W_{pc} = \dfrac{Q_{annual}}{P \times 365 \times 86400}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{day}/\text{capita}$ | Scarcity threshold = $\mathbf{1700\ \text{m}^3/yr}$ |
| 4 | Water Stress Index (WSI) | $WSI = \dfrac{Total\ Freshwater\ Withdrawal}{Total\ Renewable\ Freshwater\ Resources} \times 100$ | % | Stress if $> 25\%$; scarcity if $> 40\%$ |
| 5 | Wastewater Treatment Coverage | $C_{ww} = \dfrac{Q_{treated}}{Q_{generated}} \times 100$ | % | Target 6.3 (50% untreated reduction) |
| 6 | Water-Use Efficiency (WUE) | $WUE = \dfrac{Economic\ Output}{Water\ Consumed}$ | $\text{INR/m}^3$ or $\text{USD/m}^3$ | Target 6.4 |
| 7 | Faecal Sludge Treatment Recovery | $R_{FS} = \dfrac{V_{treated}}{V_{generated}} \times 100$ | % | Onsite sanitation core metric |
| 8 | Open Defecation Rate (ODR) | $ODR = \dfrac{N_{OD}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Pre-SBM baseline ~ 550M in India (2014) |
| 9 | Functionality Rate of Schemes | $F_{rate} = \dfrac{N_{functional}}{N_{installed}} \times 100$ | % | JJM benchmark ≥ 90% |
| 10 | BOD Loading Rate | $L_{BOD} = Q \times C_{BOD}$ | $\text{kg/day}$ | Pollution indicator for 6.3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Numerical Thresholds (often asked):**
> - **Water scarcity**: < **1700 m³/year/capita** (UN standard)
> - **Absolute scarcity**: < **500 m³/year/capita**
> - **Open Defecation Free (ODF)** status: ODR = 0%
> - **Swachh Bharat Mission (Gramin)** target: 100% toilet coverage by 2 Oct 2019 ✅ achieved
> - **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** target: 100% Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC) by **2024** (extended to 2028 for residual)
> - **SDG deadline**: 31 December **2030**

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Designers** of rural schemes use SDG indicators as Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for government contracts.
- **Funders** (World Bank, ADB, JICA) tie loans to SDG indicator progress.
- **ISRO, NRSC, IIT-Bombay's C-ward dashboards** track India's SDG 6 progress in real time using satellite data and the *India SDG Dashboard* (NITI Aayog).
- **Engineers in private consultancy** must now incorporate the "**safely managed**" criterion in DPRs (Detailed Project Reports) — meaning design must include water quality testing and faecal sludge management, not just infrastructure.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: SDG Indicator 6.1.1 — % Safely Managed Drinking Water

The JMP defines a population as using "safely managed" drinking water if they use an **improved water source** that is:
- Located **on premises**,
- **Available when needed** (no longer than 24 h interruption),
- **Free from faecal and priority chemical contamination** (E. coli < 1 CFU/100 mL, arsenic, fluoride, lead within WHO limits).

**Step-by-step derivation of the indicator formula:**

**Step 1** — Define the universe. Let the total population of the study area be $N_{total}$.

$$
N_{total} = N_{safely} + N_{basic} + N_{limited} + N_{unimproved} + N_{surface}
$$

**Step 2** — Count the population meeting all three safely managed criteria. Call this $N_{safely}$.

$$
N_{safely} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i \cdot f_i
$$

where $H_i$ is the number of households in zone $i$, and $f_i$ is the average family size in that zone.

**Step 3** — Form the ratio. The indicator value is the proportion (in %):

$$
I_{6.1.1} = \dfrac{N_{safely}}{N_{total}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4** — Substitution to a household-survey form (used in field practice by PHED engineers):

$$
I_{6.1.1} = \dfrac{\sum H_i \cdot f_i \cdot \mathbb{1}_{safe}(i)}{N_{total}} \times 100
$$

where $\mathbb{1}_{safe}(i)$ is the **indicator function** that equals 1 if zone $i$ satisfies all three safely-managed criteria, else 0.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 3-criterion test (on-premise + availability + quality) is *stricter* than the old MDG target which only checked "improved source." This is why India's MDG "drinking water coverage" was ~94% in 2015, but its *SDG* "safely managed" coverage dropped to ~55% — the criteria tightened.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Per Capita Water Availability (Critical for SDG 6.4)

**Step 1** — Define the system. Annual renewable freshwater resource is $Q_{annual}$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{year}$.

**Step 2** — Divide by total population $P$ to obtain per-capita annual availability:

$$
W_{pc} = \dfrac{Q_{annual}}{P}
$$

where $W_{pc}$ is in $\text{m}^3/\text{year}/\text{capita}$.

**Step 3** — Convert to litres per day (a more common engineering unit):

$$
W_{pc, L/day} = \dfrac{Q_{annual} \times 1000}{P \times 365}
$$

**Step 4** — Apply the **Falkenmark Water Stress Indicator** thresholds:

$$
\text{Status} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{No stress} & W_{pc} \geq 1700\ \text{m}^3/\text{year} \\
\text{Stress} & 1000 \leq W_{pc} < 1700\ \text{m}^3/\text{year} \\
\text{Scarcity} & 500 \leq W_{pc} < 1000\ \text{m}^3/\text{year} \\
\text{Absolute scarcity} & W_{pc} < 500\ \text{m}^3/\text{year}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India's National Average** (2024 estimate): $W_{pc} \approx 1486\ \text{m}^3/\text{year}$ — India is in the **"Water-stressed"** category as a country, and several river basins (Krishna, Cauvery, Pennar) are in absolute scarcity.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Functionality Rate (Core JJM/SDG-6 Metric)

**Step 1** — Define $N_{installed}$ = total number of tap connections/schemes installed under a program.

**Step 2** — Define $N_{functional}$ = subset delivering the mandated 55 lpcd (litres per capita per day) continuously, with water quality compliant with IS 10500:2012.

**Step 3** — The functionality fraction is:

$$
F_{rate} = \dfrac{N_{functional}}{N_{installed}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4** — For a village, this expands to a *weighted* index using daily supply hours:

$$
F_{rate, village} = \dfrac{1}{N_{inst}} \sum_{j=1}^{N_{inst}} \mathbb{1}_{supply}(j) \times 100
$$

where $\mathbb{1}_{supply}(j) = 1$ if connection $j$ supplies $\geq 55$ lpcd for $\geq 8$ hours/day, else 0.

> [!NOTE]
> **JJM Benchmark:** A village is declared "**Har Ghar Jal**" certified only when $F_{rate} \geq 90\%$, all households have a tap, and water quality tests pass on prescribed parameters.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: India vs Global SDG-6 Status (2024 Data)

| Indicator | India (2024) | Global (2024) | SDG 2030 Target | India's Gap |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|-----------------|-------------|
| 6.1.1 Safely managed drinking water | ~ 80% (rural ~ 60%) | ~ 73% | 100% | Closing |
| 6.2.1a Safely managed sanitation | ~ 75% (rural ~ 65%) | ~ 57% | 100% | Closing |
| 6.2.1b Hand-washing with soap | ~ 84% | ~ 71% | 100% | Closing |
| 6.3.2 Bodies of water with good quality | ~ 65% (rivers) | ~ 60% | 100% | Slow |
| 6.4.1 Water-use efficiency | Low (irrigation ~ 80% use) | Mixed | +50% efficiency | Major gap |
| 6.4.2 Water stress | 1.5 B people globally | ~ 2.4 B people | Halve | Critical |
| 6.5.1 IWRM implementation | Score 47/100 | Score 54/100 | Universal | Capacity gap |
| 6.6.1 Protected water bodies | ~ 28% area | ~ 30% | Universal | Slow |

### 3.5 Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A panchayat has a population of 12,500. A survey finds:
> - 8,200 use a piped supply on premises with E. coli < 1 CFU/100 mL and 24-h availability.
> - 2,500 use a public standpost 15 min away (improved source, E. coli 3 CFU/100 mL).
> - 1,000 use an open well.
> - 800 use river water.
>
> **Calculate the SDG 6.1.1 indicator value. Categorize the panchayat's status (safely managed / basic / limited / unimproved / surface).**

**Solution:**

**Step 1** — Identify the safely managed group (meets all 3 criteria):

$$
N_{safely} = 8200
$$

**Step 2** — Identify the basic group (improved source, ≤ 30 min, but not on premises or failed quality):

$$
N_{basic} = 2500
$$

(Note: E. coli of 3 CFU/100 mL *fails* the safely-managed threshold of < 1 CFU/100 mL.)

**Step 3** — Unimproved (open well) and surface (river):

$$
N_{unimproved} = 1000, \quad N_{surface} = 800
$$

**Step 4** — Check total population:

$$
N_{total} = 8200 + 2500 + 1000 + 800 = 12500 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5** — Calculate the indicator:

$$
I_{6.1.1} = \dfrac{8200}{12500} \times 100 = 65.6\%
$$

**Step 6** — Categorize:

- **Safely managed = 65.6%** → Below the 100% target; status = **partially safely managed**.
- **Basic = 20.0%** (2500/12500)
- **Limited = 0%** (no >30 min round trip)
- **Unimproved = 8.0%**
- **Surface = 6.4%**
- **Total unimproved + surface = 14.4%** → Engineering priority: replace the 1,800 households with a piped scheme.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Error:** Students often confuse the MDG 7.C target ("improved source") with the SDG 6.1.1 target ("safely managed"). The MDG figure can be 90%+ while the SDG figure may be 65%. Always read the criteria carefully.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The 17 SDGs — Engineering-Relevant Mind Map

The following Mermaid diagram shows the relationships between the 17 SDGs and highlights (in **highlighted nodes**) those with a direct bearing on water supply and sanitation engineering.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[SDG 1 No Poverty]
    nodeB[SDG 2 Zero Hunger]
    nodeC[SDG 3 Good Health]
    nodeD[SDG 4 Quality Education]
    nodeE[SDG 5 Gender Equality]

    nodeJ[SDG 10 Reduced Inequalities]
```
