```mermaid
    nodeM[SDG 13 Climate Action]
    nodeN[SDG 14 Life Below Water]
    nodeO[SDG 15 Life on Land]
    nodeP[SDG 16 Peace Justice]
    nodeQ[SDG 17 Partnerships]

    nodeF --> nodeC
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeF --> nodeA
    nodeF --> nodeE
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeK
    nodeF --> nodeL
    nodeF --> nodeM
    nodeF --> nodeN
    nodeF --> nodeO
    nodeF --> nodeQ
    nodeC --> nodeA
    nodeM --> nodeF
    nodeN --> nodeF
    nodeO --> nodeF

    style nodeF fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style nodeC fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeM fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeK fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The thick-bordered blue node is **SDG 6 (the focal goal)**. Green, red, and orange highlighted nodes are the SDGs with the strongest direct engineering links (health, climate, cities). All arrows represent **functional linkages** — improvement in one cascades to others.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: SDG 6 — WASH Service Delivery Pipeline

A block-level functional architecture showing how the SDG 6 service ladder is delivered in a rural Indian context.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Source: Spring, Well, Reservoir, RO]
    B[Intake Works]
    C[Headworks and Treatment Plant]
    D[Storage: OHT GSR ESR]
    E[Distribution Network: Mains Submains]
    F[Household Tap FHTC]
    G[Onsite Sanitation: Twin Pit Soak Pit]
    H[Faecal Sludge Management FSM]
    I[Treatment: Co-composting DEWATS]
    J[Reuse: Agriculture Soil Conditioner]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J

    K[Monitoring: JJM Dashboard] -.-> F
    K -.-> G
    K -.-> H
    L[Policy: SBM G JJM SBM U] -.-> K

    style A fill:#cce5ff
    style F fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Blue blocks** = water supply chain (SDG 6.1).
> - **Green blocks** = sanitation & household (SDG 6.2).
> - **Yellow blocks** = faecal sludge management (SDG 6.3).
> - **Red dashed blocks** = governance and monitoring layer (SDG 6.A/6.B).
> - The whole system is a **closed loop** — excreta from sanitation becomes the *input* for safe reuse, embodying the **circular economy** principle under SDG 12.

### 4.3 Flowchart: India-Level SDG-6 Reporting Chain

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Village Households]
    P2[Panchayat and Block Office]
    P3[District WSSO Water Supply Sanitation Org]
    P4[State PHED Public Health Engineering Dept]
    P5[Ministry of Jal Shakti DDWS Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation]
    P6[JMP WHO UNICEF Global Report]
    P7[UN Statistical Commission]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7

    P5 --> P8[India SDG Dashboard NITI Aayog]
    P8 --> P9[Parliament and SDG India Index]

    style P5 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style P6 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style P8 fill:#d62728,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How data flows up the SDG 6 chain:**
> - Households → Panchayat (monthly) → Block → District → State PHED → **Ministry of Jal Shakti** (national aggregator).
> - From India, the data goes to the **JMP** (WHO/UNICEF, Geneva) and feeds the **UN SDG Global Database**.
> - Domestically, the **NITI Aayog SDG India Index** ranks states on a 0–100 score — Kerala and Goa typically top; Bihar, Jharkhand lag.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define the term "Sustainable Development Goals" (SDGs). List any four targets of SDG 6 (Clean Water and Sanitation).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** are a set of 17 interlinked global goals adopted by the UN General Assembly in September 2015 as part of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development. They replaced the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) and are intended to be achieved by **2030**.

Four targets of **SDG 6**:
1. **6.1** — By 2030, achieve universal and equitable access to safe and affordable drinking water for all.
2. **6.2** — By 2030, achieve access to adequate sanitation and hygiene for all and end open defecation.
3. **6.3** — Improve water quality by reducing pollution, halving untreated wastewater, and increasing safe reuse.
4. **6.4** — Increase water-use efficiency and address water scarcity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Note (1 mark per definition + 1 mark for four targets listed):** Students often write *all 8* targets and run out of time. Mention any 4, in the format `6.X — statement`.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the JMP "service ladder" for drinking water. What is the difference between "Basic" and "Safely Managed" services?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **JMP (Joint Monitoring Programme) service ladder** classifies drinking water supplies into 5 progressive levels:
1. **Safely Managed** — Improved source, on premises, available when needed, **free from contamination**.
2. **Basic** — Improved source, round trip ≤ 30 min.
3. **Limited** — Improved source, round trip > 30 min.
4. **Unimproved** — Unprotected well or spring.
5. **Surface water** — River, pond, canal.

**Difference between "Basic" and "Safely Managed":**
- A **Basic** supply need only be an *improved source within 30 min*; the water may still be contaminated (e.g., E. coli 10 CFU/100 mL) and may not be available continuously.
- A **Safely Managed** supply must additionally be **(a) on premises, (b) available when needed, and (c) free from faecal and chemical contamination** as per WHO guidelines.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often say "basic = piped, safely managed = bottled." This is **wrong**. *Bottled water* is *not* classified as safely managed under JMP because of concerns about source water. The 3 criteria are the *correct* distinguishing factors.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the eight targets of SDG 6 (Clean Water and Sanitation). Highlight the relevance of each target to Indian rural water supply and onsite sanitation engineering practice.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the SDG-6 service delivery chain for a rural Indian village — from source to reuse — and indicate which SDG 6 target each block satisfies.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Eight Targets of SDG 6 — with Indian Context (7 marks)**

| Target | Statement (Summarized) | Indian Engineering Relevance |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| **6.1** | Universal, equitable, safe, affordable drinking water | Driven by **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** — 100% FHTC target; IS 10500 quality testing |
| **6.2** | Adequate sanitation, hygiene, end open defecation | Driven by **Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM-G)** — 11 crore toilets built; 2024 ODF Plus push |
| **6.3** | Improve water quality; halve untreated wastewater; increase reuse | **Namami Gange**, **NRCD** projects; STPs in 500+ towns; FSSM policy 2017 |
| **6.4** | Water-use efficiency; reduce scarcity | **Per Drop More Crop** (micro-irrigation), rainwater harvesting mandates |
| **6.5** | Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM) | **National Water Policy 2012**, river basin organisations |
| **6.6** | Protect/restore water-related ecosystems | **Wetland Rules 2017**, MGNREGA watershed works |
| **6.A** | International cooperation, capacity-building, technology transfer | World Bank-supported *HAR Ghar Jal* projects; Indo-Israel tech transfer on desalination |
| **6.B** | Local community participation | **Pani Samitis** (Village Water & Sanitation Committees), *5% women mandatory* in JJM |

**Conclusion:** India has *legislative, programmatic, and institutional* frameworks for all 8 targets; the gap is *implementation efficiency* and *functionality* of created assets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-split guide:** Target listing 1 mark × 6 = 6 marks + 1 mark for overall Indian context. Students who skip the *Indian relevance* column lose 2–3 marks.

---

**(b) SDG-6 Service Delivery Chain — Block Diagram (7 marks)**

A block diagram (already presented in SECTION 4.2) should be redrawn by the student with the following labelling:

```
[1] Source (Spring/Well/Reservoir)               → 6.1 (water source)
[2] Intake & Headworks                           → 6.3 (raw water quality)
[3] Treatment Plant                              → 6.3 (pollution control)
[4] Storage: OHT / GSR / ESR                     → 6.1 (availability)
[5] Distribution Network                          → 6.1 (accessibility)
[6] Household Tap Connection (FHTC)              → 6.1 (on-premise)
[7] Onsite Sanitation (Twin-pit / Soak-pit)      → 6.2 (containment)
[8] Faecal Sludge Management (FSM)               → 6.3 (safe disposal)
[9] Treatment: Co-composting / DEWATS            → 6.3 (reuse & recycling)
[10] Reuse in Agriculture / Soil Conditioner     → 6.4 (efficiency) + SDG 12
[11] Monitoring (JJM Dashboard, IS 10500 tests)  → 6.A/6.B (governance)
[12] Community (Pani Samiti)                      → 6.B (participation)
```

**Engineering Narrative (4 marks):**
- Blocks 1–6 deliver **SDG 6.1** (safely managed drinking water).
- Block 7 delivers **SDG 6.2** (basic/safely managed sanitation).
- Blocks 8–10 deliver **SDG 6.3** and **6.4** (wastewater treatment & reuse).
- Blocks 11–12 deliver **SDG 6.A/6.B** (means of implementation).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students draw a one-way flow from source to tap and stop. To score full marks, you **must** show the **closed loop** (sludge → treatment → reuse) and the **monitoring feedback** loop. The closed loop is what distinguishes an SDG-6 design from a *legacy MDG-era* design.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the MDG and SDG frameworks with respect to water and sanitation. Discuss the progress India has made under SDG 6, citing at least three official indicators.

**(b) [7 marks]** A panchayat has 15,000 residents. A JMP-style survey reveals:
- 9,000 have piped water on premises with E. coli < 1 CFU/100 mL and 24-h supply (safely managed).
- 3,000 use a public standpost 20 min away, E. coli 4 CFU/100 mL (basic).
- 1,500 use a community borewell, E. coli 12 CFU/100 mL (limited?/basic?).
- 1,000 use an open well (unimproved).
- 500 use river water (surface).

Calculate the SDG 6.1.1 indicator value, the open-defecation-free readiness, and suggest two engineering interventions with cost-benefit logic.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MDG vs SDG: Water & Sanitation (7 marks)**

| Aspect | **MDG 7.C** (2000–2015) | **SDG 6** (2015–2030) |
|--------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Goal structure | One sub-target under Goal 7 | Standalone **Goal 6** with 8 targets |
| Drinking water | "Improved source" (binary) | "**Safely managed**" (3-criteria test) |
| Sanitation | "Improved sanitation" | "**Safely managed sanitation** + hand-washing with soap" |
| Equity focus | National aggregate | **Disaggregated** by income, gender, urban/rural, slum |
| Cross-cutting | Standalone | **Integrated** with climate (SDG 13), health (SDG 3), ecosystems (SDG 14, 15) |
| IWRM | Not present | Explicit **Target 6.5** |
| Means of implementation | Not specified | **Targets 6.A and 6.B** (finance + community) |
| Number of targets | 1 (7.C) | 8 |
| India achievement (final) | 94% improved water; 39% improved sanitation | ~ 80% safely managed water; ~ 75% safely managed sanitation (2024) |

**India's progress under SDG 6 (3 official indicators):**
1. **Indicator 6.1.1** — Safely managed drinking water: rose from ~ 35% (2015) to ~ 80% (2024) due to JJM.
2. **Indicator 6.2.1a** — Safely managed sanitation: rose from ~ 12% (2015) to ~ 75% (2024) due to SBM-G.
3. **Indicator 6.3.2** — Water bodies with good ambient quality: ~ 65% of monitored river stretches (CPCB 2024) comply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** 3 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for the *key conceptual shift* (binary → multi-criteria), 2 marks for the three indicators with numbers.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Classify each population segment:**

| Segment | Count | Criteria Met? | Classification |
|---------|-------|---------------|----------------|
| Piped, E. coli <1, on premises, 24-h | 9,000 | All 3 | **Safely Managed** |
| Standpost 20 min, E. coli 4 | 3,000 | 2 of 3 (improved + ≤30 min) but fails *quality* | **Basic** |
| Community borewell, E. coli 12 | 1,500 | 2 of 3 (improved source + on premises?) but fails *quality* | **Basic** (still improved source) — *Limited only if > 30 min round trip* |
| Open well | 1,000 | Fails *source* | **Unimproved** |
| River | 500 | Fails *source* | **Surface** |

> **Refinement:** The 1,500 community borewell users are "Basic" (not Limited) because round trip is unspecified (assumed ≤ 30 min). They fail the *quality* criterion for "safely managed."

**Step 2 — Compute SDG 6.1.1 indicator:**

$$
N_{total} = 9000 + 3000 + 1500 + 1000 + 500 = 15000
$$

$$
I_{6.1.1} = \dfrac{9000}{15000} \times 100 = \mathbf{60.0\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Open Defecation Free (ODF) readiness (analogous for sanitation 6.2.1):**

Assuming faecal containment is reported separately, but for water-quality lens:
- *Safely managed* = 60.0%
- *At least basic* = 60.0 + 20.0 + 10.0 = **90.0%**
- *Unimproved or surface* = 6.67% + 3.33% = **10.0%** → These 1,500 households (1,000 open well + 500 river) are the engineering priority.

> **ODF-water readiness score** = % population with safely managed + at least basic = **90.0%** (above 80% threshold typically set by state PHEDs for declaring a village *Jal Suraksha*).

**Step 4 — Two engineering interventions with cost–benefit logic (2 × 2 = 4 marks):**

1. **Intervention 1: Augment the piped supply to cover the 3,000 standpost + 1,500 borewell + 1,000 open-well households.**
   - *Engineering:* Add 5,500 m of distribution main, 1 OHT (200 kL), 2 booster pumps.
   - *Cost:* ~ ₹3.0 crore (CapEx) + ₹0.15 crore/yr (O&M).
   - *Benefit:* Raises 6.1.1 from 60% to 96.7% (14,500/15,000).
   - *Per-capita cost* = ₹3.0 cr / 5,500 = ₹5,455/household.

2. **Intervention 2: Install household-level point-of-use (PoU) treatment (e.g., bio-sand filters + chlorination) for the 500 river-water households as a *transitional* measure.**
   - *Engineering:* 500 bio-sand filters @ ₹1,500 each + 1 chlorinator at the river intake.
   - *Cost:* ~ ₹7.5 lakh + ₹1 lakh/yr.
   - *Benefit:* Reduces the *surface-water users* from 500 to < 50 (those still without a tap) within 6 months, and *immediately* improves E. coli counts.
   - *Cost-per-capita* = ₹150 (very low — appropriate for *transitional* intervention).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** A *phased approach* — PoU filtration now, piped network within 18 months — is the engineering-cum-socially-optimal path to 100% safely managed SDG 6.1.1 compliance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):**
> 1. Do **not** mis-classify the 1,500 borewell users as "Limited" — they are "Basic" because their source is improved (the JMP classifies all borewells as improved *unless* cased at < 30 m depth without sanitary seal).
> 2. For ODF *readiness*, the question asks for *sanitation*, but only *water data* is given. Note in your answer that the 60% safely-managed-water figure *correlates* with but does *not* equate to ODF.
> 3. Always express per-capita cost — examiners reward *engineering economics* in PECET867.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Recap — SDGs & Water/Sanitation (Module 1):**

- ☐ The **17 SDGs** were adopted by the **UN General Assembly in September 2015** as part of the **2030 Agenda**; they replaced the **8 MDGs** (2000–2015).
- ☐ **SDG 6 = Clean Water and Sanitation** — the primary goal for our course.
- ☐ **SDG 6 has 8 targets**: 6.1 to 6.6 (outcomes) + 6.A and 6.B (means of implementation).
- ☐ The **JMP** (WHO/UNICEF) is the official global reporting body for WASH; it defines the **5-rung service ladder**.
- ☐ **Safely managed** water = improved source + on premises + available + free from contamination. **Three criteria, all must be met.**
- ☐ **Falkenmark water-stress threshold**: < 1,700 m³/year/capita = stressed; < 500 = absolute scarcity.
- ☐ **India's flagship programs** linked to SDG 6: **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** [drinking water] and **Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM-G and SBM-U)** [sanitation].
- ☐ **Jal Jeevan Mission target**: 100% **FHTC** (Functional Household Tap Connection) by **2024**; ~ 78% achieved by mid-2024; deadline extended to 2028.
- ☐ **SBM-G target**: 100% toilet coverage by **2 October 2019**; achieved. Now in **ODF-Plus** phase (faecal sludge management + grey-water).
- ☐ **Cross-cutting links**: SDG 6 ↔ SDG 3 (health), SDG 13 (climate), SDG 11 (cities), SDG 14/15 (ecosystems), SDG 5 (gender), SDG 17 (finance).
- ☐ **Indicator 6.1.1** = % safely managed drinking water; **6.2.1a** = safely managed sanitation; **6.2.1b** = hand-washing with soap; **6.3.2** = ambient water-quality of bodies; **6.4.1** = water-use efficiency; **6.4.2** = water-stress; **6.5.1** = IWRM implementation; **6.6.1** = protected water-related ecosystems.
- ☐ **Key formulas** (must memorize): $I = N_{sm}/N_{total} \times 100$; $W_{pc} = Q/P$; $WSI = Withdrawal/Resources \times 100$; $F_{rate} = N_{func}/N_{inst} \times 100$.
- ☐ **Faecal Sludge Management (FSM)** is the new frontier — **onsite sanitation** is incomplete without FSM (SDG 6.3).
- ☐ **NITI Aayog SDG India Index** ranks states annually; **Kerala and Goa** are top performers, **Bihar and Jharkhand** lag.
- ☐ **Community participation (6.B)** is *not optional* — **5% women members** mandatory in JJM Pani Samitis; failure to include it costs project funding.
- ☐ **India's per-capita water availability** ≈ 1,486 m³/year (2024) — already in the *water-stressed* category.
- ☐ **CPCB data (2024)**: ~ 65% of monitored river stretches meet the designated best-use criteria — a critical SDG 6.3.2 metric.
- ☐ In **Part B answers**, always (a) cite a **target number**, (b) show a **block diagram or table**, and (c) conclude with a **cost–benefit or Indian context** line for full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 — SDG Notes.** These 5 sections map directly to KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 of PECET867 and prepare you for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions on SDGs, WASH service ladders, and India's flagship programs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Rural Water Supply
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of Rural Water Supply

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rural Water Supply (RWS)** refers to the systematic provision of adequate, safe, and sustainable water supply infrastructure and services to habitations in rural areas (typically classified by the Census of India as villages with population below 5,000 or with non-urban characteristics), fulfilling domestic, livestock, and small-scale livelihood needs while ensuring equity, affordability, and long-term sustainability.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET867 – Module 1)**, the *Principles of Rural Water Supply* establish the foundational engineering, institutional, and socio-economic guidelines governing the planning, design, and operation of drinking water schemes in rural India, particularly under programmes like the **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** launched by the Government of India in 2019.

### Core Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

| Term | Definition (KTU Convention) |
|------|------------------------------|
| **Habitation** | A cluster of population (usually  $ \geq 300 $  in plains /  $ \geq 100 $  in hilly areas) recognized as a unit for water supply. |
| **Service Level** | Tiered delivery standards — Basic, Intermediate, or Optimal (per JJM guidelines). |
| **LPCD** | Litres Per Capita per Day — unit of per-person water allocation. |
| **FHTC** | Functional Household Tap Connection — a tap delivering water inside or near the dwelling. |
| **Source Sustainability** | Long-term availability of source water beyond the design horizon. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small village is like a **single large family** living in scattered houses around a common courtyard. Just as a mother plans how much rice, dal, and water is needed for every family member every day (plus a little extra for guests, festivals, and wastage), an engineer must plan the **quantity, quality, and timing** of water delivered to every household.

- **Quantity** → "How much water does each person need?" → Like counting glasses of water for the family.
- **Quality** → "Is the water clean and drinkable?" → Like ensuring the water pot is covered and boiled.
- **Reliability** → "Will water come every day, every hour?" → Like ensuring the tap never runs dry during a heatwave.
- **Equity** → "Does every house get it?" → Like ensuring every child at the table gets food, not just the eldest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Idea:** Rural water supply is *not* about pumping the maximum possible water — it is about delivering the **right amount, of the right quality, to the right people, at the right cost, today and 30 years from now.**

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighting)

- Standard per capita allocation in rural India (Basic Service): **$55$ LPCD**
- Optimal Service Level target under JJM: **$55$–$75$ LPCD**
- Minimum human survival requirement (physiological): **$20$ LPCD**
- Design service reservoir storage: **$33\%$ to $50\%$ of daily demand**
- Pipe sizing design velocity range: **$0.6$ m/s to $1.5$ m/s**
- Fire reserve (rural habitations, usually nil for population $< 50,000$): **Negligible**

---

## 1.4 Foundational Objectives of a Rural Water Supply Programme

The KTU 2024 syllabus outlines six pillars (modelled on the WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme and Indian National Rural Drinking Water Programme):

1. **Adequacy** — Sufficient quantity for domestic, livestock, and livelihood use.
2. **Quality** — Conformance to BIS 10500:2012 drinking water standards.
3. **Accessibility** — Water source/standpost within **$1.6$ km** in plains, **$100$ m elevation** in hills (as per CPHEEO).
4. **Affordability** — Tariff that does not exceed **$5\%$ of household income** (WHO affordability criterion).
5. **Reliability / Sustainability** — Year-round availability with assured source recharge.
6. **Equity & Inclusion** — Priority to marginalised groups, women, and the differently-abled.

---

## 1.5 Visual Anchor — The Six Pillars Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["RURAL WATER SUPPLY"]:::core
    nodeB["ADEQUACY"]:::pillar
    nodeC["QUALITY"]:::pillar
    nodeD["ACCESSIBILITY"]:::pillar
    nodeE["AFFORDABILITY"]:::pillar
    nodeF["RELIABILITY"]:::pillar
    nodeG["EQUITY"]:::pillar
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeA --> nodeG
    classDef core fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pillar fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2c4a
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deconstructing the Principles — Structured Logic

The principles of rural water supply can be examined under five sequential engineering layers. Each layer answers a specific "**Why**" and "**How**".

### Layer 1 — Demand Assessment (How much water is needed?)

- **Why?** Because every scheme must satisfy the *highest anticipated* demand over its design life, not just the present need.
- **How?** By combining **per capita demand**, **population forecast**, and **demand multipliers** (peak factor, livestock factor, institutional demand).

### Layer 2 — Source Identification & Yield Analysis (Where does the water come from?)

- **Why?** Rural sources are predominantly **groundwater-based** (bore wells, dug wells, springs) and occasionally **surface-based** (check dams, weirs, small reservoirs).
- **How?** Conduct a **geo-hydrological survey**, estimate **yield**, and check **post-monsoon** and **pre-monsoon** availability to ensure source sustainability.

### Layer 3 — Quality Treatment (Is the water safe?)

- **Why?** BIS 10500:2012 specifies $30+$ parameters. Rural water often contains **iron, fluoride, arsenic, nitrates, microbiological contaminants**.
- **How?** Apply a **treatment train** typically limited to disinfection (chlorination) for groundwater, or **Slow Sand Filters (SSF)** plus disinfection for surface sources.

### Layer 4 — Conveyance, Storage & Distribution (How does water reach the people?)

- **Why?** Hydraulic transmission losses and inequitable distribution are the most common causes of scheme failure.
- **How?** Use a **gravity system** where topography allows; otherwise a **pumping main** with an **Overhead Tank (OHT)** to balance diurnal demand fluctuations.

### Layer 5 — Institutional & Financial Sustainability (Who runs it?)

- **Why?** Hardware without a functional Village Water & Sanitation Committee (VWSC) fails within 5 years.
- **How?** Form a **Panchayat-led VWSC**, levy a **minimal tariff**, and earmark **$5\%$–$10\%$** of capital cost as an Operation & Maintenance corpus.

---

## 2.2 Service Level Definitions (Jal Jeevan Mission Framework)

| Service Level | Quantity | Quality | Delivery Mode | Coverage Criterion |
|---------------|----------|---------|---------------|--------------------|
| **Basic** | $ \geq 55$ LPCD | Potable (BIS 10500) | Standpost / Common tap | Source within $1.6$ km / $100$ m elevation |
| **Intermediate** | $ \geq 55$ LPCD | Potable | Standpost + partial FHTC | $50\%$ household connections |
| **Optimal** | $ \geq 55$–$75$ LPCD | Potable | $100\%$ FHTC | $24 \times 7$ supply, household metering |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All the following equations are exam-defining. Master the variables and units.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $\displaystyle Q_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{P \cdot q}{1000}$ | Average daily demand (m³/day) | $P$ = population; $q$ = per capita demand in LPCD |
| 2 | $\displaystyle Q_{\text{max-day}} = Q_{\text{avg}} \cdot K_1$ | Maximum daily demand | $K_1$ = $1.5$ to $2.0$ (rural) |
| 3 | $\displaystyle Q_{\text{max-hr}} = Q_{\text{max-day}} \cdot K_2$ | Maximum hourly demand | $K_2$ = $3.0$ to $4.0$ |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{combined}} = 1.8 \cdot Q_{\text{avg}} + F$ | Annual average + fire (negligible in rural) | $F$ = fire demand |
| 5 | $V_{\text{tank}} = 0.33 \cdot Q_{\text{max-day}}$ | Storage capacity (rural OHT) | Volume in m³ |
| 6 | $V_{\text{tank}} = 0.50 \cdot Q_{\text{max-day}}$ | Storage capacity (urban OHT) | Volume in m³ |
| 7 | $V_{\text{pipe}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} \cdot L$ | Volume in pipeline | $d$ = diameter; $L$ = length |
| 8 | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | Arithmetic Increase Method | $\bar{x}$ = avg decadal increment |
| 9 | $P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{n}$ | Geometric Increase Method | $r$ = geometric growth rate (%) |
| 10 | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot y$ | Incremental Increase Method | $x$ = avg increase; $y$ = avg incremental increase |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** For rural schemes, fire demand ($F$) is **assumed zero** unless specified, because population is below $50,000$. This is a frequently tested short-note question.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles outlined above directly govern:

- **Scheme DPR Preparation** (Detailed Project Reports under JJM).
- **Pipe Network Hydraulic Design** using Hardy-Cross or EPANET.
- **Pumping Machinery Selection** — power rating derived from $Q \times H / 75\eta$.
- **Tariff Engineering** — volumetric pricing linked to $55$ LPCD norm.
- **Climate-Resilient Planning** — accounting for $30\%$ source yield reduction in drought years.

---

## 2.5 Quantitative Worked Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Pre-Read:** For a village with present population $P_0 = 5,000$ and target service level of $55$ LPCD, the **average daily demand** equals:
>
> $$Q_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{5{,}000 \times 55}{1{,}000} = 275 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

This single calculation seeds every subsequent decision — OHT size, pump capacity, pipe diameter, and even the **per-household water bill**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Geometric Population Forecasting (Rural Indian Context)

The **Geometric Increase Method** is the most commonly employed forecasting tool in Indian rural water supply DPRs because rural populations grow in roughly geometric (compound) proportions between census decades.

### 3.1.1 Starting Postulate

Let the population at the **base year** (the most recent census) be $P_0$, and let the **constant geometric growth rate** be $r$ (expressed as a percentage per decade).

### 3.1.2 Mathematical Build-up

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right) \\[4pt]
P_2 &= P_1 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right) = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{2} \\[4pt]
P_3 &= P_2 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right) = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{3} \\[4pt]
\therefore\ P_n &= P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning (Row-by-Row):**

- **Row 1 — $P_1$:** After one decade, the new population is the old population multiplied by the growth factor $\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)$.
- **Row 2 — $P_2$:** After two decades, the factor compounds (multiplies again), giving a *square* of the growth factor.
- **Row 3 — $P_3$:** The pattern continues — every decade adds another multiplicative layer.
- **Conclusion — $P_n$:** By induction, after $n$ decades, the factor is raised to the $n$-th power.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full Expansion)

**Problem Statement:** A village had a population of $4{,}000$ in $2011$. The geometric growth rate observed from $2001$–$2011$ was $r = 12\%$ per decade. Forecast the population for the design year **$2041$** (i.e., $n = 30$ years $= 3$ decades).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= 4{,}000 \\
r &= 12\ \% = \dfrac{12}{100} = 0.12 \\
n &= 3 \text{ decades} \\[4pt]
P_3 &= 4{,}000 \times (1 + 0.12)^{3} \\[4pt]
&= 4{,}000 \times (1.12)^{3} \\[4pt]
&= 4{,}000 \times 1.404928 \\[4pt]
&= 5{,}619.71 \\[4pt]
\therefore\ P_{2041} &\approx 5{,}620 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rounding Rule:** Always round **up** when forecasting for design (over-design is preferred over under-design for water supply). Hence $5{,}620$ is rounded to **$5{,}700$ or $6{,}000$** depending on the engineer's margin of safety.

### 3.1.4 Determining $r$ from Past Census Data

$$
r = \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} - 1 \right] \times 100
$$

where $P_1$ is the earlier-decade population, $P_2$ is the later-decade population, and $n$ is the number of decades between them.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Storage Tank Volume

The OHT storage is sized to meet diurnal (24-hour) fluctuations. KTU convention assigns $33\%$ of $Q_{\text{max-day}}$ for rural habitation.

### 3.2.1 From First Principles

The storage must balance three fractions:

- **Equalisation storage** (compensating hourly variation): $22\%$ of $Q_{\text{max-day}}$
- **Break-down / emergency storage** (pipe repairs, pump outages): $10\%$ of $Q_{\text{max-day}}$
- **Fire reserve** (rural $\approx 0$): $0\%$ of $Q_{\text{max-day}}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{OHT}} &= V_{\text{equ}} + V_{\text{emerg}} + V_{\text{fire}} \\[4pt]
&= 0.22 \cdot Q_{\text{max-day}} + 0.10 \cdot Q_{\text{max-day}} + 0 \\[4pt]
&= 0.32 \cdot Q_{\text{max-day}} \approx 0.33 \cdot Q_{\text{max-day}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:**

- Row 1 — Sum of three independent storage buckets.
- Row 2 — Fire term dropped (rural).
- Row 3 — Numerical aggregation yields the KTU-standard $33\%$ coefficient.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Peak Demand Multiplier Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The peak factor $K_1$ is derived empirically from observed rural-flow records. For India, $K_1 = 1.5$ is adopted in arid zones, $K_2 = 3.0$ for hourly peaks.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{max-day}} &= K_1 \cdot Q_{\text{avg}} \\[4pt]
Q_{\text{max-hr}} &= K_2 \cdot Q_{\text{max-day}} \\[4pt]
\therefore\ Q_{\text{max-hr}} &= K_1 \cdot K_2 \cdot Q_{\text{avg}} = 1.5 \times 3.0 \cdot Q_{\text{avg}} = 4.5 \cdot Q_{\text{avg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This composite factor of $4.5$ is the **design rate** used to size intake, rising main, and pump sets.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide

| Forecasting Method | Best Suited When | KTU Applicability for Rural Schemes |
|--------------------|------------------|---------------------------------------|
| **Arithmetic Increase** | Slow, steady growth in declining villages | Low — rarely used for design |
| **Geometric Increase** | Steady, compound growth (most Indian villages) | **High — preferred method** |
| **Incremental Increase** | Growth rate itself is changing (urbanizing village) | Moderate — used in peri-rural fringes |
| **Logistic / S-curve** | Saturated growth near a city | Low — rare in purely rural contexts |
| **Census Cohort Method** | Detailed age-structure data available | Very High accuracy but data-intensive |

---

## 3.5 Numerical — End-to-End Demand Calculation

**Question:** A rural panchayat has $P_{2041} = 5{,}620$. Design an OHT for **$55$ LPCD** basic service.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{avg}} &= \dfrac{P \cdot q}{1000} = \dfrac{5{,}620 \times 55}{1{,}000} = 309.1 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \\[4pt]
Q_{\text{max-day}} &= 1.5 \times 309.1 = 463.65 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \\[4pt]
V_{\text{OHT}} &= 0.33 \times 463.65 \approx 153.0 \text{ m}^3 \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \text{Adopt } V_{\text{OHT}} &= 160 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (next commercial size)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final design specification:** OHT capacity **$160$ m³**, sized to the nearest standard civil-engineering tank module.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Flow — Rural Water Supply Scheme

The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture** shows the principal subsystems and the flow of water from source to consumer in a typical gravity-fed rural scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS["SOURCE\nSpring / Borewell / Surface Intake"]:::source
    nodeI["INTAKE\nSUMP & CATCHMENT"]:::source
    nodeT["TREATMENT\nSSF + Chlorination"]:::treat
    nodeP["PUMP HOUSE\n(If Pumping Required)"]:::pump
    nodeO["OHT\nOverhead Tank"]:::tank
    nodeD["DISTRIBUTION NETWORK\nPipes & Valves"]:::dist
    nodeC["STANDPOSTS\nPublic Taps"]:::user
    nodeH["HOUSE CONNECTIONS\nFHTC"]:::user
    nodeM["METERING & BILLING\nVWSC Operations"]:::mgmt

    nodeS --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeT
    nodeT --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeC --> nodeM

    classDef source fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#385723,color:#0b2c4a
    classDef treat fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806000,color:#0b2c4a
    classDef pump fill:#f4b084,stroke:#833c0c,color:#0b2c4a
    classDef tank fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2c4a
    classDef dist fill:#c6e0b4,stroke:#385723,color:#0b2c4a
    classDef user fill:#f8cbad,stroke:#833c0c,color:#0b2c4a
    classDef mgmt fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#595959,color:#0b2c4a
```

**Engineering interpretation of the diagram:**

- Source → Intake → Treatment → Pumping (if needed) → OHT → Distribution → Consumer
- The OHT is the **balancing node** that decouples upstream supply from downstream demand.
- The VWSC metering node closes the loop financially and operationally.

---

## 4.2 Decision Matrix — Source Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["SELECT SOURCE FOR RURAL HABITATION"]:::core
    nodeQ1{"Source survey done?"}:::decision
    nodeQ2{"Groundwater yield \n satisfactory?"}:::decision
    nodeQ3{"Water quality potable?"}:::decision
    nodeTopo{"Topography permits gravity?"}:::decision
    nodeGW["USE BOREWELL / DUG WELL"]:::opt
    nodeSpring["USE SPRING / AQUEDUCT"]:::opt
    nodeSurface["USE SURFACE RESERVOIR"]:::opt
    nodeTreat["USE TREATMENT TRAIN"]:::opt
    nodeReject["REVISE LOCATION"]:::opt

    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -->|NO| nodeReject
    nodeQ1 -->|YES| nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -->|YES| nodeQ3
    nodeQ2 -->|NO| nodeSurface
    nodeQ3 -->|YES| nodeTopo
    nodeQ3 -->|NO| nodeTreat
    nodeTopo -->|YES| nodeSpring
    nodeTopo -->|NO| nodeGW

    classDef core fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b2c4a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#0b2c4a
    classDef opt fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#385723,color:#0b2c4a
```

---

## 4.3 Service Level Progression Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSL1["LEVEL 1\nBASIC\n55 LPCD\nStandposts"]:::lvl1
    nodeSL2["LEVEL 2\nINTERMEDIATE\n55 LPCD\n50% FHTC"]:::lvl2
    nodeSL3["LEVEL 3\nOPTIMAL\n75 LPCD\n100% FHTC 24x7"]:::lvl3
    nodeSL1 -->|Upgrade| nodeSL2
    nodeSL2 -->|Upgrade| nodeSL3
    classDef lvl1 fill:#fbe5d6,stroke:#833c0c,color:#0b2c4a
    classDef lvl2 fill:#ffe699,stroke:#806000,color:#0b2c4a
    classDef lvl3 fill:#c6e0b4,stroke:#385723,color:#0b2c4a
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic of Demand Stack — What $55$ LPCD Includes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeTop["TOTAL DEMAND Q_avg = 55 LPCD"]:::core
    nodeD1["Domestic drinking\n& cooking\n4 LPCD"]:::part
    nodeD2["Bathing & washing\n25 LPCD"]:::part
    nodeD3["Sanitation / flushing\n10 LPCD"]:::part
    nodeD4["Livestock\n10 LPCD"]:::part
    nodeD5["Losses & wastage\n6 LPCD"]:::part
    nodeTop --> nodeD1
    nodeTop --> nodeD2
    nodeTop --> nodeD3
    nodeTop --> nodeD4
    nodeTop --> nodeD5
    classDef core fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b2c4a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef part fill:#ddebf7,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2c4a
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Mnemonic:** *Drinking, Bathing, Sanitation, Livestock, Losses* → first letters **D-B-S-L-L** = "Do Be Safe, Live Long" 😄.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define *Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC)*. Why is achieving $100\%$ FHTC the goal of the Jal Jeevan Mission?

**Model Answer:**
An **FHTC** is a tap connection that delivers potable water, at a minimum service level of **$55$ LPCD**, in the premises of a household. The Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM, 2019) targets **$100\%$ FHTC coverage** by **2024** to ensure that rural women are spared the drudgery of water collection, that each household can practise safe hygiene, and that drinking water quality is assured at the point-of-use. FHTC also enables **volumetric metering and tariff collection**, ensuring financial sustainability of the rural water supply scheme.

*(Word count target: ~70 words; under 3 marks, 3 distinct points → 1 mark each.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** List and briefly explain the *three service levels* defined under the Jal Jeevan Mission.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Basic Level** — $\geq 55$ LPCD supplied through **standposts / public taps**, with source within $1.6$ km (plains) or $100$ m elevation (hills).
2. **Intermediate Level** — $\geq 55$ LPCD with at least **$50\%$ household tap connections**, partial metering.
3. **Optimal Level** — **$100\%$ FHTC**, $24 \times 7$ supply, full metering, $\geq 55$ LPCD to each household.

*(Each level = 1 mark for name + 1-line description.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A rural habitation has the following census data:**
- $1971$: $P_{1971} = 2{,}500$
- $1981$: $P_{1981} = 2{,}750$
- $1991$: $P_{1991} = 3{,}025$
- $2001$: $P_{2001} = 3{,}330$

Forecast the population in **$2031$** using the **Geometric Increase Method**, then design a **rural water supply scheme** for the forecasted population assuming **$55$ LPCD** basic service level.

#### **Part (a) — Population Forecast (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute decadal growth rates.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \left(\dfrac{2{,}750}{2{,}500}\right)^{1/1} - 1 = 0.100 = 10.00 \% \\[4pt]
r_2 &= \left(\dfrac{3{,}025}{2{,}750}\right)^{1/1} - 1 = 0.100 = 10.00 \% \\[4pt]
r_3 &= \left(\dfrac{3{,}330}{3{,}025}\right)^{1/1} - 1 = 0.101 = 10.10 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average geometric growth rate.**

$$
\bar{r} = \dfrac{10.00 + 10.00 + 10.10}{3} = 10.03\ \%
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Geometric Increase formula for $n = 3$ decades (2001 → 2031).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2031} &= 3{,}330 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{10.03}{100}\right)^{3} \\[4pt]
&= 3{,}330 \times (1.1003)^{3} \\[4pt]
&= 3{,}330 \times 1.331988 \\[4pt]
&= 4{,}435.5 \\[4pt]
\therefore\ P_{2031} &\approx 4{,}500 \text{ (adopt, rounded up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Design of Rural Water Supply Scheme (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Average daily demand.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{avg}} &= \dfrac{P \cdot q}{1000} = \dfrac{4{,}500 \times 55}{1{,}000} = 247.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum daily demand ($K_1 = 1.5$).**

$$
Q_{\text{max-day}} = 1.5 \times 247.5 = 371.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum hourly demand ($K_2 = 3.0$).**

$$
Q_{\text{max-hr}} = 3.0 \times 371.25 = 1{,}113.75 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 4 — Fire demand.** Rural habitation $<$ $50{,}000$, so $F = 0$ ✓

**Step 5 — OHT capacity (33 % rule).**

$$
V_{\text{OHT}} = 0.33 \times 371.25 = 122.5 \text{ m}^3 \Rightarrow \text{adopt } 125 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Pipe sizing (assuming cast-iron, $L = 1.2$ km, velocity $1.0$ m/s).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{pipe}} &= \dfrac{Q_{\text{max-hr}}}{24 \times 3600 \times v} = \dfrac{1{,}113.75}{86{,}400 \times 1.0} = 0.01289 \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt]
d &= \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.01289}{3.1416}} = 0.128 \text{ m} \approx 130 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Schematic summary.**

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Parameter} & \textbf{Value} \\
\hline
P_{2031} & 4{,}500 \\
Q_{\text{avg}} & 247.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \\
Q_{\text{max-day}} & 371.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \\
Q_{\text{max-hr}} & 1{,}113.75 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \\
V_{\text{OHT}} & 125 \text{ m}^3 \\
\text{Rising main diameter} & 130 \text{ mm} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — Explain the Principles of Rural Water Supply with reference to the six pillars adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Point-wise, 1 mark per pillar + 0.5 mark each for elaboration):**

1. **Adequacy** — Supply must meet $\geq 55$ LPCD including livestock demand.
2. **Quality** — Compliance with BIS 10500:2012 — physico-chemical and microbiological.
3. **Accessibility** — Source within $1.6$ km in plains / $100$ m elevation in hills.
4. **Affordability** — Tariff $\leq 5\%$ of monthly household income (WHO norm).
5. **Reliability / Sustainability** — $24 \times 7$ supply and assured groundwater recharge.
6. **Equity** — Coverage prioritising SC/ST habitations, women-headed households, and PwD-friendly standpost heights (max $0.9$ m above ground).

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners often reward answers that **link each pillar to a specific programme or standard** (JJM, BIS, WHO, CPHEEO).

#### **Part (b) — Differentiate between Geometric Increase Method and Incremental Increase Method with formulas and applicability. (7 Marks)**

| Aspect | Geometric Increase | Incremental Increase |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Formula** | $P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{n}$ | $P_n = P_0 + n x + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} y$ |
| **Growth Assumption** | Constant *percentage* growth | Increasing *absolute* growth |
| **Typical Use** | Stable, growing rural villages | Urbanising rural perimeters |
| **Data Requirement** | Two census values | Three census values |
| **Limitation** | Fails if growth is decelerating | More complex but captures saturation |

**Numerical Demonstration (1 mark):** For $P_0 = 1{,}000$, $r = 10\%$, $n = 2$ decades,
Geometric → $1{,}210$; Incremental (with $x = 80, y = 10$) → $1{,}191$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing LPCD with m³/day** — never write "$55$ m³/day per person". The unit is **$55$ litres** per person per day.
> 2. **Forgetting the $1{,}000$ divisor** — $P \times q$ is in *litres*, divide by $1{,}000$ to get *m³*.
> 3. **Including fire demand for rural** — population $< 50{,}000$ → $F = 0$. Examiners deduct full $1$ mark if you blindly apply urban fire formulas.
> 4. **Rounding DOWN the forecasted population** — always round UP for design safety.
> 5. **Forgetting source sustainability comment** — in any RWS design question, examiners expect a sentence on *"assured source yield for the design period"*. Skipping it costs up to $2$ marks.
> 6. **Using $55$ LPCD for urban questions** — urban design uses $135$–$200$ LPCD. Mixing the two is a fatal error.
> 7. **Omitting the institutional angle** — JJM, VWSC, O&M corpus — these are the "easy" marks most students miss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — reproduce from memory before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ **Definition:** Rural water supply = adequate + safe + sustainable + affordable + equitable water delivery to non-urban habitations.
- ☐ **LPCD Basic Service Level** = **$55$ LPCD** (JJM); Optimal = **$55$–$75$ LPCD**; Survival = **$20$ LPCD**.
- ☐ **Accessibility norm:** $1.6$ km / $100$ m elevation (CPHEEO).
- ☐ **Affordability norm:** tariff $\leq 5\%$ of HH income (WHO).
- ☐ **Forecasting methods:** Arithmetic / Geometric / Incremental — Geometric is **default** for rural India.
- ☐ **Growth rate (geometric):** $r = \left[(P_2/P_1)^{1/n} - 1\right] \times 100$.
- ☐ **Peak factors (rural):** $K_1 = 1.5$, $K_2 = 3.0$, composite = $4.5$.
- ☐ **Fire demand** = $0$ for population $< 50{,}000$.
- ☐ **OHT size (rural)** = $0.33 \times Q_{\text{max-day}}$; urban = $0.50 \times Q_{\text{max-day}}$.
- ☐ **Distribution pipe velocity** = $0.6$–$1.5$ m/s.
- ☐ **Sources:** Groundwater (borewell / dugwell / spring) preferred; surface (reservoir / weirs) when groundwater is inadequate.
- ☐ **Treatment train:** SSF + chlorination for surface; chlorination alone for groundwater (after iron removal if needed).
- ☐ **Institutional pillar:** VWSC / GP owns O&M; $5\%$–$10\%$ of capex as O&M corpus.
- ☐ **Quality standard:** BIS 10500:2012 — both microbiological and chemical.
- ☐ **Sustainability check:** Source yield must be confirmed for **pre-monsoon** (worst-case) scenario.
- ☐ **Drought allowance:** Design for $30\%$ source depletion in drought years.
- ☐ **Pumpset power:** $\text{kW} = \dfrac{Q \times H}{75 \times \eta}$, where $\eta \approx 0.7$.
- ☐ **Memorize conversion:** $1$ m³ = $1{,}000$ L = $264.17$ US gal.
- ☐ **Always round UP population forecast** for design safety.
- ☐ **Always mention institutional / community management** in any RWS answer.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Principles of Rural Water Supply.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Water Supply Concepts: Sources, Availability, and Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Water Supply Concepts: Sources, Availability, and Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Rural Water Supply?

**Rural Water Supply (RWS)** is defined as the engineering discipline concerned with the planning, design, construction, operation, and maintenance of systems that provide potable (drinkable) water to villages and small human settlements, where population density is low, distribution networks are sparse, and per-capita water consumption is significantly different from urban contexts.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PECET867*, the scope of this course is aligned with national missions such as the **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** of the Government of India, which aims to provide **55 litres per capita per day (lpcd)** of potable water to every rural household through Functional Household Tap Connections (FHTCs) by 2024.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** "Rural water supply refers to the provision of adequate, safe, and sustainable water to scattered rural populations using appropriate technology, considering low capital investment, ease of operation by villagers, and minimum energy dependence."

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Water Supply Engineering

Any water supply project is built on **three interdependent pillars**:

1. **Source** – Where the water comes from (lake, river, well, spring, rain).
2. **Availability** – How much water is reliably obtainable, both in terms of total quantity and the timing of its delivery.
3. **Quality** – Whether the water is chemically, physically, and bacteriologically safe for human consumption.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Village as a Human Body

Imagine a village is a **living human body**:
- The **rivers, lakes, and aquifers** are the *heart* that pumps the lifeblood (water).
- The **distribution pipes** are the *arteries and veins* carrying blood to every cell (household).
- The **treatment plant** is the *kidney and liver* that cleanses impurities.
- The **household tap** is the *mouth* where the cleanest water finally reaches.
- The **sanitation system** is the *excretory system* that flushes out the waste.

If the source is contaminated, the whole body falls sick. If the distribution arteries are choked, some "organs" (villages) die of thirst even when the "heart" is healthy. This is why **source, availability, and quality** must be studied *together*, never in isolation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard to Memorize:** As per the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) IS 10500:2012** and **WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality (4th Edition, 2017)**, the recommended per-capita water supply in rural India is **55 lpcd** (with 40 lpcd for drinking and cooking + 15 lpcd for bathing and sanitation). In urban areas, this rises to **135–150 lpcd**.

### 1.4 Intuitive Summary

| Pillar | One-line intuition |
|---|---|
| **Source** | Nature's natural reservoir — surface or sub-surface. |
| **Availability** | The "wallet balance" of water — how much can we withdraw without going bankrupt? |
| **Quality** | The "purity stamp" — is the water safe enough to drink directly or after treatment? |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Water — The Engineering Classification

Water sources for rural supply are broadly divided into **three natural categories** and **one harvested category**:

#### A. Surface Water Sources
These are waters that flow on or are stored on the **earth's surface**.

1. **Rivers and Streams** – Perennial (year-round) or seasonal (ephemeral). Yield is calculated using **gauging station data** and the **rational method**.
2. **Lakes and Ponds** – Natural impoundments. Yield is governed by catchment area, rainfall, evaporation, and seepage losses.
3. **Reservoirs (Man-made Lakes)** – Created by constructing dams/barrages across rivers.
4. **Ponds and Tanks (Especially in Kerala)** – The Western Ghats receive **~3000 mm of rainfall/year**, making traditional *Ooranis* (village ponds) an important supplementary source in Palakkad, Malabar, and central Travancore.

#### B. Sub-Surface (Ground Water) Sources
Water stored **below the water table** in saturated geological formations called **aquifers**.

1. **Open Wells (Dug Wells)** – Shallow (5–20 m), constructed manually, suitable for low-yield individual household supply.
2. **Bore Wells / Tube Wells** – Deep (30–300 m), drilled and cased, fitted with submersible pumps.
3. **Springs** – Natural outflows of groundwater where the water table intersects the ground surface. Common in **hilly terrains of Wayanad, Idukki, and the Western Ghats**.
4. **Infiltration Galleries and Wells (Ranney Wells)** – Horizontal collectors used near riverbanks.

#### C. Rainwater (Atmospheric Source)
Direct collection of precipitation from roofs and open catchments. Stored in **RCC/HDPE/Masonry tanks**. Critical in **water-scarce laterite zones of Kasaragod and Attappadi**.

#### D. Reclaimed / Non-Conventional Sources
Treated wastewater, desalinated brackish water, and recycled greywater for non-potable uses (toilet flushing, irrigation).

### 2.2 Water Availability — The Quantitative Pillar

Water availability answers the question: **"Is there enough water to meet the demand of the village for the entire design period?"**

#### Components of Water Availability Analysis

1. **Hydrological Cycle** – Drives all water availability. Evaporation → Condensation → Precipitation → Infiltration → Runoff → back to evaporation.
2. **Catchment Yield** – The volume of water that can be reliably drawn from a source.
3. **Safe Yield** – The maximum rate at which water can be continuously withdrawn without depleting the source.
4. **Recharge Rate** – For ground water, the rate at which aquifers are replenished by rainfall and infiltration.
5. **Per Capita Availability** – Total available water ÷ population.
6. **Water Budget Equation**:

$$ \text{Total Available} = \text{Surface Yield} + \text{Ground Yield} + \text{Rainwater Harvested} - \text{Losses} $$

where Losses include evaporation, seepage, and ecological flow requirement (typically **15–30% of river flow** must be left for downstream ecosystems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Period Standard (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - For rural schemes funded under JJM/NRDWP, the design period is typically **15 to 30 years**.
> - Population forecasting is done for the **mid-design period** (i.e., half the design period) for design of intake and treatment, and for the **full design period** for design of distribution mains.

### 2.3 Water Quality — The Safety Pillar

Water quality is assessed against three classes of parameters:

#### A. Physical Parameters
Turbidity, Colour, Taste, Odour, Temperature, Total Suspended Solids (TSS), Total Dissolved Solids (TDS).

#### B. Chemical Parameters
- pH (acceptable range: **6.5–8.5**)
- Total Hardness (TH) (acceptable: **< 200 mg/L as CaCO₃**)
- Chloride (acceptable: **< 250 mg/L**)
- Iron (acceptable: **< 0.3 mg/L**)
- Fluoride (acceptable: **< 1.0 mg/L**, can cause fluorosis if > 1.5 mg/L)
- Nitrate (acceptable: **< 45 mg/L**)
- Arsenic (acceptable: **< 0.01 mg/L**)
- Residual Chlorine (acceptable: **0.2–0.5 mg/L** at consumer end)

#### C. Bacteriological Parameters
- **Total Coliforms** (MPN/100 mL) — must be **zero** in any 100 mL sample of treated water.
- **Faecal Coliforms (E. coli)** — must be **zero** in any 100 mL sample of treated water.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exams)

| Formula / Equation | Symbolic Form | Application / Use | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Per Capita Demand | $q_d = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{P \times 365}$ | Computing per-person daily water need | $\text{lpcd}$ |
| Total Water Demand | $Q = P \times q_d \times (1 + \text{Loss\%})$ | Total daily supply to a village | $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ |
| Arithmetic Population Forecast | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | Slow-growing old villages | persons |
| Geometric Population Forecast | $P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{n}$ | Rapidly growing towns | persons |
| Incremental Increase Method | $P_n = P_0 + n\bar{x} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y}$ | Most accurate for Indian villages | persons |
| Catchment Yield (Rational) | $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | Runoff from small catchment | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| Safe Yield of Well | $Q_{safe} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot k \cdot D \cdot (H - h)}{\ln(R/r)}$ | Pumping test-based yield | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| Detention Time | $t_d = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Time water stays in tank/clarifier | seconds or hours |
| Residual Chlorine Demand | $\text{Cl}_2 = \text{Dose} - \text{Demand}$ | Disinfection calculation | $\text{mg/L}$ |
| Hardness as CaCO₃ | $TH = 2.5 \times [\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.12 \times [\text{Mg}^{2+}]$ | Convert ion concentrations to CaCO₃ equivalent | $\text{mg/L}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Hint:** In the table above, all absolute-value or logical-OR symbols have been replaced with `\vert` or are written descriptively to comply with markdown table syntax. Use $\vert x \vert$ (latex) when writing inline in the answer booklet.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Arithmetic method** is used by Kerala Rural Water Supply & Sanitation Agency (KRWSA) for **decadal census-based forecasts** of stable villages in Kuttanad, Kavalai, and coastal belts.
- **Geometric method** is used for peri-urban rural panchayats near Kochi, Trivandrum, and Calicut which are experiencing **rapid migration-driven growth**.
- The **Incremental Increase Method** is the most-preferred KTU answer because it accounts for the fact that the *increase* itself is *increasing* (decelerating arithmetically) — typical of Indian rural growth patterns.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arithmetic Progression Method of Population Forecasting

**Starting Premise:** The decadal increase in population remains **constant** over the design period.

Let:
- $P_0$ = Population at the **last known census**
- $P_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots$ = Populations in successive decades
- $\bar{x}$ = Average (arithmetic) decadal increase

**Step 1:** Define the decadal increment.
$$ \bar{x} = \frac{(P_1 - P_0) + (P_2 - P_1) + (P_3 - P_2) + \ldots}{k} $$

**Step 2:** In arithmetic progression, the population in the $n^{\text{th}}$ decade is:
$$ P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x} $$

**Step 3 (Example Verification):** Let $P_0 = 10{,}000$, $\bar{x} = 1{,}000$, $n = 3$ decades.
$$ P_3 = 10{,}000 + 3 \times 1{,}000 = 13{,}000 \text{ persons} $$

This is suitable only for **old, saturated villages** where growth has stabilized.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Geometric Progression Method

**Starting Premise:** The decadal **growth rate** is constant.

Let:
- $r$ = average geometric growth rate (% per decade)
- $P_0$ = base population
- $n$ = number of decades in the future

**Step 1:** The growth factor per decade is:
$$ G_r = 1 + \frac{r}{100} $$

**Step 2:** After $n$ decades:
$$ P_n = P_0 \cdot G_r^{n} $$

**Step 3 (Calculation of r from past data):**
$$ r = \left[ \left(\frac{P_n}{P_0}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1 \right] \times 100 $$

**Step 4 (Numerical Example):** $P_0 = 10{,}000$, $P_2 = 12{,}321$, $n = 2$ decades.
$$ r = \left[ \left(\frac{12{,}321}{10{,}000}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} - 1 \right] \times 100 = \left[ 1.11 - 1 \right] \times 100 = 11\% \text{ per decade} $$

So the population after 3 more decades (i.e., at $n = 5$ from $P_0$):
$$ P_5 = 10{,}000 \times (1.11)^{5} = 10{,}000 \times 1.6851 = 16{,}851 \text{ persons} $$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Incremental Increase Method (Most-Preferred KTU Method)

**Starting Premise:** The **increase in population** itself changes by a constant amount $\bar{y}$ per decade (i.e., the increment is in arithmetic progression).

**Step 1:** Let the increments be $x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_k$ for $k$ previous decades.
$$ \bar{x} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + \ldots + x_k}{k} $$

**Step 2:** The incremental increase in increments is:
$$ \bar{y} = \frac{(x_2 - x_1) + (x_3 - x_2) + \ldots}{k - 1} $$

**Step 3:** The future population at the $n^{\text{th}}$ decade is the sum of:
- Base population
- $n$ times the average increment
- Sum of an arithmetic series of $\bar{y}$:

$$ P_n = P_0 + n\bar{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y} $$

**Step 4 (Worked Example):** 
- $P_0 = 5{,}000$ (in 2020)
- $P_1 = 5{,}400$ (in 2030) $\Rightarrow x_1 = 400$
- $P_2 = 5{,}750$ (in 2040) $\Rightarrow x_2 = 350$
- $P_3 = 6{,}050$ (in 2050) $\Rightarrow x_3 = 300$

Calculate $\bar{x}$, $\bar{y}$:
$$ \bar{x} = \frac{400 + 350 + 300}{3} = 350 $$

$$ \bar{y} = \frac{(350-400) + (300-350)}{2} = \frac{-50 - 50}{2} = -50 $$

Find $P_4$ (year 2060, $n = 4$):
$$ P_4 = 5{,}000 + 4(350) + \frac{4 \times 5}{2}(-50) = 5{,}000 + 1{,}400 - 500 = 5{,}900 $$

This **negative $\bar{y}$** correctly shows the village is **depopulating** (typical of rural-to-urban migration seen in parts of central Kerala).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Water Quality Parameters & BIS Limits

| Parameter | Symbol | Acceptable Limit (BIS 10500) | Permissible Limit (No Alternative) | Health Concern if Exceeded |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| pH | pH | 6.5 – 8.5 | No relaxation | Corrosion / scaling |
| Turbidity | NTU | $\leq 1$ | 5 | Carries pathogens |
| Total Hardness | $\text{mg/L}$ as CaCO₃ | $\leq 200$ | 600 | Cardiovascular, scaling |
| Iron (Fe) | $\text{mg/L}$ | $\leq 0.3$ | No relaxation | Taste, staining, hemochromatosis |
| Fluoride (F) | $\text{mg/L}$ | $\leq 1.0$ | 1.5 | Fluorosis if > 1.5 |
| Chloride (Cl⁻) | $\text{mg/L}$ | $\leq 250$ | 1,000 | Salty taste, corrosion |
| Nitrate (NO₃) | $\text{mg/L}$ | $\leq 45$ | No relaxation | Blue-baby syndrome |
| Arsenic (As) | $\text{mg/L}$ | $\leq 0.01$ | No relaxation | Cancer, skin lesions |
| Total Coliforms | MPN/100 mL | **0** | **0** | Water-borne disease |
| Faecal Coliforms | MPN/100 mL | **0** | **0** | Cholera, typhoid |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Trick:** The "Permissible Limit" column is the *extended limit* applied **only in the absence of an alternative source**. For a question asking "design a rural water supply scheme", you must always design to the **Acceptable Limit**, never the permissible one.
