## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]  
**Define "Safe Yield" of a well. List two factors that affect the safe yield of an open well in a rural area.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** The *Safe Yield* of a well is defined as the maximum rate at which water can be continuously withdrawn from a well (or group of wells) **without depleting the source** and without exceeding the natural recharge of the aquifer.
- **Factor 1 (1 Mark):** The permeability (*k*) and thickness (*b*) of the aquifer.
- **Factor 2 (1 Mark):** The diameter and depth of the well, and the drawdown permissible based on pump capacity.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]  
**Distinguish between "Geometric Increase Method" and "Incremental Increase Method" of population forecasting. When is each preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Geometric Increase Method (1 Mark):** Assumes a *constant decadal growth rate* $r$. Formula: $P_n = P_0 (1 + r/100)^n$. Used for **newly developing towns or peri-urban panchayats** experiencing rapid, exponential growth.
- **Incremental Increase Method (1 Mark):** Assumes the *increment in population itself increases/decreases arithmetically* by a constant $\bar{y}$. Formula: $P_n = P_0 + n\bar{x} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y}$. Used for **old, established Indian villages** where growth is non-uniform.
- **Distinction (1 Mark):** Geometric is purely multiplicative; Incremental is additive with a quadratic correction term, hence more accurate for typical Indian rural growth patterns.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a) 7 Marks:** A village had the following population data from the last four censuses:

| Year | Population |
|---|---|
| 1991 | 4,200 |
| 2001 | 4,800 |
| 2011 | 5,300 |
| 2021 | 5,650 |

Estimate the population in **2041** using the **Incremental Increase Method**. Comment on the trend. **Assume present is 2024, design period = 30 years, mid-design population is for 2039.**

**(b) 7 Marks:** With a neat sketch, list the **physical, chemical, and bacteriological parameters** used to assess the potability of rural drinking water. State the BIS 10500:2012 acceptable limits for any **three** parameters.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Calculate decadal increments.
$$ x_1 = 4{,}800 - 4{,}200 = 600 $$
$$ x_2 = 5{,}300 - 4{,}800 = 500 $$
$$ x_3 = 5{,}650 - 5{,}300 = 350 $$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Calculate average increment $\bar{x}$.
$$ \bar{x} = \frac{600 + 500 + 350}{3} = \frac{1450}{3} \approx 483.33 $$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Calculate incremental increase $\bar{y}$.
$$ y_1 = 500 - 600 = -100 $$
$$ y_2 = 350 - 500 = -150 $$
$$ \bar{y} = \frac{-100 + (-150)}{2} = -125 $$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Identify n for target year 2041.
From 2021 (last census) to 2041 = **2 decades**, so $n = 2$.

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Apply the formula.
$$ P_n = P_0 + n\bar{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y} $$
$$ P_2 = 5{,}650 + 2(483.33) + \frac{2 \times 3}{2}(-125) $$
$$ P_2 = 5{,}650 + 966.66 - 375 = 6{,}241.66 \approx 6{,}242 \text{ persons} $$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Comment.
The **negative $\bar{y}$** shows the village is **losing population** at a decelerating rate — typical of rural-to-urban migration. The scheme designer must plan for a **lower ultimate population** than simple arithmetic extrapolation.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Sketch (2 Marks):** Provide a labelled diagram with three branches (Physical / Chemical / Bacteriological) and 4 parameters under each (see Section 4.1 flow chart logic).

**Physical Parameters (1.5 Marks):**
- Turbidity (acceptable: $\leq 1$ NTU), Colour ($\leq 5$ Hazen units), Taste & Odour (Unobjectionable), TDS ($\leq 500$ mg/L).

**Chemical Parameters (1.5 Marks):**
- pH (6.5 – 8.5), Total Hardness ($\leq 200$ mg/L), Iron ($\leq 0.3$ mg/L), Fluoride ($\leq 1.0$ mg/L), Nitrate ($\leq 45$ mg/L), Chloride ($\leq 250$ mg/L).

**Bacteriological Parameters (1 Mark):**
- Total Coliforms: must be **0 MPN/100 mL** in any sample.  
- Faecal Coliforms (*E. coli*): must be **0 MPN/100 mL** in any sample.

**BIS Limits for three parameters (1 Mark):**
- pH: 6.5–8.5
- Total Hardness: $\leq 200$ mg/L
- Total Coliforms: 0 MPN/100 mL

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a) 7 Marks:** Explain the **rational method** for estimating the surface runoff yield from a small rural catchment. Define each term in the formula and discuss its limitation.

**(b) 7 Marks:** A panchayat in Palakkad requires water for a projected population of **25,000**. If the per-capita demand is **55 lpcd**, and losses (UFW + transmission) are **15%**, calculate the total daily water requirement in **m³/day** and design a **service reservoir capacity** for **12 hours** of balanced storage.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Formula (3 Marks):**
$$ Q = C \cdot I \cdot A $$

**Term Definitions (2 Marks):**
- $Q$ = Peak runoff rate (m³/s)
- $C$ = Runoff coefficient (dimensionless, 0.3 – 0.9 for various surfaces; for rural agricultural catchment, typical value is 0.4 – 0.6)
- $I$ = Rainfall intensity (m/s) for the chosen time of concentration
- $A$ = Catchment area (m²)

**Explanation (1 Mark):** The rational method assumes that the peak runoff occurs when the **entire catchment contributes** to the outlet at the rainfall intensity equal to the **time of concentration**. It is valid only for small catchments (typically $\leq 50$ km²).

**Limitation (1 Mark):** It does not account for storm variability, antecedent moisture condition, or infiltration losses over time — hence the designer must use a **safety factor of 1.2 to 1.5**.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Compute the total daily demand.
$$ Q_d = P \times q_d = 25{,}000 \times 55 = 1{,}375{,}000 \text{ L/day} $$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Add losses.
$$ Q_{gross} = Q_d \times (1 + 0.15) = 1{,}375{,}000 \times 1.15 = 1{,}581{,}250 \text{ L/day} $$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Convert to m³/day.
$$ Q_{gross} = \frac{1{,}581{,}250}{1000} = 1{,}581.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Compute the flow rate per second.
$$ Q_{s} = \frac{1{,}581.25}{24 \times 3600} = \frac{1{,}581.25}{86{,}400} \approx 0.01830 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 18.30 \text{ L/s} $$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Design ESR capacity for 12 hours.
$$ V_{ESR} = \frac{Q_{gross}}{2} = \frac{1{,}581.25}{2} = 790.6 \text{ m}^3 $$

(In practice, KTU/JJM schemes add a **fire storage of 50–100 m³** for rural panchayats, taking the final ESR capacity to **~850 m³**, typically a circular ground-level tank of **15 m diameter × 5 m depth**.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this module (KTU Board pattern):**
> 1. **Wrong formula for "n":** In population forecasting, $n$ is the number of *decades*, not the number of years. Forecasting 30 years from 2024 = $n = 3$, not $n = 30$. This single error costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing Permissible and Acceptable limits:** In quality-parameter questions, students often quote the *permissible* limit when the *acceptable* limit is asked. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting to add losses:** When asked for "total water to be produced", you must add UFW (Unaccounted For Water) and transmission losses. A 15% mark goes unrewarded.
> 4. **Units in ESR calculation:** Many students leave the answer in litres. Always convert to **m³** and mention a *circular/rectangular* tank with practical dimensions.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch:** In Q-A(b) above, the 2-mark sketch is non-negotiable. Even a hand-drawn block diagram fetches full marks.
> 6. **No mention of BIS/WHO standards:** Every quality-parameter answer MUST cite the standard (IS 10500:2012 / WHO 4th Edn.). Examiners specifically look for this citation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Three pillars of RWS:** Source, Availability, Quality — never isolated.
- [x] **Rural per-capita standard:** **55 lpcd** as per JJM / BIS / WHO.
- [x] **Design period for rural schemes:** 15–30 years (mid-design population for treatment design).
- [x] **Three population methods:** Arithmetic (constant increment), Geometric (constant rate), Incremental Increase (best for Indian villages).
- [x] **Incremental formula:** $P_n = P_0 + n\bar{x} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y}$.
- [x] **Rational method for runoff:** $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ — only for small catchments ($\leq 50$ km²).
- [x] **Physical quality parameters:** Turbidity ($\leq 1$ NTU), Colour, TDS, Taste, Odour.
- [x] **Chemical quality parameters:** pH (6.5–8.5), Hardness ($\leq 200$ mg/L), Iron ($\leq 0.3$ mg/L), Fluoride ($\leq 1.0$ mg/L), Nitrate ($\leq 45$ mg/L), Chloride ($\leq 250$ mg/L).
- [x] **Bacteriological quality:** Total & Faecal Coliforms must be **0 MPN/100 mL** in treated water.
- [x] **Source types:** Surface (rivers, lakes, tanks, ooranis), Sub-surface (open wells, bore wells, springs, infiltration galleries), Rainwater harvesting, Reclaimed.
- [x] **Kerala context:** Ooranis in Palakkad/Malabar, springs in Wayanad/Idukki, rainwater in Kasaragod/Attappadi, brackish desalination in coastal Kollam/Alleppey.
- [x] **Safe yield** of a well must consider permeability, drawdown, and recharge rate.
- [x] **Losses to add:** Always include **15% UFW + transmission losses** when computing gross water demand.
- [x] **ESR capacity rule:** $\dfrac{1}{4}$ to $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of total daily demand (12-hour storage is standard for rural).
- [x] **Cite standards:** Every answer should mention *BIS IS 10500:2012* or *WHO 2017* at least once.
- [x] **Migrating rural villages** have **negative $\bar{y}$** — design for the actual (lower) population, not the census arithmetic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Demand Estimation in Rural Communities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Water Demand Estimation in Rural Communities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 💧 Water Demand Estimation in Rural Communities

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU / CPHEEO Manual Standard):**
> **Water Demand Estimation** is the systematic engineering process of quantitatively determining the total volume of water required by a rural community over a specified **design period**, accounting for all intended end-uses (domestic, livestock, institutional, commercial, fire-fighting, and unavoidable losses), expressed in **litres per capita per day (lpcd)** or **cubic metres per day (m³/day)**.

The estimated demand dictates the **capacity of intake structures**, **treatment units**, **storage reservoirs (overhead tanks / sumps)**, and the **diameter of the distribution network**.

### 1.2 Components of Rural Water Demand (as per CPHEEO, 2021 & WHO Guidelines)

A rural water supply scheme must cater to the following demand categories:

| S.No | Demand Component | Typical % of Total |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | **Domestic Demand** (drinking, cooking, bathing, washing) | 60 – 70 % |
| 2 | **Livestock Demand** (cattle, goats, poultry) | 15 – 20 % |
| 3 | **Institutional Demand** (schools, anganwadis, PHCs) | 5 – 8 % |
| 4 | **Commercial & Religious** (shops, temples, mosques) | 2 – 5 % |
| 5 | **Fire-Fighting Demand** | 1 – 3 % |
| 6 | **System Losses** (UFs, leakage, pilferage) | 10 – 15 % |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Village Water Budget"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like planning a family grocery budget.** Before going to the market, you estimate: how many people will eat (population), what each person needs per day (per capita demand), how much extra for guests and festivals (peak demand), and how much gets wasted on the way home (transmission losses). Water demand estimation works the same way — we project population, assign a per-head quota, add buffers for peak hours and emergencies, and inflate for losses.

### 1.4 Standard Per Capita Demand Values (CPHEEO / WHO Benchmarks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Standard Values to Memorise:**
> - **Rural India (CPHEEO 2021):** **55 lpcd** (minimum service level) → **70 lpcd** (intermediate) → **100 lpcd** (full level)
> - **WHO Standard (basic access):** **50 lpcd**
> - **Cattle water requirement:** **30 – 50 litres/head/day** (large animals), **5 – 10 litres/head/day** (small animals)
> - **Poultry:** **1 – 2 litres/bird/day**
> - **Design Period:** **15 – 30 years** (rural schemes: typically **15 years** for head works, **30 years** for pipelines/ESRs)

These values are the **yardsticks** Kerala Board examiners use while setting numerical problems. Missing them in your answer = direct loss of marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Influencing Water Demand in Rural Areas

The water demand of a rural community is **not a constant**; it varies spatially and temporally. The major governing factors are:

1. **Climatic Conditions** — Higher demand in hot, dry months (March–May in Kerala).
2. **Standard of Living** — Higher income → more sanitary fixtures → higher consumption.
3. **Population Size & Density** — Larger villages = higher absolute demand but lower per capita.
4. **Cost of Water & Tariff Structure** — Metered supply reduces demand; flat-rate increases it.
5. **Quality of Water Supplied** — Saline or contaminated source → reduced use.
6. **System Pressure & Reliability** — Inadequate pressure forces users to store, reducing daily draw.
7. **Agricultural & Livestock Pattern** — Predominantly farming villages = high livestock demand.
8. **Habits & Customs** — Bathing patterns, festival-related extra use.
9. **Distribution System** — Leaking networks (typical in rural Kerala) inflate apparent demand.

## 2.2 Fundamental Formula Set — KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important revision asset for this module. Memorise every equation.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Total Daily Demand | $Q = P \times q + L + I + F$ | L/day | $P$ = population, $q$ = per capita demand |
| 2 | Average Daily Demand (ADD) | $ADD = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{365}$ | m³/day | Used for storage sizing |
| 3 | Maximum Daily Demand (MDD) | $MDD = 1.8 \times ADD$ | m³/day | Peak summer day |
| 4 | Maximum Hourly Demand (MHD) | $MHD = 1.5 \times MDD = 2.7 \times ADD$ | m³/day | Peak instantaneous hour |
| 5 | Peak Factor (PF) | $PF = \dfrac{MHD}{ADD}$ | – | Rural: 2.7 – 3.0 |
| 6 | Fire Demand (Kuchling's) | $Q_f = 3182 \sqrt{P}$ | L/min | $P$ in thousands |
| 7 | Fire Demand (Buston's) | $Q_f = 5663 \sqrt{P}$ | L/min | $P$ in thousands |
| 8 | Arithmetic Increase | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | persons | $\bar{x}$ = avg decadal increment |
| 9 | Geometric Increase | $P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n$ | persons | $r$ = geometric growth % |
| 10 | Incremental Increase | $P_n = P_0 + n \bar{x} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \bar{y}$ | persons | $\bar{y}$ = avg of incremental increase |
| 11 | Livestock Demand | $Q_L = N_L \times q_L$ | L/day | $N_L$ = no. of animals |
| 12 | Total Demand (Rural) | $Q_T = \dfrac{P \cdot q + \sum N_i q_i}{1000}$ | m³/day | Final design quantity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Vertical Bars in Equations:** The pipe symbol $\vert$ is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. KTU examiners accept both inline math and explicit units in the final numerical answer.

## 2.3 Population Forecasting Methods — Theory

Forecasting population is the **first step** before demand estimation. KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises the following three classical methods:

### (a) Arithmetic Increase Method
- Assumes population increases at a **constant absolute** rate per decade.
- Useful for **old, saturated** villages where growth has plateaued.
- Limitation: Overestimates for rapidly growing panchayats.

### (b) Geometric Increase Method
- Assumes population grows at a **constant percentage** rate.
- Most accurate for **young, expanding** rural towns (e.g., peri-urban panchayats in Ernakulam, Thrissur).
- Limitation: Predicts unrealistically high values for long design periods.

### (c) Incremental Increase Method
- A hybrid — assumes the **increase itself increases** by a constant amount.
- Most suitable for **medium-growth** Kerala rural areas.
- Most commonly used answer in KTU exam problems.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

- **ESR Capacity Design** — ESR (Elevated Service Reservoir) is sized to hold ~ **40–50 % of MDD** for rural schemes.
- **Pump Selection** — Pump flow rate $\geq$ MHD + Fire demand.
- **Pipeline Diameter** — Estimated using **Hazen-Williams or Manning's equation** with design discharge = MHD.
- **Scheme Funding** — Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) approves projects based on this exact estimation framework, so the methodology has direct policy relevance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Water Demand Equation

Starting from first principles, the **Total Water Demand ($Q_T$)** for a rural community is the sum of all component demands:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_T &= Q_{domestic} + Q_{livestock} + Q_{institutional} + Q_{commercial} + Q_{fire} + Q_{losses}
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{domestic} &= P \times q \quad \text{(P = population, q = per capita demand in lpcd)} \\
Q_{livestock} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (N_i \times q_i) \quad \text{(N_i = no. of animals of type i, q_i = per-animal demand)} \\
Q_{institutional} &= \text{(Schools, PHC, anganwadi, panchayat office) — estimated empirically} \\
Q_{fire} &= 3182 \sqrt{P} \;\; \text{(L/min) for small rural centres} \\
Q_{losses} &= 0.10 \times Q_T \quad \text{(10 % of total as system losses)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_T = 1.10 \left[ P \cdot q + \sum N_i q_i + I + F \right]\,}
$$

where the **1.10 factor** accounts for 10 % system losses. The final design quantity is in **litres/day**, and is converted to **m³/day** by dividing by 1000.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Population by Incremental Increase Method

Let $P_0$ = present population, $n$ = number of decades, $\bar{x}$ = mean decadal increase, $\bar{y}$ = mean of incremental increases.

**Decade-wise population:**
- After 1 decade: $P_1 = P_0 + x_1$
- After 2 decades: $P_2 = P_0 + (x_1 + x_2)$
- After $n$ decades:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= P_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \\
&= P_0 + n \bar{x} + \bar{y} \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i \\
&= P_0 + n \bar{x} + \bar{y} \cdot \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the standard **Incremental Increase Formula** expected in KTU answers. (Some texts write $n(n+1)/2$ using 1-indexed summation — both are accepted if the derivation is shown.)

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem

> **Problem [Adapted from KTU 2022 ESE Pattern]:**
> A rural panchayat in Thrissur district had a population of **12,000 in 2000**, **14,500 in 2010**, and **17,800 in 2020**. Estimate the population in **2040** using (a) Arithmetic, (b) Geometric, and (c) Incremental Increase methods. Also calculate the total water demand in m³/day assuming per capita demand = **70 lpcd**, cattle = **800** at 40 L/head/day, goats = **600** at 8 L/head/day, school children = **1500** at 15 L/head/day, and fire demand for the village.

### Step 1 — Compute Decadal Increments

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 14500 - 12000 = 2500 \quad (\text{2000} \to \text{2010}) \\
x_2 &= 17800 - 14500 = 3300 \quad (\text{2010} \to \text{2020}) \\
\bar{x} &= \dfrac{2500 + 3300}{2} = 2900 \quad \text{(mean decadal increase)} \\
\bar{y} &= \dfrac{3300 - 2500}{2} = 400 \quad \text{(mean of incremental increase)} \\
n &= 2 \text{ decades (2020 to 2040)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — (a) Arithmetic Method

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2040} &= P_{2020} + n \cdot \bar{x} \\
&= 17800 + 2 \times 2900 \\
&= 17800 + 5800 \\
&= 23600 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — (b) Geometric Method

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \dfrac{2500}{12000} \times 100 = 20.83\% \\
r_2 &= \dfrac{3300}{14500} \times 100 = 22.76\% \\
\bar{r} &= \dfrac{20.83 + 22.76}{2} = 21.80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2040} &= P_{2020} \left(1 + \dfrac{\bar{r}}{100}\right)^n \\
&= 17800 \times (1.218)^2 \\
&= 17800 \times 1.4835 \\
&= 26407 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — (c) Incremental Increase Method

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2040} &= P_{2020} + n \bar{x} + \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} \bar{y} \\
&= 17800 + 2(2900) + \dfrac{2(1)}{2}(400) \\
&= 17800 + 5800 + 400 \\
&= 24000 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Adopted Design Population

For KTU answers, the **Incremental Increase result is preferred** (most realistic for Kerala's growth pattern).

$$
P_{design} = 24000 \text{ persons}
$$

### Step 6 — Component-Wise Water Demand

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{domestic} &= 24000 \times 70 = 1680000 \text{ L/day} \\
Q_{cattle} &= 800 \times 40 = 32000 \text{ L/day} \\
Q_{goats} &= 600 \times 8 = 4800 \text{ L/day} \\
Q_{school} &= 1500 \times 15 = 22500 \text{ L/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Fire Demand (Kuchling's Formula)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 24 \text{ (in thousands)} \\
Q_f &= 3182 \sqrt{24} = 3182 \times 4.899 = 15590 \text{ L/min} \\
Q_f \text{ per day} &= 15590 \times 60 \times 3 \text{ (3-hr fire duration)} \\
&= 2806200 \text{ L/day} \quad \text{-- typically averaged over a day for design}
\end{aligned}
$$

For rural Kerala, fire demand is usually **not added to daily demand** but reserved as a **stand-alone volume** in ESR. For the KTU calculation, we add 50 % of $Q_f$ as a conservative estimate:

$$
Q_{fire,\,design} = 0.5 \times 2806200 = 1403100 \text{ L/day (averaged contribution)}
$$

### Step 8 — Total Demand with 10 % Losses

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_T &= 1.10 \left[ 1680000 + 32000 + 4800 + 22500 + 1403100 \right] \\
&= 1.10 \times 3142400 \\
&= 3456640 \text{ L/day} \\
&= 3456.64 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 9 — Maximum Hourly Demand (MHD)

$$
\begin{aligned}
MHD &= 2.7 \times ADD = 2.7 \times 3456.64 = 9332.93 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \\
&= \dfrac{9332.93}{24} = 388.87 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} \\
&= 108.02 \text{ L/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer Summary

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| Design Population (2040) | 24,000 persons |
| Average Daily Demand | 3,456.64 m³/day |
| Maximum Daily Demand (1.8 × ADD) | 6,221.95 m³/day |
| Maximum Hourly Demand | 9,332.93 m³/day (108 L/s) |
| Recommended ESR Capacity (~50 % MDD) | ~ 3,110 m³ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Rural Water Demand Estimation Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete decision flow from **raw census data** to **final design quantity**, as expected in KTU 14-mark theory answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect Census Data] --> B[Compute Decadal Increments x1, x2, x3]
    B --> C{Choose Forecasting Method}
    C -->|Arithmetic| D1[Arithmetic Method: Pn = P0 + n.xbar]
    C -->|Geometric| D2[Geometric Method: Pn = P0 times 1 + r/100 to power n]
    C -->|Incremental| D3[Incremental Increase: Pn = P0 + n.xbar + n times n-1 over 2 times ybar]
    D1 --> E[Select Design Population Pdesign]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Identify Demand Components]
    F --> F1[Domestic: P times q]
    F --> F2[Livestock: sum Ni times qi]
    F --> F3[Institutional and Commercial]
    F --> F4[Fire: 3182 sqrt P in L per min]
    F1 --> G[Sum Component Demands]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Add 10 to 15 percent for System Losses]
    H --> I[Compute ADD: Total per 365]
    I --> J[Apply Peak Factors: MDD = 1.8 ADD, MHD = 2.7 ADD]
    J --> K[Output: Design Quantity in cubic metre per day]
    K --> L[Sizing of Intake, Treatment, ESR, and Pipe Network]
```

## 4.2 Component-Level Block Architecture

The diagram below shows how each **demand block** feeds the central **Total Demand Engine**, mimicking the analytical structure of a KTU numerical answer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Population P persons] -->|Multiply by 70 lpcd| B1[Domestic Block]
    A2[Cattle, Goat, Poultry Counts] -->|Multiply by per head demand| B2[Livestock Block]
    A3[Schools, PHC, Anganwadi] -->|Empirical Estimates| B3[Institutional Block]
    A4[Shops, Temples, Mosque] -->|5 to 10 percent of domestic| B4[Commercial Block]
    A5[Population in Thousands] -->|Kuchling 3182 sqrt P| B5[Fire Block]
    B1 --> C[Total Demand Aggregator]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    C -->|Add 10 percent Losses| D[Final Design Quantity in cubic metre per day]
    D --> E1[ESR Capacity: 40 to 50 percent MDD]
    D --> E2[Pump Capacity: MHD plus Fire]
    D --> E3[Pipe Diameter: MHD via Hazen Williams]
    D --> E4[Treatment Plant Sizing]
```

## 4.3 Population Forecasting Method — Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start{Rural Panchayat Growth Pattern}
    start -->|Old and Saturated| m1[Arithmetic Increase Method]
    start -->|Young and Rapidly Expanding| m2[Geometric Increase Method]
    start -->|Medium Growth Kerala Typical| m3[Incremental Increase Method]
    m1 --> note1[Linear, Constant Decadal Add]
    m2 --> note2[Exponential, Constant Percent]
    m3 --> note3[Quadratic, Increment itself rises]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**List the various components of water demand in a rural community and state the CPHEEO-recommended per capita water supply standard for rural areas.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The components of rural water demand are: (i) Domestic demand (drinking, cooking, bathing, washing clothes) — major share **60 – 70 %**; (ii) Livestock demand (cattle, goats, poultry) — **15 – 20 %**; (iii) Institutional demand (schools, anganwadi, PHC, panchayat office) — **5 – 8 %**; (iv) Commercial and religious demand — **2 – 5 %**; (v) Fire-fighting demand — **1 – 3 %**; (vi) System losses (leakage, UFs) — **10 – 15 %**.

As per **CPHEEO Manual 2021**, the per capita water supply for rural India is recommended as:
- **55 lpcd** — minimum service level
- **70 lpcd** — intermediate service level
- **100 lpcd** — full service level

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Students often forget the system loss component. A full 0.5 mark is reserved for mentioning "losses" — do not skip it.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is meant by "design period" in a rural water supply scheme? Why is the design period for pipelines usually taken longer than that for pumps or treatment units?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **design period** of a water supply scheme is the number of years for which the system is designed to meet the projected demand before major expansion or replacement becomes necessary.

- **Head works (intake, raw water pump):** 15 – 25 years
- **Treatment plant:** 15 – 20 years
- **Pipelines / Distribution network:** 30 years
- **ESR (storage tank):** 30 years

Pipelines and ESRs have a **longer physical life (40 – 60 years)** and are difficult/expensive to replace once laid (especially in Kerala's narrow village roads). Hence their design period is longer. Pumps and treatment units, in contrast, have **shorter mechanical/technological life (10 – 15 years)** and are easily upgraded or replaced. Designing them for a long period would require unnecessarily large capital investment today.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Mixing litres and m³ — always state units at every step.
> 2. **Wrong Decadal Count:** If census years are 1991, 2001, 2011, the interval is **1 decade**, not 10 years. Be explicit.
> 3. **Forgetting Fire Demand:** Examiners allocate 2–3 marks specifically for fire demand calculation. Missing it = 0 marks for that sub-part.
> 4. **No Loss Allowance:** Adding 10 – 15 % for losses is mandatory; the rubric deducts 1 mark if omitted.
> 5. **Geometric % vs Decimal:** Students write $r = 20$ instead of $r = 0.20$ — a classic 1-mark killer.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Pattern — Module 1 Choice]**

A rural panchayat had the following census populations:

| Year | Population |
| :---: | :---: |
| 1990 | 8,000 |
| 2000 | 9,500 |
| 2010 | 11,400 |
| 2020 | 13,800 |

Estimate the population in **2040** using the **Geometric Increase method** and the **Incremental Increase method**. With the most suitable forecasted population, determine the **total water demand in m³/day** and the **ESR capacity**, given:

- Per capita demand = 70 lpcd
- Cattle = 600, demand 40 L/head/day
- Goats = 400, demand 8 L/head/day
- One school with 1000 students, demand 15 L/head/day
- Fire demand by Kuchling's formula
- Losses = 10 % of total
- ESR = 50 % of MDD
- MDD = 1.8 × ADD

#### **Part (a) — Population Forecast (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Decadal Growth Rates (Geometric Method)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \dfrac{9500 - 8000}{8000} \times 100 = 18.75\% \\
r_2 &= \dfrac{11400 - 9500}{9500} \times 100 = 20.00\% \\
r_3 &= \dfrac{13800 - 11400}{11400} \times 100 = 21.05\% \\
\bar{r} &= \dfrac{18.75 + 20.00 + 21.05}{3} = 19.93\% \approx 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Population in 2040 by Geometric Method**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 2 \text{ decades (2020} \to \text{2040)} \\
P_{2040,\,geo} &= 13800 \times (1.20)^2 = 13800 \times 1.44 = 19872 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decadal Increments (Incremental Increase Method)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 9500 - 8000 = 1500 \\
x_2 &= 11400 - 9500 = 1900 \\
x_3 &= 13800 - 11400 = 2400 \\
\bar{x} &= \dfrac{1500 + 1900 + 2400}{3} = 1933.33 \\
\bar{y} &= \dfrac{(1900-1500) + (2400-1900)}{3} = \dfrac{400 + 500}{3} = 300
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Population in 2040 by Incremental Increase Method**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2040,\,inc} &= 13800 + 2(1933.33) + \dfrac{2(1)}{2}(300) \\
&= 13800 + 3866.67 + 300 \\
&= 17966.67 \approx 17967 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Selection of Design Population**

For a steadily expanding panchayat with **decreasing incremental growth** (note $x_1=1500$, $x_2=1900$, $x_3=2400$ — actually increasing, so geometric is slightly more aggressive), we **adopt the Incremental Increase result** as the more conservative, realistic estimate:

$$
\boxed{P_{design} = 17967 \approx 18000 \text{ persons}}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Total Water Demand & ESR Sizing (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Component Demands**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{domestic} &= 18000 \times 70 = 1260000 \text{ L/day} \\
Q_{cattle} &= 600 \times 40 = 24000 \text{ L/day} \\
Q_{goats} &= 400 \times 8 = 3200 \text{ L/day} \\
Q_{school} &= 1000 \times 15 = 15000 \text{ L/day} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Fire Demand (Kuchling's)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 18 \text{ (in thousands)} \\
Q_f &= 3182 \sqrt{18} = 3182 \times 4.243 = 13500 \text{ L/min} \\
\text{For 3-hr fire duration per day:} \\
Q_{fire} &= 13500 \times 60 \times 3 = 2430000 \text{ L/day} \\
\text{Design addition (50 % of fire):} \\
Q_{fire,\,design} &= 1215000 \text{ L/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Demand with 10 % Losses**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{raw} &= 1260000 + 24000 + 3200 + 15000 + 1215000 = 2517200 \text{ L/day} \\
Q_T &= 1.10 \times 2517200 = 2768920 \text{ L/day} = 2768.92 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — MDD, ESR Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ADD &= 2768.92 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \\
MDD &= 1.8 \times 2768.92 = 4984.06 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \\
ESR &= 0.50 \times MDD = 0.50 \times 4984.06 = 2492.03 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| Population (Geometric, 2040) | 19,872 |
| Population (Incremental, 2040) | 17,967 |
| Adopted Design Population | 18,000 |
| Total Demand (with losses) | 2,768.92 m³/day |
| MDD | 4,984.06 m³/day |
| **Recommended ESR Capacity** | **≈ 2,492 m³** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Define **per capita demand** and **peak factor**. Explain the various **factors affecting water demand** in rural communities. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The water supply scheme of a village serves a present population of **15,000**. Assuming an arithmetic increase of **300 persons per year**, estimate the population **30 years hence**. If the per capita demand is **135 lpcd**, find (i) Average Daily Demand, (ii) Maximum Daily Demand, (iii) Maximum Hourly Demand, and (iv) Storage reservoir capacity at 40 % of MDD. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Definitions & Factors (7 marks)**

**Per Capita Demand (q):** The average amount of water required per person per day, expressed in **litres per capita per day (lpcd)**.

$$
q = \dfrac{\text{Total water supplied per day to the community}}{\text{Population served}}
$$

**Peak Factor (PF):** The ratio of the **maximum hourly demand** to the **average daily demand**, used to account for peak instantaneous loads on the distribution system.

$$
PF = \dfrac{MHD}{ADD} = 2.7 \text{ (typical for rural areas)}
$$

**Factors affecting rural water demand:** (enumerate as in Section 2.1 above; examiners expect **at least 6 factors** for full 7 marks)

> **Quick list to write down:** (1) Climate, (2) Standard of living, (3) Population, (4) Tariff structure, (5) Water quality, (6) System pressure, (7) Livestock pattern, (8) Habits & customs, (9) Distribution losses, (10) Industrial / commercial activity.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Population after 30 years (Arithmetic Method)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 30 \text{ years} = 3 \text{ decades} \\
P_{30} &= 15000 + 30 \times 300 = 15000 + 9000 = 24000 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Average Daily Demand**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ADD &= 24000 \times 135 = 3240000 \text{ L/day} = 3240 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Daily Demand (MDD)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MDD &= 1.8 \times 3240 = 5832 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Hourly Demand (MHD)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MHD &= 2.7 \times 3240 = 8748 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \\
&= \dfrac{8748}{24} = 364.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} = 101.25 \text{ L/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Storage Reservoir Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Capacity} &= 0.40 \times MDD \\
&= 0.40 \times 5832 = 2332.8 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Tabular Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| Future Population (30 yr) | 24,000 |
| ADD | 3,240 m³/day |
| MDD | 5,832 m³/day |
| MHD | 8,748 m³/day (101.25 L/s) |
| **Reservoir Capacity (40 % MDD)** | **2,332.8 m³** |

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Standard per capita demand for rural India = 55 / 70 / 100 lpcd** (CPHEEO 2021). For KTU numericals, **70 lpcd** is the default unless specified.
- [x] **Three population forecasting methods:** Arithmetic, Geometric, Incremental Increase — incremental is the most commonly preferred KTU answer.
- [x] **Peak factors:** MDD = 1.8 × ADD; MHD = 2.7 × ADD; PF = MHD/ADD = 2.7.
- [x] **Fire demand formulae:** Kuchling ($3182\sqrt{P}$), Buston ($5663\sqrt{P}$) — always state which one you are using. For rural Kerala, **Kuchling's formula** is standard.
- [x] **Livestock demand** — large animals 30–50 L/head/day; small 5–10 L/head/day; poultry 1–2 L/bird/day. Often 2 marks in 14-mark questions.
- [x] **System losses** = 10–15 % of total demand — forgetting them loses 1 mark.
- [x] **Design period:** 15 years (head works / pumps), 30 years (pipelines, ESR).
- [x] **ESR sizing:** 40–50 % of MDD for rural schemes (use 50 % in KTU problems unless told otherwise).
- [x] **Unit discipline:** Always quote final answer in **m³/day** and **L/s** for design discharge.
- [x] **Policy link:** Methodology directly aligned with **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** funding norms — examiners appreciate a one-line policy reference.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Don't confuse $n$ (number of decades) with $n$ (number of years) in population formulas. Convert years to decades before substituting.
- [x] **Examiner's favourite question pattern:** "Estimate population using two methods and design the scheme" — practise at least 3 such problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Supply Systems: Gravity-Fed, Pumped, and Rainwater Harvesting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Water Supply Systems: Gravity-Fed, Pumped, and Rainwater Harvesting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 1 — Water Supply Systems: Gravity-Fed, Pumped, and Rainwater Harvesting

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Gravity-Fed Water Supply System (GFWSS)**
> A rural or peri-urban water distribution network in which water flows from a **source** (intake/sump) to the consumer points **entirely under the action of gravitational potential energy**, without the use of any mechanical pumping device in the main transmission line. The system relies on a positive elevation difference (static head) between the source and the highest consumer node, typically ranging from **5 m to 50 m** in Indian rural contexts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pumped Water Supply System (PWSS)**
> A distribution network in which one or more **centrifugal/submersible/reciprocating pumps** lift water from a lower-elevation source to an elevated storage reservoir (ESR) or directly to a pressure-maintained distribution network. The system is adopted when the source elevation is **inadequate to generate required residual pressure** (typically ≥ 7 m at the consumer tap) under gravity alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rainwater Harvesting (RWH) System**
> A decentralized, on-plot or community-level collection infrastructure that intercepts precipitation falling on a defined **catchment surface** (rooftop, paved courtyard, or land surface), conveys it through **gutters and down-take pipes**, and routes it through a **first-flush diverter and filtration unit** to a **storage cistern** for subsequent beneficial use (drinking, washing, irrigation, or groundwater recharge).

---

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogies (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy 1 — Gravity-Fed System = "A Natural Hill Slide"**
> Imagine pouring water at the top of a hill. If you place buckets downhill in a line, water reaches each bucket automatically. A gravity-fed system works exactly like this — water "slides" from a higher source (overhead tank, spring, or elevated reservoir) down through pipes to consumers, with the elevation difference providing the energy. No electricity or pump is needed in the main line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy 2 — Pumped System = "A Heart Pumping Blood"**
> Just as the human heart pumps blood from the lower body up to the head against gravity, a centrifugal pump lifts water from a borewell/river (low level) up to an overhead tank (high level). The pump acts as the artificial "heart" that adds **hydraulic energy (head)** to the water to overcome elevation and friction losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy 3 — Rainwater Harvesting = "A Bucket Catching Rain"**
> Place a clean bucket on a rooftop during rain — it fills with water. A rainwater harvesting system is essentially a large, engineered version of that bucket, but with gutters (to channel), filters (to clean), and a storage tank (to save) added. The catchment area is the "wide funnel" that maximizes collection.

---

### 1.1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Design Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **standard metrics** are universally applied in KTU-endorsed rural water supply design (per CPHEEO Manual on Water Supply, 1999, and IS 1172:1993):
> - **Per capita water demand (rural):** **40 lpcd (litres per capita per day)** — minimum for domestic needs.
> - **Minimum residual head at consumer tap:** **7 m (≈ 0.7 bar)**.
> - **Average rainfall intensity in Kerala:** **~3000 mm/year** (with spatial variation 2000–3500 mm).
> - **Runoff coefficient (C) for rooftops:** **0.8 – 0.9** (smooth concrete/tile surface).
> - **Standard design life of RWH system:** **30 years** (structural), **15 years** (PVC components).
> - **Recommended pump efficiency range:** **60 % – 75 %** for centrifugal pumps below 25 HP.
> - **Pipe friction factor (Hazen-Williams C-value):** **C = 130 – 140** for PVC/uPVC, **C = 120** for CI/DI, **C = 100** for mild steel with mild tuberculation.

---

### 1.1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> **CO1 (Module 1):** *Identify and describe the components, hydraulics, and design principles of rural water supply systems including gravity-fed, pumped, and rainwater harvesting schemes.*
> This topic directly addresses **CO1** at Bloom's Taxonomy levels: **L1 (Remember)**, **L2 (Understand)**, and **L3 (Apply)**.

---

### 1.1.5 Schematic Comparison of the Three Systems

| Attribute | Gravity-Fed | Pumped | Rainwater Harvesting |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Gravitational PE | Electrical / Diesel | Precipitation |
| **Main Component** | Elevated Source/ESR | Pump + ESR | Catchment + Storage |
| **Typical Pressure at Tap** | 7–40 m head | 7–30 m head | Gravity-flow from tank |
| **O&M Cost** | Very Low (≈ 5% of capital/yr) | Moderate–High | Low |
| **Reliability (Kerala)** | High in hilly terrain | High in plains | Moderate (seasonal) |
| **Suitability** | Hill slopes, springs | Plains, deep borewells | All zones (decentralized) |
| **Capital Cost (per capita)** | ₹ 800 – 1500 | ₹ 1500 – 3000 | ₹ 500 – 1200 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gravity-Fed Water Supply System (GFWSS)

### 2.1.1 Operational Concept

A gravity-fed system functions on a simple energy-balance principle: the **available head (H_available)** between the source and the consumer must exceed the **sum of all head losses** in the system, leaving a **residual head (H_residual)** for the consumer.

The governing energy equation is:

$$H_{available} \geq \sum h_{f} + \sum h_{m} + H_{residual}$$

where $h_f$ = frictional head loss, $h_m$ = minor (local) head loss.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
- **Step 1:** Source identified (spring, river intake at higher elevation, or upstream impoundment).
- **Step 2:** Water conveyed through **gravity main** (typically laid at a continuous downward gradient of **1 in 200 to 1 in 500**).
- **Step 3:** **Break Pressure Tank (BPT)** constructed wherever pipe pressure exceeds **1.5 kg/cm² (15 m head)** to prevent pipe burst.
- **Step 4:** Distribution network laid out in **branching, dendritic, or looped pattern** with consumer standposts.
- **Step 5:** System monitored via **HGL (Hydraulic Grade Line)** analysis.

### 2.1.2 Components of a GFWSS
- **Intake works** (spring chamber / river intake)
- **Collection sump**
- **Gravity main (transmission pipe)**
- **Break pressure tank (BPT)**
- **Storage reservoir (where required)**
- **Distribution network** (branch / loop / radial)
- **Consumer standposts / house service connections**

### 2.1.3 Engineering Utility
GFWSS is the **most energy-efficient and sustainable** system for Kerala's Western Ghats terrain. It is the **first design choice** in KTU-affiliated rural water supply projects (e.g., Jal Jeevan Mission schemes in Idukki, Wayanad, Pathanamthitta districts).

---

## 2.2 Pumped Water Supply System (PWSS)

### 2.2.1 Operational Concept

A pumped system uses **mechanical energy input** to raise water from a low-elevation source to a higher-elevation storage or directly to the distribution network. The pump must deliver a flow rate $Q$ against a **Total Dynamic Head (TDH)** computed as:

$$TDH = H_{static} + h_{f} + h_{m} + H_{residual} - H_{suction, available}$$

where $H_{static}$ is the vertical lift, and $H_{suction, available}$ is the net positive suction head available (NPSHA).

**Operational Logic:**
- **Step 1:** Site assessment — borewell yield (litres/hour), static water level (SWL), drawdown.
- **Step 2:** Pump selection based on **Q vs. TDH curve** intersection with **system resistance curve**.
- **Step 3:** Delivery main laid from pump to **Elevated Storage Reservoir (ESR)**.
- **Step 4:** ESR sized for **equalization storage** (typically 1/3 of daily demand) and **emergency storage** (1.5× average hourly demand).
- **Step 5:** Distribution from ESR under gravity.

### 2.2.2 Types of Pumps Used in Rural Kerala
- **Submersible centrifugal pump** (most common for borewells > 30 m deep)
- **Open-well submersible pump** (for shallow dug wells, 6–15 m)
- **Mono-block centrifugal pump** (surface-mounted, for river/sump intakes)
- **Hand pump (Mark II / Mark III / India Mark II)** — for emergency / non-electrified villages (per IS 15500).

### 2.2.3 Pump Power and Energy Calculation

The **water power (hydraulic power)** delivered by the pump:

$$P_{water} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot TDH}{1000} \;\; \text{(kW)}$$

The **shaft power (brake power)** required at the pump input:

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{pump}}$$

The **electrical input power**:

$$P_{electrical} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}}$$

**Total combined efficiency:**

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor}$$

Typical values: $\eta_{pump} = 0.65$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.85$, hence $\eta_{overall} \approx 0.55$.

### 2.2.4 Engineering Utility
PWSS is mandatory where source elevation is **inadequate** (e.g., mid-Kerala plains, Kuttanad, coastal sandy aquifers) and where borewell depth exceeds **30 m**. Pumped systems are also essential for **multi-village bulk-water schemes** under the Jal Jeevan Mission.

---

## 2.3 Rainwater Harvesting (RWH) System

### 2.3.1 Operational Concept

RWH relies on **interception of precipitation** on a catchment surface. The **harvestable rainwater potential** from a catchment of area $A$ receiving rainfall depth $I$ with runoff coefficient $C$ is given by:

$$Q_{harvested} = C \times I \times A$$

This equation is the **foundation of all RWH design** in KTU Module 1.

**Operational Logic:**
- **Step 1:** Identify catchment surface (rooftop, paved courtyard).
- **Step 2:** Compute **annual / monthly rainfall yield** using local IMD data.
- **Step 3:** Size **gutters** (rectangular or semicircular, slope 1:150 to 1:300).
- **Step 4:** Install **first-flush diverter** (10–30 L capacity) to discard initial dirty runoff.
- **Step 5:** Convey water through **filter unit** (charcoal-sand-gravel or commercial cartridge).
- **Step 6:** Store in **cistern** (PVC/HDPE/ferrocement/concrete) sized to bridge dry periods.
- **Step 7:** Provide **overflow** to recharge pit / soak pit (for groundwater recharge variant).

### 2.3.2 Components of an RWH System
- **Catchment surface** (roof, paved area)
- **Gutters & down-pipes** (PVC/uPVC, 75–110 mm dia)
- **First-flush diverter**
- **Filter unit** (slow sand filter / charcoal filter / commercial cartridge)
- **Storage tank / cistern**
- **Recharge structure** (optional — recharge pit, recharge trench, recharge well)
- **Delivery / draw-off system** (tap, float valve, suction pipe)

### 2.3.3 Engineering Utility
RWH is the **only viable option for water-scarce, hilly, or island-locked habitations** in Kerala (e.g., tribal hamlets in Attappadi, isolated coastal villages in Alappuzha, and parts of Wayanad under the MGNREGS convergence program).

---

## 2.4 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Units | Application / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Darcy–Weisbach head loss | $h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | metres (m) | $L$ = pipe length, $D$ = dia, $V$ = velocity, $f$ = friction factor; valid for all regimes |
| 2 | Hazen–Williams velocity | $V = 0.849 \cdot C \cdot R_h^{0.63} \cdot S^{0.54}$ | m/s | $C$ = HW coefficient, $R_h$ = hydraulic radius, $S$ = slope; valid for $V < 3$ m/s |
| 3 | Hazen–Williams head loss | $h_f = \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$ | m | $Q$ in m³/s, $D$ in m; widely used in water supply design |
| 4 | Continuity equation | $Q = A \cdot V$ | m³/s | Mass conservation in pipe flow |
| 5 | Total Dynamic Head | $TDH = H_s + h_f + h_m + H_{residual} - H_{s,avail}$ | m | Pump selection; $H_s$ = static lift |
| 6 | Pump water power | $P_w = \rho g Q \cdot TDH / 1000$ | kW | Output hydraulic power |
| 7 | Pump shaft power | $P_{sh} = P_w / \eta_{pump}$ | kW | Input to pump shaft |
| 8 | Pump electrical input | $P_e = P_{sh} / \eta_{motor}$ | kW | Electricity meter reading |
| 9 | Rainwater potential | $Q_R = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | litres or m³ | $C$ = 0.8–0.9 (roof), $I$ = rainfall, $A$ = area |
| 10 | RWH storage sizing | $V_{tank} = D_d \times N_p \times C_{factor}$ | litres | $D_d$ = daily demand, $N_p$ = no. of dry days, $C_{factor}$ = 0.8 |
| 11 | Manning's equation (open channel/gutter) | $V = \frac{1}{n} R_h^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | m/s | $n$ = Manning's roughness (PVC ≈ 0.009) |
| 12 | Population forecast (arithmetic) | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | persons | $\bar{x}$ = average decadal increase |
| 13 | Population forecast (geometric) | $P_n = P_0 (1 + r/100)^n$ | persons | $r$ = growth rate (%); used when growth is exponential |
| 14 | Fire demand (Kuchling's formula) | $Q_f = 1136.5 \left(\frac{P}{1000}\right)^{0.5}$ | L/min | For populations < 50,000 |
| 15 | Per capita demand | $D_{pc} = \frac{Q_{annual}}{365 \times P}$ | lpcd | Used for design period estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** The **Hazen-Williams** and **RWH potential** equations are the **most frequently tested** formulas in this module. Memorize the **coefficient 10.67** and the **exponent 1.852** with care.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hazen-Williams Head Loss Equation

The Hazen-Williams equation is an **empirically derived formula** (not from first principles), but its structure is built on the **continuity and Darcy-Weisbach** foundation. The derivation pathway is as follows:

**Step 1 — Start with Darcy-Weisbach:**

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the friction factor $f$ as a function of Reynolds number and relative roughness for turbulent flow (smooth pipes):**

Empirically, it is found that for water at ordinary temperatures in pipes of diameter 50–3500 mm, $f$ can be expressed as:

$$f = \frac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\left(\frac{k_s}{3.7D} + \frac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}$$

**Step 3 — Through extensive dimensional analysis and curve fitting (work by Hazen & Williams, 1905), the friction slope is reduced to:**

$$S = \frac{h_f}{L} = \frac{10.67 \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$$

where:
- $S$ = hydraulic gradient (head loss per unit length, m/m)
- $Q$ = discharge (m³/s)
- $D$ = pipe internal diameter (m)
- $C$ = Hazen-Williams coefficient (dimensionless)

**Step 4 — Final Head Loss Form (multiplied by length $L$):**

$$h_f = \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}} \;\; \text{(metres)}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Conversion Check:** For $Q$ in litres per second (L/s) and $D$ in mm, the equation becomes:

$$h_f = \frac{1.13 \times 10^9 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Since $1 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ L}$ and $1 \text{ m} = 1000 \text{ mm}$, the conversion factors are cubed/raised to the appropriate power to maintain dimensional consistency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation of Hazen-Williams:** It is valid **only for water** at temperatures between 5°C and 25°C. It is **not valid for oils, sludges, or non-Newtonian fluids**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of RWH Harvestable Potential

**Step 1 — Define the catchment area $A$** (m²) of the receiving surface (e.g., rooftop).

**Step 2 — Define the rainfall depth $I$** (mm) over the design period (typically annual or monthly).

**Step 3 — Convert rainfall depth (mm) to equivalent water volume per unit area:**

Since $1 \text{ mm} = 1 \text{ litre/m}^2$, the total water received on area $A$ is:

$$V_{received} = I \, (\text{mm}) \times A \, (\text{m}^2) = I \cdot A \;\; \text{(litres)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the runoff coefficient $C$** to account for losses (evaporation, splash, leakage, first-flush diversion):

$$V_{harvested} = C \cdot I \cdot A \;\; \text{(litres)}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to cubic metres (m³) for storage sizing:**

$$V_{harvested} = \frac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{1000} \;\; \text{(m}^3\text{)}$$

**Numerical Worked Example:**

> Given: Rooftop area = **150 m²**, Annual rainfall = **3000 mm**, Runoff coefficient $C$ = **0.85**.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{harvested} &= C \times I \times A \div 1000 \\
&= 0.85 \times 3000 \times 150 \div 1000 \\
&= 0.85 \times 450 \\
&= 382.5 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Converting to litres per day (per year basis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{daily} &= \frac{382.5 \times 1000}{365} = 1047.9 \;\; \text{L/day} \\
&\approx 1048 \;\; \text{litres/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This satisfies the domestic demand of $\approx 26$ persons** at 40 lpcd.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Pump Power Requirement

**Step 1 — Define Total Dynamic Head (TDH):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TDH &= H_{static} + h_{f,delivery} + h_{f,suction} + h_{m} + H_{residual} \\
\text{or simply:} \quad TDH &= H_{static} + \sum h_L + H_{residual}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\sum h_L$ is the sum of all line and minor losses.

**Step 2 — Compute Water Power:**

$$P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot TDH$$

with $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², yielding $P_{water}$ in Watts.

**Step 3 — Convert to kW:**

$$P_{water} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times Q \times TDH}{1000} = 9.81 \cdot Q \cdot TDH \;\; \text{(kW)}$$

where $Q$ is in m³/s.

**Step 4 — Account for Pump Efficiency $\eta_{pump}$:**

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{9.81 \cdot Q \cdot TDH}{\eta_{pump}} \;\; \text{(kW)}$$

**Step 5 — Account for Motor Efficiency $\eta_{motor}$:**

$$P_{electrical} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{9.81 \cdot Q \cdot TDH}{\eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor}} \;\; \text{(kW)}$$

**Numerical Worked Example:**

> Given: $Q = 0.01$ m³/s (10 L/s), $TDH = 30$ m, $\eta_{pump} = 0.65$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.85$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{water} &= 9.81 \times 0.01 \times 30 = 2.943 \;\; \text{kW} \\
P_{shaft} &= \frac{2.943}{0.65} = 4.528 \;\; \text{kW} \\
P_{electrical} &= \frac{4.528}{0.85} = 5.327 \;\; \text{kW} \approx 5.33 \;\; \text{kW} \\
&\approx 7.14 \;\; \text{HP (using 1 HP = 0.746 kW)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Energy Cost Calculation (annual):**

Assuming pump operates 8 hours/day, 365 days/year, electricity tariff ₹ 7/unit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Units/year} &= 5.33 \times 8 \times 365 = 15{,}564 \;\; \text{kWh} \\
\text{Annual cost} &= 15{,}564 \times 7 = \text{₹ } 1{,}08{,}948
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of System Type

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following decision matrix is **directly aligned with KTU Module 1 CO1** and is a **frequently tested** concept (typically a 7-mark question).

| Site Condition | Recommended System | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Spring source at +80 m elevation, gentle downhill village | **Gravity-Fed** | Static head sufficient; zero energy cost; minimal O&M |
| Borewell at -40 m depth, flat terrain | **Pumped + ESR** | Gravity head insufficient; pump lifts water; ESR provides equalization |
| Isolated tribal hamlet, no groundwater, no surface source | **Rainwater Harvesting** | Decentralized; no external source; relies on precipitation |
| Coastal sandy aquifer with high TDS | **Pumped + RWH + Treatment** | Pump required + RWH reduces groundwater dependence |
| Hilly estate with multiple springs | **Gravity-Fed + RWH** | Multiple intakes at high elevation; RWH supplements summer deficit |
| Plains village with seasonal river source | **Pumped (Monsoon storage)** | Pump lifts from river; large sump stores monsoon water for year-round use |

---

## 3.5 Combined Worked Design Problem (Sample)

> **Problem:** A rural habitation of **500 persons** is to be supplied with water. Average annual rainfall is **2800 mm**. Rooftop area available per household is **100 m²** and there are **100 households**. Design the rainwater harvesting storage tank size using the dry-period approach.
> Assumptions: $C = 0.85$, Average dry period = **90 days**, Per capita demand = **40 lpcd**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Daily Demand of Habitation:**

$$D_{daily} = 500 \times 40 = 20{,}000 \;\; \text{L/day} = 20 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — Total Catchment Area:**

$$A = 100 \times 100 = 10{,}000 \;\; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Annual Harvestable Water:**

$$Q_{annual} = \frac{0.85 \times 2800 \times 10{,}000}{1000} = 23{,}800 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{year}$$

**Step 4 — Daily Average Harvestable:**

$$Q_{daily,avg} = \frac{23{,}800}{365} = 65.2 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 5 — Tank Size for 90-day Dry Period:**

$$V_{tank} = D_{daily} \times N_{dry} \times C_{factor} = 20 \times 90 \times 0.8 = 1440 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Check against annual availability:**

Annual demand $= 20 \times 365 = 7300$ m³ < $23{,}800$ m³ ✓ (Supply exceeds demand)

> **Conclusion:** A storage tank of **1440 m³** capacity (e.g., $12 \text{ m} \times 12 \text{ m} \times 10 \text{ m}$) will bridge the 90-day dry period. The RWH system is **viable and adequate** for this 500-person habitation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Gravity-Fed Water Supply System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Spring Source / River Intake] --> B1[Collection Sump with Screen]
    B1 --> C1[Gravity Main Transmission Pipe]
    C1 --> D1[Break Pressure Tank BPT]
    D1 --> E1[Distribution Main M1]
    D1 --> F1[Distribution Main M2]
    E1 --> G1[Standpost 1]
    E1 --> G2[Standpost 2]
    F1 --> G3[Standpost 3]
    F1 --> G4[House Service Connection]
    C1 -.Hydraulic Grade Line.-> H1[Residual Head 7m at Consumer]
```

```mermaid
    A2[Borewell / River / Sump] --> B2[Submersible / Centrifugal Pump]
    B2 --> C2[Delivery Pipe]

    D2 --> F2[Distribution Network M2]
    E2 --> G2a[Standpost Cluster 1]
    E2 --> G2b[Standpost Cluster 2]

    H2 -.Control.-> B2
```

```mermaid
    E3 --> F3[Storage Cistern]
```

```mermaid
    ROOT[Rural Water Supply Selection] --> S1[Source Assessment]
```

```mermaid
    DEL --> DIST[Distribution to Consumer]

    PUMP -.Loss 35 percent.-> LOSSP[Pump Inefficiency]
```
