# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 🎯 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Style]**

### **Q1. Distinguish between a gravity-fed water supply system and a pumped water supply system. Mention one typical location in Kerala where each is most suitable.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Gravity-Fed System | Pumped System |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Gravitational Potential Energy | Electrical / Diesel Mechanical Energy |
| Operating Cost | Negligible | High (power consumption) |
| Reliability in Kerala | High in hilly/Western Ghats regions | High in plains/deep aquifer regions |
| Typical Components | Spring intake + Gravity main + BPT | Borewell + Pump + ESR |
| Suitable Kerala Location | Idukki, Wayanad, Pathanamthitta (hilly) | Kottayam plains, Kuttanad, Alappuzha coast (flat) |

*(3 marks — 1 mark for each well-defined point)*

---

### **Q2. Define the term "runoff coefficient" in the context of rainwater harvesting. State its typical range for a tiled rooftop.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **runoff coefficient (C)** is defined as the **ratio of the volume of water that is actually harvested (runoff) to the volume of water that falls on the catchment surface (rainfall)**, expressed as a dimensionless fraction between 0 and 1.

**Mathematical form:**

$$C = \frac{V_{harvested}}{V_{received}}$$

**Typical range for a tiled/concrete rooftop:**

$$C = 0.8 \text{ to } 0.9$$

The value depends on surface smoothness, slope, and the first-flush diversion volume. Smooth glazed tiles have $C$ close to **0.9**, while rough concrete has $C$ around **0.8**.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks for definition + 1 mark for typical value + 0.5 mark for the explanation)*

---

## 5.2 🎯 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / KTU Model Paper Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** *Explain the components and functioning of a gravity-fed water supply system with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of a Break Pressure Tank (BPT) in such a system.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition and Principle (2 marks):**

A gravity-fed water supply system conveys water from a source at higher elevation to consumers at lower elevation **entirely under gravitational head**, without mechanical pumping. The available static head must exceed the total head losses by at least **7 m** to ensure adequate residual pressure at the consumer tap.

**2. Components (3 marks):**

- **Intake structure** — Spring chamber or river intake with coarse screen and grating.
- **Collection sump** — Receives water from intake, sized for 1–2 hours of detention.
- **Gravity main (transmission pipe)** — Typically uPVC, DI, or HDPE, laid at continuous downward slope of **1:200 to 1:500**.
- **Break Pressure Tank (BPT)** — Constructed along the gravity main to dissipate excess pressure.
- **Distribution network** — Branched, dendritic, or looped pattern, terminating at standposts or HSC.
- **Valves and chambers** — Sluice valves, air valves, scour valves at strategic locations.

**3. Role of Break Pressure Tank (BPT) (2 marks):**

When the cumulative elevation drop between source and any intermediate point exceeds the **safe pressure rating of the pipe (typically 1.5 kg/cm² ≈ 15 m head)**, the pipe is at risk of bursting. A **BPT is a small open/closed tank** constructed at that point to:
- **Drop the hydraulic grade line to ground level** (effectively resetting the pressure to atmospheric).
- **Prevent pipe rupture** due to excessive static pressure.
- **Allow re-aeration** of water (releasing trapped air).
- **Provide minor storage** for peak demand balancing.

**Sketch (to be drawn in exam):** A schematic showing source → sump → gravity main → BPT → distribution main → standposts.

---

#### **(b)** *A village of 4000 population is to be served by a gravity-fed system. The average per capita demand is 40 lpcd. The source is a spring at elevation +120 m. The village is at elevation +40 m. The gravity main is 2000 m long with diameter 150 mm. Hazen-Williams coefficient C = 130. Compute (i) the total daily demand, (ii) the average flow rate Q, and (iii) the head loss in the gravity main, and verify whether the system can deliver water to the village.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Population $P = 4000$
- Per capita demand = 40 lpcd
- Source elevation = +120 m
- Village elevation = +40 m
- Pipe length $L = 2000$ m
- Pipe diameter $D = 150$ mm = 0.15 m
- Hazen-Williams $C = 130$

**Step 1 — Total Daily Demand:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{daily} &= P \times D_{pc} \\
&= 4000 \times 40 \\
&= 1{,}60{,}000 \;\; \text{L/day} = 160 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Average Flow Rate (assuming 12-hour pumping/supply period):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{avg} &= \frac{160}{12 \times 3600} = \frac{160}{43{,}200} \\
&= 3.704 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 3.7 \;\; \text{L/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(If 24-hour supply is assumed, $Q_{avg} = 1.85$ L/s. Students should state assumption.)*

**Step 3 — Head Loss in Gravity Main (Hazen-Williams):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \frac{10.67 \times L \times Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \times D^{4.87}} \\
&= \frac{10.67 \times 2000 \times (3.704 \times 10^{-3})^{1.852}}{130^{1.852} \times (0.15)^{4.87}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computing numerator:** $(3.704 \times 10^{-3})^{1.852} \approx 5.41 \times 10^{-5}$

$$
10.67 \times 2000 \times 5.41 \times 10^{-5} = 1.155
$$

**Computing denominator:** $130^{1.852} \approx 9150$; $(0.15)^{4.87} \approx 4.92 \times 10^{-4}$

$$
9150 \times 4.92 \times 10^{-4} \approx 4.502
$$

$$
h_f = \frac{1.155}{4.502} = 0.257 \;\; \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Adequacy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{available} &= 120 - 40 = 80 \;\; \text{m} \\
H_{residual} &= H_{available} - h_f = 80 - 0.257 \approx 79.74 \;\; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $H_{residual} = 79.74$ m ≫ 7 m (minimum required), the system **CAN deliver water to the village** with abundant residual head.

> **Conclusion:** The gravity-fed system is **highly feasible** for this village. In fact, a BPT may be required at an intermediate point (e.g., at +25 m elevation) since pressure at the village end exceeds the safe pipe limit of 15 m head.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

#### **(a)** *With the help of a neat sketch, describe the components and working of a Rainwater Harvesting (RWH) system suitable for a rural household in Kerala. State the role of the first-flush diverter.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**

Rainwater harvesting is the **collection, conveyance, filtration, and storage of precipitation** falling on a defined catchment surface (typically a rooftop) for beneficial use. It is the most suitable decentralized water supply option for isolated rural households, tribal hamlets, and water-scarce regions in Kerala.

**2. Components with Functions (4 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Catchment (Rooftop)** | Receives rainfall; tiled/concrete preferred for hygiene |
| **Gutters** | Collect water from roof edge; PVC/uPVC, 100–150 mm wide, slope 1:150 to 1:300 |
| **Down-take pipe** | Carries water vertically from gutter to ground level; 75–110 mm dia |
| **First-flush diverter** | Diverts initial dirty runoff (dust, bird droppings, leaves) to waste |
| **Filter unit** | Removes fine particles; sand-gravel-charcoal or commercial cartridge |
| **Storage cistern** | Stores filtered water; HDPE / ferrocement / RCC; sized for dry period |
| **Overflow pipe** | Disposes excess water to recharge pit or drain |
| **Draw-off tap** | Draws water for domestic use with float valve |

**3. Working Principle (1 mark):**

When rain falls on the rooftop, water slides down the slope into the gutters, flows through the down-take pipe, the first-flush diverter discards the first 10–30 L of contaminated water, the filter cleans the rest, and the clean water is stored in the cistern. A float valve at the top of the cistern prevents overflow.

**4. Role of First-Flush Diverter (1 mark):**

The **first-flush diverter** is a **small chamber (10–30 L capacity) with a manual or automatic ball-valve mechanism** that:
- **Diverts the first shower of rain** (containing dust, pollen, leaves, bird droppings, atmospheric pollutants accumulated on the roof since the last rain) to a waste drain.
- **Improves water quality** significantly, especially for non-potable uses; for potable use, additional disinfection (chlorination / UV / boiling) is essential.
- **Reduces filter load** by removing the dirtiest fraction of runoff.

**Sketch:** Block diagram showing rooftop → gutter → down-pipe → first-flush diverter → filter → cistern → tap.

---

#### **(b)** *A rural school in Wayanad has a rooftop area of 500 m². The average annual rainfall is 2500 mm. The runoff coefficient is 0.85. The school has 200 students and 10 staff. (i) Compute the annual harvestable rainwater. (ii) Compute the per capita annual availability. (iii) Comment on whether the system can meet the school's annual demand of 50 lpcd.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $A = 500$ m²
- $I = 2500$ mm/year
- $C = 0.85$
- Students = 200, Staff = 10 → Total population $P = 210$

**Step 1 — Annual Harvestable Rainwater:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{annual} &= \frac{C \times I \times A}{1000} \\
&= \frac{0.85 \times 2500 \times 500}{1000} \\
&= \frac{1{,}062{,}500}{1000} = 1062.5 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{year} \\
&= 10{,}62{,}500 \;\; \text{L/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Per Capita Annual Availability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Per capita} &= \frac{10{,}62{,}500}{210} \\
&= 5059.5 \;\; \text{L/person/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to lpcd:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{lpcd available} &= \frac{5059.5}{365} = 13.86 \;\; \text{lpcd}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Required Demand:**

$$
\text{Demand} = 50 \;\; \text{lpcd}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison and Conclusion:**

| Parameter | Available | Required | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Per capita supply | 13.86 lpcd | 50 lpcd | **Insufficient** ✗ |
| Annual supply | 1062.5 m³ | 3832.5 m³ | **Insufficient** ✗ |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Demand} &= 210 \times 50 \times 365 = 38{,}32{,}500 \;\; \text{L} = 3832.5 \;\; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The RWH system with the given rooftop area is **insufficient** to meet the school's demand at 50 lpcd. To meet the demand, the required catchment area is:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> A_{required} &= \frac{3832.5 \times 1000}{0.85 \times 2500} = 1803.5 \;\; \text{m}^2
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> This is about **3.6 times** the available rooftop. Therefore, additional sources (open well recharge, community rainwater collection from a larger catchment, or groundwater backup) are required, OR the per capita demand must be reduced to **≤ 13 lpcd**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Hazen-Williams:** The most common error is using $Q$ in L/s while $D$ is in m (or vice versa). The KTU answer key requires **explicit statement of units** before substitution. Students who omit this lose 0.5–1 mark per sub-part.
>
> 2. **Forgetting $H_{residual} = 7$ m check:** Many students compute $H_{available} - h_f$ and stop, without verifying the residual head is ≥ 7 m. This loses 1 mark in 7-mark problems.
>
> 3. **RWH Numericals — Missing Runoff Coefficient:** Several students write $Q = I \times A$ without applying $C$, leading to an overestimate. KTU examiners specifically test this conceptual understanding.
>
> 4. **Pump Power — Confusing $\eta_{pump}$ vs $\eta_{motor}$:** KTU key explicitly asks for the formula $P_e = P_w / (\eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor})$. Students who use $\eta_{overall}$ without defining it lose marks.
>
> 5. **Not Drawing the Sketch:** A 7-mark question that asks for "neat sketch" without a diagram receives **at least 1.5 marks deducted** even if the description is perfect.
>
> 6. **Decimal and Significant Figure Errors:** Final answers with 4+ decimal places (e.g., 3.7041 m³/s) or fewer than 3 (e.g., 4 m³/s) are penalized. Always report answers to **3 significant figures** with units.
>
> 7. **Mixing up Average Daily Demand with Peak Hourly Demand:** Use 1.5 × average for peak factor in gravity-fed design; use 2.7 × average for fire + domestic combined demand (per CPHEEO).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Water Supply Systems: Rapid Revision Sheet**
>
> ✅ **Gravity-Fed System**
> - Operates on gravitational potential energy (zero operating cost).
> - Requires positive elevation difference (source above consumer).
> - **BPT** mandatory when static head > 15 m.
> - Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL) analysis essential.
> - Most suitable for **Kerala's Western Ghats** (Idukki, Wayanad, Pathanamthitta).
>
> ✅ **Pumped System**
> - **TDH = Static Head + Friction Loss + Minor Loss + Residual Head − NPSHA.**
> - Pump water power: $P_w = \rho g Q \cdot TDH$ (Watts).
> - Shaft power = $P_w / \eta_{pump}$; Electrical power = $P_{sh} / \eta_{motor}$.
> - Submersible pumps preferred for deep borewells; centrifugal for surface sources.
> - **ESR** sized for 1/3 daily demand equalization + emergency reserve.
>
> ✅ **Rainwater Harvesting**
> - **Governing equation:** $Q_R = C \cdot I \cdot A$ — the most tested formula.
> - Runoff coefficient $C = 0.8$–$0.9$ for rooftops.
> - Components: Catchment → Gutter → First-Flush → Filter → Cistern → Tap.
> - Storage tank sizing: $V = D_{daily} \times N_{dry\,days} \times 0.8$.
> - **Recharge variant** requires overflow to recharge pit/well — for groundwater augmentation.
>
> ✅ **Cross-Cutting Concepts**
> - **Hazen-Williams** is the workhorse friction-loss formula in KTU water-supply design.
> - **Per capita demand (rural)** = 40 lpcd (minimum, per IS 1172:1993).
> - **Residual head at consumer tap** ≥ 7 m (mandatory).
> - Always state **assumptions** in numerical answers (supply hours, peak factor, pipe material).
> - **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** — the KTU syllabus directly aligns with India's flagship rural water program; refer to it in design examples for context.
>
> ✅ **Frequently Tested Equations (Memorize)**
> 1. $h_f = \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$
> 2. $Q_R = C \cdot I \cdot A$
> 3. $P_e = \frac{9.81 \cdot Q \cdot TDH}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$
> 4. $V_{tank} = D_{daily} \times N_{dry} \times 0.8$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Implementation of Rural Water Supply Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design and Implementation of Rural Water Supply Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Rural Water Supply System (RWSS)** is an engineered, integrated network of civil, mechanical, and hydraulic infrastructure designed to deliver a safe, adequate, and sustainable quantity of potable water to dispersed, low-density human settlements (villages / habitations with populations typically below 5,000 as per Census of India norms) over a defined **design horizon (30 years, as per CPHEEO 1999 manual)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** RWSS design is the systematic application of hydraulic engineering, environmental science, and public-health principles to plan, size, and operate a multi-component water delivery system — comprising **source**, **intake**, **pumping machinery (if any)**, **treatment**, **service storage reservoir (SSR/Overhead Tank)**, and **distribution network** — while satisfying the per-capita demand (40–55 lpcd under Jal Jeevan Mission, JJM) and ensuring financial, social, and technical sustainability.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Village Tap" Concept

Imagine a small village as a **large family of 200 members living in 40 scattered houses**. Unlike a city apartment where one huge pipe feeds thousands simultaneously, a village system is more like a **shared kitchen tap**: a single protected well or spring supplies a **ground-level sump**, water is pumped (or flows by gravity) to an **overhead tank** placed on the highest house/stand, and from there it is distributed to standposts and household connections through a branched pipeline — much like the branches of a tree where the trunk is the main pipe and twigs are the distribution laterals.

The **design** is essentially answering four engineering questions:
1. *How many people will we serve?* (Population forecasting)
2. *How much water do they need?* (Demand estimation)
3. *Where will the water come from?* (Source selection & intake)
4. *How will it reach every house reliably?* (Treatment → Storage → Distribution)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Philosophy (Jal Jeevan Mission, 2019):** Every rural household must receive **55 litres per capita per day (lpcd)** of potable water through a Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC) by 2030, with assured daily delivery of at least **55 lpcd** measured at the consumer end.

### 1.3 Important Physical Constants & Design Standards

- **Per Capita Demand (Rural, JJM):** 55 lpcd (with 1.0 to 1.2 peaking factor)
- **Design Period:** 30 years for civil works; 15 years for mechanical/electrical works
- **Minimum Residual Pressure at Consumer End:** 7 m (1 bar) — *CPHEEO norm*
- **Peak Factor (PF):** 2.5 to 3.0 (rural intermittent supply)
- **System Efficiency:** 80–90% (accounting for losses)
- **Standpost / Public Tap Coverage:** 100% within 50 m walking distance
- **Storage Capacity (Service Reservoir):** 1/3 to 1/2 of daily demand
- **Minimum Pipe Diameter (Distribution):** 50 mm (for laterals), 80–100 mm (mains)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Pillars of RWSS Design

A rural water supply project moves through **seven logical steps**, each of which must be completed before the next is begun (this sequence is identical to that followed in a *Detailed Project Report (DPR)* sanctioned under JJM / NABARD / MGNREGA convergence):

1. **Baseline Survey & Demand Assessment**
   - Household census, livestock count, institutional demand (schools, anganwadis, PHCs).
   - Computation of present population $P_{0}$ and projection to design year.

2. **Source Identification & Yield Testing**
   - Inventory of sources: borewell, dug well, spring, surface pond, river intake.
   - **Source yield test** (constant-discharge pump test for 24 h) must exceed peak demand by **at least 20%** (factor of safety).

3. **Population Forecasting**
   - Selection of growth model based on decadal census trend.
   - Validation against master-plan / district census handbook.

4. **Demand Computation**
   - Add: domestic + livestock + institutional + commercial + system losses.
   - Convert to **litre-per-second (Lps)** for hydraulic design.

5. **Treatment Train Selection**
   - Decided by raw-water quality (turbidity, iron, fluoride, microbial load).
   - Rural standard: **Slow Sand Filter (SSF)** or **Rapid Sand Filter (RSF)** with disinfection.

6. **Hydraulic Design of Conveyance & Distribution**
   - Head-loss calculation using **Hazen-Williams** or **Darcy-Weisbach** equation.
   - Pumping main sized for **peak hour demand** with **2.5 m/s** velocity cap.

7. **Storage & Distribution Layout**
   - Service Reservoir (Ground Level / Overhead) sized for **1/3 of daily demand**.
   - Network: Branched (dead-end) for rural — cost-effective & simpler O&M.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why forecast population?** Because the pipe you lay today will be in service for 30 years. A 100 mm pipe that is "big enough" today will become a bottleneck by year 2045 if the village doubles — so the system must be designed for the *future* population, not the current one.
- **Why add peaking factor?** Water use is not uniform. Morning 6–9 AM and evening 5–8 PM see **2.5–3×** the average flow due to bathing, cooking, and livestock watering. The hydraulic design must handle this *worst case*.
- **Why storage of 1/3 of daily demand?** It buffers the diurnal mismatch — the pump/refill happens at uniform rate (say, 6 hours), but the consumption is bursty. The tank fills during low-demand hours and empties during peak hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Always remember the **"3-1-1 Rule"** for rural storage design:
> - **3** = Daily demand divided into **3 equal 8-hour shifts** for pumping.
> - **1/3** = Storage volume = 1/3 of daily demand (covers peak-hour + fire + breakdown reserve).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_n = P_0 (1 + r/100)^n$ | Geometric population forecast | persons | $r$ = 1 to 2 % |
| 2 | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x$ | Arithmetic increase method | persons | $x$ = decadal increase/10 |
| 3 | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{y}$ | Incremental Increase method | persons | $\bar{x}, \bar{y}$ from census |
| 4 | $Q_{avg} = \dfrac{P \cdot q}{86400}$ | Average daily flow | L/s | $q$ in lpcd |
| 5 | $Q_{peak} = Q_{avg} \times PF$ | Peak flow (peak factor $PF = 2.5$–$3.0$) | L/s | — |
| 6 | $Q_{max-day} = Q_{avg} \times MDF$ | Maximum day demand (MDF ≈ 1.8) | L/s | — |
| 7 | $Q_{fire} = 1000 \cdot \sqrt{P}$ (in Lpm) for $P \le 50{,}000$ | Fire demand (for rural) | Lpm | $P$ in thousands |
| 8 | $V_{SSR} = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot P_n \cdot q$ | Service Reservoir capacity | litres | — |
| 9 | $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$ | Hazen-Williams head loss | m | $C = 130$ (PVC/HDPE) |
| 10 | $V_{tank} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot H$ | Cylindrical tank volume | m³ | $D$ = diameter |
| 11 | $P_{residual} = GL_{tank} - GL_{consumer} - h_f - h_{minor}$ | Residual head at tap | m | $\ge 7$ m |
| 12 | $V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ (Manning) | Open-channel / drain flow | m/s | $n$ = roughness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for Rural Design:**
> - Minimum residual head at standpost: **7 m** (CPHEEO).
> - Maximum velocity in pumping main: **2.4–3.0 m/s** (to avoid water hammer).
> - Maximum velocity in gravity main: **1.5 m/s** (sediment control).
> - Minimum pipe diameter: **50 mm** (laterals), **80–100 mm** (mains).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design of a rural water supply system is a **microcosm of urban hydraulic engineering**: it requires the same fundamental understanding of fluid mechanics, demand hydraulics, and water-quality engineering, but it is constrained by **low budgets, intermittent power, limited skilled O&M manpower, and dispersed demand**. Mastery of this design enables a civil engineer to:
- Prepare technically sound **DPRs** for funding under JJM, AMRUT, NABARD.
- Work with **PHED / Kerala Water Authority (KWA)** and Panchayat engineering wings.
- Undertake **service-level benchmarking (SLB)** audits to assess whether a village has achieved "fully covered" status under JJM.
- Switch seamlessly into urban water-distribution design where the same Hazen-Williams / Hardy-Cross principles apply.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Population Forecasting — Complete Derivation

**Problem:** A village had a population of 4,200 in 2011 and 4,950 in 2021. Forecast the population in 2051 using (a) Arithmetic Increase, (b) Geometric Increase, and (c) Incremental Increase method, given a previous decade (2001) population of 3,600. Assume the design year is 2051 (i.e., $n = 30$ years from 2021).

#### (a) Arithmetic Increase Method

**Logic:** Assumes the *absolute* decadal increase remains constant.

Decadal increase from 2001 to 2011:
$$\Delta P_1 = 4200 - 3600 = 600$$

Decadal increase from 2011 to 2021:
$$\Delta P_2 = 4950 - 4200 = 750$$

Average decadal increase:
$$\bar{x} = \frac{600 + 750}{2} = 675 \text{ persons per decade}$$

For 30 years (3 decades), using $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$:
$$P_{2051} = 4950 + 3 \times 675 = 4950 + 2025 = 6975$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Step Marker:** The arithmetic method is preferred when the growth rate is *decreasing* (typical of mature / urbanizing villages).

#### (b) Geometric Increase Method

**Logic:** Assumes the *percentage* growth rate is constant.

Decadal growth rate from 2011 to 2021:
$$r = \left(\frac{P_{2021}}{P_{2011}} - 1\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{4950}{4200} - 1\right) \times 100 = 17.86\% \text{ per decade}$$

For 3 decades, $n = 3$:
$$P_{2051} = 4950 \times \left(1 + \frac{17.86}{100}\right)^{3} = 4950 \times (1.1786)^{3}$$

Computing the cube:
$$(1.1786)^2 = 1.3891$$
$$(1.1786)^3 = 1.3891 \times 1.1786 = 1.6370$$

Therefore:
$$P_{2051} = 4950 \times 1.6370 = 8103 \text{ persons}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Step Marker:** The geometric method is *mandatory* per CPHEEO for villages showing > 1 % decadal growth — which is the majority of rural Kerala.

#### (c) Incremental Increase Method

**Logic:** Assumes the *increase in the decadal increase* is constant. This is the most realistic of the three.

Decadal increase increment:
$$\bar{y} = \frac{\Delta P_2 - \Delta P_1}{1} = \frac{750 - 600}{1} = 150 \text{ persons per decade per decade}$$

Applying the formula $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{y}$ for $n = 3$ decades:
$$P_{2051} = 4950 + 3 \times 675 + \frac{3 \times 4}{2} \times 150$$
$$P_{2051} = 4950 + 2025 + 6 \times 150$$
$$P_{2051} = 4950 + 2025 + 900 = 7875 \text{ persons}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Method | Forecasted Population (2051) | Reliability |
|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic | 6,975 | Lowest |
| Geometric | 8,103 | High (for stable growth) |
| Incremental Increase | 7,875 | **Highest — CPHEEO Recommended** |

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Complete Design Calculation

**Problem:** Design a rural water supply scheme for the village of *Vattakanal* (hypothetical) for the year 2051, given:
- Present (2021) population = 4,950
- Per capita demand $q = 55$ lpcd (Jal Jeevan Mission standard)
- Peaking Factor $PF = 3.0$
- Maximum Day Factor $MDF = 1.8$
- Livestock equivalent = 1,200 (cattle + goat + poultry, each weighted)
- Institutional demand = 10 % of domestic
- System losses = 15 %
- Choose storage = 1/3 of total daily demand
- Design pumping main to deliver peak-hour demand in 6 hours of pumping.

**Step 1: Forecasted population** (from above) = **8,103 persons** (taking geometric — realistic for Kerala rural growth).

**Step 2: Compute Domestic Demand**
$$Q_{dom} = \frac{8103 \times 55}{86400} = \frac{445{,}665}{86{,}400} = 5.158 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 3: Livestock Demand (assumed 25 lpcd per livestock unit as per IS 1172 / CPHEEO)**
$$Q_{livestock} = \frac{1200 \times 25}{86400} = \frac{30{,}000}{86{,}400} = 0.347 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 4: Institutional Demand (10 % of domestic)**
$$Q_{inst} = 0.10 \times 5.158 = 0.516 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 5: Total Average Daily Demand (ADD)**
$$Q_{ADD} = 5.158 + 0.347 + 0.516 = 6.021 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 6: Apply Maximum Day Factor (MDF = 1.8) for treatment / source design**
$$Q_{max-day} = 6.021 \times 1.8 = 10.838 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 7: Apply Peaking Factor (PF = 3.0) for distribution / pumping design**
$$Q_{peak} = 6.021 \times 3.0 = 18.063 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 8: Convert to daily volume for storage**
Daily volume with losses (15 %):
$$V_{daily} = 8103 \times 55 \times 1.15 + 1200 \times 25 \times 1.15$$
$$V_{daily} = 512{,}545 + 34{,}500 = 547{,}045 \text{ L/day} \approx 547 \text{ kL/day}$$

**Step 9: Service Reservoir Capacity**
$$V_{SSR} = \frac{1}{3} \times 547{,}045 = 182{,}348 \text{ L} \approx 182.35 \text{ m}^3$$

If a circular tank of depth 3 m is selected, diameter:
$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 V}{\pi H}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 182.35}{\pi \times 3}} = \sqrt{77.39} = 8.80 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Verdict:**
> - **Source yield required:** $\ge 13 \text{ L/s}$ (1.2 × max-day demand, for safety)
> - **Pump capacity:** $18.06 \text{ L/s} \times 3.6 = 65 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$
> - **Pump running time:** 6 hours/day (requires 18.06 × 24 / 6 = **72.25 m³/h pump rating** to refill tank in 6 h)
> - **Storage:** 8.8 m diameter × 3 m depth circular Ground-Level Reservoir (GLR), feeding an Elevated Storage Reservoir (ESR) of 50 m³ for pressure equalization.

### 3.3 Pipe Sizing — Worked Example (Hazen-Williams)

**Problem:** A 1.2 km long PVC pumping main (Hazen-Williams $C = 130$) carries $Q = 18.06$ L/s = $0.01806$ m³/s from the GLR to the ESR. Available head = 12 m. Find the minimum pipe diameter such that head loss $\le 5$ m.

Rearranging Hazen-Williams for diameter:
$$D = \left(\frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot h_f}\right)^{1/4.87}$$

Substituting $L = 1200$ m, $Q = 0.01806$ m³/s, $C = 130$, $h_f = 5$ m:

First compute $Q^{1.852}$:
$$\log(0.01806) = -1.7431$$
$$1.852 \times (-1.7431) = -3.2280$$
$$Q^{1.852} = 10^{-3.2280} = 0.000593 \text{ m}^{5.556}/\text{s}^{1.852}$$

Numerator:
$$10.67 \times 1200 \times 0.000593 = 7.595$$

Denominator:
$$C^{1.852} = 130^{1.852}$$
$$\log(130) = 2.1139$$
$$1.852 \times 2.1139 = 3.915$$
$$C^{1.852} = 10^{3.915} = 8223$$
$$8223 \times 5 = 41{,}115$$

Therefore:
$$D^{4.87} = \frac{7.595}{41{,}115} \cdot h_f \text{ cancelled in step} = 0.0001847$$

Wait — re-derive carefully:
$$D^{4.87} = \frac{10.67 \times L \times Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \times h_f} = \frac{7.595}{41{,}115} = 1.847 \times 10^{-4}$$

Taking the 1/4.87 power:
$$D = (1.847 \times 10^{-4})^{1/4.87}$$

$$\log(1.847 \times 10^{-4}) = -3.7334$$
$$\frac{-3.7334}{4.87} = -0.7666$$
$$D = 10^{-0.7666} = 0.171 \text{ m} = 171 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt next standard size = 200 mm OD PVC pipe (PN 6 class).**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Step Marker:** Always recheck velocity:
> $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{0.01806}{(\pi/4)(0.171)^2} = \dfrac{0.01806}{0.02296} = 0.787$ m/s.
> This is below the 2.4 m/s cap — pipe is hydraulically safe.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
*Define the term "Design Period" as applied to rural water supply engineering. State the design period adopted for (i) civil works and (ii) mechanical/electrical works in a RWSS project.*

**Model Answer:**
The **design period** is the number of years for which the system is engineered to function efficiently without major augmentation, balancing the cost of over-building today against the cost of under-sizing tomorrow.
- **Civil works (intake, treatment, tanks, pipes):** **30 years**
- **Mechanical / Electrical works (pumps, motors, switchgear):** **15 years**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write 15 years for *both*. Losing 1 mark. Always distinguish civil vs. mechanical.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is a Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC) under the Jal Jeevan Mission? State the JJM service-level benchmark for a village to be declared "fully covered."*

**Model Answer:**
An **FHTC** is a tap water connection that delivers the prescribed quantity of potable water (≥ **55 lpcd**) at a pressure of ≥ 7 m head, on a regular and long-term basis, to every rural household.
A village is declared **"fully covered"** when **100%** of households have an FHTC and the system delivers the JJM-mandated quality (potable per IS 10500) and quantity (≥ 55 lpcd).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing only "tap connection" without specifying **quantity, quality, regularity, and pressure** leads to loss of 1.5 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Module 2 Internal Choice:** Students answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the step-by-step procedure for the design of a rural water supply system, including (i) demand assessment, (ii) source selection criteria, and (iii) treatment-train selection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A village has the following census populations: 1981 = 2,100; 1991 = 2,600; 2001 = 3,150; 2011 = 3,600; 2021 = 3,950. Forecast the population in 2051 using (i) Arithmetic Increase method, and (ii) Geometric Increase method. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-step procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Demand Assessment:** Compute present population $P_0$, project to design year, multiply by per-capita demand (55 lpcd under JJM), add livestock and institutional demand, apply peaking factor (2.5–3.0), and convert to L/s for hydraulic design.
2. **Source Selection Criteria:** 
   - Adequate yield (≥ 1.2 × max-day demand), 
   - Reliable year-round availability, 
   - Water quality (low turbidity, low microbial load, acceptable hardness/iron), 
   - Proximity to village (reduces pumping cost), 
   - Source type priority: Spring > Borewell > Dugwell > Surface.
3. **Treatment Train Selection:** Based on raw-water analysis.
   - Low turbidity (< 10 NTU) & good microbial quality → Direct chlorination.
   - Moderate turbidity (10–100 NTU) → Slow Sand Filter (SSF) + chlorination.
   - High turbidity (> 100 NTU) → Rapid Sand Filter (RSF) with coagulation + chlorination.
   - Iron > 0.3 mg/L → Aeration + Filtration + Chlorination.
   - Fluoride > 1.5 mg/L → Activated alumina / Nalgonda technique.

**(b) Population forecast (7 marks):**

**Arithmetic Increase Method:**
- $\Delta P_{81-91} = 2600 - 2100 = 500$
- $\Delta P_{91-01} = 3150 - 2600 = 550$
- $\Delta P_{01-11} = 3600 - 3150 = 450$
- $\Delta P_{11-21} = 3950 - 3600 = 350$

- $\bar{x} = \frac{500 + 550 + 450 + 350}{4} = \frac{1850}{4} = 462.5$ per decade

For $n = 3$ decades (2021 to 2051):
$$P_{2051} = 3950 + 3 \times 462.5 = 3950 + 1387.5 = 5338 \text{ persons}$$

**Geometric Increase Method:**
- $r_{81-91} = (2600/2100 - 1) \times 100 = 23.81\%$
- $r_{91-01} = (3150/2600 - 1) \times 100 = 21.15\%$
- $r_{01-11} = (3600/3150 - 1) \times 100 = 14.29\%$
- $r_{11-21} = (3950/3600 - 1) \times 100 = 9.72\%$

- $\bar{r} = \frac{23.81 + 21.15 + 14.29 + 9.72}{4} = 17.24\%$ per decade

For $n = 3$:
$$P_{2051} = 3950 \times (1 + 0.1724)^3 = 3950 \times (1.1724)^3$$

Computing:
$$(1.1724)^2 = 1.3745$$
$$(1.1724)^3 = 1.3745 \times 1.1724 = 1.6114$$

$$P_{2051} = 3950 \times 1.6114 = 6365 \text{ persons}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error in the geometric method is using the *last-decade* growth rate (9.72%) instead of the *average* decadal growth rate (17.24%). This under-forecasts by ~700 persons. Always use the *average* of all available decades unless otherwise specified.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the components of a rural water supply system with a neat sketch. List any four common source types used in rural Kerala. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rural panchayat with a forecasted 2051 population of 7,500 and 1,500 livestock units is to be supplied at 55 lpcd (domestic) and 25 lpcd (livestock). Compute (i) the average daily demand in L/s, (ii) the peak demand with a peaking factor of 2.8, and (iii) the service reservoir capacity. Institutional demand is 10 % of domestic demand; system losses are 15 %. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Components of RWSS (7 marks):**
The components in functional sequence are:
1. **Source** — Borewell, Dugwell, Spring, River/Reservoir intake.
2. **Intake structure** — Jack well or well head.
3. **Pumping machinery** — Submersible / centrifugal pump (where source is below distribution zone).
4. **Rising main** — Carries water from source to treatment plant / sump.
5. **Treatment plant** — Cascade aerator, clariflocculator, filter, chlorinator.
6. **Pure water sump** — Storage of treated water.
7. **Booster pumping station** — Pumps water to elevated tank.
8. **Elevated Service Reservoir (ESR)** — Stores 1/3 daily demand; provides pressure.
9. **Distribution network** — Branched layout with main, submain, laterals.
10. **Consumer connections** — Standposts, FHTCs, institutional connections.

**Four common source types in rural Kerala:**
1. **Open wells / Dug wells** — Common in midland laterite regions.
2. **Borewells (tube wells)** — Most common in the coastal sandy belt; yield 1–5 L/s.
3. **Springs** — Abundant in Western Ghats foothills; gravity-driven, low O&M.
4. **Surface water bodies** — Rivers, reservoirs (e.g., check dams, Kerala KWA schemes).

**(b) Demand Calculations (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $P_{2051} = 7500$ persons
- Livestock units = 1500
- Domestic per capita $q_d = 55$ lpcd
- Livestock per capita $q_l = 25$ lpcd
- Institutional = 10 % of domestic
- Losses = 15 %
- Peaking factor PF = 2.8

**(i) Average Daily Demand in L/s:**

Domestic demand:
$$Q_{dom} = \frac{7500 \times 55}{86400} = \frac{412{,}500}{86{,}400} = 4.773 \text{ L/s}$$

Livestock demand:
$$Q_{liv} = \frac{1500 \times 25}{86400} = \frac{37{,}500}{86{,}400} = 0.434 \text{ L/s}$$

Institutional demand (10 % of domestic):
$$Q_{inst} = 0.10 \times 4.773 = 0.477 \text{ L/s}$$

Net (without losses) average demand:
$$Q_{net} = 4.773 + 0.434 + 0.477 = 5.684 \text{ L/s}$$

Including 15 % losses:
$$Q_{ADD} = 5.684 \times 1.15 = 6.537 \text{ L/s}$$

**(ii) Peak Demand (PF = 2.8):**
$$Q_{peak} = 6.537 \times 2.8 = 18.30 \text{ L/s}$$

**(iii) Service Reservoir Capacity:**

Daily volume (gross, with losses):
$$V_{daily} = (7500 \times 55 + 1500 \times 25) \times 1.15$$
$$V_{daily} = (412{,}500 + 37{,}500) \times 1.15 = 450{,}000 \times 1.15 = 517{,}500 \text{ L/day}$$

Storage (1/3 rule):
$$V_{SSR} = \frac{517{,}500}{3} = 172{,}500 \text{ L} = 172.5 \text{ m}^3$$

**Design Verdict:**
- Source yield required: $\ge 1.2 \times 6.537 \times 1.8 = 14.12$ L/s (where 1.8 is MDF for source / treatment sizing)
- Pumping main to be sized for $Q_{peak} = 18.3$ L/s
- ESR: 172.5 m³ circular tank, e.g., 9 m dia × 3 m depth

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Two frequent mistakes:
> 1. *Forgetting losses* — most students omit the 1.15 factor on the daily volume. This shrinks the tank by 15 % and loses 1.5 marks.
> 2. *Confusing PF with MDF* — PF (2.8) is for distribution; MDF (1.8) is for source/treatment. Using PF to size the source over-estimates by ~50 %.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Design and Implementation of Rural Water Supply Systems**

**🔹 Core Definitions**
- ☐ RWSS = integrated system serving < 5,000-pop villages
- ☐ Design period: **30 yr (civil)**, 15 yr (mech)
- ☐ JJM target: **55 lpcd** at every rural household (FHTC)
- ☐ Min residual head at tap: **7 m**
- ☐ Storage: **1/3 of daily demand**

**🔹 Design Equations (Must Memorize)**
- ☐ $P_n = P_0(1+r/100)^n$ — Geometric
- ☐ $P_n = P_0 + n\bar{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y}$ — Incremental Increase
- ☐ $Q_{avg} = P \cdot q / 86400$ L/s
- ☐ $Q_{peak} = Q_{avg} \times PF$ where $PF = 2.5$–$3.0$
- ☐ $V_{SSR} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot P_n \cdot q$
- ☐ $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 L Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} D^{4.87}}$ — Hazen-Williams

**🔹 Design Process (7 Steps)**
1. ☐ Baseline survey + demand assessment
2. ☐ Source identification + yield testing (≥ 1.2 × max-day demand)
3. ☐ Population forecasting (incremental increase — most reliable)
4. ☐ Demand computation (domestic + livestock + institutional + losses)
5. ☐ Treatment selection (SSF / RSF / chlorination)
6. ☐ Hydraulic design (pipe sizing, pumping main, ESR)
7. ☐ Implementation (DPR → tender → construction → commissioning → O&M handover)

**🔹 Common Pitfalls (Avoid These)**
- ☐ Confusing design period for civil vs. mechanical
- ☐ Omitting peaking factor in distribution design
- ☐ Using PF (2.8) for source sizing — should be MDF (1.8)
- ☐ Forgetting 15 % system losses in storage volume
- ☐ Adopting < 50 mm diameter for laterals
- ☐ Designing the tank to be lower than the highest consumer (no pressure!)

**🔹 Kerala-Specific Notes**
- ☐ Kerala Water Authority (KWA) handles urban; **Panchayat engineering wing** handles rural (with PHED support).
- ☐ JJM-implementation in Kerala has achieved > 75 % FHTC coverage (2024 status).
- ☐ Aquifer in midland laterite: low yield — often need **multiple borewells with header system**.
- ☐ Western Ghats villages: **spring sources + gravity system** preferred over pumping (cheaper O&M).

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition: Water Source Development

**Water Source Development** refers to the systematic engineering process of identifying, tapping, harnessing, treating, and protecting natural water sources (surface water bodies and subsurface aquifers) to provide a safe, reliable, and sustainable yield of potable water for rural communities. It encompasses the design, construction, and maintenance of extraction structures such as wells, intakes, infiltration galleries, and springs, along with measures to ensure long-term quantity and quality.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for *PECET867 – Rural Water Supply and Onsite Sanitation Systems*, water source development constitutes the critical first link of the rural water supply (RWS) service chain, preceding transmission, treatment, storage, and distribution. As per the *Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)* framework — operational since **August 2019** and now covering more than **15+ crore rural households in India** — functional household tap connections depend fundamentally on robust source development.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 2 focuses on the engineering aspects of *intake structures*, *well hydraulics*, *spring development*, *yield assessment*, and *sanitary protection* of sources. These directly map to Course Outcome **CO2**: *"Design rural water supply schemes including source development, treatment, and distribution."*

## 1.2 Classification of Water Sources

Water sources for rural water supply are broadly classified into two categories:

| Category | Examples | Typical Use |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| **Surface Water Sources** | Rivers, streams, lakes, ponds, reservoirs, impoundments | Large communities (≥ 5,000 population) |
| **Groundwater Sources** | Open wells, bore wells, tube wells, springs, infiltration galleries | Small to medium communities (≤ 5,000 population) |

For rural Kerala, where the terrain is undulating, laterite formations dominate, and rainfall averages **3000 mm/year**, **groundwater sources are predominantly used** for about 70–80% of habitations.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Bank"

Imagine the Earth is a giant **savings bank** for water:
- **Rainfall** is the *deposit*.
- **Groundwater aquifers** are the *fixed-deposit accounts* that store water for years.
- **Surface water bodies (rivers, ponds)** are the *current account* — easily accessible but volatile.
- **Water source development** is the act of *opening a bank account* and installing an *ATM* (well, intake, pump) to withdraw water when needed.

Just as a bank has rules (KYC, audit, security), source development requires **sanitary protection, yield testing, and recharge planning** to remain sustainable.

## 1.4 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for Source Development

- **Yield (Q):** Volume of water extracted per unit time (m³/hr or lpm — *litres per minute*).
- **Drawdown (s):** The drop in water level from the static (rest) level when pumping occurs.
- **Specific Capacity:** Yield per unit drawdown (lpm/m).
- **Safe Yield:** Maximum rate of withdrawal that can be sustained indefinitely without depleting the source.
- **Recovery Time:** Time taken for the water level to return to its static level after pumping stops.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for Rural Kerala:** The *CPHEEO Manual on Water Supply and Treatment* (latest edition under JJM) prescribes a minimum service level of **55 lpcd (litres per capita per day)** for rural households.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Wells — Engineering Classification

### 2.1.1 Open Wells (Dug Wells)
- **Construction:** Hand-dug or mechanically excavated, typically **2 m to 10 m diameter**, depth **5 m to 20 m**.
- **Lining:** Masonry (brick/stone) or RCC rings (*rings of 0.5–1 m height*).
- **Use:** Suitable in areas with high water table (< 8 m below ground level).
- **Discharge range:** **50 to 500 lpm** depending on diameter and aquifer permeability.
- **Disadvantage:** Susceptible to contamination from surface runoff; lower yield.

### 2.1.2 Bore Wells
- **Construction:** Drilled using a rig (rotary/percussion), diameter **100 mm to 300 mm**, depth **30 m to 200 m**.
- **Casing:** PVC or mild steel casing with **screen slots** in the aquifer zone.
- **Use:** Most common for rural Kerala — taps deeper laterite or fractured-rock aquifers.

### 2.1.3 Tube Wells
- **Construction:** Similar to bore wells but with a *tube/pipe assembly* and a *strainer* section.
- **Distinction from bore well:** Tube well typically refers to a **strainer-equipped** well developed specifically for granular aquifers; bore well may be uncased in hard rock.

### 2.1.4 Driven Wells
- **Construction:** A small-diameter pipe with a drive point is hammered into shallow sandy aquifers.
- **Use:** Limited to depth < 10 m; rare in Kerala due to hard laterite.

## 2.2 Spring Development

**Spring** — a natural outflow of groundwater at the land surface where the water table intersects the topography.

### 2.2.1 Classification of Springs
- **Gravity Springs:** Water emerges from a permeable layer under gravity (most common).
- **Artesian Springs:** Water rises under pressure from a confined aquifer.
- **Tubular/Seepage Springs:** Low-yield, diffused discharge.

### 2.2.2 Spring Development Techniques
1. **Collection Chamber:** RCC chamber built around the spring eye to capture and protect discharge.
2. **Spring Box:** A sealed masonry/RCC box with an inlet, outlet, and overflow.
3. **French Drain:** A perforated pipe laid in a gravel-filled trench in the spring zone.

## 2.3 Infiltration Galleries

An **infiltration gallery** is a horizontal or slightly inclined underground drain (perforated pipe or open channel) placed in the alluvium adjacent to a river or stream. Water from the surface body seeps into the gallery through the permeable bed.

**Typical layout (textual schematic):**
- Length: **10 m to 100 m** depending on yield.
- Depth below bed: **2 m to 5 m**.
- Diameter: **0.3 m to 1.0 m**.

## 2.4 Well Hydraulics — Governing Equations

The flow of water towards a well in an unconfined aquifer was first mathematically described by **Dupuit (1863)** and modified by **Thiem (1906)**, then generalized by **Theis (1935)** and **Jacob (1940)** for transient conditions.

### 2.4.1 Steady-State Flow in Unconfined Aquifer (Dupuit Equation)
For steady-state radial flow to a fully penetrating well in an unconfined aquifer:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \cdot K \cdot (H^2 - h_w^2)}{\ln(R/r_w)}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = discharge (m³/s)
- $K$ = hydraulic conductivity (m/s)
- $H$ = static saturated thickness of aquifer (m)
- $h_w$ = water depth in the well during pumping (m)
- $R$ = radius of influence (m)
- $r_w$ = radius of well (m)

### 2.4.2 Steady-State Flow in Confined Aquifer (Thiem Equation)
For a fully penetrating well in a confined aquifer:

$$Q = \frac{2 \pi \cdot K_b \cdot b \cdot (H - h_w)}{\ln(R/r_w)}$$

Where $K_b \cdot b = T$ (transmissivity, m²/s), and $b$ = aquifer thickness (m).

### 2.4.3 Theis Equation (Transient Flow)
Used for **unsteady (transient)** flow where drawdown changes with time:

$$s = \frac{Q}{4 \pi T} \cdot W(u)$$

Where $s$ = drawdown (m), $W(u)$ = well function, $u = r^2 S / (4 T t)$, $S$ = storativity, $t$ = time since pumping began.

### 2.4.4 Jacob's Modified Equation (for small $u$, i.e., large $t$)
$$s = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \left[ \log_{10}\left(\frac{2.25 \, T t}{r^2 S}\right) \right]$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Use | Units |
|---|---------|-----|-------|
| 1 | $Q = \pi K (H^2 - h_w^2) / \ln(R/r_w)$ | Steady flow, unconfined aquifer | m³/s |
| 2 | $Q = 2 \pi T (H - h_w) / \ln(R/r_w)$ | Steady flow, confined aquifer | m³/s |
| 3 | $s = H - h_w$ | Drawdown definition | m |
| 4 | $W(u) = \int_u^{\infty} (e^{-y}/y) \, dy$ | Theis well function | dimensionless |
| 5 | $u = r^2 S / (4 T t)$ | Dimensionless time parameter | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T = K \cdot b$ | Transmissivity | m²/s |
| 7 | $Q_{safe} = Q_{recharge} - Q_{natural discharge}$ | Safe yield criterion | m³/day |
| 8 | $Q_{pump} = (Q_{test} \cdot 24 / t) \times \eta$ | Pump sizing from test yield | lpm |
| 9 | $s_p = s_w \cdot (Q_p / Q_w)^n$ | Step-drawdown scaling (Hantush-Bierschenk) | m |
| 10 | $Q = C_d A_{eff} \sqrt{2gH}$ | Discharge from an orifice/spring outlet | m³/s |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 examination, **Questions on Theis and Jacob equations are high-frequency**; memorize the logarithmic form and be able to perform **straight-line matching on semi-log paper** for the time–drawdown method.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Dupuit Equation (Unconfined Aquifer)

### Assumptions
1. Aquifer is homogeneous, isotropic, and of infinite extent.
2. Well fully penetrates the aquifer.
3. Flow is steady and radial (horizontal).
4. Darcy's law is valid.

### Step 1 — Define the Control Volume
Consider a cylindrical control surface at radius $r$ from the well center, height $H$ (saturated thickness).

**Step 2 — Apply Darcy's Law**
For radial flow through a cylindrical surface of area $A = 2 \pi r h$:

$$Q = K \cdot i \cdot A = K \cdot \frac{dh}{dr} \cdot 2 \pi r h$$

Where $i = dh/dr$ is the hydraulic gradient (positive away from the well).

**Step 3 — Separate Variables and Integrate**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q \cdot \frac{dr}{r} &= 2 \pi K \cdot h \cdot dh \\
Q \int_{r_w}^{R} \frac{dr}{r} &= 2 \pi K \int_{h_w}^{H} h \, dh \\
Q \cdot \ln\left(\frac{R}{r_w}\right) &= 2 \pi K \cdot \left[ \frac{h^2}{2} \right]_{h_w}^{H} \\
Q \cdot \ln\left(\frac{R}{r_w}\right) &= \pi K (H^2 - h_w^2) \\
Q &= \frac{\pi K (H^2 - h_w^2)}{\ln(R/r_w)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Equation (Dupuit, 1863):**

$$Q = \frac{\pi \cdot K \cdot (H^2 - h_w^2)}{\ln(R/r_w)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A fully penetrating well in an unconfined aquifer has the following data:
- $K = 12$ m/day
- $H = 20$ m (initial saturated thickness)
- $h_w = 14$ m (water depth during pumping)
- $r_w = 0.15$ m
- $R = 150$ m

**Find the steady-state discharge $Q$.**

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the applicable equation.**
Unconfined + steady state → **Dupuit Equation.**

**Step 2 — Substitute the values into $Q = \pi K (H^2 - h_w^2) / \ln(R/r_w)$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\pi \times 12 \times (20^2 - 14^2)}{\ln(150/0.15)} \\
&= \frac{\pi \times 12 \times (400 - 196)}{\ln(1000)} \\
&= \frac{37.699 \times 12 \times 204}{6.9078} \\
&= \frac{37.699 \times 2448}{6.9078} \\
&= \frac{92{,}286.6}{6.9078} \\
&\approx 13{,}360 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to lpm.**

$$Q = 13{,}360 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times \frac{1000 \text{ L}}{1 \text{ m}^3} \times \frac{1 \text{ day}}{1440 \text{ min}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= 13{,}360 \times 1000 / 1440 \\
&\approx 9278 \text{ lpm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Specific Capacity.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Drawdown, } s &= H - h_w = 20 - 14 = 6 \text{ m} \\
\text{Specific Capacity} &= Q/s = 9278 / 6 \\
&\approx 1546 \text{ lpm/m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Determine safe yield (rule of thumb: 70% of test yield).**

$$Q_{safe} = 0.70 \times 9278 \approx 6495 \text{ lpm}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Source Development Decision

| Source Type | Per Capita Yield | Capital Cost | O&M Cost | Susceptibility to Drought | Suitability in Kerala |
|-------------|------------------|--------------|----------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| Open Well (Dug) | 5–15 lpcd | Low | Low | High (seasonal) | Moderate |
| Bore Well | 20–50 lpcd | Medium | Medium | Low–Medium | High |
| Tube Well (deep) | 30–80 lpcd | Medium–High | Medium | Low | High |
| Spring | 10–30 lpcd | Low–Medium | Very Low | Medium | Very High (hilly areas) |
| Infiltration Gallery | 25–60 lpcd | High | Medium | Low | Moderate (riverside) |
| Surface Water (Pond) | 40–100 lpcd | Low (intake) | High (treatment) | Very High | Moderate |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define "safe yield" of a well. How is it determined from a pumping test?**

**Model Answer:**
The **safe yield** of a well is the maximum rate at which water can be withdrawn continuously from the well without causing an unacceptable decline in water level or permanent depletion of the aquifer. It is typically taken as **70% of the test yield** to ensure long-term sustainability and account for seasonal variation.

It is determined by:
1. Conducting a **constant-rate pumping test** for at least **24 hours** (or until steady-state drawdown is reached).
2. Recording drawdown $s$ and discharge $Q_{test}$.
3. Computing **specific capacity** = $Q_{test}/s$.
4. Applying **70% rule**: $Q_{safe} = 0.7 \times Q_{test}$.
5. For long-term assurance, plotting **time–drawdown** data and applying **Jacob's straight-line method** to obtain transmissivity $T$ and storativity $S$, which are then used to model long-term behavior.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – December 2023]
**List the components of a sanitary protection zone around a rural water source.**

**Model Answer:**
As per *CPHEEO Manual* and JJM guidelines, the sanitary protection zone (also called *sanitary surveillance zone*) consists of:
1. **Inner zone (0–15 m):** Fenced, vegetation maintained, no human or animal entry, no agricultural activity, no wastewater disposal.
2. **Intermediate zone (15–50 m):** Restricted activities; no latrines, manure pits, or chemical storage.
3. **Outer zone (50–100 m or up to aquifer boundary):** Surveillance of land use; recharge pits allowed only after water-quality impact assessment.

**Additional measures:** Diversion drains above the well, raised impervious platform, drainage away from the well, regular water-quality testing for *faecal coliforms* and *E. coli*.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer (Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — 14 Marks

**A community of 2,500 population in rural Wayanad, Kerala, requires a sustainable water source. The site investigation reveals a fractured laterite aquifer at 30 m depth. Design a bore well-based water supply scheme addressing:**
**(a)** Site selection criteria, well construction, and casing/screen design — **7 marks**
**(b)** Pumping test analysis using Jacob's method and determination of safe yield and transmissivity — **7 marks**

#### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Site Selection and Well Construction

**Step 1 — Site Selection Criteria:**
- **Geological survey:** Identify lineaments, fracture intersections, and down-thrown blocks (in laterite, fractures are water-bearing).
- **Hydrogeological survey:** Review CGWB (Central Ground Water Board) data and prior bore yields in the area.
- **Surface indicators:** Vegetation patches, dug-well behaviour in adjacent plots, electrical resistivity survey (VES — Vertical Electrical Sounding) to identify aquifer zones.
- **Social and sanitary:** Distance ≥ 50 m from latrines, septic tanks, animal sheds, and agricultural runoff zones.

**Step 2 — Bore Well Design Parameters:**
- **Depth:** 60–80 m (10 m above and below identified fracture zone).
- **Casing diameter:** 150 mm (for housing 100 mm submersible pump).
- **Casing material:** UPVC (unplasticized PVC) Class 4 (IS 12818), with appropriate wall thickness.
- **Screen section:** Slotted PVC screen, slot size **0.75–1.5 mm**, slot aperture 8–10%, length matching aquifer thickness (typically 3–6 m).
- **Gravel pack:** Pea gravel of 2–4 mm size, packed between casing and borehole wall to prevent fines migration.
- **Cement sealing:** Top 6 m sealed with cement grout to prevent surface contamination.

**Step 3 — Sanitary Protection:**
- Construct a **100 mm RCC platform** raised 0.5 m above ground level.
- Construct a **drainage apron** sloping away from the well.
- Provide a **lockable cap** on the casing.
- Fence the **15 m inner zone**.

**Summary table:**

| Component | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| Casing diameter | 150 mm UPVC, Class 4 |
| Screen slot opening | 0.75–1.5 mm |
| Screen length | 3–6 m |
| Gravel pack size | 2–4 mm |
| Top sealing | 6 m cement grout |
| Development method | Air compressor surge pumping |

#### Part (b) — Pumping Test Analysis (Jacob's Method)

**Step 1 — Pumping Test Data (assume a representative constant-rate test):**

| Time $t$ (min) | Drawdown $s$ (m) |
|-----------------|------------------|
| 1 | 0.40 |
| 2 | 0.70 |
| 5 | 1.30 |
| 10 | 1.80 |
| 20 | 2.40 |
| 50 | 3.30 |
| 100 | 3.95 |
| 200 | 4.65 |
| 500 | 5.70 |
| 1000 | 6.45 |

Constant pumping rate $Q = 1200$ lpm = 1.2 m³/min.
Observation well radius $r = 30$ m (for a single-well test, $r = r_w$ but Jacob's method is more accurate with observation well).

**Step 2 — Apply Jacob's Equation:**

$$s = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \left[ \log_{10}\left(\frac{2.25 \, T t}{r^2 S}\right) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Plot $s$ (y-axis) vs. $\log_{10} t$ (x-axis) on semi-log paper.**
The plot shows a straight line for $u \leq 0.05$ (typically for $t > 100$ min).

**Step 4 — Determine slope $\Delta s$ per $\log$ cycle.**

From the data:
- At $t = 100$ min, $s = 3.95$ m
- At $t = 1000$ min, $s = 6.45$ m
- $\Delta s$ per log cycle = $6.45 - 3.95 = 2.50$ m

**Step 5 — Compute Transmissivity $T$:**

$$T = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi \Delta s}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{2.303 \times 1.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}}{4 \pi \times 2.50 \text{ m}} \\
&= \frac{2.764}{31.416} \\
&\approx 0.088 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \\
&\approx 126.7 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Storativity $S$:**  
Using the intercept $t_0$ (where $s = 0$ on the extrapolated straight line): assume $t_0 = 0.5$ min.

$$S = \frac{2.25 \, T \cdot t_0}{r^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{2.25 \times 0.088 \times 0.5}{30^2} \\
&= \frac{0.099}{900} \\
&\approx 1.1 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

(Storativity in the order of $10^{-4}$ is characteristic of a **confined aquifer**.)

**Step 7 — Compute Safe Yield:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{test} &= 1200 \text{ lpm} \\
Q_{safe} &= 0.7 \times 1200 = 840 \text{ lpm} \\
&\approx 0.84 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 1209.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Verify Population Demand:**
Population = 2500, demand = 55 lpcd (Jal Jeevan Mission norm)
Total demand = $2500 \times 55 = 137{,}500$ L/day = **137.5 m³/day**

Since $Q_{safe} = 1209.6$ m³/day $\gg$ 137.5 m³/day, the source is **highly adequate** with a safety factor of nearly **8.8×**.

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – December 2023] — 14 Marks *(Alternative Choice)*

**Develop a comprehensive plan for a spring-based rural water supply system in the Idukki hills (Kerala). Your answer should cover:**
**(a)** Spring source investigation, discharge measurement, and spring box design — **7 marks**
**(b)** Protection, treatment, and distribution system from spring to household tap — **7 marks**

#### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Spring Source Investigation, Discharge Measurement & Spring Box Design

**Step 1 — Spring Source Investigation:**
- Conduct **geological mapping** to identify permeable–impermeable contact zones (e.g., laterite–rock interface, schist–granite boundary).
- Identify **spring eye (nascence point)** through:
  - **Geobotanical indicators** (presence of *Colocasia*, *Colocasia esculenta*, *Cyperus rotundus*).
  - **Resistivity survey** to map subsurface flow paths.
  - **Trial pits and dye tracing** using fluorescein or Rhodamine WT dye to confirm recharge zone.

**Step 2 — Discharge Measurement:**
- Construct a **temporary V-notch weir** (90° V-notch) at the natural outlet.
- Use the **V-notch discharge formula:**

$$Q = \frac{8}{15} \, C_d \, \sqrt{2g} \, \tan(\theta/2) \, H^{5/2}$$

For a 90° V-notch ($\theta = 90°$, $\tan 45° = 1$):

$$Q = 1.40 \, H^{2.47} \quad \text{(m}^3/\text{s, with H in m)}$$

- Record discharge **at least for 24 hours** to account for diurnal variation, and across **dry and wet seasons** for the safe yield.
- Assume measured $H = 0.10$ m, then $Q \approx 1.40 \times 0.10^{2.47} = 1.40 \times 0.00282 \approx 0.00395$ m³/s = **237 lpm** = 341 m³/day.

**Step 3 — Spring Box Design:**

The spring box is a sealed RCC/masonry collection chamber that captures, stores, and protects the spring discharge. Standard design (per *CPHEEO Manual*):

| Element | Specification |
|---------|---------------|
| Material | RCC M20 grade |
| Length × Breadth × Depth | 1.5 m × 1.0 m × 1.2 m |
| Inlet | 100 mm perforated pipe from spring eye with gravel filter |
| Outlet | 50 mm GI/PVC pipe to storage tank |
| Overflow | 75 mm pipe with mosquito-proof screen |
| Inspection | 60 cm × 60 cm manhole cover (lockable) |
| Sump provision | 0.2 m silt trap at bottom |
| Siting | 5 m above the highest flood level, on a stable slope |

**Design steps:**
1. Compute **storage capacity** for 24-hr demand: For 500 persons @ 55 lpcd = 27,500 L. The spring box acts as a *wet well*; main storage is in the ESR (Elevated Service Reservoir) downstream.
2. **Hydraulic gradient check:** Outlet pipe invert must be ≥ 30 cm above the spring box floor to prevent silt entrainment.
3. **Sizing of gravel filter pack** around inlet: 20 cm of graded gravel (6–20 mm) over the perforated inlet pipe.
4. **Impermeable apron** extending 2 m on all sides.

**Spring Box Diagram (Textual Description):**
A rectangular box with the **spring eye inflow at the bottom** entering through a **gravel filter**, **sediment settling zone**, and an **outlet pipe** taking water to a **storage tank**. The **overflow** is screened and discharged to a natural drain.

#### Part (b) — Protection, Treatment & Distribution

**Step 1 — Source Protection:**
- **Sanitary zone (0–15 m):** Fenced, no cultivation, no grazing.
- **Catchment protection (up to 200 m):** Ban on chemical fertilizers, soak pits, and latrines.
- **Reforestation** of catchment with native species (*bamboo, vetiver grass*) to reduce erosion.
- **Continuous flow monitoring** for turbidity (must be < 1 NTU for direct supply; otherwise, treatment needed).

**Step 2 — Treatment:**
For spring water of high quality, the minimum treatment is:
- **Slow Sand Filter (SSF):** Effective in removing turbidity, *E. coli* (up to 99%), and organic matter.
  - Filter media: 0.6–1.0 m sand (effective size 0.15–0.30 mm), 0.2–0.3 m gravel support.
  - Filtration rate: 0.1–0.2 m/hr.
  - Schmutzdecke layer (biological skin) develops in 1–2 weeks.
- **Disinfection:** Use **chlorination** with bleaching powder or chlorine tablets; maintain residual chlorine **0.2–0.5 mg/L** at the tap.

**Step 3 — Storage and Distribution:**
- **Elevated Service Reservoir (ESR):** Capacity = 1/3 of daily demand; for 500 persons, 27,500 × (1/3) ≈ **10,000 L** (10 m³); standard size 10 m³ RCC or HDPE tank on a **6–8 m staging tower**.
- **Distribution network:** HDPE PE-100 pipes (PN 8, diameter 50–90 mm) with household tap connections.
- **Stand posts:** Public tap stands for households not yet having private connections.
- **Pressure testing:** All pipes hydrostatically tested at 1.5× working pressure.

**Step 4 — Operational Protocol (Jal Jeevan Mission):**
- **Routine water-quality testing** at source, storage, and tap (for *E. coli*, pH, residual chlorine, turbidity).
- **Annual discharge test** at the V-notch weir.
- **Community-led O&M** through a *Village Water and Sanitation Committee (VWSC)*.

**Cost Estimate (approximate, for plan purposes):**

| Item | Cost (INR) |
|------|-----------|
| Spring investigation & survey | 30,000 |
| Spring box construction | 75,000 |
| Slow sand filter unit (10 m³/day) | 1,20,000 |
| 10 m³ ESR on 6 m staging | 3,50,000 |
| Distribution network (500 m HDPE) | 2,00,000 |
| Submersible pump & panel | 60,000 |
| Contingencies & commissioning | 65,000 |
| **Total** | **9,00,000** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Common Mistakes on Source Development Questions:**
> 1. **Units mismatch:** Students often mix m³/s and lpm without conversion. Always state units explicitly: **1 m³/s = 60,000 lpm**.
> 2. **Wrong equation choice:** Using Dupuit equation for a **confined aquifer** (must use Thiem). Identify aquifer type from the question's wording.
> 3. **Forgetting the 70% rule:** Safe yield is **not** the test yield.
> 4. **Ignoring sanitary protection:** A source without a sanitary zone loses full marks on design questions.
> 5. **Skipping the Jacob straight-line plot:** In numericals, you must show the **plot interpretation** — slope $\Delta s$ per log cycle — even if described verbally.
> 6. **Confusing storativity and specific yield:** For a confined aquifer, $S$ is small (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶); for unconfined, it equals the **specific yield** (typically 0.1–0.3).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Water source development** = tapping surface or groundwater in a sustainable and sanitary manner.
- ✅ Kerala rural water supply relies **primarily on groundwater (bore wells and springs)** due to high rainfall and laterite aquifers.
- ✅ **Open wells** are for shallow water tables; **bore/tube wells** for deeper fractured aquifers.
- ✅ **Springs** are the cheapest and most reliable source in hilly regions like Idukki and Wayanad.
- ✅ **Dupuit equation** is for unconfined aquifers; **Thiem equation** for confined aquifers.
- ✅ **Theis & Jacob equations** handle transient flow — use **semi-log straight-line method** for $T$ and $S$.
- ✅ **Transmissivity $T = K \cdot b$**; **Storativity $S$** reflects the water-yielding capacity of the aquifer.
- ✅ **Safe yield = 0.7 × Test yield** (rule of thumb).
- ✅ **Specific capacity** = Discharge ÷ Drawdown; high specific capacity = good well.
- ✅ **Sanitary protection** has three zones: inner (0–15 m), intermediate (15–50 m), outer (50–100 m).
- ✅ **CPHEEO Manual** and **Jal Jeevan Mission** are the reference standards for rural water supply design.
- ✅ **Jal Jeevan Mission norm:** **55 lpcd** per person, with **daily water-quality surveillance**.
- ✅ **Slow Sand Filter** is the most appropriate primary treatment for spring-fed rural systems.
- ✅ **V-notch weir** is the standard discharge measurement device for springs and small streams.
- ✅ **HDPE PE-100 pipes (PN 8)** are preferred for rural distribution in Kerala.
- ✅ Always state the **units, conversions, and assumptions** clearly in numericals.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, structure the answer as **Site Selection → Design (with table) → Construction (with diagram/description) → Hydraulic Analysis (with formula derivation) → Protection and O&M.** Boards allocate 7+7 marks clearly along this pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identifying and Protecting Water Sources: Surface Water, Groundwater, and Rainwater
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Identifying and Protecting Water Sources: Surface Water, Groundwater, and Rainwater

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Water Source Identification and Protection

In the context of **Rural Water Supply and Onsite Sanitation Systems (PECET867)**, **water source identification** refers to the systematic process of locating, assessing, and characterizing natural or constructed water sources that can serve as reliable supplies for rural communities. **Source protection** encompasses the engineering, institutional, and community-based measures implemented to prevent the contamination and overexploitation of these identified sources, thereby ensuring their long-term sustainability and potability.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** terminology, water sources for rural water supply are broadly classified into three primary categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The three primary source categories recognized by the **Ministry of Jal Shakti (Government of India)** and aligned with KTU 2024 syllabus are:
> 1. **Surface Water Sources** – Rivers, streams, lakes, ponds, reservoirs, and impounded water bodies.
> 2. **Groundwater Sources** – Wells (dug, bore, tube), springs, and aquifer systems.
> 3. **Rainwater** – Atmospheric precipitation harvested at the point of origin (rooftop) or catchment (watershed).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a rural village as a household kitchen. The chef (community) needs three reliable "pantry sources" to cook meals (sustain life):

- **The River Tap (Surface Water)** — Like a continuous, flowing kitchen tap connected to the municipal pipeline. It is abundant and easily accessible, but the water passes through many places (industries, farms) before reaching you, so it may carry impurities and requires heavy filtering (treatment).

- **The Underground Tank (Groundwater)** — Like a giant underground storage tank beneath the house. Water seeps into the soil naturally and gets filtered through layers of sand and gravel (geological filtration). It is naturally cleaner but harder to access (you need a pump or a well) and can run dry if overused.

- **The Sky Collector (Rainwater)** — Like placing buckets outside during a rainstorm. You collect water directly from the source (clouds) before it touches the ground. It is the purest form of freshwater but is **seasonal** and unpredictable, requiring storage tanks for dry periods.

The chef must **identify** which of these sources are available in the village, **assess** their quality and yield, and then **protect** them (lock the tank, filter the tap, cover the buckets) so that the family always has clean, safe water.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metrics for Source Assessment (as per CPHEEO Manual on Water Supply, India):**
> - **Per Capita Water Demand (Rural):** **40 to 135 litres per capita per day (lpcd)** depending on climate and livestock.
> - **Minimum Yield Threshold (Source):** **1.5 to 2.0 times** the average daily demand to ensure reliability during lean periods.
> - **Specific Yield of Aquifer:** Ratio of water drained by gravity to total volume of aquifer material (dimensionless, typically **0.01 to 0.30**).
> - **Standard Rainfall Measurement:** **1 mm of rain = 1 litre of water per square metre (m²)** of catchment area.
> - **Minimum Sanitary Protection Radius (for wells):** **10 to 30 metres** (as per IS 10500 and WHO guidelines).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Surface Water Sources

### 2.1.1 Definition and Types

**Surface water** is water that collects on the ground or in a stream, river, lake, reservoir, or ocean. It is the most visible and traditionally the most exploited source of water in rural India.

**Types of Surface Water Sources:**

| Type | Description | Example | Typical Use in Rural Kerala |
|---|---|---|---|
| **River / Stream** | Continuously flowing water body in a defined channel | Pampa, Bharathapuzha, Periyar | Direct intake for water supply schemes |
| **Lake / Pond** | Natural or artificial standing water body | Vembanad Lake, local temple ponds | Community bathing, irrigation, water supply |
| **Reservoir** | Large impounded water body behind a dam | Idukki, Mullaperiyar | Major regional water supply |
| **Impounded Tank** | Small constructed bunded storage | Kalliasseri canal bunds | Local village supply |

### 2.1.2 Identification of Surface Water Sources

The identification process involves:

1. **Hydrographic Survey** – Mapping of rivers, streams, lakes, and ponds using topographical maps (Survey of India) and remote sensing (GIS).
2. **Catchment Area Delineation** – Determining the geographical area that contributes runoff to the water body.
3. **Yield Assessment** – Measuring the flow rate (discharge) of the source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discharge Measurement (Surface Water Yield):**
> The yield of a river or stream is the volume of water flowing per unit time, measured in **cubic metres per second (cumecs)** or **litres per second (L/s)**.

## 2.2 Groundwater Sources

### 2.2.1 Definition

**Groundwater** is the water present beneath the Earth's surface in soil pore spaces and in the fractures of rock formations. It is held in geological formations called **aquifers**.

### 2.2.2 Types of Aquifers

| Aquifer Type | Description | Example in Kerala |
|---|---|---|
| **Unconfined Aquifer** | Upper surface is at atmospheric pressure (water table) | Shallow laterite formations |
| **Confined Aquifer** | Sandwiched between two impermeable layers under pressure | Deep crystalline rock fractures |
| **Perched Aquifer** | Small localized water body above the main water table | Laterite cap regions |
| **Artesian Aquifer** | Confined aquifer where water rises naturally above the aquifer top | Sedimentary basins of Palakkad |

### 2.2.3 Types of Wells

- **Dug Well (Open Well)** – Large diameter (1–10 m), shallow depth (5–20 m), traditional in Kerala's laterite terrain.
- **Bore Well** – Small diameter (10–30 cm), deep (30–300 m), drilled using rig.
- **Tube Well** – A bore well fitted with a pipe and strainer.
- **Dug-Cum-Bore Well** – Combines a dug well with a borehole at the bottom for higher yield.

### 2.2.4 Springs

A **spring** is a natural discharge point where groundwater flows out at the surface due to the intersection of the water table with the ground surface.

**Types of Springs:**
- **Gravity Spring** – Water flows by gravity from unconfined aquifer.
- **Artesian Spring** – Water rises under pressure from a confined aquifer.
- **Tubular / Fissure Spring** – Water emerges from rock fractures.
- **Contact Spring** – Water flows out at the contact of permeable and impermeable strata.

## 2.3 Rainwater Harvesting (RWH)

### 2.3.1 Definition

**Rainwater harvesting** is the collection, conveyance, and storage of rainwater that falls on a catchment surface (rooftop, ground, or land surface) for later use. It is the most ancient and reliable source, especially in Kerala which receives **~3000 mm of annual rainfall** (one of the highest in India).

### 2.3.2 Components of a Rainwater Harvesting System

1. **Catchment Surface** – The roof or paved area on which rain falls.
2. **Gutters and Downpipes** – Conveyance system from catchment to storage.
3. **First-Flush Diverter** – Device to discard the first shower (which carries dust and debris).
4. **Filter** – Sand or mesh filter to remove particulates.
5. **Storage Tank** – Underground or above-ground cistern to store harvested water.
6. **Recharge Structure** – Recharge pit / well / trench to enhance groundwater.

## 2.4 Source Protection

### 2.4.1 Why Protect?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — The 3 Pillars of Source Protection (WHO Guidelines):**
> 1. **Prevention of Contamination** – Stop pollutants at the source.
> 2. **Preservation of Quantity** – Prevent over-extraction and ensure sustainability.
> 3. **Community Ownership** – Engage local stakeholders in safeguarding the source.

### 2.4.2 Protection Strategies by Source Type

| Source | Protection Measures |
|---|---|
| **Surface Water (River/Lake)** | Buffer zones, restriction of bathing/washing upstream, cattle-exclusion, riparian vegetation, bank stabilization |
| **Groundwater (Well/Spring)** | Sanitary seal, parapet wall, drainage apron, prohibition of latrines within **15 m** (as per CPHEEO) |
| **Rainwater** | First-flush diverter, mesh filters, covered storage, periodic tank cleaning |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Discharge (Stream Flow)** | $Q = A \times v$ | Discharge = Cross-sectional Area × Velocity | m³/s |
| **Annual Rainwater Yield** | $V = P \times A \times C_r$ | V = Volume harvested, P = Annual rainfall, A = Catchment area, $C_r$ = Runoff coefficient | Litres |
| **Runoff Coefficient (Rooftop)** | $C_r$ (table) | Typically **0.80 to 0.90** for tiled/metal roofs | dimensionless |
| **Specific Yield** | $S_y = \dfrac{V_w}{V_a}$ | Volume of water drained / Total aquifer volume | dimensionless |
| **Drawdown (Well)** | $\Delta s = s_1 - s_2$ | Difference between static and pumping water levels | metres (m) |
| **Per Capita Storage Requirement** | $S = D \times N \times t$ | D = per capita demand, N = population, t = storage period | Litres |
| **Cylindrical Tank Volume** | $V = \pi r^2 h$ | Volume of circular storage tank | m³ |
| **Rectangular Tank Volume** | $V = L \times B \times H$ | Volume of rectangular storage | m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** In the table above, the formula $V = P \times A \times C_r$ uses the multiplication symbol ($\times$) for clarity. In board examinations, use the asterisk (*) or implied multiplication.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Annual Rainwater Harvesting Volume

The most important KTU Module 2 derivation is the relationship between rainfall depth, catchment area, runoff coefficient, and harvested volume.

### 3.1.1 Starting Principle

Rainfall depth $P$ is measured in millimetres (mm), where **1 mm of rainfall = 1 litre of water falling on 1 m² of horizontal surface**.

Let us derive the harvested volume over a catchment area $A$ for annual rainfall $P$:

**Step 1:** Express rainfall depth in metres.

$$
P_{m} = \dfrac{P_{mm}}{1000}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the total volume of rain falling on the catchment area $A$ (in m²).

$$
V_{rain} = A \times P_{m} = A \times \dfrac{P_{mm}}{1000} \;\; \text{(m}^3\text{)}
$$

**Step 3:** Not all rainfall is collected. A portion is lost due to evaporation, spillage, absorption, and the first-flush. The **runoff coefficient** $C_r$ accounts for these losses.

$$
V_{harvested} = C_r \times V_{rain}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expression for $V_{rain}$.

$$
V_{harvested} = C_r \times A \times \dfrac{P_{mm}}{1000} \;\; \text{(m}^3\text{)}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to litres (1 m³ = 1000 litres).

$$
V_{harvested} = C_r \times A \times P_{mm} \;\; \text{(litres)}
$$

This is the final **KTU Module 2 Master Formula** for rainwater harvesting.

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A rural household in Thrissur has a rooftop area of **100 m²**. The annual rainfall in the region is **2800 mm**. The runoff coefficient for the tiled roof is **0.85**. Calculate the total volume of rainwater that can be harvested annually.

**Given:**
- Catchment area, $A = 100$ m²
- Annual rainfall, $P = 2800$ mm
- Runoff coefficient, $C_r = 0.85$

**Step 1:** Apply the master formula.

$$
V_{harvested} = C_r \times A \times P
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$
V_{harvested} = 0.85 \times 100 \times 2800
$$

**Step 3:** Perform the multiplication.

$$
V_{harvested} = 85 \times 2800
$$

$$
V_{harvested} = 238{,}000 \;\; \text{litres}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to cubic metres (optional).

$$
V_{harvested} = \dfrac{238{,}000}{1000} = 238 \;\; \text{m}^3
$$
