> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** A 100 m² rooftop in Thrissur can harvest **2,38,000 litres (238 m³)** of rainwater annually — sufficient to meet the basic drinking water needs of a family of 5 for nearly a full year (at 135 lpcd).

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Storage Tank Volume

**Step 1:** Identify the per capita water demand $D$ (in lpcd).
**Step 2:** Multiply by the number of persons $N$ to get the total daily demand.

$$
Q_{day} = D \times N
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by the number of dry days $t$ that must be covered by storage.

$$
S_{tank} = Q_{day} \times t = D \times N \times t
$$

**Numerical Example:**

A village of 500 people needs to design a community water storage tank. Demand = 40 lpcd. Critical dry period = 30 days.

$$
S_{tank} = 40 \times 500 \times 30 = 6{,}00{,}000 \;\; \text{litres}
$$

Convert to m³:

$$
S_{tank} = 600 \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

If the tank is rectangular with length = 10 m, breadth = 10 m:

$$
H = \dfrac{V}{L \times B} = \dfrac{600}{10 \times 10} = 6 \;\; \text{metres}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Source Comparison

| Parameter | Surface Water | Groundwater | Rainwater |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reliability** | Seasonal (low in summer) | Generally reliable if recharge is sustained | Seasonal (only during monsoon) |
| **Treatment Cost** | High (turbidity, microbial) | Low to moderate (usually iron, hardness) | Minimal (filtration only) |
| **Vulnerability to Contamination** | High (open to dumping, sewage) | Moderate (depends on aquifer depth) | Low (collects before contact with ground) |
| **Capital Cost** | High (intake works, treatment plant) | Moderate (well drilling, pump) | Low (gutters, tank) |
| **Yield Predictability** | Variable (depends on rainfall in catchment) | More predictable | Most predictable (per mm basis) |
| **Best Use in Kerala** | Large community schemes | Domestic + small community | Supplementary domestic + recharge |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Source Classification Flowchart (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Water Sources for Rural Supply] --> B[Surface Water]
    A --> C[Groundwater]
    A --> D[Rainwater]

    B --> B1[Rivers and Streams]
    B --> B2[Lakes and Ponds]
    B --> B3[Reservoirs and Impoundments]

    C --> C1[Aquifer Systems]
    C --> C2[Wells]
    C --> C3[Springs]

    C1 --> C1A[Unconfined Aquifer]
    C1 --> C1B[Confined Aquifer]
    C1 --> C1C[Artesian Aquifer]

    C2 --> C2A[Dug Well]
    C2 --> C2B[Bore Well]
    C2 --> C3A[Tube Well]
    C2 --> C3B[Dug Cum Bore Well]

    D --> D1[Rooftop Harvesting]
    D --> D2[Surface Runoff Harvesting]
    D --> D3[Groundwater Recharge]

    classDef sourceNode fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef surfaceNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef groundNode fill:#8B4513,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef rainNode fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff

    class A sourceNode
    class B,B1,B2,B3 surfaceNode
    class C,C1,C1A,C1B,C1C,C2,C2A,C2B,C3A,C3B groundNode
    class D,D1,D2,D3 rainNode
```

## 4.2 Rainwater Harvesting System — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Rain Cloud] -->|Precipitation| A2[Catchment Surface Rooftop]
    A2 --> A3[Gutters and Downpipes]
    A3 --> A4[First Flush Diverter]
    A4 --> A5[Sand and Mesh Filter]
    A5 --> A6[Storage Tank Cistern]
    A6 --> A7[Overflow to Recharge Pit]
    A6 --> A8[Domestic Use]

    classDef inputNode fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef processNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000

    class A1,A2 inputNode
    class A3,A4,A5,A6 processNode
    class A7,A8 outputNode
```

## 4.3 Well Sanitary Protection — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    B1[Parapet Wall 60 cm above ground] --> B2[Well Head]
    B2 --> B3[Sanitary Seal Concrete Slab]
    B3 --> B4[Well Shaft]
    B4 --> B5[Strainer Section]
    B5 --> B6[Aquifer Zone]

    B7[Drainage Apron 2 m radius] --> B8[Wastewater Diversion]
    B8 --> B9[Soak Pit 15 m away]

    B10[Sanitary Protection Zone 15 m] --> B11[No Latrines]
    B10 --> B12[No Cattle Yard]
    B10 --> B13[No Manure Pits]

    classDef protectionNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef structureNode fill:#D2B48C,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef zoneNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000

    class B1,B2,B3,B7,B8,B9 protectionNode
    class B4,B5,B6 structureNode
    class B10,B11,B12,B13 zoneNode
```

## 4.4 Source Protection Decision Matrix (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Source Type?} -->|Surface| P1[Buffer Zone + Riparian Vegetation + Cattle Exclusion]
    Q1 -->|Groundwater| P2[Well Seal + Drainage Apron + Sanitary Radius]
    Q1 -->|Rainwater| P3[First Flush + Mesh Filter + Covered Tank]

    P1 --> R1[Regular Water Quality Testing]
    P2 --> R1
    P3 --> R1

    R1 --> R2[Community Awareness and Training]
    R2 --> R3[Sustainable Source]

    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef actionNode fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef resultNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000

    class Q1 decisionNode
    class P1,P2,P3,R1,R2 actionNode
    class R3 resultNode
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List the three primary sources of water for rural water supply. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The three primary sources of water for rural water supply are:

**1. Surface Water (Rivers, Lakes, Ponds):**
- *Advantage:* Large quantity available; suitable for community-level schemes.
- *Disadvantage:* Highly prone to contamination; requires extensive treatment.

**2. Groundwater (Wells, Springs, Aquifers):**
- *Advantage:* Naturally filtered; less treatment required; reliable in many regions.
- *Disadvantage:* Slow recharge; over-extraction leads to depletion and quality deterioration.

**3. Rainwater (Rooftop Harvesting):**
- *Advantage:* Pure at source; minimal treatment; reduces runoff and recharges groundwater.
- *Disadvantage:* Highly seasonal; requires large storage capacity for dry periods.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is a sanitary protection zone around a well? State its significance and minimum recommended radius as per CPHEEO.**

**Model Answer:**

A **sanitary protection zone** is the area around a well or spring within which activities that may cause contamination (such as latrines, cattle yards, manure pits, garbage dumps) are strictly prohibited.

- **Significance:** It prevents pathogens, nitrates, and other pollutants from infiltrating into the well through the soil, thereby protecting the groundwater source from biological and chemical contamination.
- **Minimum Recommended Radius:** **15 metres** as per the **CPHEEO Manual on Water Supply and Treatment** for rural water supply in India. For deep bore wells, this may be reduced to 10 m based on hydrogeological conditions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **fail to convert rainfall from mm to m³** properly — remember 1 mm on 1 m² = 1 litre.
> - Many students **forget the runoff coefficient** and assume 100% collection, which is unrealistic.
> - In source protection questions, students must mention **both engineering measures AND community participation** to score full marks.
> - Drawing a **labelled diagram** (well protection / RWH system) carries **2 to 3 marks**; do not skip it.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the components of a household **rooftop rainwater harvesting system**. List any **five design considerations** for the storage tank. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rural school building has a rooftop area of **250 m²**. The average annual rainfall in the area is **2200 mm**. The runoff coefficient of the roof is **0.80**. Calculate the **total volume of rainwater that can be harvested annually**. If the school has 200 students and the per capita demand is 45 lpcd, determine whether the harvested water is sufficient to meet the annual drinking water requirement of the school. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**Components of a Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting System (with sketch):**

```
            [Rain Cloud]
                  |
                  v
     +---------------------------+
     |   CATCHMENT (Rooftop)     |
     +---------------------------+
                  |
                  v
     +---------------------------+
     |   GUTTERS (Conveyance)    |
     +---------------------------+
                  |
                  v
     +---------------------------+
     |  FIRST FLUSH DIVERTER     |
     +---------------------------+
                  |
                  v
     +---------------------------+
     |   FILTER (Mesh / Sand)    |
     +---------------------------+
                  |
                  v
     +---------------------------+
     |   STORAGE TANK (Cistern)  |
     +---------------------------+
                  |
                  v
     +---------------------------+
     |   OVERFLOW -> RECHARGE    |
     +---------------------------+
```

**Five Design Considerations for the Storage Tank:**

1. **Storage Capacity** – Based on annual rainfall, catchment area, runoff coefficient, and dry-period demand.
2. **Material** – RCC (Reinforced Cement Concrete), HDPE, or ferrocement — must be non-toxic and watertight.
3. **Location** – Above ground (easy access) or below ground (space-saving, cool water).
4. **Cover** – Must be airtight to prevent mosquito breeding, algae growth, and contamination.
5. **Inlet and Outlet** – Inlet above water level (to avoid turbulence), outlet with floating valve or tap; overflow pipe to recharge structure.
6. **Cleaning Access** – Manhole or inspection hatch at the top.
7. **Safety** – Fencing or lock to prevent unauthorised access.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Given:**
- Catchment area, $A = 250$ m²
- Annual rainfall, $P = 2200$ mm
- Runoff coefficient, $C_r = 0.80$
- Number of students, $N = 200$
- Per capita demand, $D = 45$ lpcd

**Step 1: Calculate harvested volume per year.**

$$
V_{harvested} = C_r \times A \times P
$$

$$
V_{harvested} = 0.80 \times 250 \times 2200
$$

$$
V_{harvested} = 200 \times 2200 = 4{,}40{,}000 \;\; \text{litres/year}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate annual drinking water demand of the school.**

$$
Q_{annual} = D \times N \times 365
$$

$$
Q_{annual} = 45 \times 200 \times 365
$$

$$
Q_{annual} = 9000 \times 365 = 32{,}85{,}000 \;\; \text{litres/year}
$$

**Step 3: Compare harvested volume with demand.**

$$
V_{harvested} = 4{,}40{,}000 \;\; \text{litres}
$$

$$
Q_{annual} = 32{,}85{,}000 \;\; \text{litres}
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion.**

Since $V_{harvested}$ (4,40,000 L) is **far less** than $Q_{annual}$ (32,85,000 L), the harvested rainwater is **NOT sufficient** to meet the entire annual drinking water requirement of the school.

$$
\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{4{,}40{,}000}{32{,}85{,}000} \times 100 = 13.4\%
$$

It can meet only about **13.4%** of the demand — sufficient for drinking only, not for all purposes.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Differentiate between **surface water and groundwater sources** for rural water supply. Mention any **six protection measures** for a rural well. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A village has a population of **800**. The local panchayat wants to design a **cylindrical RCC overhead tank** to store 3 days of water. Assuming per capita demand of **55 lpcd** and a tank diameter of **6 m**, calculate the **required height of the tank**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Difference between Surface Water and Groundwater:**

| Parameter | Surface Water | Groundwater |
|---|---|---|
| Location | On ground (rivers, lakes) | Below ground (aquifers) |
| Recharge | Direct rainfall and runoff | Infiltration through soil |
| Treatment | High (turbidity, microbes) | Low (often naturally filtered) |
| Microbial Quality | Frequently contaminated | Usually safe if well-protected |
| Capital Cost | High (intake, treatment plant) | Moderate (well, pump) |
| Seasonal Variation | High flow in monsoon, low in summer | More stable year-round |

**Six Protection Measures for a Rural Well:**

1. **Parapet Wall (60 cm high)** around the well head to prevent surface runoff from entering.
2. **Sanitary Seal** — A watertight concrete cover or slab to prevent debris and animals from contaminating the well.
3. **Drainage Apron** — A 2-metre wide sloped concrete apron around the well to direct spilled water away.
4. **Sanitary Protection Zone** — A minimum **15 m radius** around the well where no latrines, cattle sheds, or manure pits are permitted.
5. **Raised Curbing on Well Head** — Prevents backflow of contaminated water.
6. **Periodic Cleaning and Disinfection** — Chlorination once in 6 months to control microbial growth.
7. **Use of Bucket with Rope or Hand Pump** — Avoids direct dipping of contaminated vessels.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Given:**
- Population, $N = 800$
- Per capita demand, $D = 55$ lpcd
- Storage period, $t = 3$ days
- Tank diameter, $d = 6$ m → radius $r = 3$ m

**Step 1: Calculate total water requirement for 3 days.**

$$
S = D \times N \times t
$$

$$
S = 55 \times 800 \times 3
$$

$$
S = 55 \times 2400 = 1{,}32{,}000 \;\; \text{litres}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to cubic metres.**

$$
S = \dfrac{1{,}32{,}000}{1000} = 132 \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Apply cylindrical volume formula.**

$$
V = \pi r^2 h
$$

$$
132 = \pi \times (3)^2 \times h
$$

$$
132 = \pi \times 9 \times h
$$

$$
132 = 9\pi h
$$

**Step 4: Solve for h.**

$$
h = \dfrac{132}{9\pi}
$$

$$
h = \dfrac{132}{28.274}
$$

$$
h = 4.67 \;\; \text{metres} \;\; \approx 4.7 \;\; \text{m}
$$

**Answer:** The required height of the cylindrical overhead tank is approximately **4.7 m**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2**
> - The **three primary water sources** for rural supply are: **Surface, Ground, and Rainwater**.
> - **Surface water** = highest contamination risk, but highest yield; examples: rivers, lakes, ponds.
> - **Groundwater** is held in **aquifers**; extracted via **dug wells, bore wells, tube wells**, and **springs**.
> - **Aquifer types** to remember: *unconfined, confined, perched, artesian*.
> - **Rainwater Harvesting Master Formula:** $V = C_r \times A \times P$ (in litres when A is in m² and P is in mm).
> - **Runoff Coefficient ($C_r$)** for rooftops is typically **0.80 to 0.90**.
> - **1 mm of rainfall** delivers **1 litre/m²** — never forget this conversion.
> - **Sanitary protection zone** around a well: minimum **15 m radius** (CPHEEO).
> - **Parapet wall** height around well: **60 cm** above ground level.
> - **Drainage apron** width: **2 m minimum**, sloped away from the well.
> - **Source Protection** has three pillars: *Prevention, Preservation, Community Ownership* (WHO).
> - **Rooftop RWH components** (in order): Catchment → Gutters → First-Flush Diverter → Filter → Storage → Recharge.
> - **Cylindrical tank volume:** $V = \pi r^2 h$; **Rectangular:** $V = L \times B \times H$.
> - For KTU board exams, always **draw a labelled diagram** for source protection or RWH questions (carries 2-3 marks).
> - Always **state units** explicitly in numerical answers to avoid losing 0.5–1 mark.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Source Contamination and Protection Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET867 - Rural Water Supply and Onsite Sanit 5sanitation Systems > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Water Source Contamination and Protection Strategies # Water Source Contamination and Protection Strategies ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Water source contamination** refers to the introduction of physical, chemical, biological, or radiological agents into a water source (surface or groundwater) at concentrations sufficient to render the water unsuitable for its intended use (drinking, irrigation, livestock, etc.). **Source protection** encompasses the engineering, institutional, and managerial measures undertaken to prevent pollution at the origin, before water is abstracted, treated, or distributed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 2):** Students must distinguish between *contamination* (presence of an impurity) and *pollution* (contamination that causes actual harm). The course emphasizes the **"multiple-barrier concept"** — protection at source, treatment, and point-of-use — as endorsed by WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality (GDWQ). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of your water source as a **fresh glass of milk poured into a transparent jug**. Once contaminants (dust, flies, bacteria) enter the jug, removing them becomes exponentially more difficult and expensive. **The smartest approach is to keep the jug covered, keep flies away, and sterilize the surroundings — not to filter the milk later**. In rural water supply, the *jug cover* is **watershed protection**, *keeping flies away* is **sanitary inspection**, and *post-filtration* is conventional treatment. Prevention at source is **100 to 1000 times cheaper** than end-of-pipe treatment. ### 1.3 Core Reference Metrics (Standard Constants) > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Values Used Throughout This Module:** > - **WHO Permissible Limit for Fecal Coliforms in Drinking Water:** **0 CFU / 100 mL** (zero tolerance). > - **Acceptable Nitrate (NO₃⁻) in Drinking Water:** **≤ 45 mg/L** (as NO₃⁻) or **≤ 10 mg/L** as Nitrogen-N. > - **Minimum Free Chlorine Residual:** **≥ 0.2 mg/L** at the tap (rural piped systems), **≥ 0.5 mg/L** at the outlet of the treatment plant. > - **Minimum Safe Setback Distance (BIS / CPHEEO):** **15 m** for a well from a septic tank / soak pit; **30 m** from a leach pit / cesspool. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Water Sources Water sources for rural supply fall into two principal classes, each with distinct contamination vulnerabilities: | **Source Type** | **Examples** | **Vulnerability** | **Typical Contaminants** | |---|---|---|---| | **Surface Water** | Rivers, streams, ponds, lakes, reservoirs | **Very High** — open to atmosphere, runoff, fecal deposition | Turbidity, coliforms, organic matter, industrial chemicals | | **Groundwater (Shallow)** | Dug wells, shallow boreholes (< 30 m) | **Moderate to High** — proximity to latrines, agricultural fields | Nitrates, fecal coliforms, fluorides, arsenic, iron | | **Groundwater (Deep)** | Deep borewells (> 30 m), tube wells | **Low to Moderate** — protected by overlying aquitards | Fluoride, arsenic (geo-chemical), hardness, salinity | | **Rainwater** | Rooftop harvesting systems | **Low (storage-dependent)** — airborne dust, bird droppings, roof material leachates | Lead, cadmium, dust particulates, microbial films | > [!IMPORTANT] > In the Indian rural context, **open dug wells remain the most common source** but also the most vulnerable — typically failing bacteriological tests due to inadequate apron, parapet, and drainage provisions. ## 2.2 Classification of Contaminants The following taxonomy is **mandatory for KTU ESE questions**: ### 2.2.1 Physical Contaminants - Suspended solids (turbidity, silt, clay) - Colour, odour, temperature - Radioactive particles (Radon-222, Radium-226 — geogenic) ### 2.2.2 Chemical Contaminants - **Inorganic:** Fluoride (F⁻ > 1.5 mg/L), Arsenic (As > 0.01 mg/L), Nitrate (NO₃⁻ > 45 mg/L), Lead (Pb > 0.01 mg/L), Iron (Fe > 0.3 mg/L), Heavy metals. - **Organic:** Pesticides (organochlorines, organophosphates), herbicides, hydrocarbons, VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds), humic substances. - **Disinfection By-Products (DBPs):** Trihalomethanes (THMs), haloacetic acids. ### 2.2.3 Biological Contaminants (Most Critical for Rural Areas) - **Bacteria:** *E. coli*, *Vibrio cholerae*, *Salmonella typhi*, *Shigella*. - **Viruses:** Rotavirus, Hepatitis A & E, Poliovirus, Norovirus. - **Protozoa:** *Giardia lamblia*, *Entamoeba histolytica*, *Cryptosporidium parvum*. - **Helminths:** *Ascaris lumbricoides* (roundworm), hookworm, *Taenia*. ### 2.2.4 Radiological Contaminants - Naturally Occurring Radioactive Materials (NORM) — Uranium, Radium, Radon. ## 2.3 The Fecal-Oral Transmission Pathway (Conceptual Foundation) > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Waterborne disease transmission is best understood through the **"F-Diagram"** developed by Wagner and Lanoix (1958). The five F-words are: > > 1. **Fluids** (water) > 2. **Fields** (soil/crops) > 3. **Fingers** (hands) > 4. **Flies** (insects) > 5. **Food** The **F-diagram** illustrates how pathogens from **faeces** spread through these five routes to a new host. **Interventions** (arrows blocking the Fs) include: - **Sanitation** blocks the source. - **Water treatment** blocks the *fluids* route. - **Handwashing** blocks the *fingers* route. - **Food hygiene** blocks the *food* route. - **Fly control** blocks the *flies* route. ## 2.4 The Multiple Barrier Approach (WHO Framework) The WHO promotes a **layered defense** to ensure water safety: 1. **Barrier 1 — Source Protection:** Watershed management, wellhead protection, control of upstream activities. 2. **Barrier 2 — Treatment:** Coagulation-flocculation, sedimentation, filtration, disinfection. 3. **Barrier 3 — Distribution & Storage:** Maintaining residual disinfectant, preventing recontamination in pipes/tanks. 4. **Barrier 4 — Point-of-Use (POU):** Household filters, boiling, SODIS (Solar Disinfection). > [!IMPORTANT] > A failure in **one barrier** is compensated by the next, giving system robustness — the cornerstone of **Water Safety Plans (WSPs)**. ## 2.5 Source Protection Strategies (Engineering & Institutional) ### 2.5.1 For Surface Water Sources - **Watershed delineation** and notification as a *Protected Area*. - **Restriction of activities:** No cattle washing, no defecation, no chemical spraying in the catchment. - **Construction of diversion channels** to divert surface runoff around the reservoir/intake. - **Riparian buffer zones** (minimum **30 m wide** for major rivers) of grass/forest cover. - **Fencing and signage** to enforce access control. ### 2.5.2 For Groundwater Sources (Wells) - **Wellhead protection zone (WHPZ):** Computed using **time-of-travel** criteria (typically **50-day** and **10-year** isochrones). - **Sanitary completion:** Concrete apron (**min 1.2 m wide** sloping outward), raised parapet (**≥ 0.6 m** above GL), drainage channel. - **Elimination of nearby latrines** or relocation of latrines **down-gradient**. - **Use of sanitary seal** in borewells (cement grout from GL to **≥ 3 m** depth). ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following equations are tested in KTU ESE numericals. Memorize the symbols, units, and boundary conditions.** | **#** | **Formula / Equation** | **Application** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Darcy's Law:** $v = K \cdot i$ | Seepage velocity in aquifer | m/day | | 2 | **Actual (Linear) Velocity:** $v_a = \dfrac{K \cdot i}{n_e}$ | True velocity of groundwater | m/day | | 3 | **Time of Travel (Setback):** $t = \dfrac{L}{v_a}$ | Minimum safe distance for contaminant migration | days | | 4 | **Setback Distance:** $L = v_a \cdot t$ | Wellhead protection radius | metres | | 5 | **Mass Balance (Decay):** $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$ | First-order die-off of pathogens (Chick's Law) | mg/L or CFU | | 6 | **Hydraulic Conductivity (Hazen):** $K = C \cdot (D_{10})^2$ | Approximation for sand filters | m/s | | 7 | **MPN Index:** Tabulated from multiple-tube fermentation | Bacteriological quality assessment | MPN/100 mL | | 8 | **Dilution Factor (Reaeration):** $D = \dfrac{C_s - C_0}{C_s - C_t}$ | Self-purification capacity of rivers | Dimensionless | > **Where:** $K$ = hydraulic conductivity, $i$ = hydraulic gradient, $n_e$ = effective porosity, $L$ = travel distance, $t$ = time, $k$ = decay constant (day⁻¹), $C_0$ = initial concentration, $C_t$ = concentration after time $t$, $D_{10}$ = effective grain size (mm), $C$ = Hazen's constant (**0.4 to 1.2**, typical **1.0**). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > The following worked derivations are **direct KTU 2024 Scheme ESE-style numericals**. Each step is shown exhaustively to meet examiner expectations of full working. --- ## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Minimum Safe Setback Distance for a Dug Well ### 3.1.1 Problem Statement A rural dug well is located in sandy soil. The local hydrogeological investigation yields the following data: - Hydraulic conductivity, $K = 15$ m/day - Hydraulic gradient, $i = 0.005$ (dimensionless) - Effective porosity, $n_e = 0.30$ - Regulatory minimum travel time for a pathogen to reach the well: $t = 50$ days (WHO 50-day isochrone criterion for viral/bacterial protection) **Required:** Compute the **minimum safe setback distance** $L$ between a pollution source (e.g., a septic tank) and the well, so that any pathogen released at the source takes **at least 50 days** to traverse the distance — sufficient time for natural die-off. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Apply Darcy's Law to compute the discharge (seepage) velocity.** The fundamental relationship governing groundwater flow through a porous medium is Darcy's Law: $$ \begin{aligned} v &= K \cdot i \end{aligned} $$ Substituting the given values: $$ \begin{aligned} v &= (15 \text{ m/day}) \times (0.005) \\ v &= 0.075 \text{ m/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** This is the *bulk* velocity (also called the *Darcy flux* or *discharge velocity*) — i.e., the volumetric flow rate per unit cross-sectional area of the aquifer, including the solid grains. --- **Step 2: Correct for effective porosity to obtain the *actual* (linear / pore) velocity.** Because groundwater flows only through the *void spaces* between grains, the actual velocity a contaminant experiences is higher than the Darcy velocity by a factor of $1 / n_e$: $$ \begin{aligned} v_a &= \frac{v}{n_e} = \frac{K \cdot i}{n_e} \end{aligned} $$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} v_a &= \frac{0.075 \text{ m/day}}{0.30} \\ v_a &= 0.25 \text{ m/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** A bacterium migrating through the voids travels at **0.25 m/day** — about **3.33 times faster** than the bulk water flux. --- **Step 3: Apply the time-of-travel equation to find the minimum safe distance.** A conservative design assumes a pathogen travels at the actual pore velocity for the required regulatory travel time: $$ \begin{aligned} L &= v_a \cdot t \end{aligned} $$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} L &= (0.25 \text{ m/day}) \times (50 \text{ days}) \\ L &= 12.5 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Step 4: Apply the engineering safety factor.** Regulatory codes (CPHEEO Manual, IS 10500 guidance) mandate that computed values be **rounded up** and **supplemented by an additional safety margin** to account for uncertainties in $K$, $i$, and $n_e$. A standard multiplier of **1.2** is used: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{\text{safe}} &= 1.2 \times L \\ L_{\text{safe}} &= 1.2 \times 12.5 \\ L_{\text{safe}} &= 15.0 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Result: Minimum safe setback distance = 15 m.** This matches the BIS/CPHEEO recommendation. The septic tank must be located **at least 15 m down-gradient** of the well. --- ## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Pathogen Decay Using Chick's Law ### 3.2.1 Problem Statement A rural river receives a slug discharge of *E. coli* from cattle wading. The initial concentration is $C_0 = 1.0 \times 10^6$ CFU/100 mL. The die-off constant for *E. coli* in fresh water under sunlight is $k = 0.40 \text{ day}^{-1}$. **Required:** Estimate the concentration after (a) 5 days, and (b) 10 days. Comment on whether the water is safe for drinking (WHO limit: 0 CFU/100 mL). ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: State Chick's Law (first-order decay kinetics).** $$ \begin{aligned} C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t} \end{aligned} $$ This assumes a homogeneous water body, constant temperature, and exponential mortality. --- **Step 2 (a): Concentration after 5 days.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_5 &= (1.0 \times 10^6) \cdot e^{-(0.40)(5)} \\ C_5 &= (1.0 \times 10^6) \cdot e^{-2.0} \end{aligned} $$ We know that $e^{-2.0} = 0.1353$. Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} C_5 &= (1.0 \times 10^6) \times 0.1353 \\ C_5 &= 1.353 \times 10^5 \text{ CFU/100 mL} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Step 3 (b): Concentration after 10 days.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{10} &= (1.0 \times 10^6) \cdot e^{-(0.40)(10)} \\ C_{10} &= (1.0 \times 10^6) \cdot e^{-4.0} \end{aligned} $$ We know that $e^{-4.0} = 0.0183$. Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} C_{10} &= (1.0 \times 10^6) \times 0.0183 \\ C_{10} &= 1.83 \times 10^4 \text{ CFU/100 mL} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Step 4: Interpret the result.** After 10 days, the *E. coli* count is still $1.83 \times 10^4$ CFU/100 mL — vastly exceeding the WHO permissible limit of 0. Therefore, **natural die-off alone is insufficient**; the water **must not be consumed without disinfection** (chlorination, boiling, or SODIS). This demonstrates the rationale for the **multiple barrier approach**. --- ## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Hazen's Approximation for Hydraulic Conductivity ### 3.3.1 Problem Statement A slow-sand filter is to be designed using local river sand. Sieve analysis yields an effective grain size $D_{10} = 0.20$ mm. Use Hazen's constant $C = 1.0$. **Required:** Estimate the hydraulic conductivity of the filter bed. ### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Recall Hazen's empirical formula (valid for filter sands):** $$ \begin{aligned} K = C \cdot (D_{10})^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Convert $D_{10}$ to centimetres** (since $K$ in cm/s requires $D_{10}$ in cm): $$ \begin{aligned} D_{10} &= 0.20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ cm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Substitute and compute:** $$ \begin{aligned} K &= 1.0 \times (0.020)^2 \\ K &= 1.0 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \\ K &= 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm/s} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Step 4: Convert to m/day** (more useful for filter loading calculations): $$ \begin{aligned} K &= 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm/s} \times \frac{1 \text{ m}}{100 \text{ cm}} \times \frac{86400 \text{ s}}{1 \text{ day}} \\ K &= 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 864 \text{ m/day} \\ K &= 0.3456 \text{ m/day} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** $K \approx 0.35$ m/day. This is in the typical range for **fine to medium filter sand** (0.1 – 1.0 m/day) and confirms suitability for a slow-sand filter with filtration rates of 0.1 – 0.2 m/day. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Matching Protection Strategy to Source | **Source Type** | **Primary Threat** | **Recommended Protection Strategy** | **Treatment Required** | |---|---|---|---| | Dug well (open) | Fecal coliforms from nearby latrines | 15 m setback, concrete apron, parapet wall, drainage | Chlorination | | Shallow borewell | Nitrates from agricultural runoff | 30 m setback from fertilizer storage, sanitary seal | Ion exchange / RO | | Deep borewell | Fluoride, Arsenic (geogenic) | Source switching, deeper aquifer abstraction | Activated alumina / RO | | River (open access) | Turbidity, pathogens, organics | Riparian buffer, cattle exclusion, diversion channel | Full conventional treatment | | Pond / Tank | Algae, stagnation, vector breeding | Desiltation, weir maintenance, mosquito control | Filtration + chlorination | | Rooftop rainwater | Roof debris, first-flush contamination | First-flush diverter, mesh filters, covered storage | Chlorination (optional) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic 1 — The F-Diagram of Fecal-Oral Transmission (Block Functional Architecture) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following Mermaid diagram represents the **classical F-Diagram** showing the **faecal-oral transmission pathway** of waterborne diseases and the engineered *barriers* (interventions) that interrupt each pathway. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeF1["FAECES
Origin of Pathogens"]:::sourceNode nodeF2["FLUIDS
Contaminated Water"]:::routeNode nodeF3["FINGERS
Hands - Poor Hygiene"]:::routeNode nodeF4["FLIES
Insect Vectors"]:::routeNode nodeF5["FIELDS
Soil and Crops"]:::routeNode nodeF6["FOOD
Uncooked Vegetables"]:::routeNode nodeF7["NEW HOST
Human Ingestion"]:::hostNode nodeB1["BARRIER 1
Sanitation - Toilets"]:::barrierNode nodeB2["BARRIER 2
Water Treatment Plant"]:::barrierNode nodeB3["BARRIER 3
Handwashing Station"]:::barrierNode nodeB4["BARRIER 4
Insect Control - Nets"]:::barrierNode nodeB5["BARRIER 5
Food Hygiene - Washing"]:::barrierNode nodeB6["BARRIER 6
Point of Use Disinfection"]:::barrierNode nodeF1 --> nodeF2 nodeF1 --> nodeF3 nodeF1 --> nodeF4 nodeF1 --> nodeF5 nodeF1 --> nodeF6 nodeF2 --> nodeF7 nodeF3 --> nodeF7 nodeF4 --> nodeF7 nodeF5 --> nodeF7 nodeF6 --> nodeF7 nodeB1 -. Blocks .-> nodeF1 nodeB2 -. Blocks .-> nodeF2 nodeB3 -. Blocks .-> nodeF3 nodeB4 -. Blocks .-> nodeF4 nodeB5 -. Blocks .-> nodeF5 nodeB6 -. Blocks .-> nodeF7 classDef sourceNode fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF classDef routeNode fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef hostNode fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 classDef barrierNode fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF ``` --- ## 4.2 Schematic 2 — Multiple Barrier Approach for Rural Water Supply ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeS["SOURCE
Watershed / Aquifer"]:::sourceNode nodeB1["BARRIER 1
Source Protection
Watershed Mgmt"]:::barrier1 nodeB2["BARRIER 2
Treatment Plant
Coag Filter Chlorinate"]:::barrier2 nodeB3["BARRIER 3
Distribution
Residual Cl2 Maintain"]:::barrier3 nodeB4["BARRIER 4
Point of Use
Household Filter Boil"]:::barrier4 nodeC["CONSUMER
Safe Drinking Water"]:::consumer nodeS --> nodeB1 --> nodeB2 --> nodeB3 --> nodeB4 --> nodeC classDef sourceNode fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF classDef barrier1 fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef barrier2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef barrier3 fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef barrier4 fill:#9D4EDD,stroke:#4A0E78,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef consumer fill:#06D6A0,stroke:#065F46,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Schematic 3 — Sanitary Completion of a Dug Well (Block Architecture of Components) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeTop["Parapet Wall
Min 0.6 m above GL"]:::component nodeApron["Concrete Apron
Min 1.2 m wide
Slope outward"]:::component nodeDrain["Drainage Channel
Divert spilled water away"]:::component nodeLining["Well Lining
Brick or Stone Masonry
Watertight joints"]:::component nodeCover["Well Cover
Sanitary seal against vectors"]:::component nodePump["Hand Pump or
Bucket with Rope Guide"]:::component nodeSurround["Surrounding Area
No latrine within 15 m
No cattle washing within 30 m"]:::component nodeTop --- nodeCover nodeApron --- nodeTop nodeDrain --- nodeApron nodeLining --- nodeDrain nodePump --- nodeLining nodeSurround --- nodePump classDef component fill:#A8DADC,stroke:#1D3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Schematic 4 — Contamination Pathways into Groundwater (Cross-Sectional Block Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeAtm["ATMOSPHERIC INPUTS
Acid rain, dust fallout"]:::sourceAtm nodeAgri["AGRICULTURAL LANDS
Fertilizers and pesticides"]:::sourceAgri nodeLat["SANITATION
Septic tanks, latrines, soak pits"]:::sourceLat nodeInd["INDUSTRY
Effluents and sludge"]:::sourceInd nodeVad["VADOSE ZONE
Unsaturated soil - filtration and decay"]:::vadose nodeAqui["AQUIFER
Saturated zone - groundwater storage"]:::aquifer nodeWell["EXTRACTION WELL
Dug well or borewell"]:::well nodeCons["CONSUMER
Contaminated water supply"]:::consumer nodeAtm --> nodeVad nodeAgri --> nodeVad nodeLat --> nodeVad nodeInd --> nodeVad nodeVad --> nodeAqui nodeAqui --> nodeWell nodeWell --> nodeCons classDef sourceAtm fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#2F4F4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef sourceAgri fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef sourceLat fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef sourceInd fill:#808080,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef vadose fill:#DEB887,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef aquifer fill:#4682B4,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF classDef well fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef consumer fill:#06D6A0,stroke:#065F46,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.5 Schematic 5 — Sanitary Survey Decision Flow (Block Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart["START
Identify Water Source in Village"]:::start nodeInspect["Conduct Walk-Over
Sanitary Inspection of Source"]:::process nodeCheck1{"Are there latrines
within 15 m?"}:::decision nodeCheck2{"Is the well covered
and sealed?"}:::decision nodeCheck3{"Is there standing
water near the source?"}:::decision nodeCheck4{"Is agricultural activity
upgradient?"}:::decision nodeAction1["RELOCATE LATRINE
OR SEAL WELL"]:::action nodeAction2["INSTALL SANITARY
COVER AND APRON"]:::action nodeAction3["PROVIDE DRAINAGE
AND FILL DEPRESSIONS"]:::action nodeAction4["CREATE BUFFER ZONE
30 m WIDE"]:::action nodeTest["Test Water Quality
Bacteriological and Chemical"]:::process nodeSafe{"Within WHO
Limits?"}:::decision nodeDeliver["SAFE
Water supply approved"]:::success nodeTreat["TREATMENT REQUIRED
Chlorinate or filter"]:::action nodeStart --> nodeInspect nodeInspect --> nodeCheck1 nodeInspect --> nodeCheck2 nodeInspect --> nodeCheck3 nodeInspect --> nodeCheck4 nodeCheck1 -- YES --> nodeAction1 nodeCheck1 -- NO --> nodeCheck2 nodeCheck2 -- NO --> nodeAction2 nodeCheck2 -- YES --> nodeCheck3 nodeCheck3 -- YES --> nodeAction3 nodeCheck3 -- NO --> nodeCheck4 nodeCheck4 -- YES --> nodeAction4 nodeCheck4 -- NO --> nodeTest nodeAction1 --> nodeTest nodeAction2 --> nodeTest nodeAction3 --> nodeTest nodeAction4 --> nodeTest nodeTest --> nodeSafe nodeSafe -- YES --> nodeDeliver nodeSafe -- NO --> nodeTreat classDef start fill:#06D6A0,stroke:#065F46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef process fill:#A8DADC,stroke:#1D3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef decision fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef action fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef success fill:#06D6A0,stroke:#065F46,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the term "Water Source Protection." Differentiate between contamination and pollution with one example each.** #### Model Answer: **Water Source Protection** is the process of safeguarding water at its origin (surface or groundwater body) by implementing engineering, institutional, and managerial measures to prevent the introduction of contaminants. | **Aspect** | **Contamination** | **Pollution** | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Presence of an undesirable substance in water | Contamination that causes actual harm to users or the ecosystem | | **Example** | Trace iron in groundwater (2 mg/L) | Iron at 2 mg/L staining clothes and unpalatable — *causes harm* | | **Example 2** | Nitrates from agriculture | Nitrates > 45 mg/L causing methemoglobinemia in infants — *causes harm* | > All pollution is contamination, but **not all contamination is pollution**. **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** - Definition of source protection — **1 mark** - Difference table — **1.5 marks** - Example pair — **0.5 mark** --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Explain the "Multiple Barrier Approach" for ensuring safe drinking water in rural areas, listing any four barriers.** #### Model Answer: The **Multiple Barrier Approach** is a WHO-promoted framework where successive protective layers ensure water safety, so that a failure in one barrier is compensated by the next. **The four mandatory barriers are:** 1. **Source Protection:** Watershed management, wellhead protection, cattle exclusion — keeps the water clean at the origin. 2. **Treatment:** Coagulation, filtration, and disinfection remove remaining impurities. 3. **Safe Distribution & Storage:** Maintains a chlorine residual in pipes and prevents recontamination. 4. **Point-of-Use Treatment:** Household boiling, SODIS, or ceramic filters — final safety net. > [!NOTE] > A real-world example is the **Slow Sand Filter + Residual Chlorination** combination used in Kerala's rural piped water schemes. **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** - Concept of layered defense — **1 mark** - Listing of any 4 barriers with brief description — **2 marks (0.5 each)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- ### Question A: **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Describe the **F-diagram** in detail, explaining how it models the transmission of waterborne diseases. Discuss **any five intervention barriers** that break the transmission routes in a rural setting. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** A rural well is located in a sandy aquifer with the following properties: - Hydraulic conductivity, $K = 8$ m/day - Hydraulic gradient, $i = 0.008$ - Effective porosity, $n_e = 0.25$ - Required minimum pathogen travel time = **50 days** Compute the **minimum safe setback distance** between the well and a proposed septic tank, applying a **safety factor of 1.25** to the theoretical distance. **\[7 marks\]** --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **Part (a) — F-Diagram Explanation (7 marks)** The **F-diagram** (Wagner & Lanoix, 1958) is a conceptual model showing the **faecal-oral transmission pathway** of enteric diseases. It identifies **five primary routes** through which pathogens from human or animal excreta reach a new host: 1. **Fluids (Water):** Consumption of contaminated drinking water — the dominant route in rural India. 2. **Fingers (Hands):** Poor hand hygiene after defecation transfers pathogens to the mouth. 3. **Flies:** Houseflies (*Musca domestica*) carry pathogens from faeces to food. 4. **Fields (Soil):** Open defecation contaminates soil; crops absorb pathogens. 5. **Food:** Unwashed vegetables and raw food transmit pathogens. **Five intervention barriers** that interrupt the F-diagram in rural settings: 1. **Sanitation Barrier:** Construction of household toilets (e.g., under Swachh Bharat Mission) eliminates open defecation at the source. 2. **Water Treatment Barrier:** Boiling, chlorination, and slow-sand filtration destroy pathogens in *fluids*. 3. **Handwashing Barrier:** Provision of soap and tippy-taps near latrines blocks the *fingers* route. 4. **Fly Control Barrier:** Insecticide-treated nets, mesh screens, and waste management block *flies*. 5. **Food Hygiene Barrier:** Washing vegetables with safe (chlorinated) water and cooking food above **70 °C** blocks the *food* route. > **The F-diagram emphasizes that the cheapest and most effective barrier is at the source — sanitation.** **Mark Split:** F-diagram explanation — **3 marks**, Five interventions — **4 marks (0.8 each)**. --- **Part (b) — Setback Distance Numerical (7 marks)** **Given:** $K = 8$ m/day, $i = 0.008$, $n_e = 0.25$, $t = 50$ days, Safety Factor (SF) = 1.25 **Step 1:** Compute Darcy velocity. $$ \begin{aligned} v &= K \cdot i \\ v &= 8 \times 0.008 \\ v &= 0.064 \text{ m/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Compute actual pore velocity. $$ \begin{aligned} v_a &= \frac{v}{n_e} = \frac{0.064}{0.25} \\ v_a &= 0.256 \text{ m/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Compute theoretical travel distance for 50 days. $$ \begin{aligned} L &= v_a \cdot t \\ L &= 0.256 \times 50 \\ L &= 12.8 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Apply safety factor. $$ \begin{aligned} L_{\text{safe}} &= \text{SF} \times L \\ L_{\text{safe}} &= 1.25 \times 12.8 \\ L_{\text{safe}} &= 16.0 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Answer: Minimum safe setback distance = 16 m.** The septic tank should be located **at least 16 m** from the well, in the **down-gradient direction**. **Mark Split:** Each computational step — **1.5 marks**, Final answer with units — **1 mark**. --- --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice): **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Discuss the **classification of water contaminants** under the four major categories (physical, chemical, biological, radiological). For each category, give **two examples** and their **WHO permissible limits** (where applicable). **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **sanitary completion of a rural dug well**. List **any five design specifications** for the apron, parapet, and surrounding drainage. **\[7 marks\]** --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **Part (a) — Classification of Contaminants (7 marks)** | **Category** | **Contaminant (Example 1)** | **Limit** | **Contaminant (Example 2)** | **Limit** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Physical** | Turbidity | ≤ **5 NTU** (WHO) | Total Suspended Solids (TSS) | ≤ **500 mg/L** (raw source) | | **Chemical – Inorganic** | Fluoride (F⁻) | ≤ **1.5 mg/L** | Arsenic (As) | ≤ **0.01 mg/L** | | **Chemical – Organic** | Pesticides (Lindane) | ≤ **2 µg/L** | Trihalomethanes (THMs) | ≤ **100 µg/L** | | **Biological** | *E. coli* (faecal coliforms) | **0 CFU / 100 mL** | *Coliphages* (viral indicator) | Absent | | **Radiological** | Radon-222 | ≤ **4 pCi/L** (EPA) | Gross Alpha activity | ≤ **0.5 Bq/L** | **Mark Split:** Naming 4 categories — **2 marks (0.5 each)**, Examples + limits — **5 marks (0.5 per cell × 10)**. --- **Part (b) — Sanitary Completion of a Dug Well (7 marks)** A well is **sanitarily complete** when its physical components prevent the entry of contaminants from the immediate surroundings into the well shaft. The standard design includes the following: **Diagram (Reference — see Schematic 3 in Section 4):** ``` PARAPET WALL (≥ 0.6 m above GL) ============================= CONCRETE APRON (≥ 1.2 m wide, slope 1:40 outward) ----------------------------------------- WELL COVER (watertight, lockable) ======================================== WELL LINING (brick/stone, watertight joints) | | | | GROUNDWATER (aquifer) | | ----- DRAINAGE CHANNEL around apron ======= GROUND LEVEL ---- SOIL STRATUM (vadose zone) ---------- AQUIFER ``` **Five Design Specifications:** 1. **Parapet Wall:** Minimum **0.6 m** above ground level to prevent surface wash from entering the well. Made of brick masonry plastered with cement mortar. 2. **Concrete Apron:** Minimum **1.2 m** wide around the well, made of M20 concrete, with a **slope of 1 in 40** outward to drain spilled water away. 3. **Drainage Channel:** A shallow channel of **15 cm × 15 cm** cross-section surrounding the apron, leading to a soak pit at least **15 m** away. 4. **Well Cover:** A watertight RCC or MS cover with a lockable access hatch to prevent vector entry and direct contamination. 5. **Sanitary Seal (for borewells):** Cement-bentonite grout from ground level to a minimum depth of **3 m** to prevent surface water from descending along the casing. > [!NOTE] > The well should be located **at least 15 m away** from any latrine/septic tank and **30 m away** from any cattle shed or fertilizer storage. **Mark Split:** Sketch/description of components — **3 marks**, Five specifications — **4 marks (0.8 each, deduct 0.8 per missing)**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE Papers:** > > 1. **Confusing Darcy velocity with actual velocity** — students often forget to divide by $n_e$. **Always state both** $v$ and $v_a$ explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting the safety factor** — CPHEEO requires a multiplier of **1.2 to 1.5** on the theoretical distance. Examiners allocate at least **1 mark** for the safety factor. > 3. **Unit errors in $K$ (cm/s vs m/day)** — a conversion mistake here cascades through the entire problem. Always convert before substituting. > 4. **Conflating "coliforms" and "E. coli"** — *total coliforms* is a broader indicator; *faecal coliforms / E. coli* is the WHO-recognized standard. Examiners deduct **0.5 marks** for the wrong indicator. > 5. **Skipping the direction specification** — the setback is *down-gradient*, not simply "away." Examiners specifically check for "down-gradient of the well" wording. > 6. **Not mentioning multiple barriers** — for any "safe water" question, the model answer must include **all four barriers** (source, treatment, distribution, POU). Partial answers lose 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Source protection is cheaper than treatment** — by a factor of 100 to 1000. This is the central message of the module. - **Classification of water sources:** Surface water (rivers, ponds) and Groundwater (shallow & deep). - **Contaminant categories:** Physical, Chemical (organic + inorganic), Biological, Radiological. - **F-Diagram:** Fluids, Fingers, Flies, Fields, Food — five routes of faecal-oral transmission. - **Multiple Barrier Approach:** Source protection → Treatment → Distribution → Point of use. - **WHO Limits (must memorize):** - **Fecal coliforms:** **0 CFU/100 mL** - **Fluoride:** **≤ 1.5 mg/L** - **Nitrate (as N):** **≤ 10 mg/L** - **Arsenic:** **≤ 0.01 mg/L** - **Residual chlorine:** **≥ 0.2 mg/L** at tap - **Key Equations:** - $v = K \cdot i$ - $v_a = K \cdot i / n_e$ - $L = v_a \cdot t$ (with safety factor 1.2–1.5) - $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$ (Chick's Law) - $K = C \cdot (D_{10})^2$ (Hazen's approximation) - **Setback Distances (CPHEEO):** - **15 m** from septic tank / soak pit - **30 m** from cattle shed / leach pit / cesspool - **Sanitary Well Completion Components:** Parapet (≥ 0.6 m), Apron (≥ 1.2 m), Cover, Drainage channel, Sanitary seal. - **WHO 50-day isochrone** is the standard for viral/bacterial protection in groundwater. - **The F-diagram and Multiple Barrier Approach are the two most repeated concepts in KTU exams** — write at least one in any Module 2 question. - **"Watershed protection"** applies to surface sources; **"Wellhead protection"** applies to groundwater sources — do not interchange the terms. ---

topics.title: Water Treatment and Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - Rural Water Supply and Onsite Sanitation Systems
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Water Treatment and Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Water Treatment — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Treatment** is defined as a sequence of physical, chemical, and biological processes engineered to remove contaminants (suspended solids, organic matter, pathogenic microorganisms, and dissolved minerals) from raw surface or ground water so that the finished water conforms to the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS IS 10500:2012)** drinking water specifications before it is supplied through a distribution network to consumers.

In the context of **rural water supply**, the treatment train is intentionally simplified, robust, low-cost, and operationally simple so that it can be managed by trained village-level operators (jal sevaks / pump operators) without continuous electrical or chemical inputs. The typical rural treatment sequence is:

**Raw Water → Intake → Screening → Plain Sedimentation → Slow Sand Filter (SSF) / Rapid Sand Filter (RSF) → Disinfection (Chlorination) → Clear Water Reservoir (CWR) → Pump House → Rising Main → Service Reservoir → Distribution Network → Consumer Tapstand**

## 1.2 Water Distribution — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Distribution System** is the network of pipes, pumps, valves, storage tanks, and consumer service connections that conveys treated water from the treatment plant (or service reservoir) to individual households, institutions, and stand-posts at adequate **quantity, quality, pressure, and continuity** to meet the design demands of the served population.

For rural India, the **Service Level Benchmark (SLB)** prescribed by the **Ministry of Jal Shakti** under the **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** mandates **55 litres per capita per day (lpcd)** of potable water through a household tap connection, with a minimum residual pressure of **0.7 m to 1.0 m of head (≈ 7–10 kPa)** at the consumer end.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Filter on a City Scale"

Imagine the process of cleaning muddy river water at home using a cloth, sand, and charcoal. The cloth removes leaves (screening), letting the water stand still allows mud to settle (sedimentation), passing it through fine sand catches the remaining particles (filtration), and finally, a small amount of chlorine or boiling kills germs (disinfection). 

A **Water Treatment Plant (WTP)** is simply an industrialised, scientifically engineered version of this same kitchen process, scaled up to serve thousands of households. 

Similarly, think of the **distribution network** as the **plumbing inside a giant apartment building** — a large overhead tank (service reservoir) feeds water downward through vertical and horizontal pipes (mains and sub-mains), and small branch pipes (laterals) deliver water to each flat (household connection). The pump is the heart, the pipes are the arteries, and the valves act as taps that control flow.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (BIS / CPHEEO Norms)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Design Standards to Remember for KTU:**
> - **Per Capita Supply (Rural):** 40 lpcd (minimum) — **55 lpcd** under JJM
> - **Per Capita Supply (Urban fringe):** 70 – 100 lpcd
> - **Maximum Daily Demand Factor:** **1.8** (peak summer)
> - **Maximum Hourly Demand Factor:** **2.7** (peak hour)
> - **Residual Free Chlorine:** **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L** after 30 minutes contact
> - **Turbidity of Treated Water:** **< 1 NTU** (ideally **< 0.5 NTU**)
> - **Design Period:** **30 years** for civil works, **15 years** for mechanical/electrical
> - **Distribution Pressure:** **12 m to 70 m** head (1.2 bar to 7 bar)

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

This is a **high-weightage topic** in Module 2 because it directly links the source (covered in Module 1) to the consumer tap. Questions frequently appear on:
- Design of sedimentation tanks (detention time, surface overflow rate)
- Slow Sand Filter (SSF) design — the workhorse of rural India
- Chlorine dose calculations
- Hydraulic design of distribution networks (Hazen-Williams / Darcy-Weisbach)
- Storage capacity of service reservoirs
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Unit Operations of a Rural Water Treatment Plant

The treatment train is built on the principle of **progressive refinement** — each stage removes a specific class of impurities before passing the water to the next.

### Stage 1: Screening (Bar Rack)
- Removes **floating debris** (leaves, rags, plastic, fish, branches).
- Coarse screens: **25 mm – 50 mm** clear opening
- Fine screens: **6 mm – 12 mm** clear opening
- Approach velocity restricted to **0.6 m/s to 0.9 m/s** to prevent damage and to avoid pulling debris *into* the screen.

### Stage 2: Plain Sedimentation (Primary Clarifier)
- Removes **settleable suspended solids** by gravity.
- Water is held in a quiescent tank; particles denser than water fall to the bottom (sludge).
- Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) = **12 – 18 m³/m²/day** for plain sedimentation in rural plants.
- Detention time (τ) = **4 to 8 hours** for plain settling.

### Stage 3: Filtration (Slow Sand Filter — Most Common in Rural India)
- Removes **fine suspended particles, bacteria, and a portion of organic matter** biologically.
- The **schmutzdecke** (German: "dirty layer") — a slimy biological mat that forms on top of the sand — is the actual filter medium; it biologically oxidises organic matter and traps microorganisms.
- Filtration rate: **0.1 to 0.2 m³/m²/hour** (≈ **100 – 200 litres/m²/hour**).
- Effective sand size (d₁₀): **0.15 mm to 0.30 mm**
- Uniformity coefficient (Cu = d₆₀/d₁₀): **1.5 to 3.0**
- Sand bed depth: **0.5 m to 1.0 m**
- Gravel support: **0.2 m to 0.4 m** in graded layers
- Underdrain system: **perforated PVC laterals** or brick channels
- Cleaning: **scraping off 1–2 cm of schmutzdecke** every 2–3 months.

### Stage 4: Disinfection (Chlorination)
- Kills or inactivates **pathogenic bacteria, viruses, and protozoan cysts**.
- Most common agent: **Calcium Hypochlorite (Ca(OCl)₂)** in tablet/bleaching powder form for rural plants; **liquid chlorine gas (Cl₂)** for larger plants.
- Contact period: **minimum 30 minutes** at peak flow.
- Dose: **0.5 to 1.0 mg/L** of free chlorine for clear water; up to **2.0 mg/L** for turbid waters.

### Stage 5: Clear Water Sump / Service Reservoir
- Provides **operational storage** and **buffering** between intermittent pump operation and variable demand.
- Volume = **15% to 25% of total daily demand** (about 4–6 hours of average supply).

## 2.2 Water Distribution Network — Configuration Types

| Network Type | Description | Use in Rural India |
|---|---|---|
| **Dead-End (Tree) System** | Single main with branched sub-mains and laterals; flow stops at endpoints. | **Most common in villages** — low cost, simple, but has stagnation at dead ends. |
| **Grid (Looped) System** | Interconnected loops; water can reach any point from multiple directions. | **Costly** — used only in large towns or OHT zones. |
| **Ring Main System** | A closed primary main with radial branches. | Used in peri-urban and municipal towns. |

## 2.3 Pipe Materials in Rural Distribution

| Material | Class | Lifespan | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **uPVC (Unplasticised PVC)** | 6 kg/cm² – 10 kg/cm² | **50+ years** | **Most common in JJM works**; lightweight, corrosion-free. |
| **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene)** | PE 80 / PE 100 | **50+ years** | Flexible, jointed by butt-fusion or electrofusion. |
| **DI (Ductile Iron)** | K7 / K9 | **80–100 years** | High pressure mains. |
| **GI (Galvanised Iron)** | Medium / Heavy | **20–30 years** | Service connections, exposed plumbing. |
| **AC (Asbestos Cement)** | Class 5, 10, 15 | **30–40 years** | Being phased out due to health concerns. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Print this. It is your exam lifeline.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Detention Time | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | $\tau$ = hours, $V$ = tank volume (m³), $Q$ = flow (m³/hr) | Sedimentation tank design |
| 2 | Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) | $SOR = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$ | $Q$ = m³/day, $A_s$ = surface area (m²) | Settling tank area |
| 3 | Population Forecast (Arithmetic Increase) | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | $P_n$ = future pop., $P_0$ = present, $\bar{x}$ = decadal increase | Design population |
| 4 | Population Forecast (Geometric) | $P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n$ | $r$ = growth rate (% per decade) | Fast-growing rural areas |
| 5 | Population Forecast (Incremental Increase) | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{y}$ | $\bar{y}$ = avg. incremental increase | **CPHEEO recommended** for towns |
| 6 | Hazen-Williams Head Loss | $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot d^{4.87}}$ | $L$ = m, $Q$ = m³/s, $d$ = m, $C$ = roughness coeff. | **Most used for distribution** |
| 7 | Hazen-Williams Velocity | $V = 0.8492 \cdot C \cdot R_h^{0.63} \cdot S^{0.54}$ | $R_h$ = hydraulic radius, $S$ = slope | Pipe sizing |
| 8 | Darcy-Weisbach Head Loss | $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{d} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $f$ = friction factor (Moody) | High-pressure mains |
| 9 | Continuity Equation | $Q = A \cdot V$ | $A$ = area, $V$ = velocity | Fundamental |
| 10 | Chlorine Dose | $W = \dfrac{D \cdot Q}{C \cdot 10^3}$ | $W$ = kg/day, $D$ = dose (mg/L), $Q$ = m³/day, $C$ = % available Cl₂ | Disinfection |
| 11 | SSF Area | $A_{SSF} = \dfrac{Q_{peak}}{v_f}$ | $Q_{peak}$ = m³/hr, $v_f$ = filtration rate (m/hr) | Filter bed sizing |
| 12 | Filter Run Time | $T = \dfrac{H_{max}}{h_{max}/t}$ | $H_{max}$ = max head loss, $h_{max}$ = critical head | Filter operation |
| 13 | Storage Capacity | $V_{SR} = (15 \text{ to } 25)\% \times Q_{day}$ | $Q_{day}$ = total daily demand | Service reservoir |
| 14 | Peak Factor Method | $Q_{max} = P_f \times P_h \times Q_{avg}$ | $P_f$ = 1.8, $P_h$ = 2.7 | Distribution main sizing |
| 15 | BOD Removal Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | $S_0$, $S_e$ = influent/effluent BOD | Water quality |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The **Hazen-Williams equation** is the engineer’s *“spreadsheet-friendly”* tool — it works directly with flow rate without needing a Moody chart, which is why every rural water supply *Pakka Estimate* (Detailed Project Report) uses it.
- The **Slow Sand Filter (SSF)** is the *“self-healing heart”* of rural WTPs because it uses no chemicals, runs on gravity, and the biological schmutzdecke actively destroys pathogens — making it the **most sustainable technology** for villages without skilled manpower.
- The **Arithmetic, Geometric, and Incremental Increase** methods of population forecast are not just academic — they decide the **diameter of every pipe** and the **capacity of every tank** in the project. Underestimating growth leads to a plant that becomes obsolete in 10 years; overestimating wastes crores of public money.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read every step. KTU examiners award *process marks* — not just the final answer. Showing a clear derivation fetches 60–80% of the marks even if the final number has a small arithmetic slip.**

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Detention Time & Surface Overflow Rate of a Sedimentation Tank

**Problem:** A rural WTP treats 2.5 MLD (Million Litres per Day) of raw water. The plain sedimentation tank is rectangular, 12 m long × 5 m wide × 3.5 m deep (water depth 3.0 m). Compute the detention time and the surface overflow rate.

**Step 1 — Convert flow to m³/hour**

$$
Q = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day} = 2500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{2500}{24} = 104.17 \text{ m}^3/\text{hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute tank volume**

$$
V = L \times B \times H_{water} = 12 \times 5 \times 3.0 = 180 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Apply detention time formula**

$$
\tau = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{180}{104.17} = 1.728 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau \approx 1.73 \text{ hours}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Plain sedimentation detention time in **rural** plants is usually kept between **4 and 8 hours** to ensure proper floc settling. The 1.73 h result is on the *low side* — the student should **recommend increasing the tank length to ~24 m** in the design comment, which is exactly the kind of practical remark KTU examiners reward.

**Step 4 — Compute Surface Overflow Rate (SOR)**

$$
SOR = \frac{Q_{day}}{A_s} = \frac{2500}{12 \times 5} = \frac{2500}{60}
$$

$$
\boxed{SOR = 41.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2/\text{day}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to use *surface area* (L × B) only, **excluding depth**. Depth affects storage volume, not overflow rate. SOR is a *flux* (flow per unit area), so only the projected horizontal area counts.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Slow Sand Filter (SSF) Sizing

**Problem:** Design a Slow Sand Filter for a village of 5,000 population with a per-capita supply of 55 lpcd. Assume the SSF works 24 hours but a back-up (standby) unit is required. Filtration rate is 0.15 m³/m²/h. Two units (one working + one standby) are proposed.

**Step 1 — Compute average daily demand**

$$
Q_{day} = 5000 \times 55 = 2{,}75{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 275 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to hourly flow**

$$
Q_{hr} = \frac{275}{24} = 11.46 \text{ m}^3/\text{hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply peak factor for filter design (use 1.5–1.8 for rural SSF, not the full 2.7, because the CWR smoothens hourly peaks)**

$$
Q_{peak} = 1.5 \times 11.46 = 17.19 \text{ m}^3/\text{hour}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute required filter area (per working unit, 1 of 2 in service)**

$$
A_{SSF} = \frac{Q_{peak}}{v_f} = \frac{17.19}{0.15} = 114.6 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Adopt a practical L:B ratio of 2:1**

If $B = b$, then $L = 2b$, and $A = 2b^2$:

$$
2b^2 = 114.6 \;\Rightarrow\; b^2 = 57.3 \;\Rightarrow\; b \approx 7.57 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{L \approx 15.14 \text{ m}, \quad B \approx 7.57 \text{ m}}
$$

> Round to **L = 15.0 m, B = 7.5 m** in design.

**Step 6 — Compute sand depth (typical SSF):**

- Sand bed: **0.75 m**
- Gravel: **0.30 m** (4 graded layers)
- Freeboard: **0.30 m**
- Supernatant water depth: **1.0 – 1.5 m** (above sand)
- Total structural depth ≈ **0.75 + 0.30 + 1.0 + 0.30 = 2.35 m**

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Chlorine Dose Calculation

**Problem:** Calculate the daily chlorine requirement (in kg) for a WTP treating 2.5 MLD with a designed dose of 0.7 mg/L. Use **bleaching powder (Ca(OCl)₂)** containing **35% available chlorine**.

**Step 1 — Apply the dose formula**

$$
W = \frac{D \times Q}{C \times 10^3} \text{ kg/day}
$$

Where:
- $D = 0.7$ mg/L
- $Q = 2{,}500{,}000$ L/day
- $C = 35\% = 35$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$
W = \frac{0.7 \times 2{,}500{,}000}{35 \times 10^3} = \frac{1{,}750{,}000}{35{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{W = 50 \text{ kg/day of bleaching powder}}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-check using pure chlorine equivalent**

Pure chlorine needed = $\frac{0.7 \times 2{,}500{,}000}{10^6} = 1.75$ kg/day
Bleaching powder needed = $\frac{1.75}{0.35} = 5.0$ kg/day ❌

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL UNIT TRAP (Killers of 3–5 marks):** The factor $10^3$ in the denominator is because we are converting from mg to **grams** (since 1 mg = $10^{-3}$ g) and then to kg. If you mistakenly use $10^6$ you will be off by a factor of 1000. The correct unit-aware derivation is:
> 
> $$W \text{ (g)} = \frac{0.7 \text{ mg/L} \times 2{,}500{,}000 \text{ L/day}}{0.35} = 5{,}000{,}000 \text{ mg/day} = 5 \text{ kg/day of pure Cl}_2$$
> 
> Converting to 35% bleaching powder: $\frac{5}{0.35} = 14.29$ kg/day. **Always show unit conversions explicitly.**

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Hazen-Williams Pipe Sizing for Distribution Main

**Problem:** A pumping main of length 1,200 m carries 25 L/s from an OHT to a village. Pipe is uPVC, C = 140. Calculate the head loss using Hazen-Williams.

**Step 1 — Use SI Hazen-Williams equation**

$$
h_f = \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot d^{4.87}}
$$

Given:
- $L = 1200$ m
- $Q = 25$ L/s = **0.025 m³/s**
- $C = 140$ (uPVC)
- Assume $d = 0.15$ m (150 mm) — try first

**Step 2 — Compute each term**

$$
Q^{1.852} = (0.025)^{1.852}
$$

$$
\ln(0.025) = -3.6889 \;\Rightarrow\; 1.852 \times (-3.6889) = -6.832
$$

$$
Q^{1.852} = e^{-6.832} = 1.076 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
C^{1.852} = (140)^{1.852}
$$

$$
\ln(140) = 4.9416 \;\Rightarrow\; 1.852 \times 4.9416 = 9.152
$$

$$
C^{1.852} = e^{9.152} = 9{,}386
$$

$$
d^{4.87} = (0.15)^{4.87}
$$

$$
\ln(0.15) = -1.8971 \;\Rightarrow\; 4.87 \times (-1.8971) = -9.239
$$

$$
d^{4.87} = e^{-9.239} = 9.69 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$
h_f = \frac{10.67 \times 1200 \times 1.076 \times 10^{-3}}{9{,}386 \times 9.69 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
h_f = \frac{13.78}{0.9096} = 15.15 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_f \approx 15.15 \text{ m for } d = 150 \text{ mm pipe}}
$$

> Velocity check: $V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.025}{\pi (0.075)^2} = 1.415$ m/s — within the **0.6 – 1.8 m/s** recommended range for uPVC. **Pipe size is acceptable.**

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Incremental Increase Population Forecast (CPHEEO Method)

**Problem:** A village had population 4,200 in 2000, 5,100 in 2010, and 6,200 in 2020. Forecast the population in 2040 using the **Incremental Increase Method**.

**Step 1 — Compute decadal increases**

$$
\Delta_1 = 5100 - 4200 = 900
$$

$$
\Delta_2 = 6200 - 5100 = 1100
$$

**Step 2 — Compute incremental increase (change in increase)**

$$
\bar{y} = \Delta_2 - \Delta_1 = 1100 - 900 = 200 \text{ per decade}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute average decadal increase**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\Delta_1 + \Delta_2}{2} = \frac{900 + 1100}{2} = 1000
$$

**Step 4 — Apply formula for $n = 2$ decades (2020 → 2040)**

$$
P_n = P_0 + n \bar{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \bar{y}
$$

$$
P_{2040} = 6200 + 2(1000) + \frac{2 \times 3}{2}(200)
$$

$$
P_{2040} = 6200 + 2000 + 600
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{2040} = 8{,}800 \text{ persons}}
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Rural vs. Urban Treatment

| Parameter | Rural WTP | Urban WTP |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Bore well / open well | Surface reservoir (river/dam) |
| **Primary Filter** | Slow Sand Filter (SSF) | Rapid Sand Filter (RSF) |
| **Filtration Rate** | 0.1 – 0.2 m³/m²/h | 5 – 15 m³/m²/h |
| **Coagulation** | Often skipped | Always with alum/polyelectrolyte |
| **Disinfection** | Bleaching powder tablet | Liquid Cl₂ or chlorinator |
| **Power** | Solar / gravity / diesel pump | Electric grid |
| **Operation** | Village jal-sewak | Trained municipal engineer |
| **Distribution** | Stand-posts / HH tap | 100% metered HH connection |
| **Cost per kL** | ₹1 – ₹3 | ₹8 – ₹15 |
