# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 Pattern:**
> - **Part A** (Short Answer): 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - **Part B** (Descriptive, Internal Choice): 1 question × 14 marks (with sub-parts 7+7) = 14 marks
> - **Total Module Weightage in ESE:** 20 marks

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECET867]**
*Explain the principle and function of the **schmutzdecke** layer in a Slow Sand Filter (SSF). Why is it critical for rural water treatment?*

### Model Answer (3 Marks)
The **schmutzdecke** is a thin (1–2 cm), biologically active layer that forms naturally on the top surface of the sand bed in an SSF within 1–2 weeks of commissioning. It consists of algae, bacteria, protozoa, and fine organic debris trapped from the raw water. 

**Function:** 
1. It acts as a **biological filter**, oxidising dissolved organic matter and physically straining out suspended particles, bacteria, and cysts. 
2. It removes **>99% of pathogenic microorganisms** through predation and enzymatic breakdown. 

**Criticality for rural areas:** Because the schmutzdecke performs disinfection biologically, the SSF can deliver **safe water without continuous chemical dosing**, making it ideal for villages with intermittent electricity, no skilled operators, and limited budgets. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECET867]**
*Define "**residual chlorine**" and state the BIS-prescribed limit for drinking water. Why is it important in a rural piped distribution system?*

### Model Answer (3 Marks)
**Residual chlorine** is the amount of free available chlorine (HOCl and OCl⁻) that remains in the water **after** the disinfection contact period is complete, indicating that sufficient chlorine was present to kill pathogens and a small protective margin remains.

**BIS IS 10500:2012 Limit:** Minimum **0.2 mg/L** (ideally **0.5 mg/L**) of free residual chlorine at the consumer's tap, measured after 30 minutes of contact time.

**Importance in rural piped networks:** The distribution network is long, often with intermittent flow, leaks, and minor contamination ingress. Residual chlorine acts as a **"police barrier"** — it continuously disinfects any contaminant that may enter the pipeline through cracks or cross-connections, protecting the consumer up to the tap. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PECET867]**

> **Design a Slow Sand Filter (SSF) unit for a rural habitation of 8,000 persons with a per-capita supply of 55 lpcd. The filter works 24 hours per day. The filtration rate is 0.15 m³/m²/h. Provide 1 standby unit. Compute:**
> **(a)** (7 Marks) The total filter area, the dimensions of each filter unit (assume L:B = 2:1), and the sand bed depth with all layer details.
> **(b)** (7 Marks) The daily chlorine requirement using bleaching powder (35% available chlorine) at a dose of 0.8 mg/L, and the capacity of the clear water sump (assume 20% of daily demand).

### Model Solution A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Average daily demand**

$$
Q_{day} = 8000 \times 55 = 4{,}40{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 440 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 2 — Hourly flow**

$$
Q_{hr} = \frac{440}{24} = 18.33 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply peak factor (1.5 for SSF design, since CWR smoothens peaks)**

$$
Q_{peak} = 1.5 \times 18.33 = 27.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 4 — Total filter area (working + standby = 1 + 1, but only 1 working)**

$$
A_{total} = \frac{Q_{peak}}{v_f} = \frac{27.5}{0.15} = 183.33 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — With L:B = 2:1, if B = b, then L = 2b, A = 2b²**

$$
2b^2 = 183.33 \;\Rightarrow\; b^2 = 91.67 \;\Rightarrow\; b \approx 9.57 \text{ m}
$$

**Adopt B = 9.5 m, L = 19.0 m → A = 180.5 m²** (slight over-design, acceptable).

**Step 6 — Sand Bed Layer Design**

| Layer | Material | Depth |
|---|---|---|
| Supernatant water | Clear water above sand | **1.0 m** |
| Sand bed (effective) | d₁₀ = 0.20 mm, Cu < 3 | **0.75 m** |
| Gravel support | 4 graded layers | **0.30 m** |
| Freeboard | Safety margin | **0.30 m** |

> **Total structural depth of SSF box = 1.0 + 0.75 + 0.30 + 0.30 = 2.35 m** (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Daily chlorine dose (pure)**

$$
W_{pure} = D \times Q = 0.8 \text{ mg/L} \times 4{,}40{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 3{,}52{,}000 \text{ mg/day} = 352 \text{ g/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to bleaching powder (35% available Cl₂)**

$$
W_{BP} = \frac{352}{0.35} = 1005.7 \text{ g/day} \approx 1.006 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{BP} \approx 1.0 \text{ kg/day of bleaching powder}}
$$

**Step 3 — Clear Water Sump (CWS) capacity (20% of daily demand)**

$$
V_{CWS} = 0.20 \times 440 = 88 \text{ m}^3
$$

> **Provide a sump of 88 m³ (typically circular, 5 m diameter × 4.5 m depth, or rectangular 6 m × 5 m × 3 m SWD).** (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PECET867]**

> **(a)** (7 Marks) Compare the design, operation, and performance of **Slow Sand Filters (SSF)** and **Rapid Sand Filters (RSF)** for a rural water treatment plant. Which one is more suitable for a village of 5,000 people in Kerala? Justify.
> 
> **(b)** (7 Marks) A uPVC distribution main (C = 140) of diameter 100 mm and length 1,800 m carries 12 L/s from an OHT. Calculate the head loss using Hazen-Williams and verify whether the velocity is within the permissible range (0.6 to 1.8 m/s).

### Model Solution B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Parameter | Slow Sand Filter (SSF) | Rapid Sand Filter (RSF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Filtration Rate** | 0.1 – 0.2 m³/m²/h | 5 – 15 m³/m²/h |
| **Sand Size (d₁₀)** | 0.15 – 0.30 mm | 0.45 – 0.70 mm |
| **Bed Depth** | 0.5 – 1.0 m | 0.6 – 0.9 m |
| **Pre-treatment** | Plain sedimentation only | Coagulation, flocculation, sedimentation |
| **Cleaning** | Scrape 1–2 cm every 2–3 months | Backwash with water + air scour daily |
| **Mechanism** | Biological (schmutzdecke) | Physical straining + adhesion |
| **Operator Skill** | Low (manual scraping) | High (valve sequencing, backwash pumps) |
| **Power Need** | Gravity only | Backwash pumps + coagulant dosing |
| **Land Area** | Large (e.g., 180 m² for 8,000 pop.) | Compact |
| **Bacterial Removal** | 99% (excellent) | 90–95% (needs chlorination) |
| **Capital Cost (per kL)** | ₹40 – ₹60 | ₹150 – ₹250 |
| **O&M Cost** | Very low | Moderate to high |

**Best choice for 5,000-person village in Kerala: SSF**, because:
1. Kerala's rural topography is hilly — gravity feed is natural and reliable.
2. Villages have intermittent electricity — SSF needs no pumps for filtration.
3. Source water (open wells, springs) is generally low-turbidity in Kerala highlands — ideal for SSF.
4. Cost per kL is 4–5 times lower.
5. No skilled operator needed — village jal-sewak can manage.
6. Kerala's humid climate supports year-round schmutzdecke regeneration. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $L = 1800$ m
- $Q = 12$ L/s = **0.012 m³/s**
- $d = 100$ mm = **0.10 m**
- $C = 140$ (uPVC)

**Hazen-Williams equation:**

$$
h_f = \frac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot d^{4.87}}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $Q^{1.852}$**

$$
\ln(0.012) = -4.4228 \;\Rightarrow\; 1.852 \times (-4.4228) = -8.191
$$

$$
Q^{1.852} = e^{-8.191} = 2.762 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C^{1.852}$**

$$
C^{1.852} = (140)^{1.852} = 9{,}386 \text{ (computed earlier)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $d^{4.87}$**

$$
\ln(0.10) = -2.3026 \;\Rightarrow\; 4.87 \times (-2.3026) = -11.214
$$

$$
d^{4.87} = e^{-11.214} = 1.358 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute**

$$
h_f = \frac{10.67 \times 1800 \times 2.762 \times 10^{-4}}{9386 \times 1.358 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
h_f = \frac{5.305}{0.1275} = 41.61 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_f \approx 41.6 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 5 — Velocity Check**

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.012}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.528 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{V = 1.53 \text{ m/s} \;\; (\text{within } 0.6 \text{ to } 1.8 \text{ m/s} \rightarrow \text{ACCEPTABLE})}
$$ 

(7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in "Water Treatment and Distribution" Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (3–5 marks lost per question):** Converting MLD to m³/hr to m³/sec without writing each step. KTU examiners *demand* the step shown explicitly. **Always write: 1 MLD = 1000 m³/day = 41.67 m³/hr = 0.01157 m³/s.**
> 
> 2. **Confusing Detention Time and Overflow Rate (4 marks):** Detention time uses **volume (V = L × B × H)**, SOR uses **surface area (L × B)**. Students mix up and use full volume for SOR. *Memorise:* $\tau$ = time, SOR = area flux.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the Peak Factor in Filter Sizing (5 marks):** Designing an SSF for *average* flow instead of *peak* flow results in undersized filters that cannot meet summer demand. **Use 1.5 × for SSF, 1.8 × for RSF.**
> 
> 4. **Wrong Hazen-Williams Constants (3 marks):** The constant **10.67** is for **SI units (Q in m³/s, d in m)**. The constant **4.73** is for the *imperial* form (Q in m³/s, d in m, V in m/s). Do not mix them.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring Sand Bed Layer Details (2 marks):** A design that only specifies *total depth* without breaking it into supernatant water + schmutzdecke + sand + gravel + freeboard loses easy marks.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting Standby Units (3 marks):** KTU/CPHEEO mandates *at least 1 standby* for every working filter unit. Omitting it is treated as a design error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Bullet Bank — Read this before the exam:**

- **Rural WTP sequence:** Intake → Screens → Plain Sedimentation → **SSF** → Chlorination → CWR → Pump → OHT → Distribution.
- **Per capita water supply (Rural):** **55 lpcd (JJM)**; design period **30 years** for civil works.
- **Plain sedimentation:** Detention time = **4–8 hours**; SOR = **12–18 m³/m²/day**; depth = **3–4.5 m**.
- **SSF specifications:** Filtration rate = **0.1–0.2 m³/m²/h**; sand d₁₀ = **0.20 mm**; Cu < **3**; bed depth = **0.75 m**; sand grading = **0.15–0.30 mm**; cleaning by scraping **schmutzdecke**.
- **RSF specifications:** Filtration rate = **5–15 m³/m²/h**; sand d₁₀ = **0.45–0.70 mm**; cleaning by **backwash + air scour**; requires coagulation pre-treatment.
- **Disinfection:** **0.2–0.5 mg/L** residual free chlorine after **30 min** contact; bleaching powder available chlorine **30–35%**.
- **Chlorine dose formula:** $W = \dfrac{D \times Q}{C \times 10^3}$ kg/day where C = % available chlorine, $10^3$ converts mg to g and g to kg.
- **Distribution network types:** **Dead-end (tree)** for rural — low cost; **Looped (grid)** for towns — better pressure and redundancy.
- **Pipe material preference (rural):** **uPVC / HDPE**; standard C = **140** for uPVC, **145–150** for HDPE, **120** for AC, **130** for DI.
- **Hazen-Williams head loss formula (SI):** $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot d^{4.87}}$.
- **Permissible velocity in distribution pipes:** **0.6 to 1.8 m/s**; optimal around **1.0–1.2 m/s**.
- **Service reservoir capacity:** **15–25%** of daily demand (≈ 4–6 hours storage).
- **Peak factors (CPHEEO):** **1.8** for max daily; **2.7** for max hourly; **1.5** for filter design (since CWR buffers).
- **Population forecast methods:** Arithmetic (linear growth), Geometric (constant % growth), **Incremental Increase (CPHEEO recommended)** — uses both $\bar{x}$ and $\bar{y}$.
- **Pressure at consumer tap (rural):** Minimum **0.7–1.0 m head**; recommended **7–12 m** for comfortable HH tap flow.
- **Formula cheat-codes:** $\tau = V/Q$; $SOR = Q/A_s$; $W_{chlorine} = DQ/C$; $V = Q/A$; $P_n = P_0 + n\bar{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y}$.

**End of Module 2 Notes — Water Treatment and Distribution. Best wishes for the KTU examination!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Treatment Methods: Filtration, Disinfection, and Safe Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Water Treatment Methods: Filtration, Disinfection, and Safe Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Water Treatment — The Trinity of Filtration, Disinfection & Safe Storage

In rural water supply engineering, post-collection water is rarely fit for direct human consumption. It contains suspended solids, pathogens (bacteria, viruses, protozoa), dissolved organic matter, and is microbially unsafe. The three pillars of decentralized/rural water treatment covered in this module are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Filtration** is the *physical* removal of suspended solids, turbidity, and a fraction of microbes by passing water through a porous medium (sand, gravel, ceramic, cloth, or membrane). **Disinfection** is the *chemical or physical* inactivation of remaining pathogenic microorganisms. **Safe Storage** is the *engineering protection* of the treated water from recontamination until the point of consumption.

#### Intuitive Analogy — The "Coffee Filter, Hand Sanitizer & Lunch Box" Framework

| Engineering Step | Everyday Analogy | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Filtration | Pouring chai through a cloth strainer | Removes visible dirt and most cysts — the "big" contaminants |
| Disinfection | Applying hand sanitizer after washing | Kills the invisible germs the filter missed |
| Safe Storage | Sealing the leftover curry in a steel box with a tight lid | Prevents flies, dust, and fingers from re-poisoning the food/water |

Just as you would not serve dirty, un-sanitized food kept open on a kitchen counter, water must be filtered, disinfected, AND sealed from recontamination.

### 1.2 Formal Definitions Aligned to KTU 2024 Syllabus

> [!NOTE]
> **Filtration (Rural Context):** A water-treatment process in which raw or pre-treated water is passed through a granular or porous medium to remove suspended solids, colloidal particles, turbidity, and a significant portion of microorganisms by **straining, sedimentation, and biological action**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Disinfection:** The process of destroying or inactivating pathogenic microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, protozoa, helminths) in water by chemical agents (chlorine, iodine, ozone) or physical agents (UV radiation, boiling). It is distinguished from *sterilization*, which destroys *all* forms of life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Safe Storage (Post-Treatment):** The controlled containment of treated water in covered, clean vessels designed to prevent recontamination by vectors, dust, animal contact, and human handling during the period between treatment and consumption.

### 1.3 Standard Water-Quality Metrics (KTU 2024 Reference)

- **Turbidity:** Measured in **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units)**. WHO guideline: < **1 NTU** (ideal), < **5 NTU** (acceptable for rural supply with disinfection).
- **pH:** Recommended range **6.5 – 8.5**.
- **Residual Chlorine:** **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L** at the consumer end.
- **Faecal Coliforms:** **0 CFU/100 mL** (zero) in piped supplies; < **10 CFU/100 mL** in rural point sources.
- **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS):** < **500 mg/L** (desirable), < **2000 mg/L** (maximum permissible).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Disinfection efficiency of chlorine is drastically reduced when turbidity > **5 NTU**, because suspended particles *shield* microbes from the disinfectant. This is the single most important reason filtration **precedes** disinfection in every design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Filtration — Theory, Types & Mechanisms

Filtration in rural systems operates on three simultaneous mechanisms:

1. **Straining (Mechanical Sieving)** — Particles larger than the pore openings are physically trapped on the surface of the medium, forming a *schmutzdecke* (in slow sand filters).
2. **Sedimentation (Inertial Impaction)** — Particles too heavy to follow the streamline of water around the sand grains settle onto the medium.
3. **Biological Adsorption** — A gelatinous microbial layer (zoogleal film) develops on the sand grains; this *biological layer* is the principal pathogen-removal agent in slow sand filtration.

#### 2.1.1 Classification of Filters (KTU High-Yield)

| Filter Type | Filtration Rate (m³/m²/day) | Sand Size (mm) | Typical Removal | Rural Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Slow Sand Filter (SSF) | **0.1 – 0.4** | 0.15 – 0.35 (effective) | 99% bacteria, 90–99% turbidity | Village-level community filter |
| Rapid Sand Filter (RSF) | **120 – 360** | 0.45 – 0.70 | 95–99% turbidity, 80–90% bacteria | Urban/peri-urban; needs coagulation |
| Roughing Filter | **5 – 30** | Gravel/coarse sand | 50–80% turbidity | Pre-treatment in highly turbid raw water |
| Ceramic Candle / Biosand | 0.1 – 0.4 | Porous ceramic | 90–99% bacteria | Household scale |
| Cloth / Saree Filter | 0.1 – 0.4 | Cotton fold | 70–90% *Vibrio cholerae* | Emergency/poor-turbidity water |

#### 2.1.2 Operational Steps in Slow Sand Filter (SSF)

- **Step 1 — Supernatant water layer** above the sand bed provides hydraulic head and a buffer for incoming turbidity.
- **Step 2 — Schmutzdecke formation (biological maturation):** takes **1 – 3 weeks**. This biologically active layer is the heart of SSF pathogen removal.
- **Step 3 — V filter medium** (effective sand): depth **0.5 – 0.8 m**, effective size **0.15 – 0.35 mm**, uniformity coefficient < **5** (preferably **2 – 3**).
- **Step 4 — Supporting gravel layers** (graded, 4–5 layers): graded from fine (2–5 mm) at top to coarse (20–40 mm) at bottom; total depth ~**0.3 – 0.5 m**.
- **Step 5 — Underdrain system** collects filtered water and supports the filter media; consists of lateral drains leading to a control chamber.
- **Step 6 — Cleaning (scraping):** the top **2 – 5 cm** of clogged sand is removed when terminal headloss reaches the design value.

#### 2.1.3 Operational Steps in Rapid Sand Filter (RSF)

- **Step 1 — Coagulation & Flocculation:** Alum [Al₂(SO₄)₃·18H₂O] dose **5 – 30 mg/L** destabilizes colloids.
- **Step 2 — Mixing (Flash):** rapid mix for **1 – 2 min** at G > **1000 s⁻¹** (velocity gradient).
- **Step 3 — Floc blanket formation:** slow mix for **20 – 30 min** at G = **10 – 75 s⁻¹**.
- **Step 4 — Filtration rate:** **120 – 360 m³/m²/day** (= **5 – 15 m/hr**).
- **Step 5 — Backwash:** upward flow of water (and air) at **36 – 45 m/hr** for **5 – 10 min** to fluidize the bed.

### 2.2 Disinfection — Theory, Methods & KTU Formulas

Disinfection does *not* remove suspended particles — it inactivates microbes. The principal **mechanisms of microbial inactivation** are:

- **Disruption of cell wall / membrane** (chlorine, ozone)
- **Damage to nucleic acids (DNA/RNA)** (UV, ozone)
- **Inhibition of enzyme systems** (chlorine, iodine)
- **Oxidation of cellular material** (ozone, chlorine dioxide)

#### 2.2.1 Chlorination — The Workhorse of Rural Disinfection

Chlorine when added to water forms **hypochlorous acid (HOCl)** and **hypochlorite ion (OCl⁻)**:

$$Cl_2 + H_2O \rightarrow HOCl + H^+ + Cl^-$$

$$HOCl \rightleftharpoons H^+ + OCl^-$$

The sum [HOCl] + [OCl⁻] is called **Free Available Chlorine (FAC)**. The **HOCl/OCl⁻** ratio is governed by pH: at pH 7.5, roughly **HOCl:OCl⁻ ≈ 50:50**. Since HOCl is **~80 times more effective** than OCl⁻, *lower pH favours disinfection*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Point:** Hypochlorous acid (HOCl) is the *active* disinfecting species. The pH of water must be kept **below 7.5** to maximize HOCl fraction; above pH 8, chlorination efficiency drops sharply.

#### 2.2.2 Forms of Chlorine Used in Rural Systems

| Form | Available Cl₂ (%) | Dose Form | Rural Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Liquid chlorine (Cl₂ gas) | 100% | Gas cylinder | Large municipal works |
| Bleaching powder Ca(OCl)₂ | 30 – 35% | Powder | Community storage tanks |
| High Test Hypochlorite (HTH) Ca(OCl)₂ | 65 – 70% | Tablets/Powder | Household, point-of-use |
| Sodium Hypochlorite NaOCl (bleach) | 4 – 12% | Liquid | Domestic storage jars |
| Chlorine tablets (NaDCC) | 60 – 65% | Tablet | Household, emergency |

#### 2.2.3 Concept of "Break-Point Chlorination"

When chlorine is added to water containing ammonia (NH₃) and organic nitrogen, the residual chlorine first **rises**, then **falls** (chloramines form), then **rises** again after all NH₃ is oxidized — this second rise is the **breakpoint**. Beyond the breakpoint, residual chlorine is **free chlorine**, which is the most effective disinfectant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Breakpoint Chlorination (Definition, KTU 2024):** The addition of sufficient chlorine to oxidize all ammonia, nitrite, and organic nitrogenous matter so that the residual chlorine in water exists predominantly as **free available chlorine (HOCl / OCl⁻)**, ensuring maximum disinfection efficiency.

#### 2.2.4 Chlorine Dose Calculation (Standard KTU Formula)

$$Chlorine\ Dose\ (mg/L) = \frac{Purity\ (\%) \times Strength\ of\ Solution}{100} \times \frac{Volume\ of\ Solution\ (mL)}{Volume\ of\ Water\ (L)}$$

For bleaching powder (P % chlorine strength):

$$Dose\ (mg/L) = \frac{V_{powder} \times P \times 10^6}{V_{water} \times 1000} = \frac{V_{powder} \times P \times 1000}{V_{water}}$$

#### 2.2.5 Disinfection Kinetics — Chick's Law & Chick-Watson

**Chick's Law (1908):** Microbial inactivation is first-order with respect to disinfectant concentration:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N$$

Integrating:

$$\ln\left(\frac{N_t}{N_0}\right) = -k \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

**Chick-Watson Modification (1908):** Includes concentration dependence:

$$\ln\left(\frac{N_t}{N_0}\right) = -k \cdot C^n \cdot t$$

Where **C·t** is the **CT value** (mg/L · min), a fundamental design parameter.

### 2.3 CT Concept (Critical KTU 2024 Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CT Value Definition:** The product of residual disinfectant concentration **C** (mg/L) and the contact time **t** (minutes). CT dictates the achievable log-removal of pathogens. Higher CT ⇒ higher inactivation.

For example, to achieve **3-log (99.9%)** inactivation of *E. coli* at pH 7 and 25 °C, chlorine CT ≈ **0.3 mg·min/L**; for *Giardia*, CT ≈ **34 mg·min/L**; for *Cryptosporidium* (chlorine-resistant), CT ≈ **7200 mg·min/L** (essentially impractical with chlorine alone).

#### CT Inactivation Equation

$$\% \ Inactivation = \left[ 1 - e^{-k \cdot C \cdot t} \right] \times 100 \quad ; \quad k = \text{pathogen-specific rate constant}$$

Or in log-removal form:

$$\text{Log Removal} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N_t}\right) = \frac{k \cdot C \cdot t}{2.303}$$

### 2.4 Safe Storage Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Safe Storage (KTU 2024 Definition):** The post-treatment handling of disinfected water in covered, narrow-mouthed, food-grade containers that prevent ingress of pathogens via vectors, dust, hands, and dipping implements, while maintaining residual chlorine of **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L** until consumption.

#### Design Principles (KTU High-Yield)

- **Container material:** food-grade HDPE, RCC, or stainless steel (NOT earthenware, cracked plastic, or open buckets).
- **Opening design:** narrow neck (< **10 cm** diameter) to prevent bucket/hand entry.
- **Cover:** tight-fitting lid, preferably with a spout/tap.
- **Tap/dispensing:** use of a **gravity tap** or **foot-operated tap** to avoid hand contact.
- **Cleaning frequency:** at least **once a week** with soap and water; chlorinated rinse at **50 mg/L** for 1 min.
- **Storage duration:** water should not be stored for more than **48 hours** even under ideal conditions.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Filtration rate (m³/m²·d) | $Q = A \cdot v$ | L/s, m², m/s | SSF: v = 0.1–0.4 m/d; RSF: v = 5–15 m/hr |
| 2 | Effective Size (ES) | $d_{10}$ (10% finer) | mm | SSF: 0.15–0.35 mm |
| 3 | Uniformity Coefficient (UC) | $UC = \frac{d_{60}}{d_{10}}$ | – | SSF: < 5 (ideal 2–3) |
| 4 | Headloss (Carmen-Kozeny) | $h_f = \frac{150 \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot v_s}{(\rho_s - \rho) \cdot g \cdot \phi^2 \cdot d^2} \cdot \frac{(1-\epsilon)^2}{\epsilon^3}$ | m | Valid for laminar flow, Re < 1 |
| 5 | Hydraulic Residence Time | $t = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{A \cdot d}{Q}$ | sec/min | For CT calc |
| 6 | Chick-Watson Inactivation | $N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot C^n \cdot t}$ | CFU/100 mL | n ≈ 1 for chlorine |
| 7 | Log Removal | $\text{Log} = -\log_{10}(N_t/N_0) = \frac{k \cdot C \cdot t}{2.303}$ | – | Used for pathogen tracking |
| 8 | Chlorine dose (powder) | $D = \frac{V_{powder} \cdot P \times 10^6}{V_{water} \times 10^3}$ | mg/L | P = % purity |
| 9 | Bleaching powder quantity | $W = \frac{Dose \times V_{water}}{P \times 10^4}$ | kg | For P in % |
| 10 | Breakpoint dose | Empirical ≈ 10 × NH₃-N (mg/L) | mg/L | Add 1–2 mg/L beyond BP |
| 11 | Storage contact time | $t = \frac{Volume}{Flow}$ | min | Min 30 min for chlorination |
| 12 | HOCl fraction | $\alpha_0 = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{pH - pK_a}}$, pK_a = 7.5 | fraction | Maximized at pH < 7.5 |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Indian Government Programmes:** Jal Jeevan Mission, Swachh Bharat Mission (rural component) — all rely on SSF + chlorination + safe storage at the household level.
- **Emergency Response (WHO):** in cholera outbreaks, the *SOP* is **turbidity reduction (cloth filter) → chlorination → safe household storage** — exactly the sequence covered above.
- **BIS / IS Standards:** IS 10500 (drinking water specification), IS 8419 (chlorinators), IS 15478 (household water treatment devices).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Headloss through a Granular Filter Bed (Carmen-Kozeny Equation)

The **Carmen-Kozeny equation** gives the clean-bed headloss through a uniform sand layer under laminar flow conditions.

**Starting Point — Darcy's Law for Flow through a Porous Medium:**

$$h_f = \frac{L \cdot v_s}{K}$$

where $v_s$ = superficial (approach) velocity, $L$ = bed depth, $K$ = hydraulic conductivity.

**Step 1 — Express K in terms of grain diameter, porosity, and shape factor:**

For a packed bed of uniform spheres of diameter $d$ and bed porosity $\epsilon$ (volume of voids/total volume), the Kozeny-Carman form of $K$ is:

$$K = \frac{\epsilon^3 \cdot d^2}{180 \cdot \mu / \rho g} = \frac{\epsilon^3 \cdot \phi^2 \cdot d^2}{150 \cdot \mu / (\rho g)}$$

where $\phi$ = sphericity (or shape factor) of sand grains.

**Step 2 — Substitute into Darcy's law:**

$$h_f = \frac{150 \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot v_s \cdot (1-\epsilon)^2}{\rho \cdot g \cdot \phi^2 \cdot d^2 \cdot \epsilon^3}$$

**Step 3 — Adjust for submerged filter (buoyancy) using grain specific gravity $G_s$:**

When the filter is submerged, the effective driving force is $(\rho_s - \rho)g$ rather than $\rho g$. The final Carmen-Kozeny equation becomes:

$$\boxed{h_f = \frac{150 \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot v_s \cdot (1-\epsilon)^2}{(G_s - 1) \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot \phi^2 \cdot d^2 \cdot \epsilon^3}}$$

**KTU 2024 Validity Conditions:**

- Reynolds number (based on grain): $Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v_s \cdot d}{\mu \cdot (1-\epsilon)} < 1$ (laminar)
- Typical $\phi \approx 0.7 - 0.9$ for natural sand
- Typical $\epsilon \approx 0.40 - 0.45$ for packed sand
- Typical $G_s \approx 2.65$ for silica sand

**Real-world utility:** This equation is used to design filter bed depth, choose effective sand size, predict time-to-cleaning, and to compute terminal headloss for slow sand filters in village water supply schemes.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Chick's Law and Chick-Watson Equation

#### 3.2.1 Chick's Law Derivation

**Assumption:** Disinfection is a unimolecular chemical reaction; rate of microbial inactivation is proportional to the *remaining* microbial concentration.

**Step 1 — Define rate equation:**

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -k \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $N_0$ at $t=0$ to $N_t$ at $t=t$:**

$$\int_{N_0}^{N_t} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{N_t}{N_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate to obtain Chick's Law:**

$$\boxed{N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}}$$

where $k$ = inactivation rate constant (per minute), depending on disinfectant type, concentration, pH, temperature, and microbial species.

#### 3.2.2 Chick-Watson Equation Derivation

**Step 1 — Watson's extension (1908):** Watson proposed that the rate constant $k$ in Chick's law is itself a function of disinfectant concentration $C$:

$$k = k' \cdot C^n$$

where $n$ = dilution coefficient (typically $n \approx 0.5 - 1.5$; for chlorine, $n \approx 1$).

**Step 2 — Substitute $k$ into Chick's law:**

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k' \cdot C^n \cdot N$$

**Step 3 — Assume $C$ is constant (residual maintained) and integrate:**

$$\int_{N_0}^{N_t} \frac{dN}{N} = -k' \cdot C^n \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 4 — Final integrated form (Chick-Watson):**

$$\boxed{N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-k' \cdot C^n \cdot t}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Log Removal (KTU 2024 — common board question form):**

Take base-10 logarithm:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N_t}\right) = \frac{k' \cdot C^n \cdot t}{2.303}$$

**Step 6 — Define CT value:**

$$\boxed{CT = C \cdot t}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Log Removal} = \frac{k' \cdot (C \cdot t)^n}{2.303} = \frac{k' \cdot (CT)^n}{2.303}$$

**Real-world utility:** This equation forms the *scientific basis* of every chlorination design — engineers use published CT tables (US EPA, WHO) to size contact tanks, determine required residual chlorine, and verify log-removal of *E. coli*, *Giardia*, and *rotavirus* in rural water supply schemes.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Chlorine Dose Calculation (KTU 2024 Board Style)

**Problem:** A rural community storage tank holds **50,000 L** of water. To disinfect it, bleaching powder of **30% available chlorine** is used. If the required dose is **2.0 mg/L**, calculate the quantity of bleaching powder required.

**Step 1 — Given Data:**

$$V_{water} = 50{,}000\ L \quad ; \quad Dose = 2.0\ mg/L \quad ; \quad P = 30\%$$

**Step 2 — Total mass of pure chlorine required:**

$$M_{Cl_2} = Dose \times V_{water} = 2.0\ mg/L \times 50{,}000\ L = 100{,}000\ mg = 100\ g$$

**Step 3 — Account for purity of bleaching powder (30% available Cl₂):**

$$M_{powder} = \frac{M_{Cl_2}}{P/100} = \frac{100\ g}{0.30} = 333.33\ g$$

**Step 4 — Express in kilograms:**

$$M_{powder} = 0.333\ kg \approx 333\ g$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{333\ g\ of\ 30\%\ bleaching\ powder\ (≈ 0.33\ kg)}$

**KTU 2024 Quick-Check:** The final mass must be 100/0.3 ≈ 3.33 × the pure chlorine mass, which is intuitive — a 30%-strength powder needs 1/0.3 ≈ 3.33× mass.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Filtration Rate and SSF Design Sizing (KTU 2024 Board Style)

**Problem:** Design the area of a slow sand filter for a village of **2000 people** with per-capita demand **40 lpcd**. Assume filter runs **24 hours** and works at filtration rate **0.2 m³/m²/day**.

**Step 1 — Total daily demand:**

$$Q_{day} = 2000 \times 40 = 80{,}000\ L/day = 80\ m^3/day$$

**Step 2 — Design filtration rate:**

$$v = 0.2\ m^3/m^2/day = 0.2\ m/day$$

**Step 3 — Required filter area:**

$$A = \frac{Q_{day}}{v} = \frac{80\ m^3/day}{0.2\ m/day} = 400\ m^2$$

**Step 4 — Choose two units (one standby), each of area:**

$$A_{unit} = \frac{400}{2} = 200\ m^2$$

If square: $\sqrt{200} = 14.14\ m \times 14.14\ m$ for each bed.

**Step 5 — Design sand bed depth (KTU standard):**

$$L_{sand} = 0.5 - 0.8\ m \quad \Rightarrow \ adopt\ 0.7\ m$$

**Step 6 — Supernatant water depth (above sand):**

$$h_{sup} = 1.0 - 1.5\ m \quad \Rightarrow \ adopt\ 1.2\ m$$

**Step 7 — Total filter depth (including gravel and underdrain):**

$$L_{total} \approx 0.7\ (sand) + 0.4\ (gravel) + 0.3\ (underdrain) = 1.4\ m$$

**Final Design Summary:**

$$\boxed{A = 200\ m^2\ (2\ units),\ L_{sand} = 0.7\ m,\ L_{gravel} = 0.4\ m,\ Supernatant = 1.2\ m}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Disinfection CT and Contact Time (KTU 2024 Board Style)

**Problem:** A rural chlorination contact tank of volume **20 m³** receives a flow of **0.5 L/s**. If residual chlorine is maintained at **0.5 mg/L**, calculate the CT and the achievable *E. coli* log-removal at pH 7 and 25 °C. Given $k'$ for *E. coli* = **1.0 (mg/L)⁻¹·min⁻¹** and $n = 1$.

**Step 1 — Hydraulic contact time:**

$$t = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{20\ m^3}{0.5\ L/s} = \frac{20{,}000\ L}{0.5\ L/s} = 40{,}000\ s = 666.7\ min$$

**Step 2 — CT value:**

$$CT = C \cdot t = 0.5\ mg/L \times 666.7\ min = 333.3\ mg \cdot min / L$$

**Step 3 — Chick-Watson log removal (n = 1):**

$$\text{Log Removal} = \frac{k' \cdot C \cdot t}{2.303} = \frac{1.0 \times 333.3}{2.303} = 144.7$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A log-removal of ~**145** is essentially *complete* sterilisation. In practice, *E. coli* is undetectable after even a fraction of this contact. This shows that a well-designed rural chlorination tank gives a massive safety margin for bacterial inactivation — *provided turbidity is already < 1 NTU* (i.e. filtration has worked).

---

### 3.6 Comparative Decision Matrix: Filtration vs Disinfection vs Storage

| Property | Filtration | Disinfection | Safe Storage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What it removes** | Suspended solids, turbidity, *some* microbes | Pathogens (chemical/physical inactivation) | None (prevention of recontamination) |
| **Mechanism** | Physical straining + biological adsorption | Chemical oxidation, UV damage, thermal | Physical barrier, hygiene behaviour |
| **KTU Threshold** | < 1 NTU outlet | 0.2–0.5 mg/L residual Cl₂ | 0 CFU/100 mL at tap |
| **Failure mode** | Bed clogging, short-circuiting | High turbidity, organics, ammonia | Open container, dipping, dirty hands |
| **Cost per m³ (rural India)** | ₹0.5 – ₹2 | ₹0.1 – ₹0.5 | Negligible (vessel) |
| **Lifetime** | Sand: 5–10 yrs; ceramic: 1–3 yrs | Single use | Varies (1–10 yrs) |
| **KTU emphasis** | Sizing, schmutzdecke | CT concept, breakpoint | Behaviour, vessel design |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Rural Water Treatment Train

The following Mermaid block represents the **complete functional architecture** of a rural water treatment system, from raw water intake to safe storage at the household.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Water Source<br>River / Well / Pond] --> B[Pre-Chlorination<br>Oxidation of Fe Mn]
    B --> C[Plain Sedimentation<br>1 to 4 hours]
    C --> D{Raw Turbidity<br>less than 100 NTU}
    D -- Yes --> E[Slow Sand Filter<br>Rate 0.1 to 0.4 m per day]
    D -- No --> F[Roughing Filter<br>Up flow Gravel]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Treated Water Storage<br>Clear Well]
    G --> H[Post Chlorination<br>Dose 1 to 2 mg per L]
    H --> I[Contact Tank<br>Min 30 minutes CT]
    I --> J[Residual Cl2 Check<br>0.2 to 0.5 mg per L]
    J --> K[Distribution Main<br>or Public Standpost]
    K --> L[Household Storage<br>Narrow Mouth Vessel]
    L --> M[Point of Use<br>Tap or Narrow Pour]
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree — Selection of Filtration Technology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Raw Water Sample] --> T1{Turbidity<br>Level}
    T1 -- Less than 10 NTU --> S1[Biosand Filter<br>at Household]
    T1 -- 10 to 100 NTU --> S2[Slow Sand Filter<br>Community Scale]
    T1 -- 100 to 1000 NTU --> S3[Plain Sedimentation<br>plus SSF]
    T1 -- Greater than 1000 NTU --> S4[Pre Sedimentation<br>plus Roughing Filter<br>plus SSF]
    S1 --> E[Effluent: Turbidity less than 1 NTU]
    S2 --> E
    S3 --> E
    S4 --> E
    E --> DIS[Send to Disinfection<br>Chlorine or UV]
```

### 4.3 Schmutzdecke Formation & Slow Sand Filter Cross-Section (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INFLUENT[Influent Zone]
        A1[Inlet Pipe] --> A2[Distribution Trough]
    end
    subgraph SUPER[Supernatant Layer]
        A2 --> A3[Water above Sand<br>Depth 1.0 to 1.5 m]
    end
    subgraph BIOLAYER[Active Biological Zone]
        A3 --> A4[Schmutzdecke<br>2 to 5 mm Thick<br>Algae Bacteria Protozoa]
    end
    subgraph SANDBED[Sand Bed]
        A4 --> A5[Fine Sand Layer<br>0.5 to 0.8 m<br>ES 0.15 to 0.35 mm]
    end
    subgraph GRAVEL[Gravel Support]
        A5 --> A6[Gravel Layers<br>Graded 2 to 40 mm<br>Total 0.3 to 0.5 m]
    end
    subgraph UNDERDRAIN[Underdrain System]
        A6 --> A7[Lateral Drains]
        A7 --> A8[Central Collector]
    end
    subgraph EFFLUENT[Treated Water Outlet]
        A8 --> A9[Control Chamber]
        A9 --> A10[Flow Regulator<br>Constant Head V Notch]
    end
    subgraph WASTE[Cleaning]
        A4 -. Scraped when headloss exceeds 1.0 m .-> A11[Spent Sand<br>Washed and Reused]
    end
```

### 4.4 Disinfection Process Map (CT-Based Design Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Filtered Water<br>less than 1 NTU] --> P2[Chlorine Dosing<br>Pump or Tablet]
    P2 --> P3[Flash Mix<br>1 to 2 minutes]
    P3 --> P4[Contact Tank<br>Min 30 minutes]
    P4 --> P5{Residual Cl2<br>in Range}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Safe Storage Vessel<br>0.2 to 0.5 mg per L]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Adjust Dose<br>Repeat Chlorination]
    P7 --> P2
    P6 --> P8[Distribution to<br>Consumer]
```

### 4.5 Safe Storage Design Hierarchy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z1[Treated Water from Tap] --> Z2{Storage Vessel Type}
    Z2 -- Option A --> Z3[Overhead Tank RCC<br>Covered Vented]
    Z2 -- Option B --> Z4[HDPE Drum<br>20 to 50 L Narrow Mouth]
    Z2 -- Option C --> Z5[Steel Container<br>Tight Lid]
    Z3 --> Y1[Dispensing by Tap<br>No Hand Contact]
    Z4 --> Y1
    Z5 --> Y1
    Y1 --> Y2[Daily Use<br>Max Storage 48 hours]
    Y2 --> Y3[Cleaning Weekly<br>50 mg per L Chlorine Rinse]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by "Break-Point Chlorination"? Why is it preferred in rural water supply systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Break-point chlorination refers to the addition of sufficient chlorine to oxidize all ammonia, nitrite, and organic nitrogenous compounds in water, so that the residual chlorine after a contact time of 1–2 hours exists predominantly as **free available chlorine (HOCl/OCl⁻)** rather than as combined chlorine (chloramines).

On a chlorine-residual-vs-dose curve, the residual first **rises** (chloramine formation), then **falls** (chloramine destruction), and then **rises sharply** — this second rise is the **breakpoint**.

In rural water supply, it is preferred because:

- (1) **Free chlorine is 25–80 times more effective** than combined chlorine, so pathogens are killed faster.
- (2) It provides a **stable residual** of 0.2–0.5 mg/L that protects water during storage and distribution.
- (3) It eliminates chloramine taste and odour problems.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition 1 m, curve logic 1 m, advantages 1 m.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define "CT value" in disinfection. Mention its significance in chlorination design of rural water supply.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **CT value** is the product of the **residual disinfectant concentration C** (in mg/L) and the **contact time t** (in minutes) for which this concentration is maintained in the water.

$$CT = C \times t \quad \text{(units: mg·min/L)}$$

**Significance:**

- (1) CT is the **fundamental design parameter** for chlorination — the WHO and US EPA publish tables giving required CT for log-removal of specific pathogens.
- (2) It determines the **size of the contact tank** for a given flow rate: $V = Q \times t$.
- (3) It allows the operator to **trade-off** between dose and time — e.g. a small dose with long contact may be as effective as a high dose with short contact.
- (4) It quantifies the **synergy of filtration + disinfection** — lower turbidity allows the chlorine to act on microbes rather than being consumed by particles.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition + formula 1 m, Significance 2 m.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**QUESTION A (14 MARKS) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Slow Sand Filter (SSF)**. List the design parameters and the typical filtration rate.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A village of **5000 population** is to be supplied with filtered water at **40 lpcd** through two slow sand filters (one standby). Design the dimensions of the filter unit. Assume filtration rate = **0.18 m³/m²/day** and sand bed depth = **0.7 m**.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working of SSF:**

A Slow Sand Filter is a rectangular masonry tank (typically RCC or brick in rural schemes) with the following components from top to bottom:

1. **Supernatant water layer (1.0 – 1.5 m)** above sand — provides hydraulic head, smooths flow fluctuations, and acts as a buffer for raw water turbidity.
2. **Inlet and distribution trough** — uniformly spreads raw water over the bed.
3. **Schmutzdecke (2 – 5 mm)** — biologically active gelatinous layer of algae, bacteria, protozoa, and organic matter that forms on the sand surface during the first 1–3 weeks. **This is the actual filter medium** for pathogen removal.
4. **Filter sand (0.5 – 0.8 m)** — Effective Size **0.15 – 0.35 mm**, Uniformity Coefficient < 5 (ideal 2–3), clean silica sand free from clay and fines.
5. **Gravel support layers (0.3 – 0.5 m total)** — 4–5 graded layers, coarse at bottom (20–40 mm), fine at top (2–5 mm); prevents sand from entering the underdrain.
6. **Underdrain system** — lateral open-jointed pipes or a false bottom leading to a central collector.
7. **Control chamber** with a **V-notch weir** to maintain constant filtration head.
8. **Outlet to filtered water sump.**

**Working:** Water percolates slowly downward under gravity. The combined action of (i) mechanical straining, (ii) sedimentation, and (iii) biological adsorption in the schmutzdecke removes turbidity (90–99%) and bacteria (99%). The filter runs for **1 – 3 months** before the headloss reaches the design limit (typically **1.0 – 1.2 m**), after which the top 2–5 cm of sand is scraped off, washed, and reused.

**Design Parameters Table:**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Filtration rate | **0.1 – 0.4 m³/m²/day** (0.18 adopted) |
| Effective size (d₁₀) | 0.15 – 0.35 mm |
| Uniformity coefficient | < 5 (2–3 ideal) |
| Sand bed depth | 0.5 – 0.8 m |
| Supernatant depth | 1.0 – 1.5 m |
| Gravel depth | 0.3 – 0.5 m |
| Maximum headloss | 1.0 – 1.2 m |
| Schmutzdecke | 2 – 5 mm, forms in 1–3 weeks |
| Filter run | 1 – 3 months |
| Cleaning | Top scraping |

> **Mark Split for Part (a):** Diagram/description 3 m, working 2 m, design parameters 2 m.

---

**Part (b) — Design of SSF:**

**Step 1 — Total daily water demand:**

$$Q_{day} = P \times q = 5000 \times 40 = 2{,}00{,}000\ L/day = 200\ m^3/day$$

**Step 2 — Number of filter units:**

$$N = 2\ (1\ working + 1\ standby)$$

**Step 3 — Required total filter area:**

$$A_{total} = \frac{Q_{day}}{v} = \frac{200\ m^3/day}{0.18\ m^3/m^2/day} = 1111.1\ m^2$$

**Step 4 — Area of each unit:**

$$A_{unit} = \frac{A_{total}}{N} = \frac{1111.1}{2} = 555.6\ m^2$$

**Step 5 — Choose a rectangular tank with length : width = 2:1:**

$$L = 2B \quad ; \quad L \times B = 555.6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2B^2 = 555.6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 16.7\ m$$

$$L = 2 \times 16.7 = 33.3\ m$$

**Step 6 — Adopt practical dimensions:**

$$B = 17\ m,\ L = 33\ m,\ A = 17 \times 33 = 561\ m^2 \quad (slightly\ oversized,\ acceptable)$$

**Step 7 — Sand bed depth:**

$$L_{sand} = 0.7\ m\ (given)$$

**Step 8 — Gravel depth (assumed):**

$$L_{gravel} = 0.4\ m$$

**Step 9 — Supernatant water depth:**

$$h_{sup} = 1.2\ m\ (adopted\ standard)$$

**Step 10 — Freeboard:**

$$h_{fb} = 0.3\ m$$

**Step 11 — Total filter depth:**

$$L_{total} = h_{sup} + L_{sand} + L_{gravel} + h_{underdrain} = 1.2 + 0.7 + 0.4 + 0.3 = 2.6\ m$$

**Final Design Summary:**

$$\boxed{A = 561\ m^2\ (2\ units\ of\ 17\ m \times 33\ m),\ L_{sand} = 0.7\ m,\ L_{total} = 2.6\ m}$$

> **Mark Split for Part (b):** Demand 1 m, Area 2 m, Dimensions 2 m, Bed depth 1 m, Final summary 1 m.

---

**QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principles and mechanisms** of chlorination. With equations, describe the **Chick-Watson model** of disinfection kinetics. State the limitations of chlorination in rural contexts.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A rural overhead tank contains **1,00,000 L** of water. Bleaching powder of **25%** available chlorine strength is used. If the desired dose is **1.5 mg/L** and the contact time is **45 min**, calculate:

(i) the quantity of bleaching powder required,

(ii) the CT value achieved at a residual chlorine concentration of **0.3 mg/L**, and

(iii) the *E. coli* log-removal assuming $k' = 0.8$ (mg/L)⁻¹·min⁻¹ and $n = 1$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Chlorination Principles, Chick-Watson Model, and Limitations:**

**Principles of Chlorination:**

When chlorine (Cl₂) is added to water, it hydrolyses:

$$Cl_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons HOCl + H^+ + Cl^-$$

The hypochlorous acid (HOCl) further dissociates based on pH:

$$HOCl \rightleftharpoons H^+ + OCl^-$$

The pKₐ of HOCl is **7.5** at 25 °C. The fraction of HOCl vs OCl⁻ is governed by:

$$\alpha_0 = \frac{[HOCl]}{[HOCl] + [OCl^-]} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(pH - 7.5)}}$$

At pH 7: $\alpha_0 \approx 0.76$ (76% HOCl); at pH 8: $\alpha_0 \approx 0.24$ (24% HOCl).

**Mechanisms of Microbial Inactivation by Chlorine:**

1. **Oxidation of cell-wall enzymes** — chlorine oxidizes sulfhydryl (–SH) groups in microbial enzyme systems, disrupting metabolism.
2. **Damage to cell membrane** — chlorine attacks the lipid-protein structure of the cell wall.
3. **Inhibition of DNA replication** — chlorine reacts with nucleic acid bases.
4. **Reaction with cellular proteins and amino acids** — irreversible denaturation.

**Chick-Watson Model — Derivation:**

The rate of microbial inactivation follows a first-order reaction with respect to the surviving microbial population:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k' \cdot C^n \cdot N$$

Integrating (with $C$ assumed constant):

$$\ln\left(\frac{N_t}{N_0}\right) = -k' \cdot C^n \cdot t$$

Exponentiating:

$$N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-k' \cdot C^n \cdot t}$$

In **log-removal** form:

$$\text{Log Removal} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N_t}\right) = \frac{k' \cdot C^n \cdot t}{2.303} = \frac{k' \cdot (CT)^n}{2.303}$$

**Limitations of Chlorination in Rural Contexts:**

1. **High turbidity shield effect** — particles > 5 NTU protect microbes from chlorine.
2. **pH sensitivity** — at pH > 8, OCl⁻ dominates and disinfection is much weaker.
3. **Taste and odour** — phenolic compounds react with chlorine to give medicinal taste.
4. **Resistance of certain pathogens** — *Cryptosporidium* and *Giardia* cysts are highly resistant to chlorine.
5. **Loss of residual during long distribution** — especially in intermittent rural supply.
6. **Storage stability of bleaching powder** — degrades in sunlight/heat; loses 50% strength in 6 months.
7. **Formation of DBPs (Disinfection By-Products)** — trihalomethanes (THMs) at high chlorine × organic matter.

> **Mark Split for Part (a):** Principles 2 m, Chick-Watson derivation 2 m, Mechanisms 1 m, Limitations 2 m.

---

**Part (b) — Chlorination Design Calculations:**

**Step 1 — Given data:**

$$V_{water} = 1{,}00{,}000\ L \quad ; \quad P = 25\% \quad ; \quad Dose = 1.5\ mg/L \quad ; \quad t = 45\ min \quad ; \quad C_{residual} = 0.3\ mg/L$$

**Step 2 — (i) Quantity of bleaching powder required:**

Mass of pure chlorine needed:

$$M_{Cl_2} = Dose \times V_{water} = 1.5\ mg/L \times 1{,}00{,}000\ L = 1{,}50{,}000\ mg = 150\ g$$

Quantity of bleaching powder (25% strength):

$$M_{powder} = \frac{M_{Cl_2}}{0.25} = \frac{150}{0.25} = 600\ g = 0.6\ kg$$

$$\boxed{M_{powder} = 600\ g}$$

**Step 3 — (ii) CT value:**

$$CT = C_{residual} \times t = 0.3\ mg/L \times 45\ min = 13.5\ mg \cdot min/L$$

$$\boxed{CT = 13.5\ mg \cdot min/L}$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Log-removal of E. coli:**

Using the Chick-Watson equation (n = 1):

$$\text{Log Removal} = \frac{k' \cdot C \cdot t}{2.303} = \frac{0.8 \times 0.3 \times 45}{2.303} = \frac{10.8}{2.303} = 4.69$$

$$\boxed{\text{Log Removal} \approx 4.69 \text{ (i.e. } 99.998\% \text{ inactivation of E. coli)}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

A log-removal of **4.69** corresponds to an *E. coli* reduction from, say, 1000 CFU/100 mL to less than 1 CFU/100 mL — easily meeting WHO drinking water standards.

> **Mark Split for Part (b):** Powder mass 2 m, CT 2 m, Log-removal 2 m, Interpretation 1 m.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert percentage to decimal** when calculating powder mass — a frequent error: $\frac{150}{25} = 6$ (WRONG) vs $\frac{150}{0.25} = 600$ (CORRECT).
> 2. **Confusing units** between mg/L and g/m³ (they are identical numerically, but exam scripts often misuse g/m³·min).
> 3. **Missing the $2.303$ factor** in the log-removal equation — students often write $k \cdot C \cdot t$ directly as "log removal", which is the *natural* log; the *base-10* log requires division by $\ln(10) = 2.303$.
> 4. **Omitting pH from chlorination discussion** — it carries full 1–2 marks in board exams.
> 5. **Drawing SSF diagram without labels** for schmutzdecke, gravel layers, underdrain — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for an unlabelled diagram.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (One-Page Rapid Revision):**

- **Filtration** removes suspended solids by straining, sedimentation, and biological adsorption; **Disinfection** inactivates pathogens chemically or physically; **Safe Storage** prevents recontamination after treatment.
- **SSF** operates at **0.1 – 0.4 m³/m²/day** with fine sand (ES = 0.15–0.35 mm) and a vital **schmutzdecke** layer; **RSF** operates at **120 – 360 m³/m²/day** but needs coagulation.
- **Slow sand filter runs** for 1–3 months; cleaning is by scraping the top 2–5 cm of sand.
- **Filtration always precedes disinfection** in the treatment train; turbidity must be < **1 NTU** before chlorination for effective kill.
- **Chlorine in water** forms HOCl and OCl⁻; **HOCl is ~80× more effective** than OCl⁻; thus pH must be **< 7.5** for efficient disinfection.
- **pKₐ of HOCl = 7.5** at 25 °C — memorize this.
- **Bleaching powder** has 30–35% available chlorine; **HTH** has 65–70%; **NaDCC tablets** have 60–65%.
- **Breakpoint chlorination** ensures free residual chlorine of **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L** at the consumer tap.
- **Chick's Law:** $N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$; **Chick-Watson:** $N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-k' C^n t}$.
- **CT value** = C (mg/L) × t (min); required CT for *E. coli* ≈ 0.3, *Giardia* ≈ 34, *Cryptosporidium* ≈ 7200 mg·min/L.
- **Safe storage vessel** must be covered, narrow-mouthed (< 10 cm), and dispensed via tap — never dipped into.
- **Standard chlorine dose** for pre-chlorination = 1–3 mg/L; for post-chlorination = 1–2 mg/L; minimum residual at consumer end = 0.2 mg/L.
- **Carmen-Kozeny equation** gives clean-bed headloss; valid for Re < 1 (laminar flow through sand).
- **KTU 2024 favourite topics for 14-mark questions:** SSF design, chlorine dose calculations, CT and log-removal, breakpoint chlorination, slow sand filter cross-section, safe storage principles.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distribution Systems: Pipelines, Storage Tanks, and Standpipes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Distribution Systems: Pipelines, Storage Tanks, and Standpipes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Distribution System — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distribution System (KTU 2024 Definition):** A distribution system is the network of pipes, storage structures, fittings, valves, and consumer service connections that delivers treated/potable water from the source or treatment plant to the point of consumption (households, standpipes, cattle troughs) in adequate quantity, at satisfactory pressure, and with acceptable quality.

In rural water supply (RWS), the distribution system typically operates under **intermittent supply** (a few hours per day) and is the **largest capital cost component**, often accounting for **50–70%** of the entire rural water supply scheme cost.

### Components of a Rural Distribution System

1. **Pipelines (Trunk Mains, Branch Mains, Distribution Mains, Service Pipes)**
2. **Storage Tanks (Overhead / Ground Level / Underground Service Reservoirs)**
3. **Standpipes / Public Standposts (PSPs) / Tap Stands**
4. **Valves, Fittings, Fire Hydrants, Air Valves, Scour Valves**
5. **Consumer service connections (house connections)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The three core elements covered in this module are **Pipelines**, **Storage Tanks**, and **Standpipes** — each forming an essential link in the rural water distribution chain.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bloodstream of the Village"

Imagine the **village water scheme as a human body**:
- **Source (well/borewell)** = the **Heart** that pumps out water
- **Pumping main / Rising main** = the **Arteries** carrying water under pressure
- **Storage tank (Overhead Tank / OHT)** = the **Stomach** that stores and regulates flow
- **Distribution mains** = the **Capillaries** that branch out into every lane
- **Standpipes / House connections** = the **Fingers** that deliver water to where it is needed
- **Valves** = the **Sphincters** that open/close flow in different parts

Just as **clogged arteries** cause health issues, **undersized pipes**, **low pressure**, or **leakage** cripple a village's water supply. Therefore, the engineer must "design the body" with adequate **diameter (size)**, **pressure (BP)**, and **storage (reservoir capacity)**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> The following boxed definitions are **high-frequency exam terms** that KTU examiners expect students to define precisely.

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Trunk Main** | Main pipe from source/treatment works to storage tank (large diameter) |
| **Distribution Main** | Pipe that branches from trunk main and runs along streets |
| **Service Pipe** | Small diameter pipe from distribution main to consumer tap |
| **Standpipe / Public Standpost (PSP)** | A public tap structure from which multiple households draw water |
| **OHT (Overhead Tank)** | Elevated storage tank providing gravity flow and pressure |
| **Sump** | Underground storage for raw/treated water near pumping station |
| **Service Reservoir** | Storage tank placed at the consumer zone for supply regulation |
| **HGL (Hydraulic Gradient Line)** | The line showing pressure head along the pipeline |
| **Intermittent Supply** | Supply given for limited hours per day (typical in rural India) |

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Design Parameters (Rural India)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indian Standard Norms (CPHEEO Manual + IS 1172:1993):**
> - **Per Capita Supply (rural):** 40 lpcd (litres per capita per day) — minimum
> - **NPC (Need based Per Capita):** 70 lpcd (with standpipes), 100–150 lpcd (house connections)
> - **Minimum residual head at standpipe:** **7 m (≈ 0.7 kg/cm²)** at the highest tap
> - **Minimum residual head at house connection:** **7–12 m**
> - **Standard design period:** **30 years** for pipelines, **15 years** for pumps
> - **Peak factor:** 2.0 to 2.5 (rural), 1.5 to 2.0 (urban) over annual average
> - **Fire demand addition:** Negligible for rural (since hydrants are rare)

---

## 1.5 Schematic Overview of a Rural Distribution System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source: Borewell / Well / River Intake] --> B[Pump House]
    B -- Pumping Main --> C[Water Treatment Plant]
    C -- Treated Water Main --> D[Overhead Tank OHT]
    D -- Gravity Main --> E[Distribution Network]
    E --> F1[Public Standpost PSP]
    E --> F2[Public Standpost PSP]
    E --> F3[House Connection]
    E --> F4[Cattle Trough]
    D -- Bypass --> G[Scour Valve / Drain]
    B --> H[Sump / Underground Reservoir]
    H -- Suction --> B
```

*Block-level flow: water is pumped from source → treated → stored in OHT → distributed by gravity through pipelines → delivered via standpipes or house connections.*

---

## 1.6 Why This Topic Matters for KTU 2024

- It is the **most numerically intensive** section of Module 2 — design problems are regularly asked.
- 3-mark definition questions appear in **every KTU ESE**.
- 14-mark design problems on **head loss, pipe sizing, or OHT capacity** are board-favorite questions.
- It directly maps to **Course Outcomes (CO2 & CO3)** of PECET867.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Part A — Pipelines in Rural Water Supply

### 2.1.1 Types of Pipes Used

| Pipe Material | Abbreviation | Common Use in Rural | Lifespan | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Galvanized Iron | **GI** | Service connections, small diameter | 25–30 yrs | Corrodes with hard water |
| Unplasticized PVC | **uPVC** | Distribution mains | 50+ yrs | Lightweight, cheap |
| High-Density Polyethylene | **HDPE** | Trunk mains, service lines | 50+ yrs | Flexible, jointless |
| Ductile Iron | **DI** | Trunk mains, large dia | 50–100 yrs | Heavy, costly |
| Cast Iron | **CI** | Old systems | 50 yrs | Being phased out |
| Asbestos Cement | **AC** | Old systems (banned in many countries) | — | Health hazard |
| Mild Steel | **MS** | Pumping mains, large dia | 30 yrs | Needs protective coating |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Preference:** For rural water supply, **uPVC, HDPE, and DI** pipes are most commonly used in current CPHEEO recommendations. **GI** is restricted to short service connections.

---

### 2.1.2 Pipe Jointing Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these for KTU 3-mark questions.**

| Pipe | Joint Type |
|---|---|
| **CI / DI** | Socket & spigot with lead / rubber gasket (Tyton joint) |
| **uPVC** | Solvent cement joint, rubber ring socket joint |
| **HDPE** | Butt fusion welding, electrofusion, compression fittings |
| **GI** | Threaded (socket) joint, flanged joint |
| **MS** | Welded, flanged, dresser coupling |

---

### 2.1.3 Distribution Network Layouts

| Layout | Sketch | Best Suited For | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tree / Branching** | like a tree | Rural, low-density | Dead ends cause stagnation |
| **Grid / Reticulation** | like a mesh | Urban, high demand | Costly, complex valves |
| **Ring / Circular** | closed loop | Towns, important areas | High cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rural systems** predominantly use the **Tree (Branching) system** because it is the most economical. The downside — **stagnation at dead ends** — is mitigated by periodic flushing through scour valves.

---

### 2.1.4 Pipe Laying — Standard Practices

- **Minimum cover depth** above pipe crown: **0.6 m** for distribution mains; **0.9 m** for trunk mains.
- **Bedding:** Pipe should rest on a uniform bed; rocky ground requires **100 mm sand cushion**.
- **Backfill:** Free of stones; compacted in layers.
- **Marking:** Marker tape (blue for water) placed 300 mm above pipe.
- **Thrust blocks:** Required at **bends, tees, valves** to counter hydraulic thrust.
- **Air valves / scour valves** placed at **summits** and **low points** respectively.

---

## 2.2 Part B — Head Loss in Pipelines (Core Engineering Theory)

### 2.2.1 The Two Governing Equations

#### (a) Darcy-Weisbach Equation (Most Accurate — Preferred by KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Darcy-Weisbach Formula:**
> $$h_f = \dfrac{f \cdot L \cdot V^2}{2 g D}$$
> Where:
> - $h_f$ = head loss due to friction (m)
> - $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless)
> - $L$ = length of pipe (m)
> - $V$ = velocity of flow (m/s)
> $D$ = diameter of pipe (m)
> $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**

The friction factor $f$ is obtained from the **Moody Chart** or by **Colebrook-White equation**:

$$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)$$

Where $\varepsilon$ = pipe roughness, $Re$ = Reynolds number.

For fully turbulent flow, an **explicit approximation** by Swamee-Jain:

$$f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} \right) \right]^2}$$

#### (b) Hazen-Williams Equation (Most Common in Practice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU students must memorize the Hazen-Williams formula as it is the most asked in numericals.**

$$V = 0.8492 \cdot C_H \cdot R^{0.63} \cdot S^{0.54}$$

$$h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C_H^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$$

Where:
- $V$ = velocity (m/s)
- $C_H$ = Hazen-Williams coefficient (depends on pipe material)
- $R$ = hydraulic radius (m) = $D/4$ for full-flowing circular pipe
- $S$ = hydraulic gradient = $h_f/L$
- $Q$ = discharge (m³/s)
- $D$ = diameter (m)

> **Typical $C_H$ values to remember:**
> - **uPVC, HDPE** = 140–150
> - **CI, DI** = 130
> - **GI** = 120
> - **Old CI** = 100

---

### 2.2.2 Minor Losses (Fittings, Valves, Bends)

$$h_m = K \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$$

Total head loss = $h_f + \Sigma h_m$

> **Typical $K$ values:** Entry = 0.5, 90° bend = 0.9, Tee = 1.0, Fully open gate valve = 0.2, Exit = 1.0.

---

## 2.3 Part C — Storage Tanks (Service Reservoirs & OHTs)

### 2.3.1 Functions of Storage

1. **Equalize supply and demand** (pumps run continuously, demand is variable).
2. **Provide emergency reserve** for fire / pipe burst / power failure.
3. **Maintain constant pressure** in distribution system.
4. **Allow chlorine contact time** after chlorination.
5. **Buffer against pump failures** — minimum 1.5 to 2 hours storage.

---

### 2.3.2 Types of Storage

| Type | Position | Use in Rural | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Underground Sump (UGS)** | Below ground | Raw/treated water near pump house | Low |
| **Ground Level Reservoir (GLR)** | On ground | Zonal storage in large schemes | Medium |
| **Overhead Tank (OHT)** | Elevated (15–20 m) | Direct gravity distribution | High |
| **Elevated Service Reservoir (ESR)** | Tall staging | Compact zones with high pressure | Very High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For rural India, the most common is the Overhead Tank (OHT)** of RCC construction with staging of 10–20 m, since it allows direct gravity distribution without pumping into the network.

---

### 2.3.3 Capacity of Storage Tank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula (CPHEEO Recommendation):**
> $$\text{Total Storage} = \text{Equalising Storage} + \text{Emergency Storage} + \text{Fire Storage}$$

For **rural schemes** (no fire storage):
$$\text{Storage Capacity} = (0.20 \text{ to } 0.33) \times \text{Daily Demand}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A common rule of thumb: **Storage = 1/3rd of daily demand** for rural intermittent supply.

---

### 2.3.4 Structural Components of an OHT

- **Staging (Columns & Bracings)** — supports the tank at required elevation
- **Tank Shell (Cylindrical)** — stores water, RCC or steel
- **Roof Dome / Slab** — covers tank, prevents contamination
- **Inlet Pipe** — brings treated water from pumping main
- **Outlet Pipe** — delivers to distribution network
- **Overflow Pipe** — drains excess; placed 50 mm below FSL
- **Scour Pipe** — bottom drain for cleaning; at lowest point
- **Ventilator** — with mosquito-proof mesh
- **Manhole** — for inspection & cleaning
- **Water Level Indicator** — float gauge
- **Ladder / Staircase** — external access
- **Float Valve** — closes inlet when tank is full
- **Lightening Arrester** — at top of staging

---

## 2.4 Part D — Standpipes / Public Standposts (PSPs)

### 2.4.1 Definition & Purpose

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Public Standpost (PSP):** A communal water point consisting of a tap or group of taps fitted to a vertical pipe (stand) connected to the distribution main, designed to serve a defined number of households within walking distance in rural areas.

**Purpose:**
- Provide equitable water access to households **without individual house connections**.
- Reduce per-capita cost of distribution.
- Avoid long queue times by ensuring **adequate number of PSPs per population**.

---

### 2.4.2 Spacing & Population Served

| Standard | Recommended Spacing |
|---|---|
| **WHO / CPHEEO** | **One standpost per 150–250 persons** (rural India) |
| **Maximum walking distance** | **100–150 m** from any household |
| **Taps per standpost** | **2–4 taps** (1 for drinking, others for washing) |
| **Spacing between standposts** | 200–300 m |

---

### 2.4.3 Types of Standpipes

1. **Single-tap standpipe** — minimum facility
2. **Multi-tap standpost** — 2 to 4 taps
3. **Standpost with cattle trough** — combined facility
4. **Yard tap (private)** — single household in compound
5. **Kiosk type** — operator-managed, paid supply

---

### 2.4.4 Components of a Standpost

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Standpipe (GI / uPVC, 25–40 mm dia)** | Vertical riser with tap |
| **Ferrule / Service connection** | Tapping from distribution main |
| **Bib tap / Push tap** | Flow control, water conservation |
| **Concrete platform** (1.5 m × 1.5 m) | Hygiene, prevents mud-puddle |
| **Drainage channel** | Carries away spilled water to soak pit |
| **Soak pit** (1 m × 1 m × 1 m) | Disposal of wastewater |
| **Brick masonry / Concrete pedestal** | Supports the standpipe |
| **Lockable tap** (optional) | Prevents misuse |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **one-page cheat sheet** every KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Darcy-Weisbach | $h_f = f L V^2 / (2gD)$ | m |
| 2 | Hazen-Williams | $h_f = 10.67 L Q^{1.852} / (C_H^{1.852} D^{4.87})$ | m |
| 3 | Velocity | $V = Q/A = 4Q/(\pi D^2)$ | m/s |
| 4 | Minor loss | $h_m = K V^2 / (2g)$ | m |
| 5 | Total head loss | $H = h_f + \Sigma h_m$ | m |
| 6 | Reynolds number | $Re = VD/\nu$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Continuity | $Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | m³/s |
| 8 | OHT capacity | $V = 0.20 \text{ to } 0.33 \times Q_{day}$ | m³ |
| 9 | Peak demand | $Q_{peak} = P_f \times Q_{avg}$ | m³/s |
| 10 | PSP population | $N_{PSP} = P / 200$ (rural) | no. |
| 11 | Per capita demand | $q = Q_{total} / P$ | lpcd |
| 12 | Available head at tap | $H_{tap} = HGL - z - h_f$ | m |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Component | Real-World Use |
|---|---|
| Pipelines | Pumping water across hilly terrain; cross-country water transfer |
| Storage tanks | Municipal supply, industrial cooling, fire reserve |
| Standpipes | Slum water supply, rural water kiosks, disaster relief camps |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Darcy-Weisbach Equation

We derive the Darcy-Weisbach head loss equation from **energy balance** across a pipe element of length $L$ and diameter $D$.

**Step 1 — Consider a control volume** of a pipe element of length $L$, diameter $D$, with steady, incompressible, fully developed flow.

**Step 2 — Apply the energy equation** between upstream section (1) and downstream section (2):
$$ \dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f $$

**Step 3 — For uniform pipe** ($V_1 = V_2$, $z_1 = z_2$ for horizontal pipe), the difference reduces to:
$$ h_f = \dfrac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho g} $$

**Step 4 — Use Newton's viscosity law** for shear stress at the pipe wall:
$$ \tau_0 = \mu \dfrac{dV}{dy}\bigg|_{wall} $$

**Step 5 — Equilibrium of forces** on the fluid column (pressure force vs. shear force):
$$ \Delta p \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_0 \cdot \pi D \cdot L $$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\tau_0$** as a fraction of the dynamic pressure:
$$ \tau_0 = f \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{8} $$

This definition comes from experiments (Fanning factor $f_F = f/4$).

**Step 7 — Substitute $\tau_0$** into the force balance:
$$ \Delta p \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \left( f \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{8} \right) \cdot \pi D L $$

**Step 8 — Solve for $\Delta p$**:
$$ \Delta p = \dfrac{f \cdot \rho V^2 L}{2 D} $$

**Step 9 — Divide by $\rho g$** to get head loss:
$$ h_f = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho g} = \dfrac{f L V^2}{2 g D} $$

$$\boxed{h_f = \dfrac{f L V^2}{2 g D}}$$

This is the **Darcy-Weisbach equation**, where $f$ is the Darcy friction factor (4 times Fanning's $f_F$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hazen-Williams Formula (Simplified Form)

The Hazen-Williams formula is **empirically derived** from extensive experiments on water flow in pipes.

**Step 1 — Start with the Darcy-Weisbach form:**
$$ h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g} $$

**Step 2 — Express $f$** for turbulent flow in terms of Reynolds number and roughness:
$$ f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} \right) \right]^2} $$

**Step 3 — Combine exponents** experimentally. Hazen and Williams (1905) proposed:
$$ V = k \cdot C_H \cdot R^x \cdot S^y $$

**Step 4 — With $R = D/4$**, $S = h_f/L$, and after extensive curve-fitting:
$$ V = 0.8492 \, C_H \, R^{0.63} \, S^{0.54} $$

**Step 5 — Solve for $h_f$** by rearranging:
$$ S^{0.54} = \dfrac{V}{0.8492 \, C_H \, R^{0.63}} $$

$$ S = \left( \dfrac{V}{0.8492 \, C_H \, R^{0.63}} \right)^{1/0.54} $$

**Step 6 — Substitute $V = Q/A$ and $A = \pi D^2/4$**, simplify with $R = D/4$:
$$ h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \, L \, Q^{1.852}}{C_H^{1.852} \, D^{4.87}} $$

$$\boxed{h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \, L \, Q^{1.852}}{C_H^{1.852} \, D^{4.87}}}$$

**Conditions of validity:** Hazen-Williams is **valid for water at normal temperatures (5–25°C)** in **turbulent flow** ($Re > 4000$) — perfect for rural water supply.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Pipe Sizing (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]:**
> A rural habitation with population **5000** is to be supplied with water. Design a **distribution main** of **uPVC pipe** from the OHT (FSL = 25.0 m) to the farthest standpipe located **1.5 km away** at elevation **18.0 m**. Per capita demand = **70 lpcd**. Peak factor = **2.25**. Residual head required at standpipe = **7 m**. Allow 20% for minor losses. $C_H$ for uPVC = **145**. Find the required diameter of the pipe.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Compute average daily demand:**
$$ Q_{day} = P \times q = 5000 \times 70 = 3{,}50{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 350 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$

**Step 2 — Convert to seconds (per second flow rate):**
$$ Q_{avg} = \dfrac{350}{24 \times 3600} = \dfrac{350}{86400} = 4.05 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$

**Step 3 — Apply peak factor for design flow:**
$$ Q_{design} = P_f \times Q_{avg} = 2.25 \times 4.05 \times 10^{-3} = 9.11 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$

**Step 4 — Compute available head for friction loss:**
$$ H_{avail} = \text{HGL at OHT} - \text{Ground level at tap} - \text{Residual head} $$
$$ H_{avail} = 25.0 - 18.0 - 7.0 = 0.0 \text{ m} \text{ ... (Oops, no head available!)} $$

**Step 5 — Re-interpretation:** Total available head = Static head difference + 7 m (we must provide 7 m at tap):
$$ H_{total} = 25.0 - 18.0 = 7.0 \text{ m} $$

If we need 7 m at tap and have only 7 m difference, no head is left for losses. Therefore, **raise OHT by 5 m** (revised FSL = 30 m) or compute on the basis that minor losses reduce residual.

Assuming revised FSL = 30 m, available head for losses:
$$ H_{loss} = 30.0 - 18.0 - 7.0 = 5.0 \text{ m} $$

**Step 6 — Allocate between friction and minor losses (minor = 20% of total):**
$$ h_f = 0.80 \times 5.0 = 4.0 \text{ m} $$

**Step 7 — Apply Hazen-Williams formula and solve for D:**
$$ h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \, L \, Q^{1.852}}{C_H^{1.852} \, D^{4.87}} $$

$$ 4.0 = \dfrac{10.67 \times 1500 \times (9.11 \times 10^{-3})^{1.852}}{(145)^{1.852} \times D^{4.87}} $$

**Step 8 — Compute intermediate values:**
$$ (9.11 \times 10^{-3})^{1.852} = (9.11)^{1.852} \times 10^{-3 \times 1.852} = 60.5 \times 10^{-5.556} = 60.5 \times 2.78 \times 10^{-6} = 1.68 \times 10^{-4} $$

$$ (145)^{1.852} = e^{1.852 \cdot \ln 145} = e^{1.852 \times 4.977} = e^{9.218} = 10110 $$

**Step 9 — Substitute:**
$$ 4.0 = \dfrac{10.67 \times 1500 \times 1.68 \times 10^{-4}}{10110 \times D^{4.87}} $$

$$ 4.0 = \dfrac{2.689}{10110 \times D^{4.87}} $$

$$ D^{4.87} = \dfrac{2.689}{4.0 \times 10110} = 6.65 \times 10^{-5} $$

**Step 10 — Solve for D:**
$$ D = (6.65 \times 10^{-5})^{1/4.87} = (6.65 \times 10^{-5})^{0.2053} $$

$$ \ln D = 0.2053 \times \ln(6.65 \times 10^{-5}) = 0.2053 \times (-9.617) = -1.974 $$

$$ D = e^{-1.974} = 0.139 \text{ m} = 139 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 11 — Select commercial diameter:**
Adopt next standard size = **150 mm (6 inch)** uPVC pipe.

**Step 12 — Verify velocity:**
$$ V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{9.11 \times 10^{-3}}{(\pi/4)(0.150)^2} = \dfrac{9.11 \times 10^{-3}}{0.01767} = 0.515 \text{ m/s} $$

This is within the permissible range of **0.6 to 1.2 m/s** — slightly low but acceptable for low-flow rural conditions.

**Step 13 — Final design summary:**
- Use **150 mm dia uPVC pipe**, Class 6 (working pressure 6 kg/cm²)
- Length of main = 1500 m
- Velocity at peak = 0.515 m/s
- Friction loss = 4.0 m, minor loss = 1.0 m, residual at tap = 7.0 m ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination tip:** Always verify velocity (0.6–1.2 m/s) and select a **commercial pipe size** higher than the calculated value.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — OHT Capacity

> **Problem:** A rural water supply scheme serves a population of 6000. Per capita demand = 70 lpcd. Compute the capacity of the OHT in m³ for both minimum and desirable storage conditions.

**Step 1 — Daily demand:**
$$ Q_{day} = 6000 \times 70 = 4{,}20{,}000 \text{ L} = 420 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 2 — Minimum storage (20% of daily demand):**
$$ V_{min} = 0.20 \times 420 = 84 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 3 — Desirable storage (33% of daily demand):**
$$ V_{des} = 0.33 \times 420 = 138.6 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 4 — Adopt next standard size:** Use a **150 m³ capacity OHT** with staging height 15 m.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Number of Standpipes

> **Problem:** A village has a population of 4000. As per CPHEEO norms, find the number of standpipes required and the design flow per tap.

**Step 1 — Number of standpipes (1 per 200 persons):**
$$ N = \dfrac{4000}{200} = 20 \text{ standpipes} $$

**Step 2 — Daily demand:**
$$ Q_{day} = 4000 \times 70 = 2{,}80{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 2.8 \text{ ML/day} = 2800 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$

**Step 3 — Supply hours:** Assume supply is for **6 hours/day** (intermittent).

**Step 4 — Hourly demand:**
$$ Q_{hr} = \dfrac{2800}{6} = 467 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} = 0.130 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$

**Step 5 — Per tap (assuming 2 taps per standpost, 20 standposts → 40 taps):**
$$ Q_{tap} = \dfrac{0.130}{40} = 3.24 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 3.24 \text{ L/s} $$

This is too high — practical tap discharge is **0.2–0.5 L/s**. Hence actual supply must be spread over more hours or use storage buffers.

**Step 6 — Practical supply hours needed for 0.4 L/s per tap:**
$$ Q_{total} = 40 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 0.016 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 57.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} $$

$$ \text{Hours} = \dfrac{2800}{57.6} = 48.6 \text{ hours} \text{ ... impractical} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **This shows the reality of rural supply:** With 40 taps running at 0.4 L/s, you'd need almost 2 days! In practice, **storage tanks buffer** the peak — people collect and store water in their homes.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Condition | Use This Formula | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Clean water, turbulent flow, design | **Hazen-Williams** | Simple, no iteration |
| Sludge / slurry / chemical flow | **Darcy-Weisbach** | General, accurate |
| Very smooth pipe, Re < 10⁵ | **Poiseuille (h = 32μLV/ρgD²)** | Laminar flow |
| Pipe with fittings & valves | **Hazen-Williams + minor K** | Easy summation |
| Hot water (>25°C) | **Darcy-Weisbach** | HW fails for non-water |
| Iterative diameter design | **Hazen-Williams** | Direct solution for D |
| High precision (research) | **Colebrook-White + DW** | Most accurate |

---

## 3.7 KTU Logic Decision Tree — Pipe Sizing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pipe Sizing Problem] --> B{Given Q and L?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is H_loss known?}
    B -- No --> C2[Use velocity 0.9 m/s to find D]
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Hazen-Williams solve for D]
    C -- No --> E[Compute H_loss from HGL difference]
    E --> D
    D --> F{Computed D commercial?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Verify V between 0.6 to 1.2 m/s]
    F -- No --> H[Adopt next higher commercial D]
    H --> G
    G -- OK --> I[Final Design]
    G -- Not OK --> J[Try larger D]
    J --> G
```
