```mermaid
        S10 --> S11[Soak Pit 1 m x 1 m x 1 m]
        S9 --> S12[Brick Pedestal with Plaster]
    end
    S13[Operator / User] -.Uses.-> S5
    S13 -.Uses.-> S6
```

*Detailed architecture of a 3-tap public standpost with proper drainage and soak pit arrangement.*

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Pipeline Network Layout Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tree_Network
        T1[OHT] --> T2[Main 150 mm]
        T2 --> T3[Branch 100 mm]
        T2 --> T4[Branch 100 mm]
        T3 --> T5[Standpipe 1]
        T3 --> T6[Standpipe 2 End Dead End]
        T4 --> T7[Standpipe 3]
        T4 --> T8[Standpipe 4 End Dead End]
    end
    subgraph Grid_Network
        G1[OHT] --> G2[Ring Main 200 mm]
        G2 --> G3[Loop A]
        G2 --> G4[Loop B]
        G3 --> G5[Standpipe 1]
        G3 --> G6[Standpipe 2]
        G4 --> G7[Standpipe 3]
        G4 --> G8[Standpipe 4]
        G3 -.Interconnection.-> G4
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Tree Network** (left) — common in rural; **Grid Network** (right) — provides redundancy and is preferred in urban areas.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Pipeline Fittings & Valves

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Distribution Main] --> B1[Isolating Sluice Valve]
    B1 --> C1[Pressure Reducing Valve]
    C1 --> D1[Tee Junction]
    D1 --> E1[Branch 1: 100 mm to Standpost]
    D1 --> E2[Branch 2: 100 mm to Next Junction]
    E1 --> F1[Ferrule Connection]
    F1 --> G1[Service Pipe to Tap]
    A2[Summit Point] --> AV[Air Valve Kinetic]
    A3[Low Point] --> SV[Scour Valve]
    A4[Bend 90 deg] --> TB[Thrust Block]
```

*Block-level mapping of the various fittings — sluice valves, PRVs, tees, ferrules, air valves, scour valves, and thrust blocks — that are critical accessories in pipeline design.*

---

## 4.6 Schematic — Hydraulic Gradient Line (HGL) Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OHT[OHT FSL 30 m] -- Static Head 12 m --> P1[Point 1: km 0]
    P1 -- Friction 1.5 m --> P2[Point 2: km 0.5]
    P2 -- Friction 1.5 m --> P3[Point 3: km 1.0]
    P3 -- Friction 1.5 m --> P4[Point 4: km 1.5 TAP]
    GL[Ground Level varies 18 to 20 m]
    OHT -.HGL 30 m to 25.5 m.-> P4
    P4 -.Residual 7 m.-> TAP[Tap Elevation 18 m]
```

> The HGL is the imaginary line joining the **pressure head** at every point along the pipeline. At the tap, the difference between HGL and the tap elevation = residual head.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Short Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

---

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> *List any three types of pipes used for rural water supply. State two advantages of uPVC pipes over GI pipes.*

**Model Answer:**

Three common pipe materials for rural water supply:
1. **uPVC (Unplasticized Polyvinyl Chloride)**
2. **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene)**
3. **Ductile Iron (DI)**

Two advantages of uPVC over GI:
- **Corrosion resistance:** uPVC does not corrode in contact with hard or aggressive water, while GI pipes are susceptible to internal scaling and external rusting.
- **Lower cost & lighter weight:** uPVC pipes are cheaper, easier to transport, and require no special jointing tools (compared to threaded GI pipes which need skilled labour).

*(Any two valid points accepted.)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

> *What is a Public Standpost (PSP)? State CPHEEO norms for the number of persons served by one PSP in rural areas.*

**Model Answer:**

**Public Standpost (PSP):** A communal water point consisting of a tap or group of taps fitted to a vertical standpipe connected to the distribution main, providing water to multiple households without individual connections.

**CPHEEO Norm:** **One standpost per 150–250 persons** in rural areas. The maximum walking distance should not exceed **100–150 m** from any household.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Design Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

> **(a)** A rural water supply scheme distributes water through 1500 m of uPVC pipe from an OHT (FSL = 28 m) to a standpost at elevation 19 m. Compute the **diameter of the distribution main** for a peak flow of 8.0 × 10⁻³ m³/s. Residual head required = 7 m. Assume 20% of total head loss is due to minor losses. Take $C_H$ = 145.
> **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe with a neat sketch the **components of a Public Standpost (PSP)**. List the CPHEEO norms for design of standposts in rural water supply.
> **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part A(a):**

**Step 1 — Available head for losses:**
$$ H_{avail} = 28 - 19 - 7 = 2 \text{ m} $$

**Step 2 — Friction head loss = 80% of total:**
$$ h_f = 0.8 \times 2 = 1.6 \text{ m} $$

**Step 3 — Hazen-Williams formula:**
$$ h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \, L \, Q^{1.852}}{C_H^{1.852} \, D^{4.87}} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**
$$ 1.6 = \dfrac{10.67 \times 1500 \times (8.0 \times 10^{-3})^{1.852}}{145^{1.852} \times D^{4.87}} $$

$$ (8.0 \times 10^{-3})^{1.852} = (8.0)^{1.852} \times 10^{-5.556} = 47.7 \times 2.78 \times 10^{-6} = 1.326 \times 10^{-4} $$

$$ 145^{1.852} = e^{1.852 \times 4.977} = e^{9.218} = 10110 $$

**Step 5 — Solve:**
$$ 1.6 = \dfrac{10.67 \times 1500 \times 1.326 \times 10^{-4}}{10110 \times D^{4.87}} $$

$$ 1.6 = \dfrac{2.122}{10110 \times D^{4.87}} $$

$$ D^{4.87} = \dfrac{2.122}{1.6 \times 10110} = 1.312 \times 10^{-4} $$

$$ D = (1.312 \times 10^{-4})^{0.2053} = 0.117 \text{ m} = 117 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 6 — Adopt commercial size:** **150 mm dia uPVC pipe, Class 6**

**Step 7 — Verify velocity:**
$$ V = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{(\pi/4)(0.15)^2} = 0.453 \text{ m/s} \quad (\text{OK, in range}) $$

**Final Answer:** Adopt **150 mm uPVC pipe**.

---

#### **Solution to Part A(b):**

**Components of a Public Standpost (PSP):**

1. **Vertical Standpipe** — GI / uPVC, 25–40 mm dia, 1.2 m above ground
2. **Bib Taps (2–3 nos)** — for drinking, washing, cleaning
3. **Service Pipe** — connects standpipe to ferrule
4. **Ferrule / Tapping** — saddle clamp on distribution main
5. **Brick Masonry Pedestal** — supports the tap at convenient height
6. **Concrete Platform** — 1.5 m × 1.5 m, prevents mud and stagnation
7. **Drainage Channel** — slope 1:100, carries spilled water
8. **Soak Pit** — 1 m × 1 m × 1 m filled with bricks/rubble

**CPHEEO Norms for Rural Standposts:**

- **One standpost per 150–250 persons**
- **Maximum walking distance:** 100–150 m
- **Minimum taps per standpost:** 2 (one for drinking, one for washing)
- **Minimum residual head at tap:** 7 m
- **Hygiene:** Concrete platform, drainage, soak pit mandatory
- **Spacing between adjacent standposts:** 200–300 m

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

> **(a)** Differentiate between **Overhead Tank (OHT)** and **Underground Sump (UGS)**. Explain the various **components of an OHT** with their functions.
> **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A village of population 8000 has a daily demand of 70 lpcd. Compute the **capacity of the OHT** required. Also determine the **number of public standposts** required as per CPHEEO norms and design the standpost drainage.
> **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a):**

**Difference Table:**

| Aspect | OHT (Overhead Tank) | UGS (Underground Sump) |
|---|---|---|
| **Position** | Elevated on staging (10–20 m high) | Below ground level |
| **Function** | Provides gravity pressure to distribution | Stores raw/treated water near pump house |
| **Cost** | High (staging cost) | Low |
| **Pumping** | Pumped into OHT; then flows by gravity | Pumped out from sump into rising main |
| **Pressure** | Generates required head | No head generation |
| **Capacity** | 20–33% of daily demand | 1–2 hours of pump capacity |
| **Maintenance** | Difficult (height) | Easy (ground level access) |

**Components of OHT with Functions:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Tank Shell (RCC cylindrical)** | Holds the water, sized for required capacity |
| **Roof Dome/Slab** | Prevents contamination, dust, sunlight |
| **Staging (Columns + Bracings)** | Supports tank at required elevation |
| **Inlet Pipe with Float Valve** | Allows treated water in, stops when full |
| **Outlet Pipe with Sluice Valve** | Draws water for distribution |
| **Overflow Pipe** | Discharges excess water, 50 mm below FSL |
| **Scour Pipe** | Bottom drain for cleaning, sludge removal |
| **Manhole** | Access for inspection and cleaning |
| **Ventilator** | Air circulation, fitted with mesh |
| **Water Level Indicator** | Float gauge to monitor storage |
| **Lightning Arrester** | Protects staging from lightning strikes |
| **Ladder/Staircase** | External access to top |

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b):**

**Step 1 — Daily demand:**
$$ Q_{day} = 8000 \times 70 = 5{,}60{,}000 \text{ L} = 560 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 2 — OHT capacity (1/3rd of daily demand for rural):**
$$ V_{OHT} = 0.33 \times 560 = 184.8 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 3 — Adopt next standard size:** **200 m³ capacity OHT** with staging 15 m high.

**Step 4 — Number of standposts (1 per 200 persons):**
$$ N_{PSP} = \dfrac{8000}{200} = 40 \text{ standposts} $$

**Step 5 — Design of standpost drainage:**

Each standpost should have:
- **Concrete platform:** 1.5 m × 1.5 m × 0.10 m thick
- **Drainage channel:** 100 mm wide × 50 mm deep, slope 1:100, length ~2 m
- **Soak pit:** 1 m × 1 m × 1 m deep, filled with brickbats/rubble, top covered with RCC slab
- **Spillway capacity:** should handle peak tap flow of 0.5 L/s
- **Screening:** Mosquito-proof mesh on soak pit vent

**Final Answer:** OHT = 200 m³, Number of standposts = 40, drainage per standpost as above.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** $Q$ in Hazen-Williams must be in **m³/s**, not L/s or L/day. Marks deducted for unit inconsistency.
> 2. **Mixing up Hazen-Williams and Darcy-Weisbach:** The HW formula is **only for water at 5–25°C in turbulent flow** — not for sewage or hot water.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply peak factor:** Use $P_f = 2.0$ to $2.5$ for rural design. Average demand is **not** the design demand.
> 4. **Not adding minor losses:** Always account for valves, bends, and entry/exit (typically 10–20% of friction loss).
> 5. **Skipping the HGL check:** The residual head at the **highest and farthest tap** must be ≥ 7 m. Many students forget to verify this.
> 6. **Selecting non-commercial pipe size:** Compute D, then **round UP** to next commercial size (50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 mm).
> 7. **OHT capacity error:** Using only equalising storage. Don't forget **emergency reserve** (1.5–2 hours of supply).
> 8. **PSP spacing confusion:** Stating "1000 persons per PSP" — wrong. CPHEEO norm is **150–250 persons per PSP**.
> 9. **Not drawing labelled diagrams:** In 7-mark questions, **a neat sketch is mandatory** — minimum 1.5–2 marks are awarded for diagram quality.
> 10. **Missing scour valve / air valve locations:** Forgetting to mention these in pipeline layout.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-glance master revision list for KTU 2024 exam — read 5 times before entering the hall.**

### **Pipelines**
- ☐ Common pipe materials: uPVC, HDPE, DI, GI, CI, MS, AC
- ☐ uPVC is most preferred for rural due to cost, life, ease of jointing
- ☐ Distribution networks: Tree (rural), Grid (urban), Ring (loop)
- ☐ Minimum depth of cover: 0.6 m (dist), 0.9 m (trunk)
- ☐ Thrust blocks at bends, tees, valves
- ☐ Air valve at summits, scour valve at low points
- ☐ Marker tape (blue) 300 mm above pipe
- ☐ Standard velocity: 0.6 to 1.2 m/s
- ☐ Standard pipe sizes: 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 mm

### **Head Loss Formulas**
- ☐ Darcy-Weisbach: $h_f = f L V^2 / (2gD)$ — most accurate
- ☐ Hazen-Williams: $h_f = 10.67 L Q^{1.852} / (C_H^{1.852} D^{4.87})$ — most used
- ☐ Minor losses: $h_m = K V^2/(2g)$
- ☐ Total loss = friction + minor (add 20% for fittings)
- ☐ $C_H$ values: uPVC/HDPE = 140, CI/DI = 130, GI = 120, old CI = 100

### **Storage Tanks**
- ☐ OHT provides pressure for gravity distribution
- ☐ UGS stores water near pump house
- ☐ Storage capacity = 0.20 to 0.33 × daily demand (rural)
- ☐ Design period = 30 years
- ☐ OHT staging height: 10–20 m
- ☐ Components: inlet, outlet, overflow, scour, ventilator, manhole, float valve, water level indicator
- ☐ Overflow placed 50 mm below FSL
- ☐ Dead storage of ~1 m for sediment

### **Standpipes / PSPs**
- ☐ 1 PSP per 150–250 persons (CPHEEO)
- ☐ Max walking distance 100–150 m
- ☐ 2–4 taps per standpost
- ☐ Tap discharge: 0.2 to 0.5 L/s
- ☐ Platform 1.5 m × 1.5 m
- ☐ Soak pit 1 m × 1 m × 1 m
- ☐ Residual head at tap ≥ 7 m
- ☐ Standpipe 25–40 mm dia GI/uPVC

### **Design Standards (RURAL)**
- ☐ Per capita demand: 40 lpcd (min), 70 lpcd (NPC)
- ☐ Peak factor: 2.0–2.5
- ☐ Intermittent supply: 4–6 hours/day
- ☐ Design period: 30 years (pipes), 15 years (pumps)
- ☐ Velocity in pipe: 0.6–1.2 m/s
- ☐ Hazen-Williams $C_H$ for new uPVC: 140–145

### **Numericals to Practice**
- ☐ Pipe diameter using Hazen-Williams
- ☐ Head loss for given Q and D
- ☐ OHT capacity calculation
- ☐ Number of PSPs for a population
- ☐ HGL / residual head verification
- ☐ Population served by a network

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operation and Maintenance Strategies for Rural Water Supply Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operation and Maintenance Strategies for Rural Water Supply Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Operation and Maintenance (O&M)** in the context of rural water supply systems refers to the **systematic set of technical, administrative, financial, and managerial activities** required to ensure that a water supply scheme continues to deliver the **intended quantity and quality of water** to the beneficiary community throughout its **design life (typically 15–30 years)**, at an **acceptable level of service** and at the **lowest sustainable cost**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, O&M is not viewed as a post-construction activity but as a **continuous life-cycle process** that begins with the commissioning of the scheme and extends through the entire service period. It encompasses:

- **Operation**: Day-to-day running of the system (pump operation, valve regulation, chlorination, billing, water distribution management).
- **Maintenance**: Preventive, curative, and predictive interventions to keep the system functional (pipeline repairs, pump servicing, tank desilting, meter replacement).
- **Management**: Institutional, financial, and community-level arrangements to ensure sustainability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** O&M is considered the **most critical determinant of scheme sustainability**. Statistics indicate that nearly **30–40% of rural water supply schemes in India become non-functional within 5 years** of construction primarily due to absence of structured O&M strategies — making this topic a high-weightage area for KTU examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Ownership" Intuition

Imagine you have just bought a new car (your **newly commissioned water supply scheme**). What happens if you:

| Car Analogy | Rural Water Supply Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Never change the engine oil | Pipeline corrosion, pump seizure |
| Ignore warning dashboard lights | Leakage, low pressure, contamination go unnoticed |
| Skip annual servicing | No preventive maintenance → major breakdowns |
| Don't pay road tax / insurance | No financial sustainability → scheme shuts down |
| Don't train a driver | Community cannot operate valves, chlorinators |
| No spare tyre in the boot | No spare parts inventory → prolonged outages |

**Just as a car needs regular servicing, trained drivers, fuel, insurance, and road-worthiness checks, a rural water supply scheme needs structured O&M, trained operators, sustainable financing, water quality monitoring, and a spare-parts inventory.**

### 1.3 Why O&M is Critical — The Three Pillars of Sustainability

> [!NOTE]
> A rural water supply scheme is **sustainable** only when **all three pillars** are simultaneously satisfied. Failure of any one pillar causes cascading failure of the others.

1. **Technical Sustainability** — The system is functional, water is potable, and breakdowns are minimal.
2. **Financial Sustainability** — Operation costs (electricity, chemicals, spares, operator salary) are recovered through tariffs or grants.
3. **Institutional/Social Sustainability** — The community owns the system, trained manpower is available, and Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs) are actively involved.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics for Rural Water Supply O&M

The following metrics are universally used to benchmark the performance of O&M programs (bold values are typical KTU-recommended benchmarks):

- **Service Level (Quantity):** **55 lpcd (litres per capita per day)** as per the **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) 2022** norm.
- **Service Level (Quality):** All 12 parameters of **BIS IS 10500:2012** must be within acceptable limits (bacteriological and chemical).
- **System Uptime:** **≥ 95%** (the system should be operational for at least 347 days/year).
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** **≥ 180 days** for handpumps, **≥ 365 days** for piped schemes.
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):** **≤ 48 hours** for piped schemes, **≤ 24 hours** for handpumps.
- **Cost Recovery Ratio:** **≥ 1.0** (annual O&M cost recovered through tariffs).
- **Functionality Rate:** **≥ 90%** (as per National Rural Drinking Water Programme norms).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Maintenance Strategies

Maintenance activities for rural water supply systems are classified into **three primary categories** based on the timing and nature of intervention:

#### 2.1.1 Preventive Maintenance (PM)
- **Definition:** Scheduled, planned interventions carried out **before a failure occurs** to keep the system in working condition.
- **Examples:** Pump greasing every 3 months, chlorinator inspection monthly, valve exercising quarterly, tank desilting annually, pipe network flushing semi-annually.
- **Logic:** *Why* — Reduces unexpected breakdowns, extends asset life, lowers life-cycle cost.
- **Best Practice:** Always maintain a **Preventive Maintenance Schedule (PMS)** charted on a calendar.

#### 2.1.2 Corrective (Curative) Maintenance (CM)
- **Definition:** Reactive maintenance carried out **after a failure has occurred** to restore the system to working condition.
- **Examples:** Repair of a burst pipe, replacement of a burnt-out pump motor, fixing a leaking valve.
- **Logic:** *How* — Emergency repair teams with spare parts inventory are deployed to minimize downtime.
- **Drawback:** Higher long-term cost; service disruption is unavoidable.

#### 2.1.3 Predictive (Condition-Based) Maintenance (PdM)
- **Definition:** Maintenance based on **real-time monitoring of asset condition** (vibration, temperature, pressure sensors, water quality probes).
- **Examples:** Replacing a pump bearing when vibration analysis shows a rising trend; replacing a chlorinator nozzle when dosing rate drops.
- **Logic:** *Why* — Most cost-efficient, requires instrumentation and SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) — feasible only in larger rural schemes.

#### 2.1.4 Comparison Matrix

| Strategy | Timing | Cost | Downtime | Skill Required | Suitability for Rural |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Preventive | Scheduled | Medium | Planned, low | Medium | **Highly suitable** |
| Corrective | After failure | High (long-term) | Unplanned, high | Low | Necessary as backup |
| Predictive | Condition-based | Low (long-term) | Minimal | High | Limited to large schemes |

### 2.2 Institutional Framework for O&M in Rural Areas

The **Rajiv Gandhi National Drinking Water Mission (RGNDWM)** and **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** prescribe a **5-tier institutional model** for O&M:

1. **Village Water and Sanitation Committee (VWSC)** — grass-root level, community-managed.
2. **Gram Panchayat (GP)** — owns the scheme, signs O&M agreements.
3. **Block-level PHED (Public Health Engineering Department)** — technical support, training.
4. **District Water and Sanitation Mission (DWSM)** — coordination, monitoring.
5. **State Water and Sanitation Mission (SWSM)** — policy, funding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Under JJM, the **VWSC or Pani Samiti** is mandated to undertake **75% of O&M activities** at the village level, with the remaining 25% supported by the GP/Block. This is a **favourite examination topic**.

### 2.3 Water Tariff and Cost Recovery

Financial sustainability of O&M hinges on the principle of **"user pays"** — beneficiaries contribute towards recurring costs.

#### 2.3.1 Components of O&M Cost

- **Energy cost** (electricity for pumps) — typically **50–60%** of total O&M cost.
- **Chemical cost** (chlorine, alum) — **5–10%**.
- **Spare parts and repair cost** — **15–20%**.
- **Operator salary** — **10–15%**.
- **Miscellaneous (admin, transport)** — **5–10%**.

#### 2.3.2 Key Formulae (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — O&M and Cost Recovery**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{O\&M} = C_E + C_{Ch} + C_S + C_{Op} + C_{Misc}$ | Total annual O&M cost | ₹/year |
| 2 | $A_{Annual} = Q_{avg} \times 365 \times P_{design}$ | Annual volume of water supplied | m³/year |
| 3 | $UC = \dfrac{C_{O\&M}}{A_{Annual}}$ | Unit cost of water | ₹/m³ |
| 4 | $T = \alpha \cdot UC$ | Tariff per m³ (α = recovery factor, 0 ≤ α ≤ 1.5) | ₹/m³ |
| 5 | $RR = \dfrac{T \cdot A_{Billed}}{C_{O\&M}}$ | Cost Recovery Ratio (target ≥ 1.0) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | System availability (reliability index) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{req} = \dfrac{\gamma \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta}$ | Pump power requirement | kW |
| 8 | $C_E = P_{req} \times t \times R_{unit}$ | Annual energy cost | ₹/year |
| 9 | $D_{cap} = 0.012 \times P_{design}$ | Chlorine dose (12 mg/L) | kg/day |
| 10 | $N_{va} = 1 + 0.05(N-1)$ | Number of valve operation/year (factor of 1.05) | count |

> **Where:**
> $Q_{avg}$ = average daily discharge (m³/day), $P_{design}$ = design population, $Q$ = discharge (m³/s), $H$ = total head (m), $\gamma$ = 9810 N/m³, $\eta$ = pump efficiency, $t$ = annual pumping hours, $R_{unit}$ = electricity tariff (₹/kWh), $N$ = design life in years, $N_{va}$ = number of valve actuations, $A_{Billed}$ = annual water billed (m³).

**Real-World Utility:** Formula (6) — System availability $A$ — is the most powerful single metric used by KTU examiners to assess O&M performance. A piped scheme with MTBF = 300 days and MTTR = 5 days has $A = 300/(300+5) = 0.984$, i.e., **98.4% availability** (acceptable).

### 2.4 The O&M Cycle — A Continuous Loop

The O&M process is **not a one-time activity** but a **closed-loop cycle** consisting of six phases:

1. **Plan** — Annual O&M plan with budget, manpower schedule, spare parts list.
2. **Operate** — Day-to-day running (pump operation, chlorination, billing).
3. **Monitor** — Water quality testing, pressure/flow measurement, energy auditing.
4. **Maintain** — Preventive and corrective interventions.
5. **Record** — Documentation of all O&M data in registers/software.
6. **Review & Improve** — Quarterly review meetings; corrective actions for next cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Plan → Operate → Monitor → Maintain → Record → Review"** cycle is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in KTU Module 2 — make sure you can draw the cycle and explain each node in detail.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: System Availability (Reliability Index)

The **system availability** $A$ is the fraction of time a system is operational and ready to deliver water.

**Step 1: Define MTBF and MTTR.**

$$MTBF = \frac{\text{Total Operational Time in a Period}}{\text{Number of Failures in that Period}}$$

$$MTTR = \frac{\text{Total Downtime (Repair Time) in a Period}}{\text{Number of Failures in that Period}}$$

**Step 2: Express total available time.**

$$T_{Total} = T_{Operational} + T_{Downtime}$$

**Step 3: Multiply numerator and denominator by the number of failures (n).**

$$T_{Total} = n \cdot MTBF + n \cdot MTTR = n \cdot (MTBF + MTTR)$$

**Step 4: Define availability as the fraction of time the system is operational.**

$$A = \frac{T_{Operational}}{T_{Total}} = \frac{n \cdot MTBF}{n \cdot (MTBF + MTTR)}$$

**Step 5: Cancel n from numerator and denominator.**

$$\boxed{A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}}$$

**Worked Example (KTU-style):** A rural piped water supply scheme experiences **6 pump failures per year**. Total operational time in the year = **340 days**. Total downtime = **25 days**. Calculate the availability.

**Step 1:** Compute MTBF.
$$MTBF = \frac{340}{6} = 56.67 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2:** Compute MTTR.
$$MTTR = \frac{25}{6} = 4.17 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3:** Compute availability.
$$A = \frac{56.67}{56.67 + 4.17} = \frac{56.67}{60.84} = 0.9315 = 93.15\%$$

**Conclusion:** Availability is 93.15%, which is **below the KTU benchmark of 95%** — indicating that corrective action is required to reduce downtime (e.g., by maintaining a spare parts inventory or training operators).

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: O&M Cost Estimation and Unit Cost of Water

**Worked Example:** A rural piped water supply scheme serves a **design population of 5000**. Average per capita supply = **55 lpcd**. Annual O&M cost components are:

| Component | Symbol | Annual Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy | $C_E$ | 1,20,000 |
| Chemicals | $C_{Ch}$ | 15,000 |
| Spare parts & repair | $C_S$ | 30,000 |
| Operator salary | $C_{Op}$ | 36,000 |
| Miscellaneous | $C_{Misc}$ | 9,000 |

Electricity tariff $R_{unit}$ = ₹ 6/kWh. Pumping hours per day = 8 hours. **Calculate: (a) Total O&M cost, (b) Annual water volume, (c) Unit cost of water, (d) Recommended tariff (assume α = 1.1).**

**Step (a): Total O&M cost**

$$C_{O\&M} = C_E + C_{Ch} + C_S + C_{Op} + C_{Misc}$$

$$C_{O\&M} = 1,20,000 + 15,000 + 30,000 + 36,000 + 9,000$$

$$\boxed{C_{O\&M} = 2,10,000 \text{ ₹/year}}$$

**Step (b): Annual volume of water supplied**

$$A_{Annual} = Q_{avg} \times 365 \times P_{design}$$

First compute $Q_{avg}$:
$$Q_{avg} = 55 \text{ lpcd} \times 5000 = 2,75,000 \text{ L/day} = 275 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}$$

Then:
$$A_{Annual} = 275 \times 365 = 1,00,375 \text{ m}^3\text{/year}$$

$$\boxed{A_{Annual} = 1,00,375 \text{ m}^3\text{/year}}$$

**Step (c): Unit cost of water**

$$UC = \frac{C_{O\&M}}{A_{Annual}} = \frac{2,10,000}{1,00,375} = 2.092 \text{ ₹/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{UC \approx ₹\, 2.09 \text{ per m}^3}$$

**Step (d): Recommended tariff (α = 1.1)**

$$T = \alpha \cdot UC = 1.1 \times 2.092$$

$$\boxed{T = ₹\, 2.30 \text{ per m}^3}$$

**Step (e): Cost Recovery Ratio** (if collection efficiency = 80%)

$$A_{Billed} = 0.80 \times 1,00,375 = 80,300 \text{ m}^3\text{/year}$$

$$Revenue = T \times A_{Billed} = 2.30 \times 80,300 = 1,84,690 \text{ ₹/year}$$

$$RR = \frac{1,84,690}{2,10,000} = 0.879$$

$$\boxed{RR = 0.88 < 1.0 \text{ — financial sustainability NOT achieved}}$$

**Conclusion:** The scheme is running at a **deficit of ₹ 25,310/year**. The Gram Panchayat must either increase tariff, improve collection efficiency to >95%, or seek a **15% O&M grant** from the SWSM under JJM norms.

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Chlorine Demand and Bleaching Powder Quantity

**Problem:** A village overhead tank holds **50,000 litres** of water. Required chlorine dose = **2 mg/L**. Available bleaching powder strength = **30% available chlorine**. Calculate the bleaching powder required per day.

**Step 1: Chlorine demand (kg/day)**

$$D_{cap} = \text{Dose} \times \text{Volume} = 2 \text{ mg/L} \times 50,000 \text{ L} = 1,00,000 \text{ mg} = 0.1 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2: Quantity of bleaching powder**

$$Q_{BP} = \frac{D_{cap}}{\text{Strength}} = \frac{0.1 \text{ kg}}{0.30}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{BP} = 0.333 \text{ kg/day} = 333 \text{ g/day}}$$

**Step 3: Check residual chlorine after 24 hours (assume 0.5 mg/L consumed).**

$$C_{residual} = 2.0 - 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

Since $C_{residual} \geq 0.2$ mg/L (IS 10500 limit) and $\leq 1.0$ mg/L is desirable, the **dose is acceptable**.

> [!NOTE]
> The chlorine dosing calculation is one of the most commonly tested sub-parts (3 marks) in KTU Part A questions on O&M of disinfection systems.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing an O&M Strategy

| Situation / Scheme Type | Recommended Primary O&M Strategy | Secondary Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single village piped scheme (< 5000 pop) | **Community-based preventive** | Corrective backup | Limited budget, high community involvement |
| Multi-village scheme (> 5 schemes) | **Block-level PHED preventive** | Predictive | Centralized monitoring feasible |
| Handpump-dominated area | **Spot source corrective + preventive** | Predictive (depth sensor) | Handpumps are discrete, low-cost units |
| Solar-powered scheme | **Preventive (panel cleaning)** | Corrective | Solar panels need regular cleaning for efficiency |
| SCADA-monitored scheme | **Predictive (full)** | Preventive | Sensors available for vibration, pressure, chlorine |
| Seasonal spring-fed scheme | **Corrective + adaptive** | Preventive (pre-monsoon) | Source yield varies; storage is critical |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
