## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, PECET867]**

Define **Operation and Maintenance (O&M)** of a rural water supply system. List any **four common reasons for failure** of rural water supply schemes within 5 years of construction.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Operation and Maintenance (O&M) refers to the **systematic set of technical, administrative, financial and managerial activities** required to ensure that a rural water supply scheme continues to deliver the intended quantity and quality of water to the beneficiary community throughout its design life, at the lowest sustainable cost.

**Four common reasons for failure (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **Lack of community ownership and participation** — schemes handed over without training or institutional mechanism collapse within 2–3 years.
2. **Inadequate financial planning** — no tariff system; recurring costs (electricity, chemicals, spares) not met.
3. **Absence of preventive maintenance** — only corrective ("breakdown") maintenance is practised, leading to cumulative deterioration.
4. **Insufficient trained manpower** — no trained operator at village level; technical support from PHED unavailable.

*(Any other valid reason like poor water quality monitoring, vandalism, political interference may also be awarded marks.)*

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, PECET867]**

Distinguish between **Preventive Maintenance** and **Corrective Maintenance** for a rural piped water supply scheme. Give **one example** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Preventive Maintenance | Corrective Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| **Timing** | Scheduled, before failure | After failure occurs |
| **Cost** | Lower life-cycle cost | Higher long-term cost |
| **Downtime** | Planned, minimal | Unplanned, often long |
| **Approach** | Proactive | Reactive |

**Example of Preventive (1 mark):** Greasing the pump bearings and inspecting the chlorinator **every 3 months** as per the annual maintenance schedule.

**Example of Corrective (1 mark):** **Repairing a burst pipe** in the distribution network after it has leaked, causing water loss and supply disruption.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Module 2 carries **two full 14-mark questions**, and the student must answer **any one**. The question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PECET867, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **six-phase O&M cycle** for a rural water supply scheme. Describe in detail the activities to be carried out in the **"Plan"** and **"Monitor"** phases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rural piped water supply scheme has experienced **8 pump failures in one year**. Total operational time in the year is **330 days** and total downtime is **35 days**. Calculate: **(i)** MTBF, **(ii)** MTTR, **(iii)** System availability, and **(iv)** comment on whether the scheme meets the KTU benchmark of 95% availability. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — The Six-Phase O&M Cycle (7 marks)

The O&M cycle is a **continuous closed-loop process** comprising six interdependent phases. The cycle ensures that no single activity is performed in isolation and that **lessons from one cycle feed into the next**.

**Phase 1 — PLAN (1.5 marks):**
- Prepare an **Annual O&M Plan** before the start of the financial year.
- Components: budget for energy/chemicals/spares/manpower; preventive maintenance schedule (calendar); spare parts list; manpower deployment; training calendar for VWSC members.
- Outcome: a single **Annual O&M Plan document** approved by the Gram Panchayat and submitted to the Block PHED.

**Phase 2 — OPERATE (1 mark):**
- Day-to-day running: switching pumps on/off, regulating valves, dosing chlorine, recording readings, attending to consumer complaints, collecting tariff.

**Phase 3 — MONITOR (1.5 marks):**
- Continuous or periodic measurement of **key performance indicators (KPIs)**.
- **Water quality** — bacteriological testing (MFT / coliform count) monthly; residual chlorine testing daily at the tail end; chemical parameters (pH, turbidity, hardness) quarterly.
- **Quantity** — flow meters at the source, reservoir, and consumer ends; pressure gauges.
- **Energy** — monthly electricity bill and kWh consumed per m³ water supplied (specific energy consumption).
- **Financial** — monthly tariff collection vs. expenditure.

**Phase 4 — MAINTAIN (0.5 marks):**
- Carry out preventive maintenance as per the schedule; deploy corrective maintenance teams when failures occur.

**Phase 5 — RECORD (0.5 marks):**
- Maintain **logbooks** (pump hours, chlorine doses, complaints), **stock registers** for spares and chemicals, and **asset registers** with serial numbers and dates of installation.

**Phase 6 — REVIEW AND IMPROVE (1 mark):**
- Hold **quarterly review meetings** with VWSC, GP, and Block officials.
- Compare actual KPIs against targets; identify deviations; prepare **corrective action plan** for the next cycle.

> **Examiner Tip:** A **neat labelled diagram of the six-phase cycle** (Plan → Operate → Monitor → Maintain → Record → Review, with a feedback arrow from Review back to Plan) is worth **2 additional impression marks** in the valuation.

#### Part (b) — Availability Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** Number of failures, n = 8; Operational time = 330 days; Downtime = 35 days; Total time = 365 days.

**Step 1: Compute MTBF.** **(1.5 marks)**
$$MTBF = \frac{\text{Total Operational Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{330}{8} = 41.25 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2: Compute MTTR.** **(1.5 marks)**
$$MTTR = \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{35}{8} = 4.375 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3: Compute System Availability.** **(2 marks)**
$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{41.25}{41.25 + 4.375} = \frac{41.25}{45.625} = 0.9041$$

$$\boxed{A = 90.41\%}$$

**Step 4: Comment on KTU benchmark.** **(2 marks)**
The KTU/JJM benchmark for piped water supply schemes is **≥ 95% availability**. The calculated availability of **90.41%** is **below the benchmark by ~4.6 percentage points**. The Gram Panchayat and VWSC should:
- **Reduce downtime** by maintaining a critical spares inventory at the pump house (target MTTR ≤ 2 days).
- **Increase operational reliability** by overhauling the pump annually (target MTBF ≥ 90 days).
- **Train local operators** to handle minor repairs immediately.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall in Question A(b):**
> - **Do not** use total calendar days (365) directly as the denominator for availability. The correct formula is $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$, where MTBF and MTTR are in the **same time unit (days)**. Mixing units (e.g., days for MTBF and hours for MTTR) is the **most common mistake** and results in loss of 2–3 marks.
> - **Do not** forget to **state the formula before substitution**. KTU examiners award **1 mark** separately for writing the correct formula. Many students lose this mark by directly writing the numerical answer.
> - **Do not** omit the **comment on the benchmark** (Part b-iv). Even a one-line "availability is below 95%, corrective action required" fetches the remaining 2 marks.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PECET867, Module 2]**

**(a)** Discuss the **institutional framework** for operation and maintenance of rural water supply schemes in India. Explain the roles of **VWSC, Gram Panchayat, Block PHED, DWSM and SWSM**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rural water supply scheme serves a population of **4000** with a per capita supply of **55 lpcd**. Annual O&M cost is **₹ 1,80,000** and annual water billed is **1,00,000 m³**. The Gram Panchayat proposes a tariff of **₹ 2.50 per m³**. Calculate the **Cost Recovery Ratio** and comment on financial sustainability. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Institutional Framework (7 marks)

The O&M of rural water supply schemes in India follows a **decentralized 5-tier institutional model** prescribed by the National Rural Drinking Water Programme (NRDWP) and Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM).

**Tier 1 — Village Water and Sanitation Committee (VWSC) / Pani Samiti (2 marks):**
- Grass-root level body; **minimum 50% women members** (mandatory under JJM).
- Functions: day-to-day operation, chlorination, minor repairs, tariff collection, water quality testing using field test kits, awareness generation.
- Receives **10% of scheme cost as corpus fund** for O&M (under JJM).
- Maintains **village water security plan**.

**Tier 2 — Gram Panchayat (1.5 marks):**
- **Owns the scheme** in the village.
- Approves the tariff; mobilizes community contribution; supervises VWSC; carries out **75% of O&M activities** at the village level.
- Receives **15% of scheme cost as O&M fund** from SWSM.

**Tier 3 — Block PHED / Block Development Office (1.5 marks):**
- Provides **technical support**: water quality testing in district labs, training of VWSC members, major repair assistance, design of new schemes.
- Functions under the technical guidance of the **Executive Engineer, PHED**.

**Tier 4 — District Water and Sanitation Mission (DWSM) (1 mark):**
- Chaired by the **District Collector**.
- Coordinates between blocks, GPs, and state; consolidates district-level O&M reports; sanctions major repair budgets.

**Tier 5 — State Water and Sanitation Mission (SWSM) (1 mark):**
- Apex state-level body for policy, funding, capacity building, and inter-departmental coordination.
- Sanctions the State Annual Action Plan; releases funds to DWSM; monitors JJM dashboards.

#### Part (b) — Cost Recovery Ratio (7 marks)

**Given:** $C_{O\&M}$ = ₹ 1,80,000/year; Tariff $T$ = ₹ 2.50/m³; Annual water billed $A_{Billed}$ = 1,00,000 m³/year.

**Step 1: Calculate Annual Revenue.** **(2 marks)**
$$Revenue = T \times A_{Billed} = 2.50 \times 1,00,000 = 2,50,000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 2: Write the Cost Recovery Ratio formula.** **(1.5 marks)**
$$RR = \frac{\text{Annual Revenue}}{C_{O\&M}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute and compute.** **(1.5 marks)**
$$RR = \frac{2,50,000}{1,80,000} = 1.389$$

$$\boxed{RR = 1.39}$$

**Step 4: Comment on financial sustainability.** **(2 marks)**
Since $RR = 1.39 > 1.0$, the scheme is **financially sustainable** and generates a **surplus of ₹ 70,000/year (38.9% above O&M cost)**. The surplus can be:
- Deposited in a **maintenance reserve fund** for major repairs and asset replacement.
- Used to **subsidize the poorest households** (below poverty line) with free or reduced-tariff connections.
- Invested in **system augmentation** (additional storage, larger pumps) to improve service level.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall in Question B(b):**
> - **Do not confuse** "Annual water supplied" with "Annual water billed". The Cost Recovery Ratio is computed using **billed water** (i.e., water for which tariff is actually collected), not the total volume supplied. Mixing these up is a common error worth **2 marks**.
> - **Do not omit the comment on sustainability.** A bare numerical answer of 1.39 is incomplete; students must explicitly state **whether RR > 1.0 or RR < 1.0** and interpret the result. The **interpretation carries 2 marks** in KTU valuation.
> - **Currency units must be consistent.** Express both revenue and O&M cost in the same unit (₹/year). Mixing ₹/month with ₹/year is a frequent mistake.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Operation and Maintenance of Rural Water Supply Systems**

- **O&M Definition:** A continuous life-cycle process of operating, maintaining, and managing a water supply scheme to ensure sustainable service.
- **Three pillars of sustainability:** Technical, Financial, Institutional — all three must hold simultaneously.
- **Three types of maintenance:** Preventive (scheduled), Corrective (after-failure), Predictive (condition-based).
- **Benchmarks to memorize:** **55 lpcd (quantity)**, **95% uptime**, **MTBF ≥ 180 days (handpump) / 365 days (piped)**, **MTTR ≤ 48 hours (piped) / 24 hours (handpump)**, **RR ≥ 1.0**.
- **Five-tier institutional framework:** VWSC → GP → Block PHED → DWSM → SWSM.
- **VWSC composition:** Minimum 50% women members (mandatory under JJM).
- **Six-phase O&M cycle:** Plan → Operate → Monitor → Maintain → Record → Review (with feedback loop).
- **Cost components (rough split):** Energy 50–60%, Spare parts 15–20%, Operator salary 10–15%, Chemicals 5–10%, Misc 5–10%.
- **System availability formula:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$. Always use **same time unit** for MTBF and MTTR.
- **Cost Recovery Ratio:** $RR = (T \times A_{Billed}) / C_{O\&M}$. Sustainable if $RR \geq 1.0$.
- **Unit cost of water:** $UC = C_{O\&M} / A_{Annual}$ (₹/m³). Tariff is typically set at $T = 1.0 \text{ to } 1.2 \times UC$.
- **Chlorine demand:** $D_{cap} = \text{Dose (mg/L)} \times \text{Volume (L)} \times 10^{-6}$ (in kg).
- **Bleaching powder quantity:** $Q_{BP} = D_{cap} / \text{Strength of powder}$.
- **JJM standard dose:** **2 mg/L free chlorine** at the consumer end; residual **≥ 0.2 mg/L** as per IS 10500.
- **Documentation is mandatory:** Logbooks, stock registers, asset registers, complaint registers.
- **Capacity building:** Every VWSC should undergo **at least 2 days of training per year** on O&M.
- **Spare-parts rule of thumb:** Maintain a **minimum 3-month stock** of fast-moving spares (seals, gaskets, fuses, bearings, chlorinator parts).
- **Functionality rate** (NRDWP/JJM metric): **≥ 90%** of schemes should be fully functional at any point of time.
- **Common reasons for scheme failure:** Lack of community ownership, no tariff, no trained operator, no preventive maintenance, vandalism, source depletion.
- **Golden formula to remember for KTU:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ — appears in almost every Part B question on O&M reliability.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — PECET867 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monitoring and Evaluation of Water Supply Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Monitoring and Evaluation of Water Supply Services # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) of Water Supply Services** is a systematic, continuous, and structured process of collecting, analysing, and interpreting data on the planning, implementation, and operational performance of rural water supply schemes against pre-defined standards, indicators, and service level benchmarks (SLBs), with the objective of ensuring reliable, equitable, sustainable, and safe drinking water delivery to all rural households. In the context of the **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** and the **Ministry of Jal Shakti (MoJS)**, M&E is anchored to the **Service Level Benchmark (SLB)** framework consisting of **5 service delivery indicators** and **5 institutional indicators**, which are measured at the **village, scheme, district, state, and national** levels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PECET867 / Module 2):** Monitoring and Evaluation is a high-weightage topic that directly maps to the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) Service Level Benchmarks, functionality assessment, and sustainability metrics for rural water supply schemes. ## 1.2 The 5 + 5 Service Level Benchmark (SLB) Framework | Service Delivery Indicators | Institutional Indicators | |------------------------------|--------------------------| | 1. Coverage (% of households with tap connections) | 1. % of schemes with dedicated O&M staff | | 2. Functionality (% of schemes delivering designed quantity) | 2. % of schemes with user charges collected | | 3. Water Quality (% samples meeting BIS 10500) | 3. % of schemes with asset management plan | | 4. Regularity of Supply (hours/day) | 4. % expenditure on O&M against budget | | 5. Equity (coverage of SC/ST/AAY households) | 5. % of schemes with women participation in GPWSC | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Health Checkup" Imagine a rural piped water scheme as a **living patient**: - **Monitoring** is like the **daily health checkup** — checking pulse, BP, and temperature regularly (daily water flow, chlorine residual, complaints). - **Evaluation** is the **annual master health checkup** — ECG, blood tests, full body scan (functionality audit, SLB score, sustainability assessment). If you only monitor, you catch symptoms. If you only evaluate, you wait too long. **Both are required** to keep the patient (the water scheme) healthy throughout its design life of **30 years** (typical life of PWS assets). > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for Exams:** > - **Monitoring** = Continuous / Process-oriented / Tracks inputs & outputs. > - **Evaluation** = Periodic / Outcome-impact oriented / Assesses effectiveness and sustainability. > Both together form the **M&E Cycle**: Plan → Implement → Monitor → Evaluate → Feedback → Revise Plan. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The M&E Logical Framework (LogFrame) — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Inputs** → Finance, materials, manpower, technology, raw water source. 2. **Activities** → Construction of intake, OHT, distribution network, household connections. 3. **Outputs** → Number of tap connections, length of pipeline laid, schemes commissioned. 4. **Outcomes** → Hours of supply, quantity delivered, water quality compliance. 5. **Impact** → Reduction in water-borne diseases, time saved by women, school attendance of girls. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why M&E?** Without M&E, the **Service Level Benchmark (SLB)** score cannot be measured, **Functionality Status** of the scheme cannot be ascertained, and **JJM incentives / penalties** cannot be triggered. ## 2.2 Functionality Assessment — The Core Evaluation Parameter A water supply scheme is classified into one of four functional categories based on the **quantity of water supplied (lpcd)** and **hours of supply per day**: - **Fully Functional (FF):** Supplying ≥ **55 lpcd** for ≥ 8 hours/day with all systems operational. - **Partially Functional (PF):** Supplying **40–55 lpcd** or 6–8 hours/day with minor defects. - **Non-Functional (NF):** Supplying < 40 lpcd or < 6 hours/day due to system failure. - **Not Functional / Disconnected:** Scheme abandoned or source dried up. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Error:** Confusing "Partially Functional" with "Non-Functional". Partial functionality **still counts as functionality** for JJM reporting — the focus is on restoring the deficient component (e.g., pump repair, leak fix), not rebuilding the scheme. ## 2.3 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) — M&E Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Memorise These Equations:** | # | Indicator | Formula / Definition | Target / Unit | KTU Tag | |---|-----------|---------------------|---------------|---------| | 1 | **Coverage** | (Households with FHTC / Total Households) × 100 | % (Target: 100%) | High Yield | | 2 | **lpcd Served** | (Total Volume Supplied per Day / Total Beneficiary Population) | litres per capita per day (Target: 55) | High Yield | | 3 | **Functionality %** | (Number of Functional Schemes / Total Schemes) × 100 | % | High Yield | | 4 | **Hours of Supply** | Weighted average of hours supply is available at consumer end | hours/day (Target: 24×7 in JJM) | High Yield | | 5 | **Water Quality Compliance** | (Samples Passing BIS 10500 / Total Samples Tested) × 100 | % (Target: 100%) | High Yield | | 6 | **Cost Recovery Ratio** | Annual Revenue / Annual O&M Expenditure | Ratio (Target: ≥ 1.0) | Medium | | 7 | **O&M Expenditure Ratio** | (Actual O&M Spend / Budgeted O&M) × 100 | % | Medium | | 8 | **Per Capita O&M Cost** | Annual O&M Cost / Beneficiary Population | ₹ per capita per year | Medium | | 9 | **Sustainability Index** | Composite score of 4 sub-indices (Financial, Technical, Institutional, Environmental) | 0 to 1 | High Yield | | 10 | **Downtime Ratio** | (Hours of System Failure / Total Hours in Year) × 100 | % | Medium | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note for Tables:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol is intentionally avoided inside mathematical cell expressions. All absolute or dividing values use plain text spacing to preserve Markdown table integrity. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The M&E framework is operationalised in the field via: - **JJM Dashboards** (national, state, district, village-level real-time monitoring). - **IMIS** (Integrated Management Information System). - **PHEEMIS** (Public Health Engineering MIS used by Kerala Water Authority). - **mWater / JalTapp / Jal Saksham mobile apps** for community-based monitoring. - **Field Test Kits (FTKs)** for on-site bacteriological and chemical testing by 5-water testings per village per month (per JJM). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Functionality % Calculation **Problem:** A district has 240 rural water supply schemes. An M&E survey reports: - Fully Functional = 132 - Partially Functional = 68 - Non-Functional = 30 - Disconnected = 10 Compute: (a) Functionality %, (b) Failure %. **Step 1 — Identify totals and functional schemes.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Schemes} &= 132 + 68 + 30 + 10 = 240 \\ \text{Functional Schemes} &= \text{FF} + \text{PF} = 132 + 68 = 200 \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* In JJM, both Fully Functional and Partially Functional schemes are considered "functional" because they are delivering some water. Only Non-Functional and Disconnected schemes are "non-functional." **Step 2 — Calculate Functionality %.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Functionality \%} &= \frac{\text{Functional Schemes}}{\text{Total Schemes}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{200}{240} \times 100 \\ &= 83.33\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Calculate Failure %.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Failure \%} &= \frac{\text{Non-Functional} + \text{Disconnected}}{\text{Total Schemes}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{30 + 10}{240} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{40}{240} \times 100 = 16.67\% \end{aligned} $$ **Verification:** 83.33 % + 16.67 % = 100 % ✓ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — lpcd Service Level Calculation **Problem:** A Single Village Scheme (SVS) serves 1,500 people. The OHT supplies 67.5 m³/day. Compute lpcd and assess against JJM standard. **Step 1 — Convert volume to litres.** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{day}} &= 67.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 1000 \text{ L/m}^3 = 67{,}500 \text{ L/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate lpcd.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{lpcd} &= \frac{Q_{\text{day}}}{\text{Population}} = \frac{67{,}500}{1500} = 45 \text{ L/person/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compare with benchmark.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{JJM Minimum Standard} &= 55 \text{ lpcd} \\ \text{Current lpcd} &= 45 \text{ lpcd} \\ \text{Deficit} &= 55 - 45 = 10 \text{ lpcd} \end{aligned} $$ **Inference:** The scheme is **Partially Functional** (40 ≤ 45 < 55 lpcd). M&E action: augment source yield or reduce NRW (Non-Revenue Water). ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Cost Recovery Ratio (CRR) **Problem:** A Gram Panchayat Water Supply & Sanitation Committee (GPWSC) collects ₹ 4.8 lakh/year as user charges. Annual O&M cost is ₹ 6.5 lakh. Compute CRR and comment on financial sustainability. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CRR} &= \frac{\text{Annual Revenue}}{\text{Annual O\&M Cost}} \\ &= \frac{4{,}80{,}000}{6{,}50{,}000} \\ &= 0.738 \end{aligned} $$ **Inference:** Since CRR < 1.0, the scheme is **financially unsustainable** in the long term. The GPWSC must revise the tariff structure, plug illegal connections, or seek JJM viability gap funding. ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Water Quality Compliance % **Problem:** In a PWS scheme, the PHED tested 250 water samples in a quarter. 215 passed BIS 10500 for all parameters. 28 failed on residual chlorine, and 7 failed on turbidity. Compute compliance %. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Compliance \%} &= \frac{215}{250} \times 100 = 86\% \end{aligned} $$ **Inference:** 14 % failure indicates either inadequate chlorination dosing or source contamination. M&E action: recalibrate the dosing pump and conduct source protection. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Indicator-by-Indicator Evaluation Logic | Indicator | Data Source | Frequency | Tools Used | Threshold for "Pass" | |-----------|-------------|-----------|------------|----------------------| | Coverage | JJM Dashboard, Census | Monthly | GIS, Household Survey | ≥ 100 % of targeted HHs | | lpcd | Flow meters, OHT logbook | Daily | Ultrasonic flowmeter, telemetry | ≥ 55 lpcd | | Hours of Supply | Consumer survey, SCADA | Weekly | Smart meters, complaints | ≥ 8 hours (JJM short-term), 24×7 long-term | | Water Quality | District Water Quality Lab | 5 samples/village/month | FTK, H2S strip, lab analysis | 100 % BIS 10500 | | Functionality | PHED asset register | Quarterly | Field inspection, JJM App | ≥ 90 % functional | | Cost Recovery | GPWSC accounts | Annually | Audited statements | CRR ≥ 1.0 | | Sustainability | Composite Index | Annually | Field survey + financial audit | Index ≥ 0.7 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The M&E Cycle — Top-Level Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Planning and Design Stage] --> B[Implementation and Construction] B --> C[Scheme Commissioned] C --> D[Continuous Monitoring
Flow, Quality, Hours, Complaints] D --> E{Performance vs SLB
Comparison} E -->|Below Standard| F[Corrective Action
Repair, Dose Adjust, NRW Reduction] F --> D E -->|Meets Standard| G[Annual Third Party Evaluation
SLB Scoring, Sustainability Index] G --> H[Feedback to Design Stage
Lessons Learnt Document] H --> A G --> I[Reporting to State and National JJM Dashboard] I --> J[Incentive and Penalty Decision] ``` ## 4.2 Service Level Benchmark Scoring Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SDI[Service Delivery Indicators] A1[Coverage] --> A5[SLB Score Service] A2[Functionality] --> A5 A3[Water Quality] --> A5 A4[Regularity Hours] --> A5 end subgraph II[Institutional Indicators] B1[O and M Staffing] --> B5[SLB Score Institutional] B2[User Charges] --> B5 B3[Asset Management Plan] --> B5 B4[Women in GPWSC] --> B5 end A5 --> C[Composite SLB Score 0 to 100] B5 --> C C --> D{Village Ranking on JJM Dashboard} D -->|Above 90| E[Blue Category Excellent] D -->|75 to 90| F[Green Category Good] D -->|60 to 75| G[Yellow Category Needs Improvement] D -->|Below 60| H[Red Category Critical Action Required] ``` ## 4.3 Sustainability Index Composition ```mermaid flowchart TD SUST[Rural Water Supply Sustainability Index] --> FIN[Financial Sub Index
Tariff Recovery, Budget Allocation] SUST --> TEC[Technical Sub Index
Asset Condition, Functionality, NRW] SUST --> INS[Institutional Sub Index
GPWSC Strength, Womens Participation] SUST --> ENV[Environmental Sub Index
Source Sustainability, Catchment Protection] FIN --> S[Weighted Composite Score] TEC --> S INS --> S ENV --> S S --> OUT{Index Score Interpretation} OUT --> P1[Greater than 0.8 Highly Sustainable] OUT --> P2[0.6 to 0.8 Moderately Sustainable] OUT --> P3[0.4 to 0.6 At Risk] OUT --> P4[Less than 0.4 Not Sustainable Immediate Intervention] ``` ## 4.4 Field Data Flow for M&E ```mermaid flowchart LR V[Village Level 5 Water Tests per Month] --> GP[Gram Panchayat Upload to JJM App] GP --> BL[Block Level Consolidation by Block Coordinator] BL --> DT[District Water Quality and Monitoring Cell] DT --> ST[State Water Sanitation Mission SWSM] ST --> NJ[National JJM Dashboard Delhi] PHED[PHEED Asset and Flow Data] --> DT CONS[Consumer Satisfaction Survey Quarterly] --> DT ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **List any five Service Level Benchmarks (SLBs) for rural water supply as per the Jal Jeevan Mission framework.** **Model Answer:** The five service level benchmarks under JJM are: 1. **Coverage** — Percentage of rural households with Functional Household Tap Connections (FHTCs). 2. **Quantity Supplied** — Litres per capita per day (lpcd) of water delivered, benchmarked at **55 lpcd**. 3. **Regularity of Supply** — Hours of supply per day at the consumer end (target: 8 hours short-term, 24×7 long-term). 4. **Water Quality** — Percentage of samples conforming to **IS 10500:2012** drinking water specifications. 5. **Functionality** — Percentage of schemes in fully or partially functional condition delivering the designed quantity. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between Monitoring and Evaluation in the context of rural water supply schemes.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Monitoring | Evaluation | |-----------|------------|------------| | **Timing** | Continuous, ongoing | Periodic, episodic (mid-term, end-term) | | **Focus** | Inputs, activities, outputs | Outcomes, impact, sustainability | | **Purpose** | Tracks day-to-day performance | Judges overall effectiveness and value-for-money | | **Data** | Routine operational data | Survey, audit, and impact data | | **Frequency** | Daily to monthly | Annually or at scheme milestones | | **Conducted by** | Scheme operator, GPWSC, PHED | Third-party agency, SWSM, independent consultants | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a) Explain the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) Service Level Benchmark framework in detail. List the five service delivery indicators and five institutional indicators. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The Service Level Benchmark (SLB) framework, adapted by the Ministry of Jal Shakti under the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM), provides a standardised yardstick to compare the performance of rural water supply schemes across villages, blocks, districts, and states. The framework is divided into two indicator groups. **A. Five Service Delivery Indicators (relate to the consumer):** 1. **Coverage** — % of rural households provided with Functional Household Tap Connections (FHTC). Target: 100 % by 2024 (extended to 2028 in some states). 2. **Quantity of Water Supplied (lpcd)** — Volumetric supply per beneficiary per day. Minimum standard = **55 lpcd** as per IS 1172. 3. **Regularity of Supply** — Hours of supply available at the consumer tap per day. JJM short-term target: minimum 8 hours; long-term: 24×7 pressurised supply. 4. **Water Quality** — % of samples (taken 5 per village per month) complying with **IS 10500:2012** across all physico-chemical and bacteriological parameters. 5. **Functionality of Scheme** — % of schemes in fully or partially functional state, assessed quarterly through the JJM mobile app and field inspection. **B. Five Institutional Indicators (relate to the service provider):** 1. **Operation & Maintenance Staffing** — Existence of dedicated, trained O&M staff at scheme level. 2. **User Charges & Cost Recovery** — Tariff is being collected and CRR ≥ 1.0. 3. **Asset Management Plan (AMP)** — A documented plan exists for repair, replacement, and life-cycle management of all assets. 4. **O&M Expenditure Utilisation** — % of allocated O&M budget actually spent (target ≥ 90 %). 5. **Women Participation in GPWSC** — At least 50 % representation of women in the Gram Panchayat Water Supply & Sanitation Committee. These 10 indicators are aggregated to give a composite **SLB score (0–100)** that is published on the JJM dashboard and forms the basis of state-level ranking and incentive allocation. --- **(b) A district has the following inventory of 200 rural water supply schemes. Compute the Functionality % and Comment on the M&E action required.** | Category | Count | |---|---| | Fully Functional | 110 | | Partially Functional | 55 | | Non-Functional | 25 | | Disconnected | 10 | **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Identify functional schemes.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Functional} &= \text{FF} + \text{PF} = 110 + 55 = 165 \\ \text{Non-Functional} &= 25 + 10 = 35 \\ \text{Total} &= 165 + 35 = 200 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate Functionality %.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Functionality \%} &= \frac{165}{200} \times 100 = 82.5\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — M&E Action Required.** - The district's functionality is **82.5 %**, which is **below the JJM target of 100 %** functional schemes. - The **25 Non-Functional** schemes require immediate **asset condition survey** to identify causes (pump failure, dried source, pipeline damage, electrical failure). - The **10 Disconnected** schemes need source restoration or scheme merger decisions. - **Partially Functional schemes (55)** require minor repair interventions (chlorinator, valve replacement, leak repair) to upgrade to Fully Functional status. - The District Water & Sanitation Mission (DWSM) must prepare a **revival action plan** with a 90-day timeline and upload progress to the JJM dashboard weekly. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a) Discuss the concept of Sustainability Index for rural water supply systems. Explain its four sub-indices with their indicators. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The **Sustainability Index (SI)** is a composite metric used in M&E to assess the long-term viability of a rural water supply scheme beyond initial commissioning. While coverage and functionality measure immediate service delivery, the Sustainability Index measures whether the scheme can continue to deliver over its full design life of **30+ years**. The Index is computed as a weighted average of four sub-indices, each scored from **0 to 1**: **1. Financial Sub-Index (Weight 30 %)** - Cost Recovery Ratio (CRR) - Timely release and utilisation of O&M budget - Adequacy of tariff relative to O&M cost **2. Technical Sub-Index (Weight 30 %)** - Asset condition (intake, OHT, pumps, mains, distribution) - Non-Revenue Water (NRW) percentage - Functionality status of scheme components **3. Institutional Sub-Index (Weight 20 %)** - Strength of GPWSC (elections, meetings, record-keeping) - Trained manpower availability - Existence of Asset Management Plan (AMP) **4. Environmental Sub-Index (Weight 20 %)** - Source yield trend over the last 5 years - Catchment area protection - Groundwater recharge measures - Climate resilience (drought/flood risk) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SI} &= 0.30 \times S_{\text{fin}} + 0.30 \times S_{\text{tech}} + 0.20 \times S_{\text{inst}} + 0.20 \times S_{\text{env}} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** - SI ≥ 0.8 → Highly Sustainable - 0.6 ≤ SI < 0.8 → Moderately Sustainable - 0.4 ≤ SI < 0.6 → At Risk - SI < 0.4 → Not Sustainable (Immediate intervention required) --- **(b) A GPWSC reports the following data for a Multi-Village Scheme (MVS): Total population served = 12,000; OHT capacity = 6 lakh litres; actual daily supply = 5.04 lakh litres. Compute the lpcd served and assess the scheme against JJM quantity benchmark. Also calculate the cost recovery ratio given annual revenue of ₹ 9 lakh and annual O&M cost of ₹ 12 lakh. Comment on financial sustainability. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Convert supply to litres.** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{day}} &= 5.04 \text{ Lakh L/day} = 5.04 \times 10^5 \text{ L/day} = 504{,}000 \text{ L/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate lpcd.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{lpcd} &= \frac{504{,}000}{12{,}000} = 42 \text{ L/person/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compare with benchmark.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{JJM Standard} &= 55 \text{ lpcd} \\ \text{Deficit} &= 55 - 42 = 13 \text{ lpcd} \\ \text{Status} &= \text{Partially Functional (PF) since } 40 \le 42 < 55 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Cost Recovery Ratio.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CRR} &= \frac{\text{Revenue}}{\text{O\&M Cost}} = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} = 0.75 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Comments on Sustainability.** - The scheme provides only **42 lpcd**, falling **13 lpcd short** of the JJM benchmark → **Partially Functional**. - The **CRR of 0.75** means revenue covers only 75 % of O&M cost → **Financially unsustainable** in the long term. - M&E recommendations: 1. Augment source yield or reduce NRW (leak detection, ILI survey) to raise lpcd to 55. 2. Revise tariff to achieve CRR ≥ 1.0, with cross-subsidisation for BPL families. 3. Submit a sustainability revival plan to the SWSM for technical and financial handholding. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:** > 1. **Miscalculating Functionality %:** Many students add FF + PF + NF and divide by FF+PF — this is **wrong**. Correct formula is (FF+PF)/Total × 100. > 2. **Mixing units:** lpcd requires volume in **litres/day** and population in **persons**. Converting m³ → litres (× 1000) is the most common place students lose 1 mark. > 3. **Ignoring the "5 + 5" structure:** The SLB framework has exactly **5 service delivery + 5 institutional** indicators. Writing 6 or 4 is a typical mistake. > 4. **Forgetting CRR target:** JJM requires CRR ≥ 1.0 for financial sustainability; merely stating "CRR is low" without referencing the threshold is considered incomplete. > 5. **M&E vs. Audit confusion:** Evaluation is **not** a financial audit — it covers technical, financial, institutional, and environmental performance holistically. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **Important Things to Remember (KTU 2024 Module 2 — M&E):** - **M&E = Monitoring (continuous) + Evaluation (periodic)** for rural water supply. - The **SLB framework** has **5 service delivery** + **5 institutional** indicators. - **Coverage**, **Functionality**, **Water Quality**, **Regularity**, and **Quantity (lpcd)** are the 5 service delivery pillars. - **Functionality %** = (FF + PF) / Total × 100; **NF + Disconnected** are non-functional. - **JJM quantity benchmark** = **55 lpcd** (per IS 1172:1993). - **Hours of supply benchmark** = ≥ 8 hours/day (short-term), 24×7 (long-term). - **Water quality benchmark** = **IS 10500:2012**; minimum **5 samples per village per month** under JJM. - **Cost Recovery Ratio (CRR) ≥ 1.0** = financially sustainable; < 1.0 = subsidised/unsustainable. - **Sustainability Index** = weighted average of 4 sub-indices: Financial (30 %), Technical (30 %), Institutional (20 %), Environmental (20 %). - **5 + 10 scoring**: SLB composite score published on JJM dashboard ranks villages/districts/states. - **GPWSC** = institutional body responsible for scheme-level M&E at village. - **mWater, JalTapp, JJM App, IMIS, PHEEMIS** are key digital M&E platforms. - **Third-Party Monitoring (TPM)** is mandatory under JJM at key milestones. - **Logical Framework (LogFrame)** approach links Inputs → Activities → Outputs → Outcomes → Impact. - A scheme with **CRR < 1** and **SI < 0.6** is classified **"At Risk"** and requires immediate DWSM intervention. - **Equity indicator** = proportion of SC/ST/AAY households covered relative to their share in the village population. - **Sustainability threshold** of **30 years** is the typical design life of PWS assets (intake, OHT, pump, mains).

topics.title: Importance of Sanitation for Public Health and Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Importance of Sanitation for Public Health and Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanitation (WHO Definition):** The provision of facilities and services for the safe disposal of human urine and faeces, together with the maintenance of hygienic conditions, through services such as garbage collection and wastewater disposal, so as to break the cycle of disease.

> [!NOTE]
> **Public Health (Winslow, 1920):** "The science and art of preventing disease, prolonging life, and promoting health through the organized efforts and informed choices of society, organizations, public and private communities, and individuals."

> [!NOTE]
> **Environmental Sanitation:** The control of all those factors in man's physical environment which exercise a deleterious effect on his physical, mental and social well-being. It includes domestic water supply, sullage disposal, refuse disposal, excreta disposal, vector control, food hygiene, and housing.

### 1.2 Classification of Sanitation (UN-JMP Framework)

| Category | Description |
|---|---|
| **Basic Sanitation** | Improved facilities not shared with other households |
| **Limited Sanitation** | Improved facilities shared between two or more households |
| **Unimproved Sanitation** | Pit latrines without slab, open pits, hanging latrines |
| **Open Defecation** | Disposal of human faeces in fields, forests, water bodies, etc. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Global Statistics (WHO/UNICEF JMP 2023):**
> - **4.2 billion** people lack safely managed sanitation
> - **494 million** people still practice open defecation
> - **829,000 deaths** annually attributed to inadequate WASH (Water, Sanitation, Hygiene)
> - **1.4 million** deaths could be prevented annually with universal WASH access
> - **Diarrhoeal diseases** account for **1.7 million** deaths per year globally

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The City as a Living Body":**
> Think of a community as a giant human body. Just as a body has kidneys to filter out waste and a skin to block pathogens, a community needs **sanitation infrastructure** (sewers, latrines, treatment plants) to filter out human waste and a **hygiene barrier** (handwashing, clean water) to block disease-causing pathogens. When sanitation fails, it is exactly like a body's immune system collapsing — pathogens leak into the "circulatory system" (water supply), spread through "veins" (rivers and groundwater), and infect the entire "body" (the population). The F-diagram of disease transmission is essentially showing you where in this biological community the "wound" is open.

### 1.4 Historical Perspective

The **sanitation revolution** began in the mid-19th century. **Dr. John Snow's** work on cholera in London (1854) demonstrated that contaminated water transmitted disease. **Sir Edwin Chadwick's** "Report on the Sanitary Condition of the Labouring Population" (1842) established that sanitation was a public health priority, leading to the **Public Health Act of 1848** in the UK. This marked the formal beginning of sanitation engineering as a discipline.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The F-Diagram of Faecal-Oral Disease Transmission

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The F-Diagram** is the single most important conceptual model in sanitation engineering. It illustrates the multiple pathways by which pathogens from human faeces reach a new host. Every sanitation intervention targets one or more of these pathways.

The five primary "F" pathways are:

1. **Faeces** — the source/reservoir of pathogens
2. **Fluids** — waterborne transmission (drinking, bathing, washing)
3. **Fields** — soil contamination (crops, vegetables, geophagy)
4. **Flies** — vector-borne transmission (faeces → food)
5. **Fingers** — person-to-person contact (poor hygiene)

Secondary "F" pathways include: **Food, Floods, Fomites (inanimate objects), and Flies (mechanical vectors).**

### 2.2 Sanitation Barriers (Multi-Barrier Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Multi-Barrier Approach** is a defence-in-depth strategy where multiple independent barriers prevent pathogen transmission. Even if one barrier fails, others provide continued protection.

| Barrier Level | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1st Barrier (Primary)** | Containment of faeces | Latrines, toilets, septic tanks |
| **2nd Barrier** | Treatment of waste | Anaerobic digesters, drying beds |
| **3rd Barrier** | Safe reuse/disposal | Composting, safe discharge |
| **4th Barrier** | Personal hygiene | Handwashing with soap |
| **5th Barrier** | Safe water supply | Protected wells, treated water |

### 2.3 Disease Classification (Sanitation-Related)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Disease Groups Associated with Poor Sanitation:**

- **Faecal-Oral Diseases:** Diarrhoea, cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A & E, polio
- **Vector-Borne Diseases:** Malaria, dengue (linked to stagnant water/poor drainage)
- **Geohelminth (Worm) Infections:** Roundworm (*Ascaris*), hookworm, whipworm, schistosomiasis
- **Skin & Eye Infections:** Trachoma, scabies, fungal infections (linked to poor personal hygiene)

### 2.4 Economic Impact Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **WHO returns-on-investment estimates:** Every **US$ 1** invested in sanitation yields a return of **US$ 5.50** through reduced healthcare costs and increased productivity (Hutton, 2012 framework).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Equations and Metrics for KTU Examinations:**

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year)** | $\text{DALY} = \text{YLL} + \text{YLD}$ | Years | Disease burden quantification |
| **YLL (Years of Life Lost)** | $\text{YLL} = \sum d_x \cdot e_x$ | Years | Premature mortality |
| **YLD (Years Lived with Disability)** | $\text{YLD} = \sum i \cdot dw \cdot t$ | Years | Morbidity burden |
| **Mortality Rate (Water-related)** | $\text{MR} = \dfrac{D_{WR}}{P} \times 10^5$ | per 100,000 | Public health metric |
| **Open Defecation Rate (ODR)** | $\text{ODR} = \dfrac{N_{OD}}{N_{HH}} \times 100$ | % | Coverage gap indicator |
| **Sanitation Coverage** | $\text{SC} = \dfrac{N_{HH,\text{imp}}}{N_{HH,\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | SDG 6.2 indicator |
| **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | $\text{BCR} = \dfrac{\text{PV Benefits}}{\text{PV Costs}}$ | ratio | Economic justification |
| **Reduction in Disease Incidence** | $\Delta I = I_0 - I_i = I_0 \cdot (1 - RR)$ | cases/year | Intervention impact |

Where:
- $d_x$ = number of deaths at age $x$
- $e_x$ = life expectancy at age $x$
- $i$ = incidence, $dw$ = disability weight, $t$ = duration
- $D_{WR}$ = water-related deaths, $P$ = population
- $N_{OD}$ = households practicing open defecation
- $N_{HH}$ = total households
- $RR$ = relative risk reduction from intervention

### 2.6 The Sanitation Service Ladder (JMP 2023)

> [!NOTE]
> The **JMP Sanitation Ladder** ranks service levels from least to most safe:

1. **Surface water disposal** (worst) — disposal directly into water bodies
2. **Open defecation** — in fields, forests, beaches
3. **Unimproved facilities** — pit latrines without slab, hanging toilets
4. **Limited service** — improved facility shared between ≥ 2 households
5. **Basic service** — improved facility not shared with other households
6. **Safely managed service** (best) — excreta safely contained and treated off-site

### 2.7 Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) Linkage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SDG 6.2 Targets:** By **2030**, achieve access to adequate and equitable sanitation and hygiene for all, and end open defecation, paying special attention to the needs of women and girls and those in vulnerable situations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Estimating Disease Burden Reduction from Sanitation

**Problem Context:** A rural district of 500,000 people has a baseline diarrhoeal disease incidence of 2.5 episodes per person-year. Studies show improved sanitation reduces diarrhoeal disease by 36%. Calculate the expected reduction in episodes and economic savings.

**Given:**
- Population, $P = 500{,}000$ persons
- Baseline incidence rate, $I_0 = 2.5$ episodes/person-year
- Relative Risk Reduction, $RR = 0.36$ (from sanitation intervention)
- Average cost per diarrhoeal episode (treatment + lost productivity) = ₹500

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate baseline number of episodes per year.

$$
N_0 = I_0 \times P
$$

Substituting values:

$$
N_0 = 2.5 \times 500{,}000 = 1{,}250{,}000 \text{ episodes/year}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate reduction in episodes.

$$
\Delta N = N_0 \times RR
$$

$$
\Delta N = 1{,}250{,}000 \times 0.36 = 450{,}000 \text{ episodes/year averted}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate new incidence after intervention.

$$
N_i = N_0 - \Delta N = 1{,}250{,}000 - 450{,}000 = 800{,}000 \text{ episodes/year}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify using the relative risk formula.

$$
N_i = N_0 \times (1 - RR)
$$

$$
N_i = 1{,}250{,}000 \times (1 - 0.36) = 1{,}250{,}000 \times 0.64 = 800{,}000 \text{ episodes/year} \checkmark
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate annual economic benefit.

$$
E_{\text{saved}} = \Delta N \times C_{\text{episode}}
$$

$$
E_{\text{saved}} = 450{,}000 \times 500 = \text{₹} 22{,}50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹} 22.5 \text{ crore/year}
$$

**Result:** Improved sanitation in this district would prevent **450,000 episodes** of diarrhoea annually and save **₹ 22.5 crore** in direct and indirect costs.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Calculating DALYs from Sanitation-Related Deaths

**Context:** A region reports 1,200 annual deaths from waterborne diseases and 8,500 cases of severe morbidity (average 7 days per case, disability weight 0.25). Average life expectancy = 70 years; mean age at death = 35 years.

**Step 1:** Calculate Years of Life Lost (YLL).

$$
\text{YLL} = \sum (d_x \times e_x)
$$

$$
\text{YLL} = 1{,}200 \times (70 - 35) = 1{,}200 \times 35 = 42{,}000 \text{ person-years}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate Years Lived with Disability (YLD).

$$
\text{YLD} = \sum (i \times dw \times t)
$$

$$
\text{YLD} = 8{,}500 \times 0.25 \times \left(\frac{7}{365}\right)
$$

$$
\text{YLD} = 8{,}500 \times 0.25 \times 0.01918 = 40.75 \text{ person-years}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate total DALYs.

$$
\text{DALY} = \text{YLL} + \text{YLD} = 42{,}000 + 40.75 \approx 42{,}041 \text{ DALYs/year}
$$

**Result:** The total disease burden equals approximately **42,041 DALYs/year** — equivalent to losing 42,041 healthy years of life annually in this region.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Linking Sanitation to Public Health Outcomes

> [!NOTE]
> **The Sanitation–Public Health–Environment Causal Chain:**

| Level | Component | Mechanism | Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Sanitation infrastructure | Safe containment/treatment | Toilet coverage (%) |
| **Process** | Behaviour & hygiene | Pathogen interruption | Handwashing rate (%) |
| **Output** | Reduced exposure | Lower pathogen load in environment | E. coli count (CFU/100 mL) |
| **Outcome** | Reduced morbidity | Fewer disease episodes | Incidence rate |
| **Impact** | Reduced mortality & DALYs | Healthy population | Life expectancy, GDP contribution |

---

### 3.4 Environmental Impact Analysis (Quantified)

| Environmental Compartment | Without Sanitation | With Sanitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface water** | Faecal coliforms: $10^5$–$10^7$ CFU/100 mL | $< 10^3$ CFU/100 mL (compliant) |
| **Groundwater** | Nitrate: $> 50$ mg/L; Coliforms: present | Nitrate: $< 10$ mg/L; Coliforms: absent |
| **Soil** | Helminth eggs: $> 200$ eggs/g | $< 1$ egg/g (after treatment) |
| **Air quality (odour)** | H$_2$S, NH$_3$ detected | Negligible when sealed |
| **Vector population** | High fly/mosquito density | Reduced breeding sites |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WHO Drinking Water Standard for Faecal Coliforms:** **0 CFU/100 mL** (must not be detectable in any 100 mL sample).

---

### 3.5 Why Sanitation Matters: The Three Pillars Argument

**Pillar 1 — Public Health:**
- Prevents **diarrhoeal diseases** (40% reduction with improved sanitation per Fewtrell et al., 2005 meta-analysis)
- Prevents **helminth infections** (roundworm, hookworm, whipworm)
- Prevents **trachoma** (60% reduction, Emerson et al., 2000)
- Reduces **child mortality** (under-5 mortality drops by 7.9% per 10% increase in toilet coverage)

**Pillar 2 — Environment:**
- Protects **water bodies** from faecal pollution
- Prevents **eutrophication** of lakes and rivers
- Protects **groundwater** aquifers
- Reduces **greenhouse gas emissions** through proper methane capture (in anaerobic systems)
- Enables **safe nutrient recycling** (recycling of N, P, K in agriculture)

**Pillar 3 — Socio-Economic Development:**
- Saves **healthcare expenditure** (₹ 22.5 crore example above)
- Increases **school attendance** (especially for girls, during menstruation)
- Saves **time** (estimated 1 billion hours/day globally spent on open defecation, WHO)
- Enhances **dignity and safety** (reduces sexual assault during open defecation)
- Boosts **tourism and property values** in clean areas

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
