**GIS-based mapping** of sources, habitations, and infrastructure #### Phase 2 — Feasibility Study & Technical Options Analysis - **Source feasibility**: yield testing, water quality (IS 10500), aquifer mapping - **Technology selection matrix** considering: - Topography (gravity vs. pumped) - Source yield vs. demand - Power availability & reliability - Capital and O&M cost - Community technical capacity #### Phase 3 — Detailed Project Report (DPR) Preparation - **Hydraulic design** of conveyance, treatment, storage, distribution - **Cost estimates** (as per CPWD/State PWD/DSR — District Schedule of Rates) - **Financial plan**: convergence with MGNREGA, SBM-G, 15th FC, CSR, NABARD - **Environmental & Social Safeguard** screening #### Phase 4 — Appraisal & Approval - **Technical Sanction** by competent authority (Executive Engineer / Superintending Engineer) - **Administrative Sanction** by funding agency - **Tender processing** through e-procurement (GeM / state portals) #### Phase 5 — Implementation & Construction - **Contract management** with milestones, quality control (IS codes) - **IEC (Information, Education, Communication)** for community behavior change - **Skill development** of local masons, plumbers - **Convergence** with other departments (irrigation, rural roads, power) #### Phase 6 — Operation, Maintenance & Monitoring - **Handing over** to Gram Panchayat with **5-year O&M plan** - **Asset inventory** and **GIS tagging** - **Water Quality Monitoring & Surveillance (WQMS)** — 5 NABL-accredited labs per district + field test kits - **Annual audit & social audit** - **Performance indicators**: % FHTC, water quality compliance, % revenue recovery, hours of supply, % grievances resolved > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Focus:** The "**Project Cycle Approach**" is the single most-tested topic from this module. Memorize the **6 phases**, the **role of VWSC**, and the **flow of funds** (Centre → State → District → Block → GP → VWSC). ### 2.2 Institutional & Stakeholder Framework The **multi-tier institutional structure** for rural water supply in India is: | Tier | Body | Primary Role | |---|---|---| | National | Ministry of Jal Shakti, Department of Drinking Water & Sanitation (DDWS) | Policy, funding, monitoring | | State | Kerala Water Authority (KWA), Suchitwa Mission, KRWSA (Kerala Rural Water Supply & Sanitation Agency) | Implementation, technical support | | District | District Water & Sanitation Mission (DWSM) | Convergence, planning, quality lab | | Block | Block Resource Centre (BRC) | Capacity building, supervision | | Gram Panchayat | Standing Committee on Water & Sanitation | Approval, O&M oversight | | Village | VWSC / Paani Samiti | O&M, tariff collection, day-to-day management | > [!NOTE] > In Kerala, the **Kerala Rural Water Supply & Sanitation Agency (KRWSA)** under the **Jalanidhi** program pioneered community-managed rural water supply. The **Haritha Keralam Mission** integrates water, sanitation, and waste management at the GP level. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes / Standards | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Geometric population forecast** | $P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n$ | persons | Used when growth rate $r$ is constant | | 2 | **Arithmetic increase method** | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | persons | $\bar{x}$ = average decadal increase | | 3 | **Geometric increase method** | $P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n$ | persons | $r$ = decadal growth rate (%) | | 4 | **Incremental increase method** | $P_n = P_0 + n\bar{x} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y}$ | persons | $\bar{y}$ = avg. increment in increment | | 5 | **Logistic (S-curve) method** | $P_n = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + m \cdot e^{-kn}}$ | persons | $P_s$ = saturation population | | 6 | **Per capita demand (Design)** | $q_d = (\text{LPCD}) \times \text{PF} \times \text{UF}$ | Lpcd | PF = peak factor, UF = UFW factor | | 7 | **Average daily demand** | $Q_{avg} = \dfrac{P_n \cdot q_d}{1000}$ | m³/day | | | 8 | **Maximum daily demand** | $Q_{max} = Q_{avg} \times \text{MDDF}$ | m³/day | MDDF = 1.5–1.8 (rural); 1.2–1.5 (urban) | | 9 | **Peak hourly demand** | $Q_{peak} = Q_{max} \times \text{PHF}$ | m³/day | PHF = 1.5–2.0 | | 10 | **Source yield check** | $Q_{yield} \geq Q_{peak} \times (1 + \text{UFW fraction})$ | m³/day | UFW = 15–25% for new systems | | 11 | **Storage capacity (sum of)** | $V_{total} = V_{eq} + V_{fire} + V_{emergency}$ | m³ | $V_{eq}$ = balancing storage (typically 1/3 of $Q_{max}$) | | 12 | **Equivalent balancing storage** | $V_{eq} = 0.25 \text{ to } 0.30 \cdot Q_{max}$ | m³ | CPHEEO manual | | 13 | **Head loss (Hazen-Williams)** | $h_f = 10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852} \cdot C^{-1.852} \cdot d^{-4.87}$ | m | $Q$ in m³/s, $d$ in m | | 14 | **Cost per capita (capital)** | $C_{cap} = \dfrac{\text{Total capital cost}}{P_{design}}$ | ₹/person | Used for benchmarking | | 15 | **Annualized O&M cost** | $C_{O\&M} = \dfrac{\text{Annual O\&M expense}}{P_{served} \times 365}$ | ₹/Lpcd | For tariff fixing | | 16 | **Tariff (block rate)** | $T = T_0 + (V - V_0) \cdot r_{inc}$ | ₹ | Progressive block tariff | | 17 | **Pay-back period** | $T_{pb} = \dfrac{\text{Initial investment}}{\text{Annual net cash inflow}}$ | years | | | 18 | **Net Present Value** | $NPV = \sum \dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | ₹ | $i$ = discount rate | | 19 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio** | $BCR = \dfrac{\sum B_t / (1+i)^t}{\sum C_t / (1+i)^t}$ | — | $BCR \geq 1 \Rightarrow$ feasible | | 20 | **Service coverage** | $SC = \dfrac{N_{FHTC}}{N_{households}} \times 100$ | % | JJM KPI | | 21 | **Sustainability index** | $SI = w_1 \cdot F_{tech} + w_2 \cdot F_{fin} + w_3 \cdot F_{inst} + w_4 \cdot F_{env}$ | 0–1 | $w_i$ = weights, $F$ = scores | > [!CAUTION] > **Markdown Table Symbol Notice:** All absolute-value / parallel notations are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent the vertical pipe `|` from breaking table rendering. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility | Sector | Application | |---|---| | **Public Health** | Reduction in waterborne diseases (diarrhea, cholera, typhoid) by 40–60% with FHTCs | | **Women Empowerment** | Saves 30–90 minutes/day per household in water collection | | **Education** | Increased school attendance, especially of girls | | **Rural Economy** | Reduces drudgery, enables micro-enterprises (dairy, food processing) | | **Disaster Resilience** | Multi-village schemes with redundancy reduce climate-related risk | | **SDG Alignment** | Direct contribution to **SDG 6** (Clean Water & Sanitation) and **SDG 5, 3, 4, 10** | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Population Forecasting — Incremental Increase Method (Most Asked in KTU) > **Why this method?** Rural populations show **decelerating growth** as they approach saturation (urbanization pulls people away). Incremental increase is the KTU-preferred method for rural design over a 15–30 year horizon. **Given:** - $P_0 = 12{,}000$ (current population, base year) - Last 3 decades' populations: 9,500 (3 decades ago), 10,800 (2 decades ago), 12,000 (1 decade ago) - Design period $n = 15$ years - Decadal growth rate not constant — use **incremental increase** **Step 1 — Compute decadal increase $\bar{x}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} x_1 &= 10{,}800 - 9{,}500 = 1{,}300 \\ x_2 &= 12{,}000 - 10{,}800 = 1{,}200 \\ \bar{x} &= \frac{x_1 + x_2}{2} = \frac{1{,}300 + 1{,}200}{2} = 1{,}250 \text{ persons/decade} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute increment in decadal increase $\bar{y}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} y_1 &= x_2 - x_1 = 1{,}200 - 1{,}300 = -100 \text{ (decelerating)} \\ \bar{y} &= \frac{y_1}{1} = -100 \text{ persons/decade per decade} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the incremental increase formula (n = 1.5 decades):** $$ \begin{aligned} P_n &= P_0 + n\bar{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y} \\ P_{15} &= 12{,}000 + (1.5)(1{,}250) + \frac{(1.5)(1.5+1)}{2}(-100) \\ &= 12{,}000 + 1{,}875 + \frac{(1.5)(2.5)}{2}(-100) \\ &= 12{,}000 + 1{,}875 + (1.875)(-100) \\ &= 12{,}000 + 1{,}875 - 187.5 \\ &= 13{,}687.5 \approx 13{,}688 \text{ persons} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** The population of the village is expected to be **~13,688** at the end of the design period. Note the negative $\bar{y}$ reflects rural-urban migration — a realistic KTU-relevant insight. ### 3.2 Design Demand Calculation (Full Worked Example) **Given (continuing the above):** - $P_{15} = 13{,}688$ persons - Per capita supply (Kerala norm) = **70 LPCD** - Peak factor (PF) = **1.5** (rural intermittent supply) - UFW (Unaccounted For Water) = **15%** (new system) - MDDF = 1.6, PHF = 1.8 **Step 1 — Average daily demand:** $$ Q_{avg} = \frac{13{,}688 \times 70}{1000} = 958.16 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 2 — Maximum daily demand:** $$ Q_{max} = 958.16 \times 1.6 = 1{,}533.06 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 3 — Peak hourly demand:** $$ Q_{peak} = 1{,}533.06 \times 1.8 = 2{,}759.51 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \quad \text{or} \quad 31.94 \text{ L/s} $$ **Step 4 — Source yield check (with UFW):** $$ Q_{required, source} = Q_{max} \times (1 + 0.15) = 1{,}533.06 \times 1.15 = 1{,}763.02 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$ > **Decision rule:** Select a source (well/scheme intake) whose sustainable yield $\geq 1{,}763 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ and includes a 20% safety margin → design yield ≥ **2{,}116 m³/day**. ### 3.3 Storage Tank Sizing (Overhead Tank — Sum of Components) **Step 1 — Equivalent balancing storage (one-third of max day demand):** $$ V_{eq} = \frac{1}{3} \times Q_{max} = \frac{1}{3} \times 1{,}533.06 = 511.02 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 2 — Fire storage (rural: assume 1 fire hydrant × 2 hrs × 1 L/s):** $$ V_{fire} = 1 \times 2 \times 3600 \times \frac{1}{1000} = 7.2 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 3 — Emergency storage (15% of $V_{eq}$ for breakdowns/quality issues):** $$ V_{emergency} = 0.15 \times 511.02 = 76.65 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 4 — Total storage:** $$ V_{total} = V_{eq} + V_{fire} + V_{emergency} = 511.02 + 7.2 + 76.65 = 594.87 \text{ m}^3 $$ > **Standard practice:** Round up to the nearest 50 m³ → **OHT capacity = 600 m³**. For Kerala's hilly terrain, prefer **ground-level service reservoirs (GLSR)** at high elevations with **gravity distribution**. ### 3.4 Tariff Structuring — Cost Recovery Model **Assumptions:** - Total project capital cost: ₹ 5 crore for 4 villages (~15,000 households) - Annual O&M cost: ₹ 25 lakh - Annual debt servicing (if loan): ₹ 15 lakh - Annual billable volume: $Q_{max} \times 365 = 1{,}533 \times 365 = 5{,}59{,}545$ m³ ≈ 5.6 ML/year **Step 1 — Total Annual Cost (TAC):** $$ TAC = O\&M + \text{Debt} = 25 + 15 = 40 \text{ lakh} = ₹ 40{,}00{,}000 $$ **Step 2 — Uniform volumetric tariff (if 100% recovery):** $$ T = \frac{TAC}{V_{billed}} = \frac{4{,}000{,}000}{5{,}59{,}545} = ₹ 7.15 \text{ per m}^3 \quad \text{or} \quad ₹ 0.00715/\text{L} $$ **Step 3 — Household monthly bill (average 70 LPCD × 5 persons × 30 days = 10,500 L = 10.5 m³):** $$ \text{Monthly bill} = 10.5 \times 7.15 = ₹ 75.07 \approx ₹ 75/\text{month} $$ > **Affordability check:** Should be **≤ 5% of household monthly income**. For a BPL family (₹ 3,000/month), this is 2.5% — **acceptable** under JJM guidelines. **Step 4 — Lifeline block tariff (pro-poor):** | Slab | Volume | Rate (₹/m³) | |---|---|---| | Lifeline | 0–8 m³/month | 3.00 | | Domestic | 8–20 m³/month | 7.00 | | Commercial | > 20 m³/month | 15.00 | This balances **equity** (subsidized lifeline) and **sustainability** (cross-subsidy from commercials). ### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Technology Selection | Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Gravity (Score 1–5) | Pumping (Score 1–5) | Score $\times$ Weight (G) | Score $\times$ Weight (P) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Capital cost (lower is better) | 0.20 | 3 | 2 | 0.60 | 0.40 | | O&M simplicity | 0.25 | 5 | 2 | 1.25 | 0.50 | | Power dependence | 0.20 | 5 | 1 | 1.00 | 0.20 | | Reliability | 0.15 | 4 | 3 | 0.60 | 0.45 | | Community capacity | 0.10 | 4 | 2 | 0.40 | 0.20 | | Environmental impact | 0.10 | 5 | 3 | 0.50 | 0.30 | | **Total weighted score** | **1.00** | | | **4.35** | **2.05** | > **Decision:** **Gravity-based system is preferred** (score 4.35 > 2.05) — typical for Kerala's undulating terrain with highland sources. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 RWSS Project Management Cycle (PMBOK + JJM Aligned) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Phase 1: Identification and Need Assessment
Baseline Survey, Gap Analysis, GIS Mapping] --> B[Phase 2: Feasibility Study
Source Yield Test, Technology Options, DPR Skeleton] B --> C[Phase 3: Detailed Project Report
Hydraulic Design, Cost Estimate, Financial Plan] C --> D[Phase 4: Appraisal and Approval
Technical and Administrative Sanction] D --> E[Phase 5: Implementation
Construction, Quality Control, IEC, Training] E --> F[Phase 6: Operation Maintenance and Monitoring
VWSC Ownership, Tariff, WQMS, Social Audit] F --> G[Project Closure and Handing Over to GP] G --> H[Long Term Sustainability and M and E] H -.Feedback Loop.-> A ``` ### 4.2 Institutional and Funding Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Ministry of Jal Shakti DDWS] -->|JJM Grant| B[State DWS Kerala] B -->|State Share| C[District Water and Sanitation Mission DWSM] C -->|Block Allocation| D[Gram Panchayat] D -->|Approval and Oversight| E[VWSC Paani Samiti] E -->|Tender and Execution| F[Contractor or SHG] F -->|Construction| G[In FHTC and Sanitation Assets] G -->|Handover| E E -->|Tariff and UCs| D D -->|Audit and Reports| C C -.Monitoring Data.-> A H[Convergence MGNREGA 15FC SBMG NABARD CSR] --> D I[Suchitwa Mission Kerala] --> D ``` ### 4.3 Stakeholder Responsibility Matrix (RACI Style) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Planning Phase P1[Baseline Survey] --> P2[Community Consultation] P2 --> P3[DPR Preparation] end subgraph Implementation Phase I1[Tendering] --> I2[Construction Supervision] I2 --> I3[Quality Control] I3 --> I4[Commissioning and FHTC] end subgraph O and M Phase O1[Asset Handover to GP] --> O2[VWSC O and M Plan] O2 --> O3[Water Quality Surveillance] O3 --> O4[Social and Financial Audit] end P3 --> I1 I4 --> O1 VWSC[VWSC and Paani Samiti] -.Owns.-> O2 GP[Gram Panchayat] -.Approves.-> P3 GP -.Approves.-> I1 KRWSA[KRWSA] -.Technical Support.-> P3 KRWSA -.Capacity Building.-> O2 DWSM[DWSM] -.Funds.-> I1 DWSM -.Monitors.-> O4 ``` ### 4.4 Demand-Supply Balance Schematic (Worst-Case Sizing) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Population Projection Pn] --> B[Average Daily Demand Qavg] B --> C[Apply MDDF] --> D[Max Daily Demand Qmax] D --> E[Apply PHF] --> F[Peak Hourly Qpeak] F --> G[Add UFW Allowance] --> H[Source Yield Requirement] H --> I{Available Source Yield} I -- Yes --> J[Proceed to Storage and Pumping Sizing] I -- No --> K[Demand Management or Source Augmentation] D --> L[OHT Storage Veq plus Vfire plus Vemerg] ``` ### 4.5 Tariff and Financial Sustainability Block ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Annual O and M Cost plus Debt Service] --> B[Compute Uniform Tariff] B --> C{Equity and Affordability Check} C -- Below 5 percent income --> D[Apply Slab Tariff] C -- Above 5 percent income --> E[Demand Reduction Measures] D --> F[Lifeline Block 0 to 8 m3 Low Rate] D --> G[Domestic Block 8 to 20 m3 Standard Rate] D --> H[Commercial Block above 20 m3 Cross Subsidy Rate] F --> I[Monthly Collection via VWSC] G --> I H --> I I --> J[Coverage greater than 90 percent Revenue Recovery] J --> K[Sustainable O and M and Reserve Fund] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** *Explain the concept of Village Water and Sanitation Committee (VWSC) and its role in the planning and management of rural water supply projects.* **Model Answer (Key Points, ~120 words):** The **Village Water and Sanitation Committee (VWSC)** — also called *Paani Samiti* in many states — is a **community-owned statutory body** constituted under the **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** guidelines, comprising 10–15 members with **at least 50% women** and representation from weaker sections. **Roles:** - **Planning**: prepare the **Village Action Plan (VAP)** through participatory rural appraisal - **Implementation**: supervise construction, certify quality, facilitate community contribution - **Operation & Maintenance**: operate the scheme, collect tariff, manage funds, conduct WQMS - **Monitoring**: hold quarterly *gram sabhas*, conduct social audit, maintain asset register VWSC is the **backbone of demand-driven, community-managed** rural water supply, replacing the earlier top-down government-controlled model. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** *List the six phases of the RWSS project cycle. Why is the "handing over" phase considered the most critical for long-term sustainability?* **Model Answer (Key Points, ~100 words):** The **six phases** are: 1. **Identification & Need Assessment** (baseline survey) 2. **Feasibility Study** (source, technology, options) 3. **DPR Preparation** (design, cost, financial plan) 4. **Appraisal & Approval** (technical/administrative sanction) 5. **Implementation** (construction, IEC, training) 6. **O&M and Monitoring** (VWSC handover, tariff, WQMS, social audit) **Why handover is critical:** 70% of rural water schemes in India fail within 5 years because of poor **ownership transfer**. Handover ensures the **community, VWSC, and GP** accept the asset, commit to O&M funding, and have a **5-year O&M plan** with trained manpower, sparing inventory, and an asset register. Without it, no scheme is sustainable. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Skipping population forecast justification** — always state *why* a particular method (arithmetic, geometric, incremental) is chosen for a given rural scenario. > 2. **Confusing MDDF and PHF** — MDDF is on daily demand, PHF is on hourly demand. Applying both to the same base is the #1 error. > 3. **Forgetting UFW allowance** in source sizing — a major KTU mark-loser. > 4. **Omitting community contribution %** in financial planning — JJM mandates 5% (plains) or 10% (hilly). > 5. **Not drawing diagrams** in project cycle questions — KTU awards 2 marks just for the cycle flow diagram. > 6. **Ignoring O&M** in feasibility — KTU 2024 specifically tests *sustainability*, not just design. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **Q. (a)** A rural habitation in Kerala has the following population data: 4,500 (1971), 5,200 (1981), 5,800 (1991), 6,200 (2001), 6,400 (2011). The current population (2024) is to be projected for designing a new water supply scheme. - **(i)** Compute the population at the end of the design period using the **Incremental Increase Method** (design period = 15 years, i.e., year 2039). - **(ii)** Justify the choice of this method over geometric increase for rural Kerala habitations. **(7 marks)** **Solution:** **Given data:** - $P_0$ (2024) — extrapolate from 2011: $P_{2011} = 6{,}400$, decadal growth ~3% → $P_{2024} \approx 6{,}400 \times (1.03)^{1.3} \approx 6{,}400 \times 1.039 = 6{,}650$. For simplicity, take $P_0 = 6{,}650$. - Last two decadal increases (2011–2021 ≈ 6,400 × 1.03 → 6,592; 2001–2011: 6,400–5,800 = 600; 1991–2001: 5,800–5,200 = 600; 1981–1991: 5,200–4,500 = 700). **Step 1 — Recent two decadal increases:** $$ x_1 = 6{,}200 - 5{,}800 = 400, \quad x_2 = 6{,}400 - 6{,}200 = 200 $$ **Step 2 — Average decadal increase $\bar{x}$:** $$ \bar{x} = \frac{400 + 200}{2} = 300 \text{ persons/decade} $$ **Step 3 — Increment in decadal increase $\bar{y}$:** $$ y_1 = x_2 - x_1 = 200 - 400 = -200, \quad \bar{y} = -200 $$ **Step 4 — Apply formula for $n = 1.5$ decades (15 years):** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{15} &= 6{,}650 + (1.5)(300) + \frac{(1.5)(1.5+1)}{2}(-200) \\ &= 6{,}650 + 450 + (1.875)(-200) \\ &= 6{,}650 + 450 - 375 \\ &= 6{,}725 \text{ persons} \end{aligned} $$ > **Note:** The negative $\bar{y}$ reflects Kerala's **demographic transition** — birth rates have fallen and rural-urban migration is steady. The Incremental Increase Method captures this reality. **Justification (2 marks):** - Geometric increase assumes **constant % growth** — invalid when growth is decelerating (Kerala has below-replacement TFR of 1.6). - Incremental increase accounts for the **decline in the rate of increase**, giving a **realistic and conservative** design population. - CPHEEO manual recommends incremental increase for **matured/saturated rural populations** — typical of Kerala. --- **Q. (b)** For the population projected in (a), design the **water supply scheme** with the following data: - Per capita supply: 70 LPCD - Peak factor: 1.5, MDDF: 1.6, PHF: 1.8 - UFW: 15% - Fire demand: 1 hydrant × 2 hrs × 1 L/s Compute: **(i)** Average, maximum, and peak hourly demand; **(ii)** Source yield requirement; **(iii)** OHT capacity. **(7 marks)** **Solution:** **Step 1 — Average daily demand:** $$ Q_{avg} = \frac{6{,}725 \times 70}{1000} = 470.75 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 2 — Maximum daily demand:** $$ Q_{max} = 470.75 \times 1.6 = 753.20 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 3 — Peak hourly demand:** $$ Q_{peak} = 753.20 \times 1.8 = 1{,}355.76 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \quad \text{or} \quad 15.69 \text{ L/s} $$ **Step 4 — Source yield requirement (with UFW):** $$ Q_{source} = 753.20 \times 1.15 = 866.18 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \quad \text{(+20% safety margin} = 1{,}039 \text{ m}^3/\text{day)} $$ **Step 5 — OHT storage:** $$ V_{eq} = \frac{1}{3} \times 753.20 = 251.07 \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ V_{fire} = 1 \times 2 \times 3600 \times \frac{1}{1000} = 7.2 \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ V_{emergency} = 0.15 \times 251.07 = 37.66 \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ V_{total} = 251.07 + 7.2 + 37.66 = 295.93 \text{ m}^3 \approx 300 \text{ m}^3 $$ --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** **Q. (a)** Discuss the **institutional framework** for planning and management of rural water supply and sanitation projects in India. With a neat schematic, describe the **flow of funds from Ministry of Jal Shakti to the Gram Panchayat level** under the Jal Jeevan Mission. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution Outline:** **Institutional Framework (multi-tier):** 1. **National Level:** Ministry of Jal Shakti (MoJS), Department of Drinking Water & Sanitation (DDWS), National Jal Jeevan Mission (NJJM) — apex body for policy, budget allocation, and national monitoring. 2. **State Level:** State Water & Sanitation Mission (SWSM) / Kerala: **KRWSA** (Kerala Rural Water Supply & Sanitation Agency), Kerala Water Authority (KWA), Suchitwa Mission — implements, supervises, and provides technical support. 3. **District Level:** **District Water & Sanitation Mission (DWSM)** under Zilla Parishad — convergence, planning, fund flow, water quality lab. 4. **Block Level:** **Block Resource Centre (BRC)** — capacity building, social mobilization. 5. **Gram Panchayat:** Standing Committee on Water & Sanitation — approval, monitoring. 6. **Village Level:** **VWSC / Paani Samiti** (min 10–15 members, 50% women) — owns, operates, and maintains the scheme. **Flow of Funds (JJM):** ``` MoJS (DDWS) → State Govt. (DoRD) → DWSM → Gram Panchayat → VWSC ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ 15th FC Grants State Share Block Team O&M Fund SBM-G Converge KRWSA Tech BRC Support Tariff Collection ``` **Key features of JJM funding:** - Centre-State split: **90:10** for NE/Himalayan; **50:50** for other states (60:40 for Kerala in some schemes) - **15% of funds** tied to performance on output indicators - **5% community contribution** (10% for hilly/difficult areas) - **75% of expenditure** to be incurred at GP level (decentralization mandate) > **Drawing (2 marks):** Schematic as shown in Section 4.2 of these notes (must include all 6 tiers + funding sources). --- **Q. (b)** Explain the **sustainability framework** for rural water supply projects. Discuss the **five pillars of sustainability** and their key performance indicators (KPIs) with reference to the JJM dashboard. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution Outline:** The **5-Pillar Sustainability Framework** for RWSS, as per the **Ministry of Jal Shakti's JJM Operational Guidelines (2022)**, is: | Pillar | Definition | Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) | |---|---|---| | 1. **Technical Sustainability** | Functional infrastructure with adequate O&M | % functional schemes, % FHTCs, hours of supply, water quality compliance (% samples meeting IS 10500) | | 2. **Financial Sustainability** | Cost recovery and tariff collection | Tariff collection efficiency (% billed vs. collected), % expenditure on O&M, % schemes with O&M reserve fund | | 3. **Institutional Sustainability** | VWSC functional and trained | % VWSCs trained & active, % schemes with 5-year O&M plan, social audit conducted Y/N | | 4. **Environmental Sustainability** | Source protection, WQMS | % sources with recharge structures, % habitations with WQMS testing (field test kits), nitrate/fluoride levels | | 5. **Social Sustainability & Equity** | Inclusivity, gender, vulnerable groups | % women in VWSC, % BPL/PVTG families with connections, % grievances resolved within 30 days | **JJM Dashboard (https://jaljeevanmission.gov.in):** - 16 output indicators, 9 outcome indicators - **Real-time tracking** of FHTC coverage, water quality, fund utilization - **Star rating** for districts on sustainability (5-star framework) - Quarterly **performance review** by Ministry **Sustainability Index (composite score):** $$ SI = 0.25 \cdot S_{tech} + 0.20 \cdot S_{fin} + 0.25 \cdot S_{inst} + 0.15 \cdot S_{env} + 0.15 \cdot S_{soc} $$ where each $S$ is a normalized score (0–1) and the sum of weights = 1. A scheme with $SI \geq 0.7$ is considered **sustainable** for 15+ years. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024** **Project Management & Planning** - ☐ **6 phases** of RWSS project cycle: Identification → Feasibility → DPR → Appraisal → Implementation → O&M - ☐ **PMBOK 5 process groups** mapped to RWSS: Initiation (Identification), Planning (DPR), Execution (Construction), Monitoring & Control (WQMS + Social Audit), Closure (Handover) - ☐ **Village Action Plan (VAP)** is the cornerstone of JJM planning **Institutional Framework** - ☐ **6-tier structure:** MoJS → State SWSM → DWSM → BRC → GP → VWSC - ☐ **VWSC composition:** 10–15 members, **≥ 50% women**, representation from SC/ST/women/PVTG - ☐ **KRWSA** is the nodal agency for rural water supply in Kerala - ☐ **Suchitwa Mission** handles sanitation in Kerala - ☐ **Convergence** with **MGNREGA, SBM-G, 15th FC, NABARD, CSR** **Population Forecasting (KTU-Favoured)** - ☐ **Arithmetic Increase** — constant linear growth - ☐ **Geometric Increase** — constant % growth; $P_n = P_0(1 + r/100)^n$ - ☐ **Incremental Increase** — variable % growth; preferred for **matured/decelerating** rural populations - ☐ **Logistic / S-curve** — for long-term saturation - ☐ **KTU rule:** design population = max of $P_n$ and $1.1 \times P_0$ **Demand & Design** - ☐ **LPCD:** 55 (JJM min) | 70 (Kerala norm) | 100–150 (urban) - ☐ **MDDF** = 1.5–1.8 (rural) — applied to *daily* demand - ☐ **PHF** = 1.5–2.0 — applied to *hourly* demand - ☐ **UFW** = 15–25% — added at source sizing - ☐ **OHT storage** = $V_{eq} + V_{fire} + V_{emergency}$; $V_{eq} = (1/3) \cdot Q_{max}$ - ☐ **Hazen-Williams** for pipe flow; **Darcy-Weisbach** for precise calculations **Financial & Tariff** - ☐ **Community contribution:** 5% (plains), 10% (hilly) - ☐ **Centre:State funding** = 50:50 (Kerala) or 90:10 (NE) - ☐ **Lifeline block** = 0–8 m³ at subsidized rate (equity) - ☐ **Cross-subsidy** = commercial > domestic > lifeline (efficiency + equity) - ☐ **Affordability ceiling** = **5% of household monthly income** - ☐ **BCR ≥ 1** for project to be economically viable **Sustainability (5 Pillars)** - ☐ Technical | Financial | Institutional | Environmental | Social - ☐ **JJM Star Rating** — district-level composite score - ☐ **WQMS** — 5 NABL labs/district + field test kits (5 parameters) - ☐ **Social audit** — mandatory annually by GP **Critical Standards & Norms** - ☐ **IS 10500** — drinking water quality - ☐ **CPHEEO Manual** — design standards - ☐ **NRDWP / JJM guidelines** — funding, KPIs - ☐ **Swachh Bharat Mission (Gramin)** — ODF Plus framework - ☐ **ODF Plus** = ODF + solid & liquid waste management **Common KTU Pitfalls (Avoid!)** - ❌ Using only geometric projection for Kerala villages - ❌ Forgetting UFW in source sizing - ❌ Skipping community contribution % - ❌ Treating O&M as an afterthought - ❌ Confusing MDDF and PHF application - ❌ Not drawing the institutional flow diagram (lose 2 marks!) > **Final Word:** Planning & management is the **"soft" backbone** of rural water supply — design may be perfect, but without community ownership, financial sustainability, and institutional support, the scheme will fail. KTU 2024 specifically rewards answers that integrate **technical + social + financial** dimensions. ---

topics.title: Planning Water Supply and Sanitation Projects: Needs Assessment and Feasibility Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Planning Water Supply and Sanitation Projects: Needs Assessment and Feasibility Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Project Planning** in the context of rural water supply and sanitation (WSS) is defined as a systematic, multi-stage decision-making process that establishes project objectives, identifies interventions, mobilizes resources, and sequences activities to deliver sustainable water and sanitation services to a defined rural population over a specified design horizon. It forms the precursor to detailed engineering design, procurement, and construction.

**Needs Assessment (NA)** is a diagnostic and participatory process that quantitatively and qualitatively determines the *existing* level of service, the *gap* between present conditions and desired service standards, the *causal factors* of inadequate service, and the *priorities* of the beneficiary community for water supply and sanitation infrastructure.

**Feasibility Study (FS)** is a comprehensive technical, financial, institutional, environmental, and social appraisal conducted to determine whether a proposed WSS project is *viable, sustainable, and implementable* under the prevailing geographic, economic, demographic, and socio-cultural conditions. It acts as the gatekeeper between project conceptualization and capital investment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 PECET867 syllabus (Module 4), the three pillars — **Planning, Needs Assessment, and Feasibility Study** — are the foundation blocks for any rural WSS project funded by agencies such as the **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM), MGNREGS, NABARD, or World Bank**. Skipping any of these stages typically results in project failure, asset non-utilization, or community rejection.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are planning to **build a new kitchen in your house**. Before laying a single brick, you must answer several questions:

- *What do I cook every day, and for how many people?* (this is the **Needs Assessment** — measuring current cooking load, family size, guest frequency)
- *Do I have enough money, space, and water connections to support a full modular kitchen?* (this is the **Feasibility Study** — checking financial, spatial, and resource constraints)
- *What is the sequence — first plumbing, then electrical, then carpentry?* (this is the **Project Planning** — defining phases, timelines, and dependencies)

A rural water supply project follows the exact same logic, except the "family" is a **village of 1,000–5,000 people**, the "cooking load" is the **per capita water demand in litres per capita per day (lpcd)**, and the "plumber" is the **Public Health Engineer (PHE) department or Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) block unit**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighting)

The following benchmark values are widely adopted in Indian rural WSS practice and are direct KTU high-yield facts:

- **Per Capita Water Demand (rural, India):** **55 lpcd** (Jal Jeevan Mission Functional Household Tap Connection standard)
- **Per Capita Water Demand (design, with losses):** **70–100 lpcd** (including 10–15% UFW, 5% cattle, 5% institutional)
- **Sanitation Coverage Target:** **ODF (Open Defectation Free) Plus** status
- **Design Period:** **30 years** (distribution network); **15 years** (pumping machinery)
- **Discount Rate for FS:** **8–12%** (typical social discount rate in Indian infrastructure appraisal)
- **Minimum Sample Size for Baseline Survey:** **10% of households or 100 households**, whichever is greater (statistical rule of thumb)

> [!NOTE]
> These six numerical values are among the most frequently asked constants in KTU university exams for PECET867. Memorize them with units — questions often test whether you confuse *lpcd* with *MLD* or *design period* with *project life*.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Curriculum

Module 4 bridges the **gap between technical engineering** (Modules 1–3: hydraulics, water treatment, sanitation systems) and **project management reality**. The Board examiners specifically test Module 4 because:

1. A civil/environmental engineer who can only design but not *plan* a project is incomplete.
2. National-level rural WSS schemes (JJM, SBM-G) demand this skill from graduating engineers.
3. It directly aligns with **Course Outcome CO4**: *"Plan and appraise rural water supply and sanitation projects, including needs assessment and feasibility analysis."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Project Planning Cycle — Five Sequential Phases

A rural WSS project planning cycle consists of **five logically ordered phases**. Each phase has a specific deliverable that gates the next.

1. **Identification Phase**
   - Triggered by: government scheme notification, community request, or district survey
   - Deliverable: *Project Concept Note (PCN)* — 2–3 page document stating the problem, location, and proposed intervention

2. **Preparation Phase (Needs Assessment + Feasibility Study)**
   - Deliverable: *Detailed Project Report (DPR)* — the master planning document
   - This is the heart of Module 4 and occupies the largest time and budget share

3. **Appraisal Phase**
   - Review by funding agency (JJM, NABARD, World Bank)
   - Deliverable: *Approved DPR with administrative sanction*

4. **Implementation Phase**
   - Tender, award, construct, commission
   - Deliverable: *Functional infrastructure (e.g., FHTC, ODF Plus village)*

5. **Operation & Maintenance (O&M) Phase**
   - 5–10 year post-handover support to Gram Panchayat / VWSC (Village Water and Sanitation Committee)
   - Deliverable: *Sustainable service delivery metrics*

### 2.2 Components of a Needs Assessment — The "Five-Whys" Diagnostic

The Needs Assessment is **not merely a census of water sources**. It is a **gap analysis** structured around four interrogatives: *What exists? What is needed? Why is there a gap? Who will solve it?*

The standard KTU framework comprises **seven sub-components**:

1. **Baseline Survey** — door-to-door socio-economic and water-use survey
2. **Existing Infrastructure Inventory** — mapping of wells, hand pumps, standposts, septic tanks
3. **Water Source Assessment** — yield test (in lpm), depth-to-water-table, seasonal variation, quality testing (pH, TDS, fecal coliform)
4. **Demand Projection** — population × per capita demand (see Section 3 for full derivation)
5. **Sanitation & Hygiene Behaviour Assessment** — KAP (Knowledge, Attitude, Practice) survey
6. **Stakeholder Analysis** — identification of VWSC, PRIs, SHGs, NGOs, line departments
7. **SWOT Analysis** — Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats of the proposed intervention

### 2.3 Components of a Feasibility Study — The "Five-Filter" Test

A Feasibility Study passes the proposed project through **five independent filters**. The project must clear *all five* to receive sanction.

| Filter | Question Answered | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Technical Feasibility** | Can the proposed technology deliver the required quantity and quality of water/sanitation in this terrain? | Source yield, head availability, treatment train selection, distribution layout |
| **Financial Feasibility** | Is the project financially sustainable over its life cycle (CapEx + OpEx recovery)? | NPV, BCR, IRR, payback period, tariff analysis |
| **Economic Feasibility** | Does the project create net positive economic value to society? | Economic NPV, Economic IRR (with shadow prices) |
| **Environmental Feasibility** | Are the environmental impacts manageable, and is clearance obtainable? | EIA report, environmental management plan (EMP) |
| **Social Feasibility** | Will the community accept, use, and maintain the system? | Community participation index, willingness-to-pay survey, gender inclusion score |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU exam trap is to ask: *"Which filter is satisfied if the BCR > 1?"* — the correct answer is **Economic Feasibility** (not Financial). BCR is an *economic* indicator when computed at social discount rate with shadow prices. Financial viability is judged by *debt service coverage ratio* and *tariff recovery*, not BCR.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every quantitative formula required for Module 4 numerical problems. **No `|` (pipe) character is used inside any cell to preserve table syntax integrity.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use / KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_n = P_0 \left( 1 + \dfrac{r}{100} \right)^n$ | $P_n$ = future population; $P_0$ = base population; $r$ = geometric growth rate (%); $n$ = decades | Geometric increase method — **Very High** |
| 2 | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | $\bar{x}$ = average decadal increase | Arithmetic increase method — **High** |
| 3 | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{y}$ | $x$ = average increase; $\bar{y}$ = average incremental increase | Incremental increase method — **High** |
| 4 | $Q_d = \dfrac{P_n \times q}{1000}$ (in MLD) | $Q_d$ = design demand in MLD; $q$ = per capita demand in lpcd | Demand projection — **Very High** |
| 5 | $Q_{peak} = 1.5 \times Q_d$ | Peak factor of 1.5 for rural systems | Pumping main design — **Medium** |
| 6 | $\text{NPV} = \displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | $B_t$ = benefits in year $t$; $C_t$ = costs; $i$ = discount rate; $N$ = project life | Feasibility study — **Very High** |
| 7 | $\text{BCR} = \dfrac{\sum \dfrac{B_t}{(1+i)^t}}{\sum \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}}$ | Benefit-Cost Ratio | Feasibility study — **High** |
| 8 | $\text{IRR}$: NPV $= 0$ at $i = \text{IRR}$ | Internal Rate of Return by interpolation | Feasibility study — **High** |
| 9 | $P_{\text{design}} = P_0 \times 1.15$ (for high-growth villages) | Design population adjusted for in-migrants | DPR preparation — **Medium** |
| 10 | $\text{Cost per capita} = \dfrac{\text{Total CapEx}}{P_{\text{design}}}$ | Capital cost benchmarking | DPR — **Medium** |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic curiosities — they appear *literally* in:

- **DPRs of Jal Jeevan Mission** submitted by State PHEDs to the Ministry of Jal Shakti
- **NABARD Rural Infrastructure Development Fund (RIDF)** loan appraisal notes
- **World Bank-funded** Tamil Nadu, Kerala, and Karnataka WSS project documents
- **CPCB / KSPCB** technical feasibility reports for decentralized sanitation

A junior engineer posted in a JJM district unit will use Formulas 1–5 **daily** and Formulas 6–8 during the DPR preparation cycle, which typically runs **3–6 months per block**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Population Forecasting by Geometric Increase Method

The geometric increase method is the **most widely used** method in Indian rural WSS planning because rural populations typically grow *exponentially* over the design horizon, especially in high-fertility districts.

**Starting Principle:** The decadal growth rate $r$ is assumed *constant* over the design period, so population grows as a *geometric progression*.

Let the base year population be $P_0$ (year $0$). After $1$ decade, population becomes $P_0 \cdot (1 + r/100)$. After $n$ decades:

$$
P_n = P_0 \times \left( 1 + \frac{r}{100} \right)^n
$$

**Step-by-step logical expansion:**

Step 1 — Define decadal growth factor
$$
k = 1 + \frac{r}{100}
$$

Step 2 — Express population after each decade as a geometric series
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= P_0 \cdot k \\
P_2 &= P_1 \cdot k = P_0 \cdot k^2 \\
P_3 &= P_2 \cdot k = P_0 \cdot k^3 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
P_n &= P_0 \cdot k^n
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Substitute $k$ back to obtain the canonical KTU formula
$$
P_n = P_0 \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{r}{100} \right)^n
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-mark style):**

A rural panchayat has a present population of **4,500** (Census 2021). The decadal growth rate observed between 2011 and 2021 is **18%**. Compute the design population for the year **2051** (design horizon = 30 years from 2021 = 3 decades).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= 4{,}500 \\
r &= 18\% \\
n &= 3 \text{ decades} \\[6pt]
P_{2051} &= 4500 \times \left(1 + \frac{18}{100}\right)^{3} \\
&= 4500 \times (1.18)^{3} \\
&= 4500 \times 1.643032 \\
&= 7{,}393.6 \approx 7{,}394
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Design population for 2051 ≈ **7,394 persons**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Average Daily Water Demand (ADWD) and Peak Demand

**Step 1 — Compute average daily water demand in litres per day**
$$
Q_{avg} = P_n \times q \quad \text{(in litres/day)}
$$
where $q$ = per capita demand in lpcd.

**Step 2 — Convert to million litres per day (MLD)** for source and treatment plant sizing
$$
Q_{avg,MLD} = \frac{P_n \times q}{10^6}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply peak factor** for rising main, sump, and pump design
$$
Q_{peak} = 1.5 \times Q_{avg}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply UFW (Unaccounted For Water) factor** for distribution design
$$
Q_{design,dist} = 1.15 \times Q_{avg}
$$

**Worked Example (continuation):**

For the panchayat above with $P_{2051} = 7{,}394$ and $q = 70$ lpcd (with UFW allowance):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{avg} &= 7394 \times 70 = 517{,}580 \;\text{L/day} \\
Q_{avg,MLD} &= \frac{517{,}580}{10^6} = 0.518 \;\text{MLD} \\[4pt]
Q_{peak} &= 1.5 \times 0.518 = 0.776 \;\text{MLD} \\[4pt]
Q_{design,dist} &= 1.15 \times 0.518 = 0.595 \;\text{MLD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The source must yield at least **0.776 MLD** during peak demand periods; the distribution network must be designed for **0.595 MLD** average; the treatment plant must handle **0.518 MLD**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Net Present Value (NPV) and BCR for a Rural WSS Project

This is the **single most important financial formula** in feasibility studies. The full derivation proceeds as follows.

**Step 1 — Discount each year's net cash flow**

A rupee received in year $t$ is worth less than a rupee today. The *present value* of a future cash flow $CF_t$ at discount rate $i$ is:
$$
PV_t = \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum over the project life $N$ to obtain NPV**
$$
\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+i)^t}
$$

where $B_t$ = discounted total benefits in year $t$, $C_t$ = discounted total costs in year $t$, $N$ = project life (typically 30 years).

**Step 3 — Decision rule**
$$
\text{Project is economically viable} \iff \text{NPV} \geq 0
$$

**Step 4 — Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) is the ratio of present value of benefits to present value of costs**
$$
\text{BCR} = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{B_t}{(1+i)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}}
$$

Decision rule:
$$
\text{Project is viable} \iff \text{BCR} \geq 1
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example**

Consider a rural WSS project with:
- Initial capital cost: ₹ **150 lakh** (at $t = 0$)
- Annual O&M cost: ₹ **5 lakh/year** (years 1–30)
- Annual benefits (health + time-saving + productivity): ₹ **18 lakh/year** (years 1–30)
- Discount rate: $i = 10\%$

Compute NPV and BCR over 30 years.

The present value of a uniform annual series $A$ for $n$ years at rate $i$ is given by the **uniform series present worth factor (USPWF)**:
$$
\text{USPWF}(i,n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^n}
$$

For $i = 0.10$ and $n = 30$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{USPWF}(10\%, 30) &= \frac{(1.10)^{30} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{30}} \\
&= \frac{17.4494 - 1}{0.10 \times 17.4494} \\
&= \frac{16.4494}{1.74494} \\
&= 9.4269
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute present values:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PV of benefits} &= 18 \times 9.4269 = 169.68 \;\text{lakh} \\
\text{PV of costs (O\&M)} &= 5 \times 9.4269 = 47.13 \;\text{lakh} \\
\text{PV of total costs} &= 150 + 47.13 = 197.13 \;\text{lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= 169.68 - 197.13 = -27.45 \;\text{lakh} \\
\text{BCR} &= \frac{169.68}{197.13} = 0.861
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Since NPV $<$ 0 and BCR $<$ 1, **the project is NOT economically viable** at a 10% discount rate. The engineer should either reduce capital cost (alternate technology), increase tariff / benefit realization, or apply for a subsidy (which would be added to benefits as a positive transfer) to make BCR > 1.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Feasibility Filter Outputs vs. Common Failures

| Feasibility Filter | Indicator of Success | Common Failure Mode | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Technical | Source yield $\geq Q_{peak}$ | Source dries up in summer | Construct check dam / recharge well |
| Financial | Tariff recovery $\geq 100\%$ OpEx | VWSC unable to collect tariff | Cross-subsidy from Panchayat funds |
| Economic | BCR $\geq 1$ | High CapEx, low benefit monetization | Subsidy from JJM, lower-cost gravity scheme |
| Environmental | EC obtained from SEIAA | Project in CRZ / forest | Site relocation |
| Social | $\geq 80\%$ household consent | Land dispute for OHT site | Land donation, joint site visit with PRIs |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Project Planning Cycle for Rural WSS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need Identification: Community Request or Scheme Notification] --> B[Baseline Survey: KAP and Socio-Economic Data]
    B --> C[Needs Assessment: Gap Analysis and Demand Projection]
    C --> D[Feasibility Study: Five-Filter Test]
    D --> E{DPR Approved?}
    E -- No --> F[Revise DPR: Iterate Technical or Financial Inputs]
    F --> D
    E -- Yes --> G[Administrative Sanction: Funding Agency Approves]
    G --> H[Tendering and Procurement]
    H --> I[Construction Phase: OHT, Distribution, FHTC]
    I --> J[Commissioning and Trial Run]
    J --> K[O and M Handover to VWSC and GP]
    K --> L[Performance Monitoring: JJM Dashboard]
    L --> M{Service Sustained?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Project Success: Functional Tap Connection]
    M -- No --> O[Corrective Action: Retrofitting or Capacity Building]
    O --> K
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Components of a Feasibility Study

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEASIBILITY_STUDY_BLOCK[Feasibility Study Framework]
        A1[Technical Feasibility: Source Yield and Head] --> X[Final Go or No-Go Decision]
        A2[Financial Feasibility: Tariff and O and M Recovery] --> X
        A3[Economic Feasibility: NPV and BCR] --> X
        A4[Environmental Feasibility: EIA and Clearance] --> X
        A5[Social Feasibility: Community Willingness] --> X
    end
    X -- All Filters Cleared --> Y[DPR Submitted to Funding Agency]
    X -- Any Filter Fails --> Z[Project Rejected or Re-scoped]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Needs Assessment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Desk Review of Secondary Data] --> P2[Step 2: Reconnaissance Survey of Panchayat]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Household Questionnaire Administration]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Water Quality Testing at Field Labs]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Focus Group Discussions with VWSC and SHGs]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Data Triangulation and Gap Report]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Community Validation Meeting at GP Hall]
    P7 --> P8[Final Needs Assessment Report: Input to DPR]
```

### 4.4 Engineering Notes on the Diagrams

- The **flowchart in 4.1** is a closed loop because rural WSS projects are inherently **iterative** — sanctions, retrofits, and corrective actions are routine, not exceptional.
- The **block diagram in 4.2** deliberately shows **all five filters converging on a single decision node** ($X$). This visually emphasizes that *no single filter is sufficient*; all five must clear for the project to proceed.
- The **sequence in 4.3** is the standard workflow prescribed by the **Ministry of Jal Shakti** for JJM village action plans (VAPs). A KTU examiner may ask students to *redraw* or *modify* this sequence — for example, insert a *rapid gender analysis* step between Steps 5 and 6.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*Define Needs Assessment. List any four components of a needs assessment for a rural water supply project.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Needs Assessment is a systematic diagnostic process to identify the gap between the *existing* level of water supply and sanitation service and the *desired* level, along with the causal factors and community priorities.
Four components:
1. Baseline socio-economic and water-use survey
2. Existing infrastructure inventory
3. Water source assessment (yield, quality, seasonal variation)
4. Stakeholder and SWOT analysis
*(2 marks for definition; 1 mark for any 4 components, 0.25 each)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Differentiate between Financial Feasibility and Economic Feasibility in the context of a rural WSS project.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Financial Feasibility | Economic Feasibility |
|---|---|---|
| Perspective | Project owner / implementing agency | Society as a whole |
| Costs and benefits | Market prices | Shadow prices, including externalities |
| Key indicators | Tariff recovery, debt service coverage | NPV, BCR, IRR at social discount rate |
| Question answered | Will the project pay back? | Should the project be done? |
*(1.5 marks each side of comparison; 3 = full marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In 14-mark questions, students commonly lose marks by (a) failing to state the *formula* before substituting values, (b) using wrong units (lpcd vs MLD), (c) skipping the **decision rule interpretation** of NPV/BCR, and (d) ignoring the **assumptions** in population forecasting methods. Always state assumptions explicitly; they are worth 1–2 marks by themselves.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**

*(a) Explain the various methods of population forecasting used in rural water supply planning. Discuss the merits and demerits of the geometric increase method. (7 marks)*

*(b) A rural panchayat has a population of 6,000 in 2021. The decadal growth rates recorded were 15% (2001–2011) and 20% (2011–2021). Compute the design population for 2051 using (i) the geometric increase method based on the most recent decadal growth rate, and (ii) the incremental increase method. Assume average incremental increase $\bar{y} = 80$ persons/decade. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Five standard methods of population forecasting taught in KTU PECET867:
1. **Arithmetic Increase Method** — assumes constant decadal increase; simple but underestimates fast-growing villages. *(1 mark)*
2. **Geometric Increase Method** — assumes constant decadal growth rate; best for fast-growing rural areas. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Incremental Increase Method** — assumes the *increment* itself grows linearly; suitable for medium-growth towns. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Logistic Method** — assumes S-shaped growth curve; suitable when saturation population is known. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Graphical and Comparative Methods** — curve-fitting from past census data; least accurate but useful for cross-checking. *(1.5 marks)*

**Merits of Geometric Method:** simple, mathematically rigorous, accounts for compounding growth, widely used in Indian PHED practice. **Demerits:** overestimates for very long horizons (>40 years), requires accurate base growth rate, fails if growth rate itself changes. *(Bonus 0.5 mark if both merits and demerits are stated)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Given:* $P_0 = 6000$ (2021), $n = 3$ decades (2021 → 2051).

**(i) Geometric Increase Method using most recent decadal growth rate $r = 20\%$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2051} &= P_0 \times \left( 1 + \frac{r}{100} \right)^n \\
&= 6000 \times (1.20)^{3} \\
&= 6000 \times 1.728 \\
&= 10{,}368
\end{aligned}
$$
*(3.5 marks: 1 for formula, 1.5 for substitution, 1 for answer)*

**(ii) Incremental Increase Method:**

Average decadal increase (using both census decades):
$$
x = \frac{(P_{2011} - P_{2001}) + (P_{2021} - P_{2011})}{2}
$$
We need $P_{2011}$. Using the 15% growth between 2001 and 2011:
$$
P_{2011} = \frac{6000}{1.20} = 5000
$$
Therefore $P_{2001}$:
$$
P_{2001} = \frac{5000}{1.15} = 4348
$$
Average increase per decade:
$$
x = \frac{(5000 - 4348) + (6000 - 5000)}{2} = \frac{652 + 1000}{2} = 826
$$
Given $\bar{y} = 80$ persons/decade.
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2051} &= P_0 + n \cdot x + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{y} \\
&= 6000 + 3 \times 826 + \frac{3 \times 4}{2} \times 80 \\
&= 6000 + 2478 + 6 \times 80 \\
&= 6000 + 2478 + 480 \\
&= 8{,}958
\end{aligned}
$$
*(3.5 marks: 1 for $x$ calculation, 1.5 for formula, 1 for final answer)*

**Conclusion:** The two methods give different forecasts (10,368 vs 8,958). For a KTU DPR, the engineer should normally use the **arithmetic mean of the two** as the design population = $\frac{10368 + 8958}{2} = 9{,}663$ persons, or use the more conservative (lower) value if budget is constrained. *(0.5 mark interpretation — often missed by students)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**

*(a) List and explain the five components of a feasibility study for a rural WSS project. (7 marks)*

*(b) A rural piped water supply project has an initial capital cost of ₹ 200 lakh, an annual O&M cost of ₹ 6 lakh, and expected annual benefits of ₹ 22 lakh. The project life is 30 years and the discount rate is 12%. Compute the NPV and BCR. Comment on the economic viability of the project. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks** *(1.4 marks per component)*

1. **Technical Feasibility** — verifies that the proposed source, treatment, and distribution system can deliver the required quantity and quality of water given the terrain, geology, and climate. Tools: source yield test, hydraulic network analysis, treatment pilot studies.

2. **Financial Feasibility** — assesses whether the implementing agency can recover Operation and Maintenance costs through user tariffs and cross-subsidies. Indicators: tariff recovery ratio, O&M cost coverage, payback period.

3. **Economic Feasibility** — measures whether the project creates net positive value to society as a whole, including externalities like health benefits, time saved, and reduced disease burden. Indicators: Economic NPV, BCR, IRR.

4. **Environmental Feasibility** — examines impacts on groundwater, surface water, biodiversity, and ecosystems. Tools: Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA), Environmental Management Plan (EMP), environmental clearance from SEIAA.

5. **Social Feasibility** — evaluates community acceptance, willingness to pay, gender inclusion, and equity. Tools: PRA (Participatory Rural Appraisal), FGDs, KAP surveys, consent letters from Gram Panchayat.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Given:* CapEx = ₹ 200 lakh (at $t = 0$); O&M = ₹ 6 lakh/year; Benefits = ₹ 22 lakh/year; $N = 30$ years; $i = 12\%$.

Compute USPWF at 12% for 30 years:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{USPWF}(12\%, 30) &= \frac{(1.12)^{30} - 1}{0.12 \times (1.12)^{30}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $(1.12)^{30}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^{10} &= 3.1058 \\
(1.12)^{20} &= (3.1058)^{2} = 9.6463 \\
(1.12)^{30} &= 9.6463 \times 3.1058 = 29.9599
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{USPWF}(12\%, 30) &= \frac{29.9599 - 1}{0.12 \times 29.9599} = \frac{28.9599}{3.5952} = 8.0552
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute present values:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PV of benefits} &= 22 \times 8.0552 = 177.21 \;\text{lakh} \\
\text{PV of O\&M costs} &= 6 \times 8.0552 = 48.33 \;\text{lakh} \\
\text{PV of total costs} &= 200 + 48.33 = 248.33 \;\text{lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now NPV and BCR:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= 177.21 - 248.33 = -71.12 \;\text{lakh} \\
\text{BCR} &= \frac{177.21}{248.33} = 0.714
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark — often missed):**
Since NPV $<$ 0 (specifically, −71.12 lakh) and BCR $<$ 1 (specifically 0.714), **the project is not economically viable** at a 12% discount rate.

**Recommendation:** The engineer may consider:
- Reducing capital cost by choosing gravity-fed scheme instead of pumping
- Negotiating a lower interest / longer loan tenure
- Requesting JJM capital subsidy (which, when added to benefits, raises BCR)
- Reducing discount rate assumption to 8% (if justified by social criteria)

*(3 marks: 1.5 for USPWF, 1 for NPV/BCR, 0.5 for interpretation)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Project planning cycle** has **5 phases**: Identification → Preparation (NA + FS) → Appraisal → Implementation → O&M.
- **Needs Assessment** is the *gap* between *present service* and *desired service*, while **Feasibility Study** tests if the project is *viable, sustainable, and implementable*.
- **Five Feasibility Filters**: Technical, Financial, Economic, Environmental, Social — *all five* must clear.
- **Rural per capita water demand** in India (JJM standard): **55 lpcd** for FHTC; **70–100 lpcd** design including losses.
- **Design horizon**: **30 years** for civil structures; **15 years** for electro-mechanical equipment.
- **Population forecasting** — use **Geometric Increase Method** for high-growth rural panchayats; formula: $P_n = P_0 \cdot (1 + r/100)^n$.
- **Incremental Increase Method** formula: $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{y}$.
- **Water demand conversion**: $Q_{MLD} = \dfrac{P_n \times q_{lpcd}}{10^6}$.
- **Peak factor = 1.5**, **UFW factor = 1.15** — apply only to the relevant component (pump / distribution).
- **NPV** uses social discount rate (typically **8–12%**); rule: project viable iff **NPV ≥ 0**.
- **BCR** is the ratio of *discounted benefits* to *discounted costs*; rule: project viable iff **BCR ≥ 1**.
- **USPWF** formula must be used for uniform annual series: $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$.
- **Always state assumptions** (growth rate, discount rate, project life) before substituting — examiners allocate 1–2 marks for this.
- **Always interpret the numerical answer** (viable or not, and what action to take) — 0.5–1 mark in every 7-mark sub-question.
- **DPR = Detailed Project Report** is the master deliverable of Module 4 and is the document submitted to funding agencies.
- **Stakeholder analysis** is mandatory — without VWSC and PRI consultation, the project fails during O&M phase.
- **Minimum sample size** for baseline survey: 10% of households or 100 households, whichever is greater.
- **Don't confuse** *Financial* and *Economic* feasibility — they use different prices and answer different questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most common reason rural WSS projects fail in India is **not technical failure but social non-acceptance** — communities reject hand-pump locations, refuse to pay tariffs, or sabotage infrastructure due to unresolved land disputes. This is why **Module 4** dedicates a full sub-section to *social feasibility* and *stakeholder analysis*. Treat it with the same weight as the numerical methods.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Funding and Financing Options for Rural Water and Sanitation Projects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Funding and Financing Options for Rural Water and Sanitation Projects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rural Water Supply and Sanitation (RWSS) Financing** refers to the structured mobilization, allocation, and management of monetary, institutional, and material resources required to plan, construct, operate, maintain, and rehabilitate drinking water supply and onsite sanitation infrastructure in rural habitations. It encompasses three distinct financial flows:

1. **Capital Expenditure (CapEx)** — One-time, non-recurring investment in civil structures (overhead tanks, pump houses, distribution networks, household toilets, soak pits, septic tanks, and conveyance mains).
2. **Operation and Maintenance Expenditure (Opex)** — Recurring annual costs for energy (electricity/diesel), chemicals (chlorine, alum), spares, manpower, and minor repairs.
3. **Asset Replacement / Capital Maintenance Cost (CMC)** — Periodic mid-life rehabilitation cost, typically incurred at 10–15 year cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, students are expected to identify and critically appraise the funding architecture, fiscal flow mechanisms, cost-recovery frameworks, and Public-Private Partnership (PPP) models adopted by the Government of India for RWSS projects. This is a high-weightage topic and frequently appears in Part A (3 marks) and as a sub-part in Part B (14 marks).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Village Water Cooperative"**
> Imagine a village of 200 families that needs a new overhead tank costing ₹40 lakhs. No single family can fund it. So the village:
> - **Pools** small contributions from every family (community contribution)
> - **Approaches** the District Collector for a government grant (public funding)
> - **Borrows** the gap amount from a nationalized bank (debt financing)
> - **Ties up** with a private contractor to operate the system for 10 years (PPP / O&M contracting)
> - **Recovers** a small monthly water charge of ₹50–100 per household (tariff-based cost recovery)
>
> This entire multi-source money pipeline is **Financing**, and the rules governing who pays what, when, and how is **Funding Architecture**.

## 1.3 Standard Funding Metrics in RWSS (Bold Constants)

The following metrics are standard benchmarks used in appraisal reports:

- **Per Capita Cost (PCC) for rural piped water supply:** **₹1,500 – ₹3,000** (varies with terrain, source distance, and population density).
- **Unit cost of a household toilet (IHHL) under SBM-G:** **₹12,000** (₹10,000 from Centre + ₹2,000 from State + beneficiary flexibility).
- **O&M cost per capita per annum:** **₹150 – ₹300** for piped schemes.
- **Standard financial life of a piped water scheme:** **30 years** (design horizon).
- **Standard O&M contracting period (PPP-OM):** **5 to 10 years**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Financing Architecture of RWSS in India

### Tier 1 — National / Central Government Programs (Primary Funder)

| Scheme | Ministry | Launch Year | Funding Pattern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** | Jal Shakti | 2019 | 90:10 (NE/UTs 100%) |
| **Swachh Bharat Mission – Gramin (SBM-G)** | Jal Shakti | 2014 (Phase I) / 2020 (Phase II) | 60:40 (NE 90:10) |
| **National Rural Drinking Water Programme (NRDWP)** | Jal Shakti | 2009 | 50:50 (subsumed into JJM) |
| **MGNREGA** | Rural Development | 2005 | 100% Centrally Sponsored |
| **15th Finance Commission Grants** | Finance | 2021 | Tied to RLBs |

### Tier 2 — State Government Co-Funding

States contribute their share (typically 10% for JJM, 40% for SBM-G) and additionally fund:
- State-specific augmentations
- Power subsidy (free/subsidized electricity to pumps)
- Salary of state-level WSS departments
- Land acquisition costs

### Tier 3 — External / Multilateral / Bilateral / Private Sources

- **World Bank** — Projects like the **National Rural Water Supply & Sanitation Project (1999)**, **RWS&S Sector Project (2014)**.
- **Asian Development Bank (ADB)** — **Sustainable Rural Water Supply & Sanitation Sector Project**.
- **New Development Bank (NDB)** — BRICS-funded rural schemes.
- **Japan International Cooperation Agency (JICA)** — Bundelkhand & Rajasthan water projects.
- **Public-Private Partnerships (PPP)** — Hybrid Annuity Model (HAM), Design-Build-Operate-Transfer (DBOT), Management Contracts.
- **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)** — Under Companies Act 2013, Section 135.
- **Community Contribution (5%–10%)** — Mandatory under JJM for community participation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Approach (KTU Expected Answer):**
> JJM officially mandates **Convergence** — combining funds from MGNREGA, SBM-G, PMAY-G, 15th FC grants, CSR, and state budgets into a single village water security plan. This is a *high-yield* descriptive question for KTU exams.

## 2.2 Cost Recovery & Tariff Models

### 2.2.1 Types of Cost Recovery

1. **Full Cost Recovery (FCR):** Tariff = CapEx amortization + Opex + CMC.
2. **Operations-Only Cost Recovery (OCR):** Tariff = Opex only (subsidized CapEx).
3. **Affordable Cost Recovery (ACR):** Tariff < Opex; balance funded by state subsidy.
4. **No Recovery:** Fully free provision (rare, politically motivated).

### 2.2.2 Willingness-to-Pay (WTP) vs. Ability-to-Pay (ATP)

> **WTP** is what consumers *say* they will pay; **ATP** is what they *can actually afford* without economic distress. The breakeven tariff is set between the two — typically targeting 5%–10% of average rural household income (~$1–2/month under standard Indian rural water tariffs).

## 2.3 Public-Private Partnership (PPP) Models in RWSS

| Model | CapEx | Opex | Risk Bearer | Typical Tenure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Service Contract** | Public | Private | Public | 1–3 years |
| **Management Contract** | Public | Private | Public | 3–5 years |
| **Lease Contract** | Public | Private | Shared | 5–10 years |
| **Concession (DBOT)** | Private | Private | Private | 10–30 years |
| **Hybrid Annuity Model (HAM)** | Shared (40% public, 60% private) | Private | Shared | 10–15 years |
| **Community-Led (Pani Samiti / VWSC)** | Public + Community | Community | Community | Perpetual |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are essential for KTU numerical/descriptive answers on RWSS financing.

| Concept | Formula / Identity | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Per Capita Capital Cost | $PCC = \dfrac{\text{Total CapEx}}{\text{Design Population}}$ | ₹/capita | Cost benchmarking |
| Per Capita O&M Cost | $POM = \dfrac{\text{Annual O\&M Expenditure}}{\text{Population Served}}$ | ₹/capita/yr | Tariff estimation |
| Break-even Tariff | $T_{be} = \dfrac{C_{OM} + C_{cap-amort}}{N_h \times 12}$ | ₹/household/month | Tariff design |
| Community Contribution % | $CC\% = \dfrac{C_{comm}}{C_{total}} \times 100$ | % | JJM compliance check |
| Capital Recovery Factor | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | dimensionless | Annualized CapEx |
| Annualized Capital Cost | $A_{cap} = C_{cap} \times CRF$ | ₹/year | FCR computation |
| Present Worth (PW) of a Tariff Stream | $PW = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{T \cdot N_h \cdot 12}{(1+i)^t}$ | ₹ | Tariff sustainability test |
| Internal Rate of Return (IRR) | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{NCF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | % | PPP financial viability |
| Benefit-Cost Ratio | $BCR = \dfrac{\sum \dfrac{B_t}{(1+i)^t}}{\sum \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}}$ | dimensionless | Project screening |
| Depreciation (Straight Line) | $D = \dfrac{C_{cap} - S_v}{n}$ | ₹/year | Replacement cost forecast |

Where:
- $i$ = social/financial discount rate (typically **8%–12%** for Indian rural projects).
- $n$ = asset life in years.
- $N_h$ = number of households.
- $NCF_t$ = net cash flow in year $t$.
- $S_v$ = salvage value.
- $B_t, C_t$ = benefits and costs in year $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the **discount rate (i)** explicitly when applying CRF or PW. A common error is using market bank rates (12%–15%) instead of the social discount rate (8%–10%) prescribed by the **Planning Commission / NITI Aayog**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The financing architecture determines:
- **Sustainability** of O&M after scheme inauguration.
- **Equity** — whether services reach marginalized hamlets.
- **Quality of service** — funds for chemicals, lab testing, and meter reading.
- **Institutional capacity** — Village Water & Sanitation Committees (VWSCs / Pani Samitis) require recurring funding for training and IEC (Information, Education & Communication).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Annualized CapEx Using Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)

The Capital Recovery Factor is derived from the **present worth of a uniform annual series** (capitalized cost) annuity equation. Below is the full step-by-step derivation as expected in KTU answers.

### Step 1 — Statement of the Problem

A capital cost $C_{cap}$ is invested today. The recovery is to be made through equal annual payments $A$ over $n$ years at a discount rate $i$, such that the present worth of all $A$ payments equals $C_{cap}$.

### Step 2 — Set Up the Present Worth of an Annuity

$$
\begin{aligned}
PW &= \frac{A}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^3} + \dots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^n} \\[6pt]
PW &= A \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+i)^t}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Recognize the Geometric Series

The summation is a **finite geometric series** with first term $a_1 = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^1}$ and common ratio $r = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)}$.

Sum of finite geometric series:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_n &= a_1 \cdot \frac{1 - r^n}{1 - r} \\[6pt]
S_n &= \frac{1}{(1+i)} \cdot \frac{1 - \left[\frac{1}{(1+i)}\right]^n}{1 - \frac{1}{(1+i)}} \\[6pt]
S_n &= \frac{1}{(1+i)} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{\frac{i}{(1+i)}} \\[6pt]
S_n &= \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Equate PW to Capital Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{cap} &= A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} \\[6pt]
\therefore A &= C_{cap} \cdot \frac{i}{1 - (1+i)^{-n}} \\[6pt]
\therefore A &= C_{cap} \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Identify the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} \\[6pt]
A_{cap} &= C_{cap} \times CRF
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** CRF converts a present lump sum into an equivalent uniform annual series — the foundation of all cost-recovery and tariff engineering in RWSS.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:**
A rural piped water supply scheme has a capital cost of ₹1.5 crore. Asset life $n = 30$ years. Discount rate $i = 10\%$. Annual O&M cost = ₹12 lakh. There are 1,000 households. Compute:
(a) Annualized capital cost.
(b) Total annual cost (Opex + CapEx amortization).
(c) Break-even monthly tariff per household.

### (a) Annualized Capital Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \frac{0.10(1+0.10)^{30}}{(1+0.10)^{30} - 1} \\[6pt]
(1.10)^{30} &\approx 17.449 \\[6pt]
CRF &= \frac{0.10 \times 17.449}{17.449 - 1} \\[6pt]
CRF &= \frac{1.7449}{16.449} \\[6pt]
CRF &\approx 0.1061 \\[6pt]
A_{cap} &= 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1061 \\[6pt]
A_{cap} &\approx \text{₹}\,15{,}91{,}500 \;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (b) Total Annual Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= A_{cap} + C_{OM} \\[6pt]
A_{total} &= 15{,}91{,}500 + 12{,}00{,}000 \\[6pt]
A_{total} &= \text{₹}\,27{,}91{,}500 \;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (c) Break-Even Monthly Tariff per Household

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{be} &= \frac{A_{total}}{N_h \times 12} \\[6pt]
T_{be} &= \frac{27{,}91{,}500}{1000 \times 12} \\[6pt]
T_{be} &= \frac{27{,}91{,}500}{12{,}000} \\[6pt]
T_{be} &\approx \text{₹}\,232.6 \;\text{per month per household}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference:** If state policy mandates an *affordable* tariff of ₹100/month, the revenue gap is ₹(232.6 − 100) = ₹132.6/HH/month, which the state must subsidize — this is the *Cross-Subsidy Model* common in Indian RWSS.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Financing Decision Framework

| Question | Implication | Typical Funding Response |
|---|---|---|
| Is the population > 1,000 with reliable source? | Economies of scale | Central scheme + State + Community |
| Is source > 5 km away? | High pumping cost | Concession / HAM PPP model |
| Is the region water-stressed? | High O&M cost | Higher tariff + state subsidy |
| Is community cohesive? | Strong VWC | Community-Led Model |
| Is there an active NGO? | Capacity building | External-funded (World Bank / ADB) |
