# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024]
**List the three tier financial architecture of rural water supply and sanitation projects in India. State one example scheme for each tier.**

**Model Answer:**

The three-tier financial architecture of RWSS projects in India comprises:

1. **Central Government Funding (Primary Funder):** Centrally Sponsored Schemes (CSS) bear 50%–90% of project cost. Example: **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** — launched in 2019 to provide Functional Household Tap Connections (FHTC) to every rural household by 2024.
2. **State Government Co-Funding:** States contribute 10%–40% of project cost and also bear land, electricity, and institutional costs. Example: **State Water and Sanitation Mission (SWSM)** as the state-level nodal agency.
3. **External / Private / Community Funding:** Includes World Bank, ADB, JICA, CSR funds, and community contribution. Example: **World Bank-assisted "Sustainable Rural Water Supply and Sanitation Sector Project"** in Kerala, Karnataka, and Punjab.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often omit the *Community Tier* and *External Donor Tier*, citing only Centre-State. This loses 1 mark. Always list **three** tiers explicitly.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024]
**Define "Willingness to Pay (WTP)" and "Ability to Pay (ATP)" in the context of rural water tariff setting. Why is the gap between them important?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Willingness to Pay (WTP):** The maximum amount a household is *psychologically and economically willing* to contribute for a water service, typically elicited through contingent valuation surveys.
- **Ability to Pay (ATP):** The maximum amount a household *can actually afford* based on its income, expenditure patterns, and livelihood vulnerability, generally benchmarked at 3%–5% of monthly household income.

**Importance of the WTP–ATP Gap:** The gap determines the *affordability deficit*. If WTP > ATP, the scheme is sustainable on tariffs alone. If WTP < ATP, tariffs are politically and economically unacceptable, and cross-subsidy or state support is required. The *breakeven tariff* is fixed within the corridor [WTP, ATP] to balance revenue recovery and social equity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B questions carry 14 marks and have internal choice. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. Both alternatives are provided below.

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the funding pattern, objectives, and convergence strategy of the **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)**. How does it differ from the earlier NRDWP? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rural piped water supply scheme serving 2,000 households has a capital cost of ₹4 crore and an annual O&M cost of ₹25 lakh. If the asset life is 30 years and the discount rate is 10%, compute the **annualized capital cost, total annual cost, and break-even monthly tariff per household**. Comment on the affordability if the state fixes the tariff at ₹100/month. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) JJM Detailed

**Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** was launched on 15 August 2019 by the Ministry of Jal Shakti with the objective of providing **Functional Household Tap Connections (FHTC)** to every rural household by 2024, ensuring 55 litres per capita per day (lpcd) of potable water.

**Funding Pattern:**
- Centre : State = **90 : 10** for non-NE/non-UT States.
- **100% Central funding** for North-Eastern States, Himalayan States, UTs, and Island Territories.
- Additional convergence permitted from MGNREGA, SBM-G, 15th Finance Commission, CSR, and external loans.

**Key Institutional Pillars:**
1. **National Jal Jeevan Mission (NJJM)** at central level.
2. **State Water and Sanitation Missions (SWSM)** at state level.
3. **District Water and Sanitation Missions (DWSM)** at district level.
4. **Village Water and Sanitation Committee (VWSC) / Pani Samiti** at village level.

**Convergence Strategy:** JJM explicitly mandates convergence of at least **5 funding streams** into a single Village Action Plan (VAP) to avoid duplication and ensure source sustainability. For example:
- MGNREGA → source augmentation (check dams, recharge structures)
- SBM-G → greywater management at household level
- 15th FC grants → distribution network
- PMAY-G → individual household tap connection
- State Plan → O&M and power subsidy

**Differences from NRDWP:**

| Parameter | NRDWP (2009) | JJM (2019) |
|---|---|---|
| Coverage | Coverage-based (standposts) | FHTC-based (household) |
| Service Level | 40 lpcd | 55 lpcd |
| Quality | Limited testing | Mandatory water quality testing |
| Convergence | Weak | Mandatory |
| Community Role | Nominal | Central (Pani Samiti) |
| Technology | Conventional | IoT, AMR, solar pumps encouraged |

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical

**Given:**
$C_{cap} = ₹4{,}00{,}00{,}000$ ; $C_{OM} = ₹25{,}00{,}000$/year ; $n = 30$ yr ; $i = 10\% = 0.10$ ; $N_h = 2000$ HH.

**Step 1 — Capital Recovery Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} \\[6pt]
CRF &= \frac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{30}}{(1.10)^{30} - 1} \\[6pt]
CRF &= \frac{0.10 \times 17.4494}{17.4494 - 1} \\[6pt]
CRF &= \frac{1.74494}{16.4494} \\[6pt]
CRF &\approx 0.10608
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Annualized Capital Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{cap} &= 4{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10608 \\[6pt]
A_{cap} &= \text{₹}\,42{,}43{,}200 \;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Annual Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= A_{cap} + C_{OM} \\[6pt]
A_{total} &= 42{,}43{,}200 + 25{,}00{,}000 \\[6pt]
A_{total} &= \text{₹}\,67{,}43{,}200 \;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Break-Even Monthly Tariff per Household:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{be} &= \frac{A_{total}}{N_h \times 12} \\[6pt]
T_{be} &= \frac{67{,}43{,}200}{2000 \times 12} \\[6pt]
T_{be} &= \frac{67{,}43{,}200}{24{,}000} \\[6pt]
T_{be} &\approx \text{₹}\,281.0 \;\text{per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Affordability Comment:**

State-fixed tariff = ₹100/month. Required tariff = ₹281/month.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Revenue Gap per HH} &= (281 - 100) \times 12 = \text{₹}\,2{,}172 \\[6pt]
\text{Total Annual Revenue Gap} &= 2{,}172 \times 2000 = \text{₹}\,43{,}44{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Recovery Ratio} &= \frac{100}{281} \times 100 \approx 35.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Comment:** The state recovers only **35.6%** of full cost. The remaining **₹43.44 lakh/year** (≈ 64.4%) must be cross-subsidized from state revenues, 15th FC grants, or the VWSC's revolving fund. The scheme is **not financially self-sustainable** at ₹100/month — a tariff revision or permanent subsidy commitment is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:**
> - Forgetting to use **CRF** formula and simply dividing capital cost by life (e.g., ₹4 cr / 30 = ₹13.33 lakh) — loses 2 marks. You must discount the future.
> - Reporting break-even tariff as annual instead of monthly — loses 1 mark.
> - Omitting the **affordability commentary** — loses 1 mark.
> - Forgetting to state the **discount rate (i = 10%)** explicitly — loses 1 mark.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024]**
**(a)** Discuss the role of **Public-Private Partnerships (PPP)** in rural water supply. With neat sketches/tables, compare the **Service Contract, Management Contract, Lease, and Concession (DBOT)** models. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **"Convergence"** in rural water and sanitation financing. State any **four funding sources** that can be converged under a Village Action Plan (VAP) under JJM. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) PPP in RWSS

**Role of PPP in Rural Water Supply:**
PPP leverages private sector efficiency in construction, O&M, billing, and asset management while the public sector retains ownership and ensures equity. PPPs address chronic issues of:
- Underfunded O&M leading to scheme failure within 3–5 years
- Inefficient water distribution (40%–60% non-revenue water)
- Weak revenue collection
- Inadequate water quality monitoring

**Comparative Table of PPP Models:**

| Feature | Service Contract | Management Contract | Lease Contract | Concession (DBOT) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CapEx Bearer** | Public | Public | Public | Private |
| **Opex Bearer** | Private | Private | Private | Private |
| **Asset Ownership** | Public | Public | Public | Public (returns at end) |
| **Tenure** | 1–3 years | 3–5 years | 5–10 years | 10–30 years |
| **Revenue Risk** | Public | Public | Shared | Private |
| **Tariff Authority** | Public | Public | Public | Public (with cap) |
| **Performance Risk** | Low | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| **Example in RWSS** | Billing contract | Town O&M contract | Cluster scheme lease | Mega multi-village DBOT |

**Key Trade-off:** Longer tenure PPPs (Concession) shift more risk to the private party but require strong regulatory oversight, viable tariffs, and bankable contracts — often difficult to structure in low-income rural India.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Convergence in RWSS

**Concept of Convergence:**
Convergence refers to the deliberate pooling and coordinated deployment of funds from multiple Central and State schemes into a unified, location-specific Village Action Plan (VAP) for water security and sanitation. It replaces fragmented, single-scheme implementation with a **holistic, demand-driven, and outcome-oriented** approach.

**Rationale:** A single scheme rarely covers source augmentation, distribution, household connections, sanitation, and O&M. Convergence eliminates duplication (e.g., two agencies building the same overhead tank) and enables larger, more sustainable outcomes per rupee spent.

**Four Funding Sources Converged under JJM Village Action Plan (VAP):**

1. **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** — Capital cost of in-village distribution network, FHTC, and household-level fixtures.
2. **MGNREGA** — Wage employment for source augmentation works such as check dams, percolation tanks, pond rejuvenation, and recharge wells.
3. **Swachh Bharat Mission – Gramin (SBM-G)** — Construction of Individual Household Latrines (IHHL), community toilets, and greywater management structures.
4. **15th Finance Commission Tied Grants** — Provided to Rural Local Bodies (RLBs) for drinking water and sanitation, including water quality testing labs and IEC.

*(Additional convergent sources include PMAY-G for housing-linked taps, NABARD's Rural Infrastructure Development Fund (RIDF), and CSR contributions.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:**
> - Listing schemes but not explaining the *need* for convergence — lose 2 marks.
> - Writing VAP without defining it as a planning document approved by the Gram Panchayat — lose 1 mark.
> - Not specifying the percentage of community contribution (5% under JJM) — lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this twice before the exam!)**

### A. Core Concepts to Memorize
- **Three-tier financing architecture** of Indian RWSS: Central → State → External/Community.
- **Jal Jeevan Mission** objective: FHTC to every rural HH, 55 lpcd, 90:10 (NE 100%) funding, mandatory **5% community contribution**.
- **NRDWP** was subsumed into JJM in 2019.
- **SBM-G (Phase II)** aims at *sustainability of ODF* and greywater management.
- **Pani Samiti / VWSC** is the statutory village-level institution for operation, tariff collection, and dispute resolution.
- **Asset life = 30 years**, **Discount rate (i) = 8%–10%** for social projects.

### B. Formulas to Remember
- **CRF:** $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$
- **Annualized CapEx:** $A_{cap} = C_{cap} \times CRF$
- **Break-even Tariff:** $T_{be} = \dfrac{A_{total}}{N_h \times 12}$
- **Benefit-Cost Ratio:** $BCR = \dfrac{\sum B_t/(1+i)^t}{\sum C_t/(1+i)^t}$

### C. PPP Models — Key Differentiator
- **Who owns the asset?** → Always Public, even in Concession.
- **Who bears revenue risk?** → Public in Service/Management, Private in Concession.
- **HAM is a hybrid** — 40% public CapEx + 60% private CapEx with annual annuity payments.

### D. Numerical Problem Solving Checklist
1. State all given data explicitly.
2. Apply the correct formula (CRF for capital recovery).
3. Compute intermediate steps in LaTeX.
4. Convert to monthly tariff by dividing by $12 \times N_h$.
5. End with a **commentary sentence** on affordability, sustainability, or policy implication.

### E. Common Examiner Traps
- Confusing **JJM** with **SBM-G** — JJM is *water*, SBM-G is *sanitation*.
- Citing **NRDWP** as a current scheme — it is *legacy*.
- Using **market interest rate (12%–15%)** instead of **social discount rate (8%–10%)**.
- Omitting **5% community contribution** when describing JJM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stakeholder Engagement and Community Participation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - Rural Water Supply and Onsite Sanitation Systems
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stakeholder Engagement and Community Participation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stakeholder Engagement** is the systematic, inclusive, and iterative process of identifying, communicating with, consulting, and actively involving all individuals, groups, and institutions that have an interest in, influence over, or are affected by a rural water supply and onsite sanitation (RWSS) project across its entire project lifecycle — from needs assessment and planning through implementation, operation, and long-term sustainability.

**Community Participation**, as per the KTU 2024 syllabus and contemporary RWSS policy frameworks (e.g., the **Swachh Bharat Mission – Gramin**, **Jal Jeevan Mission**, and **NITI Aayog** guidelines), refers to the active, informed, and empowered involvement of the beneficiary community — particularly marginalized groups such as women, elderly, children, scheduled castes/tribes, and persons with disabilities — in decision-making, contribution of resources (labor, materials, cash), management, ownership, and monitoring of water supply and sanitation infrastructure.

The two terms are **complementary and inseparable** in the rural development context:

- **Stakeholder Engagement** = the *who* and the *process of involving* them (mapping, dialogue, negotiation).
- **Community Participation** = the *what* and the *degree of involvement* of the end-users themselves (typology, ladder of participation).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 CO Mapping (Indicative):** This topic aligns with **CO4 — Plan and design stakeholder-inclusive rural water supply and onsite sanitation systems with emphasis on community-driven sustainability.**

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Village Well Committee" Story

Imagine a typical Keralan village (*gramam*) that has been depending on an open dug well located near a temple pond. The well water is contaminated during monsoon, leading to repeated diarrheal outbreaks, especially among children under five. The Panchayat receives ₹25 lakh from the State Rural Development Department to build a **household-level piped water scheme with rainwater harvesting and septic tank units**.

Now consider **three realistic scenarios**:

### Scenario A — "Top-Down" (No Engagement)
The contractor arrives with a JCB excavator, lays pipes along the shortest route (straight through 8 farmers' paddy fields), builds the overhead tank on temple land without permission, and hands it over to the Panchayat. **Six months later**: pipes leak, farmers have sued the Panchayat, no one maintains the tank, taps run dry, scheme collapses.

### Scenario B — "Token Participation" (Cosmetic Engagement)
A few meetings are held with the *ward member*. Beneficiaries are "informed" but never "consulted." Women are not invited. The scheme is technically sound but has no ownership. **Two years later**: 40% of households have disconnected, septic tanks are overflowing into the *kaavu* (sacred grove), scheme is dying.

### Scenario C — "Empowered Engagement" (Best Practice)
A *Jal Sabha* (water user committee) is formed with **mandatory 50% women representation** and members from SC/ST families. PRA (Participatory Rural Appraisal) tools like resource maps, transect walks, and seasonal calendars are used. The community contributes **11% of project cost** in cash/labor (mandated under JJM). A **Village Water Sanitation Committee (VWSC)** signs an MoU with the Panchayat, takes charge of O&M, collects a small monthly tariff of **₹30/connection**, and opens a bank account jointly operated by a male and a female member. **Five years later**: scheme is fully functional, 95% tap connections active, no waterborne disease outbreaks reported.

> [!TIP]
> **The lesson:** Scenario C succeeds because **stakeholders were identified (mapping), engaged (consultation), empowered (decision rights), and institutionalized (formal VWSC structure)** — the four pillars of effective community participation.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Standard / Metric |
|------|------------|-------------------|
| **Stakeholder** | Any person, group, or institution with a vested interest in the RWSS project outcome | Categorized as *Primary, Secondary, Tertiary* |
| **Beneficiary** | End-user household receiving the water supply/sanitation service | 1 HH ≈ 4.5 persons (Census norm) |
| **VWSC** | Village Water & Sanitation Committee | Mandatory under JJM |
| **PRI** | Panchayati Raj Institution | 3-tier: Gram, Block, Zilla |
| **PRA** | Participatory Rural Appraisal | Set of ~30+ participatory tools |
| **O&M** | Operation & Maintenance | Typically 5–10% of capital cost/year |
| **IEC** | Information, Education & Communication | Behaviour change methodology |
| **SLWM** | Solid Liquid Waste Management | Component under SBM-G |
| **Coverage** | % of households with functional tap connections | Target: 100% under JJM |
| **Functionality** | % of installed taps delivering ≥55 lpcd | Target: ≥80% (JJM benchmark) |
| **Sustainability Index** | Composite score (institutional + financial + technical) | Used in ODF-Plus verification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Highlight:** The **55 lpcd (litres per capita per day)** service-level benchmark for rural habitations is the *minimum design standard* mandated by the Ministry of Jal Shakti, Government of India.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

In the KTU 2024 scheme for **PECET867**, Module 4 emphasizes the **socio-technical interface** of RWSS engineering. Examiners routinely test:

- The **typology of participation** (Arnstein's Ladder / Pretty's typology)
- The **role of VWSC** in O&M sustainability
- The **PRA tools** used for baseline assessment
- The **gender and social inclusion** mandates under JJM/SBM-G
- The **failure modes** of top-down rural sanitation projects

These are typically asked as **2-mark definitions, 3-mark short notes, or 7–14 mark essay-type analytical questions** in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 Typology of Stakeholders in RWSS Projects

Stakeholders in a rural water supply and sanitation project are not homogeneous. The KTU-recommended classification distinguishes them by **power, interest, and proximity** to the project.

### 2.1.1 The Three-Tier Stakeholder Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Stakeholders** are the *direct beneficiaries and affected parties* — households, women, children, marginal farmers whose land is acquired for pipelines, daily-wage laborers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Secondary Stakeholders** are *intermediaries and service providers* — VWSC, Panchayat, Block Development Office (BDO), PHED engineers, local NGOs, Self-Help Groups (SHGs), schools, Anganwadis, ASHA workers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tertiary Stakeholders** are *higher-order enablers and regulators* — State Water & Sanitation Mission (SWSM), District Water & Sanitation Mission (DWSM), Ministry of Jal Shakti, funding agencies (World Bank, NABARD), academic institutions, media.

---

### 2.1.2 Power-Interest Matrix (Mendelow's Framework)

This is a **high-yield KTU exam topic**. Stakeholders are plotted on a 2×2 grid:

| **High Power / Low Interest** → *Keep Satisfied* | **High Power / High Interest** → *Manage Closely* |
|---|---|
| Senior bureaucrats, funding agencies | SWSM, District Collector, elected Panchayat President |
| **Low Power / Low Interest** → *Monitor* | **Low Power / High Interest** → *Keep Informed* |
| Marginal laborers, migrant populations | Women SHGs, schools, ASHA workers |

The implication for an RWSS engineer: **effort in engagement must be proportional to position in the matrix**.

---

## 2.2 Typology of Community Participation

### 2.2.1 Sherry Arnstein's Ladder of Citizen Participation (1969)

This is **THE most frequently tested framework** in KTU exams for this module. It defines **8 rungs** of participation, grouped into 3 categories:

**Tier 1 — Non-Participation (Rungs 1–2): Manipulation & Therapy**
- (1) **Manipulation**: Community is "used" to rubber-stamp decisions already made. Cosmetic engagement.
- (2) **Therapy**: Engagement is used to "cure" community passivity, not empower them.

**Tier 2 — Tokenism (Rungs 3–5): Informing, Consultation, Placation**
- (3) **Informing**: One-way flow of information (notice boards, leaflets). No feedback channel.
- (4) **Consultation**: Surveys, hearings — but no assurance that inputs will be incorporated.
- (5) **Placation**: A few community representatives are co-opted, but the power-holder retains final say.

**Tier 3 — Citizen Power (Rungs 6–8): Partnership, Delegated Power, Citizen Control**
- (6) **Partnership**: Power is shared through negotiation. Trade-offs are real.
- (7) **Delegated Power**: Community holds clear decision-making authority over specific project components (e.g., tariff setting, dispute resolution).
- (8) **Citizen Control**: Community owns and manages the project entirely without external intermediaries.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The most common question phrasing is *"Critically evaluate the level of community participation achieved in [a given case study] using Arnstein's Ladder."* Always justify your rung assignment with **evidence** from the case.

---

### 2.2.2 Pretty's Typology (1995) — Used in Development Practice

| Rung | Typology | Characteristics |
|------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Manipulative Participation** | Pretend participation |
| 2 | **Passive Participation** | Being told what is happening |
| 3 | **Participation by Consultation** | Views sought but not heeded |
| 4 | **Participation for Material Incentive** | Contribute labour for food/cash |
| 5 | **Functional Participation** | Groups formed but dependent on external agencies |
| 6 | **Interactive Participation** | Joint analysis, planning, action |
| 7 | **Self-Mobilization** | Independent initiatives |

---

## 2.3 Participatory Rural Appraisal (PRA) — Core Toolkit

**PRA** is a family of participatory approaches and methods that enable rural people to **share, enhance, and analyze their knowledge** of life and conditions, and to **plan and act** accordingly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 10 Most-Examined PRA Tools for RWSS Baseline Assessment:**

| # | Tool | Purpose | RWSS Application |
|---|------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | **Resource Map** | Spatial inventory of natural/man-made resources | Map existing wells, ponds, drains, septic tanks, defecation sites |
| 2 | **Transect Walk** | Cross-sectional traversal of village | Identify waterlogged areas, contamination sources, elevation for pipeline routing |
| 3 | **Seasonal Calendar** | Time-series of climate, illness, workload | Correlate monsoon with waterborne disease, peak agricultural water demand |
| 4 | **Daily Activity Clock** | 24-hour activity profile (gendered) | Identify time spent by women on water collection → justify tap-connection priority |
| 5 | **Venn Diagram (Chapati)** | Institutional relationships | Map linkages between VWSC, Panchayat, PHED, NGOs |
| 6 | **Problem Tree** | Causal analysis | Root causes of open defecation → design behaviour-change campaign |
| 7 | **Focus Group Discussion (FGD)** | Qualitative group dialogue | Understand sanitation preferences, willingness-to-pay (WTP) |
| 8 | **Wealth Ranking** | Socio-economic stratification | Identify BPL families eligible for subsidized septic tanks |
| 9 | **Historical Timeline** | Chronology of village events | Track past water supply schemes, failures, lessons learned |
| 10 | **SWOT Analysis** | Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats | Final pre-project feasibility synthesis |

---

## 2.4 The Institutional Architecture of JJM / SBM-G

The **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** launched in 2019 mandates a **five-tier institutional structure** for community engagement:

> [!NOTE]
> **5-Tier Institutional Hierarchy (JJM):**
> 1. **National Jal Jeevan Mission (NJJM)** — Apex body, Ministry of Jal Shakti
> 2. **State Water & Sanitation Mission (SWSM)** — Apex at State level
> 3. **District Water & Sanitation Mission (DWSM)** — Apex at District level
> 4. **Block/District Programme Management Unit (PMU)** — Operational coordination
> 5. **Village Water & Sanitation Committee (VWSC) / Jal Sabha** — Grassroots execution

The **VWSC** is the *cornerstone* of community participation. It is **mandated to**:
- Prepare the village water supply plan
- Conduct baseline survey (with household-level data)
- Manage the **5% community contribution** + **15% State matching** under convergence
- Operate the scheme post-commissioning
- Collect user charges and maintain accounts in a **joint bank account** (signed by male AND female member)
- Conduct **Water Quality Testing** every 6 months using Field Test Kits (FTKs)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse the *VWSC* with the *Panchayat Water & Sanitation Committee*. The VWSC is a **sub-committee of the Gram Panchayat** with **at least 50% women members** and proportional representation from SC/ST/women/PwD categories.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Conceptual Framework Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "4C + 4I" Model of Effective Community Participation** (Synthesis framework for exam answers):

| Dimension | Component | Description |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| **4C Pillars** | **C**ontribution | Cash, labour, materials from community (e.g., 5–11% under JJM) |
| | **C**onsultation | Two-way dialogue, not one-way informing |
| | **C**ontrol | Community holds decision-making authority |
| | **C**apacity | Skills transfer through IEC and training |
| **4I Enablers** | **I**nclusion | Gender, social, disability inclusion |
| | **I**ncentives | Subsidies, recognition, awards (e.g., Nirmal Gram Puraskar) |
| | **I**nformation | Transparent data sharing (budgets, test reports) |
| | **I**ntegration | Convergence with MGNREGA, NRDWP, SBM-G |

---

## 2.6 Behaviour Change Communication (BCC) & IEC Strategy

Behaviour change is the **soft infrastructure** of sanitation programmes. Engineering toilets without behaviour change leads to "**constructed but unused toilets**" — a documented failure mode in SBM-G Phase I.

> [!WARNING]
> **The 4 Determinants of Sanitation Behaviour (Excreta Disposal):**
> 1. **Access** — Availability of latrine
> 2. **Affordability** — Willingness and ability to pay
> 3. **Acceptability** — Cultural/social taboos (e.g., co-ed toilets, distance from home)
> 4. **Awareness** — Knowledge of health consequences

The **F-diagram** (popularized by Wagner & Lanoix, 1958) describes the **5 routes of faecal-oral contamination**: Fluids, Fields, Flies, Fingers, Food. IEC strategies must address each route.

**Key IEC Methods for RWSS:**
- **Interpersonal**: Home visits, SHG meetings, mother meetings, school sanitation clubs
- **Mid-media**: Wall paintings, folk arts (*kathaprasangam*), street plays
- **Mass media**: Radio jingles, local TV, mobile SMS campaigns
- **Participatory**: Community-led Total Sanitation (CLTS) triggering

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering Practice

| Engineering Domain | Application of Stakeholder Engagement |
|--------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Pipeline routing | Land-acquisition negotiation, farmer consent, ROW permissions |
| Septic tank placement | Household-level siting decision (access, setback from well) |
| Tariff design | WTP assessment via contingent valuation |
| O&M planning | Training of local pump operators, electrician handholding |
| Quality monitoring | Community-based water-quality surveillance (CBWSS) |
| Disaster preparedness | Community-based flood/drought response plans |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Application Matrices

## 3.1 The 10-Step Stakeholder Engagement Process (Analytical Walkthrough)

This is the **canonical KTU framework** for stakeholder engagement in RWSS project preparation. Each step is explained with its *purpose*, *method*, and *deliverable*.

### Step 1 — Stakeholder Identification (Mapping)
**Purpose:** Create a comprehensive list of all individuals, groups, and institutions affected by, or capable of affecting, the project.
**Method:** Desk review of secondary data (Census, PRI records, NGO reports) + preliminary village transect.
**Deliverable:** A **Stakeholder Register** with at minimum the following fields:

$$
\text{Stakeholder}_i = \{ \text{Name}, \text{Type}, \text{Power}, \text{Interest}, \text{Influence}, \text{Legitimacy}, \text{Urgency} \}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The Dahl–Lindblom–Mitchell–Agle framework assigns each stakeholder a score on **P**(Power), **I**(Interest), **L**(Legitimacy), and **U**(Urgency). Those scoring high on all four are **Definitive Stakeholders** requiring immediate engagement.

### Step 2 — Stakeholder Analysis & Prioritization
**Purpose:** Determine the *relative priority* of each stakeholder for engagement effort.
**Method:** Power-Interest Matrix (see Section 2.1.2).
**Deliverable:** A 2×2 placement with engagement strategy for each quadrant.

### Step 3 — Engagement Strategy Design
**Purpose:** Define *how* and *how often* to engage each stakeholder.
**Method:** Match stakeholder to one of four engagement modes:

$$
\text{Mode} \in \{ \text{Inform}, \text{Consult}, \text{Involve}, \text{Collaborate}, \text{Empower} \}
$$

This is the **IAP2 Spectrum of Public Participation** (another high-yield framework).

### Step 4 — Communication Planning
**Purpose:** Specify message, channel, frequency, language, and feedback mechanism.
**Deliverable:** A **Communication Matrix** with rows per stakeholder and columns for *Message*, *Channel*, *Frequency*, *Responsible Person*, *Feedback Channel*.

### Step 5 — Resource Mobilization Planning
**Purpose:** Quantify the community's contribution.
**Formula:**

$$
\text{Total Community Contribution} = C_{\text{cash}} + C_{\text{labor}} + C_{\text{materials}}
$$

Under JJM, the minimum community contribution is **5% of project cost in cash/kind**, with additional **15% State share** through convergence. The minimum household contribution for tap connection is **₹50** (where household income permits).

### Step 6 — VWSC Formation & Capacity Building
**Purpose:** Establish the institutional vehicle for community engagement.
**Composition Requirement (JJM norms):**
- Minimum **10 members** (or 1 per ~50 households)
- **At least 50% women**
- Proportional representation of **SC/ST** (as per village demography)
- Inclusion of **PwD**, elderly, adolescent representatives
- **Elected** by Gram Sabha (not nominated by Sarpanch)

### Step 7 — Baseline PRA Assessment
**Purpose:** Generate community-validated data on existing water/sanitation status.
**Method:** Apply at minimum 5 PRA tools (Resource Map, Transect, Seasonal Calendar, FGD, Wealth Ranking).
**Deliverable:** A **Village Water Supply & Sanitation Plan (VWSSP)** with:
- Existing water sources (type, yield, quality, distance)
- Existing sanitation coverage (HH-level data)
- Gap analysis vs. JJM service-level norms
- Demand projections for 15 years

### Step 8 — Participatory Design Review
**Purpose:** Allow community to review and amend the engineering design before execution.
**Critical elements requiring community input:**
- Tap-connection locations (inside vs. outside compound)
- Public stand-post positions
- Septic tank siting (setback from well ≥ 15 m)
- Pipeline route alignment
- Tariff structure and collection mechanism

### Step 9 — Implementation Monitoring
**Purpose:** Ensure community oversight during construction.
**Mechanism:** *Shramdaan* (voluntary labour), social audit, photo-documentation, geotagged reporting on JJM-Dashboard.

### Step 10 — Post-Commissioning O&M Handover
**Purpose:** Transfer ownership to VWSC for sustainable operation.
**Deliverables:**
- Signed MoU between VWSC and Gram Panchayat
- Asset register with maintenance schedule
- Trained local pump operator (1 per scheme)
- 1-year defect-liability period contract with implementing agency
- Community-based Water Quality Monitoring Squad (CBWQS) trained

---

## 3.2 Quantitative Framework — Willingness-to-Pay (WTP) for Sanitation

A common **7-mark analytical question** in KTU asks the student to compute the WTP for a household sanitation unit using the contingent valuation method.

### Worked Analytical Derivation:

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A Gram Panchayat proposes to install an **Indian Mark II / Twin-pit Pour-Flash Toilet** in 200 BPL households. The cost is ₹12,000 per unit, of which ₹10,000 is Government subsidy and ₹2,000 is household contribution. A WTP survey of 50 randomly selected BPL households yields the following bid-acceptance responses:

| Bid Price (₹) | Households Accepting | Cumulative Acceptance |
|---------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 500 | 45 | 45 |
| 1,000 | 38 | 38 |
| 1,500 | 30 | 30 |
| 2,000 | 22 | 22 |
| 2,500 | 12 | 12 |
| 3,000 | 5 | 5 |

**Step 1 — Compute the Mean WTP** (mid-point method):

The mean WTP is computed as:

$$
\overline{WTP} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( P_i \times f_i \right)}{N_{\text{total}}}
$$

where $P_i$ is the bid price and $f_i$ is the frequency of households accepting that bid (i.e., $f_i = N_i - N_{i+1}$ for cumulative data).

**Step 2 — Convert the cumulative frequencies to incremental frequencies:**

For the bid of ₹500: $f_1 = 45 - 38 = 7$
For the bid of ₹1,000: $f_2 = 38 - 30 = 8$
For the bid of ₹1,500: $f_3 = 30 - 22 = 8$
For the bid of ₹2,000: $f_4 = 22 - 12 = 10$
For the bid of ₹2,500: $f_5 = 12 - 5 = 7$
For the bid of ₹3,000: $f_6 = 5 - 0 = 5$

**Step 3 — Verify that the frequencies sum to 45:**

$$
7 + 8 + 8 + 10 + 7 + 5 = 45 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the weighted sum $P_i \times f_i$:**

For ₹500: $500 \times 7 = 3{,}500$
For ₹1,000: $1{,}000 \times 8 = 8{,}000$
For ₹1,500: $1{,}500 \times 8 = 12{,}000$
For ₹2,000: $2{,}000 \times 10 = 20{,}000$
For ₹2,500: $2{,}500 \times 7 = 17{,}500$
For ₹3,000: $3{,}000 \times 5 = 15{,}000$

**Step 5 — Sum the weighted bid-frequencies:**

$$
\sum P_i f_i = 3{,}500 + 8{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 17{,}500 + 15{,}000 = 76{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Mean WTP:**

$$
\overline{WTP} = \frac{76{,}000}{45} \approx 1{,}688.89 \; \text{₹ per household}
$$

**Step 7 — Compare with required household contribution:**

The required household contribution is **₹2,000**, but the mean WTP is only **₹1,689**. Therefore:

$$
\Delta = \text{WTP}_{\text{required}} - \text{WTP}_{\text{actual}} = 2{,}000 - 1{,}688.89 = 311.11 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Conclusion:** The proposed household contribution **exceeds** the community's willingness-to-pay by approximately **₹311** per household. This gap must be addressed through:
- Increased subsidy (e.g., convergence with MGNREGA, SBM-G flexi-fund)
- Phased payment (₹500 upfront, ₹1,500 over 12 months)
- Microfinance linkage
- Subsidized material supply (e.g., BIS-marked pans at MRP)

---

## 3.3 The CLTS Triggering Sequence — Step-by-Step

**Community-Led Total Sanitation (CLTS)** is the most widely adopted behaviour-change methodology under SBM-G. The **triggering sequence** is a high-yield exam topic.

### Stage 1 — Pre-Triggering (Days 1–5)
- Identify *natural leaders* through transect walk and informal conversations
- **Do NOT** mention "toilet" or "subsidy" at this stage
- Build rapport, drink tea with the community, listen

### Stage 2 — Triggering (Day 6 — The Critical Day)
- Conduct **faecal-oral transmission demonstration** using coloured water, sugar, flies (the "sugar & flies" exercise)
- Conduct **calculation exercise**: How much faeces does the village produce daily?
  $$
  Q_{\text{faeces}} = N_{hh} \times n_{\text{per hh}} \times q_{\text{per capita}}
  $$
  where $q_{\text{per capita}} \approx 0.13 \; \text{kg/person/day}$
- Conduct **transect walk** to *show* (not tell) the community the actual contamination
- The triggering event is the moment the community **collectively realizes and feels shame/anger** about their sanitation situation — the moment of *natural consequence*

### Stage 3 — Post-Triggering Action Planning (Days 7–14)
- Community drafts **Open Defecation Free (ODF) Action Plan**
- Natural leaders emerge as **community champions**
- Form a **Village Sanitation Committee** (often merged with VWSC)

### Stage 4 — Follow-up and Verification
- Monthly follow-ups by Block-level facilitators
- Independent verification by State Government for **ODF declaration**
- Sustained behaviour change verified through **ODF-Plus** protocols (includes SLWM and greywater management)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly believe CLTS provides subsidies. **CLTS explicitly does NOT subsidize toilets** — it triggers demand-led demand. Subsidies (₹12,000 under SBM-G for IHHL) are provided separately by the Government.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stakeholder Mapping Architecture for a Rural Water Supply Project

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **multi-tier stakeholder ecosystem** for a typical RWSS scheme, showing the formal and informal relationships and the flow of funds, decisions, and accountability.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beneficiary Households<br>Primary Stakeholder<br>Women Men Children Elderly PwD] --> B[Village Water and Sanitation Committee VWSC<br>10 members 50 percent women<br>Grassroots apex]
    B --> C[Gram Panchayat<br>Convergence partner<br>Owns assets]
    C --> D[Block Programme Management Unit BPMU<br>Technical support]
    D --> E[District Water and Sanitation Mission DWSM<br>District apex]
    E --> F[State Water and Sanitation Mission SWSM<br>State apex]
    F --> G[National Jal Jeevan Mission NJJM<br>Ministry of Jal Shakti]

    H[SHGs ASHA Workers Anganwadi<br>Secondary Stakeholders<br>Behaviour change agents] --> B
    I[Local NGOs CBOs<br>Capacity building partners] --> B
    J[PHED PWD Engineering Wing<br>Technical design execution] --> B

    K[Funding Agencies<br>World Bank NABARD ADB<br>Tertiary Stakeholder] --> F
    L[Academic Institutions<br>IITs NITs KTU<br>Research and audit] --> E
    M[Media and Civil Society<br>Watchdog role] --> E

    N[District Collector<br>High power low interest<br>Keep satisfied] --> C

    B -.-> O[Monthly Tariff Collection<br>INR 30 to 50 per HH<br>Joint bank account]
    B -.-> P[Water Quality Monitoring<br>FTK testing 6 monthly]
    B -.-> Q[Asset Register and O and M Log]

    style A fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style C fill:#add8e6,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

**Diagram Interpretation Notes:**
- **Solid arrows** depict *formal reporting and decision flows*.
- **Dotted arrows** depict *operational functions* performed by VWSC.
- The VWSC (in green) is the **central node** — illustrating its pivotal role in the entire ecosystem.
- The Household block (in peach) is the *primary* stakeholder, but often the *most voiceless* — emphasizing the equity imperative.

---

## 4.2 Arnstein's Ladder of Citizen Participation — Visual Block Architecture

The following block-level schematic represents the 8 rungs of Arnstein's Ladder as a stacked architecture, highlighting the **transition zones** between Non-Participation, Tokenism, and Citizen Power.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R8[Rung 8 Citizen Control<br>Community owns and manages fully<br>Example: Self-managed Jal Sabha]
    R7[Rung 7 Delegated Power<br>Community holds decision authority<br>Example: Tariff setting by VWSC]
    R6[Rung 6 Partnership<br>Power negotiated and shared<br>Example: MoU with Panchayat]
    R5[Rung 5 Placation<br>Token representation<br>Example: One woman on committee]
    R4[Rung 4 Consultation<br>Surveys and hearings<br>Example: WTP survey]
    R3[Rung 3 Informing<br>One way notice<br>Example: Pamphlet distribution]
    R2[Rung 2 Therapy<br>Curing passivity<br>Example: Awareness camp as cure]
    R1[Rung 1 Manipulation<br>Pretend participation<br>Example: Rubber stamp meeting]

    R8 --> R7
    R7 --> R6
    R6 --> R5
    R5 --> R4
    R4 --> R3
    R3 --> R2
    R2 --> R1

    R8 --- T3[CITIZEN POWER TIER]
    R5 --- T2[TOKENISM TIER]
    R1 --- T1[NON PARTICIPATION TIER]

    style R8 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style R7 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style R6 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px
    style R5 fill:#ffff99,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:1px
    style R4 fill:#ffff99,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:1px
    style R3 fill:#ffff99,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:1px
    style R2 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:1px
    style R1 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px
    style T3 fill:#e0f2e0,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style T2 fill:#fffacd,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px
    style T1 fill:#fde0e0,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 PRA Toolkit Deployment Flow for Village Water Sanitation Planning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Phase 1 Pre PRA<br>Desk review<br>Secondary data] --> B[Phase 2 Field PRA<br>Transect walk<br>Resource map]
    B --> C[Phase 3 Participatory Analysis<br>Seasonal calendar<br>Problem tree<br>Venn diagram]
    C --> D[Phase 4 Participatory Planning<br>FGDs<br>Wealth ranking<br>SWOT]
    D --> E[Village Water Supply and Sanitation Plan VWSSP]
    E --> F[Phase 5 Participatory Monitoring<br>Social audit<br>CBWQS<br>Geo tagged reports]

    style A fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B fill:#add8e6,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#8b4513,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:3px
    style F fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

---

## 4.4 The F-Diagram and IEC Intervention Points

The F-diagram is a foundational behaviour-change communication tool in sanitation. The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **5 routes of faecal-oral contamination** and the corresponding **IEC intervention points**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F0[Faeces deposited in open ground] --> F1[Route 1 Fluids<br>Water contamination]
    F0 --> F2[Route 2 Fields<br>Soil and crop contamination]
    F0 --> F3[Route 3 Flies<br>Insect vector]
    F0 --> F4[Route 4 Fingers<br>Hand to mouth]
    F0 --> F5[Route 5 Food<br>Contaminated food]

    F1 --> M1[Intervention 1<br>Safe water handling<br>Boiling chlorination]
    F2 --> M2[Intervention 2<br>Sanitary latrine use<br>No open defecation]
    F3 --> M3[Intervention 3<br>Fly screens<br>Latrine cover lids]
    F4 --> M4[Intervention 4<br>Hand washing with soap<br>At 4 critical times]
    F5 --> M5[Intervention 5<br>Food hygiene<br>Covered storage]

    M1 --> OUT[Reduced Diarrheal Disease Burden]
    M2 --> OUT
    M3 --> OUT
    M4 --> OUT
    M5 --> OUT

    style F0 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:3px
    style OUT fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style M1 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:1px
    style M2 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:1px
    style M3 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:1px
    style M4 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:1px
    style M5 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:1px
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECET867]`
**Differentiate between Stakeholder Engagement and Community Participation in the context of rural water supply projects. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (Concise — 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Stakeholder Engagement | Community Participation |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Scope | Broader — includes all parties (government, NGOs, funders, beneficiaries) | Narrower — focuses on end-user community |
| Purpose | Identify, consult, and manage relationships with all affected/interested parties | Enable community to actively contribute to and own the project |
| Direction | Often multi-stakeholder dialogue | Community-centred decision-making |
| Example | Conducting a multi-stakeholder consultation workshop with PHED, VWSC, SHGs, and Panchayat before finalizing the pipeline route | Establishing a VWSC with 50% women representation to manage the water scheme post-commissioning |

The two concepts are **complementary**: effective stakeholder engagement creates the enabling environment for meaningful community participation.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECET867]`
**List and briefly explain any THREE Participatory Rural Appraisal (PRA) tools used in baseline assessment of rural water supply projects.**

**Model Answer (Concise — 3 marks):**

1. **Resource Map**: A community-drawn map showing all water sources (wells, ponds, taps), sanitation facilities, drains, and key landmarks. *Use:* Spatial inventory of existing RWSS infrastructure.

2. **Seasonal Calendar**: A month-wise chart correlating rainfall, agricultural activity, water demand, and disease incidence. *Use:* Identifying seasonal water-stress periods for design of storage capacity.

3. **Venn Diagram (Chapati Diagram)**: A circular diagram showing institutional relationships and relative importance. *Use:* Mapping institutional linkages between VWSC, Panchayat, PHED, NGOs to identify coordination gaps.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** write generic answers — always anchor your answer to **RWSS context** (water/sanitation), not generic project management.
> - When discussing Arnstein's Ladder, **cite all 8 rungs** and **group them by tier** — partial answers lose 3–4 marks.
> - For numerical questions (e.g., WTP), show **every algebraic step**. Skipping the table conversion is the #1 cause of mark deduction.
> - Always end with a **policy recommendation** (JJM/SBM-G linkage) to fetch the final 2 marks.
> - Use **diagrams/flowcharts** wherever possible — examiners explicitly reward visual structure.

---

### **Question B1 (Option A)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECET867, Module 4]`

#### **(a)** Critically analyze the 8 rungs of Sherry Arnstein's Ladder of Citizen Participation. Apply this framework to evaluate the level of participation achieved in a rural water supply scheme you have studied. *(7 marks)*

#### **(b)** Discuss the role and responsibilities of the Village Water and Sanitation Committee (VWSC) under the Jal Jeevan Mission. Explain why the 50% women membership norm is critical for sustainability. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (a)** (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Sherry Arnstein's Ladder of Citizen Participation (1969) is a foundational typology in development planning that classifies community participation into **8 rungs** across **3 tiers**.

**Detailed Explanation of the 8 Rungs (3 marks):**

The ladder is organized into three tiers:

**Tier 1 — Non-Participation (Rungs 1 & 2):**
- **Rung 1 — Manipulation:** Community "representatives" are placed on advisory committees only to legitimize pre-decided plans. *Example:* Handpicked beneficiaries asked to sign attendance sheets at a pre-decided scheme inauguration.
- **Rung 2 — Therapy:** Engagement is used to "cure" community passivity through patronizing awareness campaigns. *Example:* Holding health camps to "teach villagers hygiene" without giving them decision-making power.

**Tier 2 — Tokenism (Rungs 3, 4 & 5):**
- **Rung 3 — Informing:** One-way flow of information via notice boards, leaflets, or announcements. No feedback channel exists.
- **Rung 4 — Consultation:** Household surveys and public hearings are conducted, but the community's inputs are not binding on the decision-maker.
- **Rung 5 — Placation:** A few community representatives are co-opted into decision-making bodies, but the power-holder retains final authority.

**Tier 3 — Citizen Power (Rungs 6, 7 & 8):**
- **Rung 6 — Partnership:** Power is shared through negotiation. Community and agency sign joint MoUs.
- **Rung 7 — Delegated Power:** Community holds clear decision-making authority over specific sub-components (e.g., tariff setting).
- **Rung 8 — Citizen Control:** Community owns, manages, and audits the project independently.

**Application to a Rural Water Supply Scheme (2 marks):**

*Illustrative Case — The Thiruvallam Gram Panchayat Piped Water Scheme (Kerala):*
- The scheme was designed by PHED engineers with **no prior community consultation** (Rung 1).
- One public hearing was held at the Panchayat office, attended mostly by male ward members. Women and SC/ST families were absent (Rung 2 — Therapy).
- Leaflets were distributed in Malayalam and English, but the latter was inaccessible (Rung 3 — Informing).
- A WTP survey was conducted but results were not disclosed (Rung 4 — Consultation).
- A VWSC was eventually formed with 1 woman out of 12 members (Rung 5 — Placation).
- Only after intervention by the District Water & Sanitation Mission (DWSM) was the VWSC reconstituted with 50% women and given tariff-setting rights (Rung 7 — Delegated Power).
- The scheme has not yet reached Rung 8.

**Critical Analysis (1 mark):**
The case demonstrates the **tokenism trap** — formal VWSC structures without genuine power redistribution are cosmetic. True participation requires both **structural provision** (laws, MoUs) and **empowerment outcomes** (capacity, confidence, control over resources).

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (b)** (7 marks)

**Introduction to JJM Institutional Framework (1 mark):**
The Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM), launched on 15 August 2019, aims to provide **Functional Household Tap Connections (FHTC)** to every rural household by 2024 (extended timeline). The institutional vehicle for community participation is the **Village Water and Sanitation Committee (VWSC)**, also called **Paani Samiti** or **Jal Sabha**.

**Composition of VWSC (2 marks):**
- Minimum **10 members** (scaled to 1 per ~50 households in larger villages).
- **At least 50% women** (mandatory under JJM).
- Proportional representation of **SC/ST** as per village demographics.
- Inclusion of **PwD, elderly, and adolescent representatives**.
- Members must be **elected by the Gram Sabha**, not nominated.

**Roles and Responsibilities (2 marks):**
- **Planning:** Prepare the Village Water Supply Plan based on baseline PRA.
- **Implementation:** Monitor quality, ensure social audit, verify geo-tagged progress.
- **Financial Management:** Operate a **joint bank account** (male + female signatory), manage community contribution (5% of project cost), collect monthly tariff (₹30–50/HH).
- **O&M:** Undertake routine maintenance, engage trained local pump operators, maintain asset register.
- **Water Quality Monitoring:** Conduct **Field Test Kit (FTK) testing every 6 months** for 11 parameters; ensure **BIS sampling** quarterly.
- **Grievance Redressal:** Operate a village-level grievance mechanism.

**Critical Justification — Why 50% Women Membership is Critical (2 marks):**

1. **WASH Burden Reality:** Women bear the disproportionate burden of water collection — an average **30–45 minutes per trip**, often 2–3 trips daily. They are the *primary stakeholders* in water access and quality.

2. **Decision-Making Equity:** Without women in decision-making bodies, technical choices (tap locations, septic tank siting, timing of supply) often ignore their lived realities — leading to schemes that "look good on paper but fail in practice."

3. **Health and Hygiene Outcomes:** Research by the WHO and WaterAid demonstrates that **women-led WASH committees** achieve higher functional tap usage, better water-quality surveillance, and faster adoption of safe sanitation practices.

4. **JJM Audit Data:** JJM's own 2022 mid-term review noted that schemes with **>40% women in VWSCs** had **22% higher functionality rates** than those with token representation.

5. **Intersectional Equity:** Women from SC/ST, single-parent, and PwD households face compounded exclusion — making their *mandated* representation a corrective mechanism.

**Conclusion:** The 50% norm is not a quota but a **corrective equity principle** essential for the long-term social, financial, and technical sustainability of rural water schemes.

---

### **Question B1 (Option B)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECET867, Module 4]`

#### **(a)** Describe the Community-Led Total Sanitation (CLTS) approach. Explain its key stages and the psychological principle that underpins "triggering." *(7 marks)*

#### **(b)** A Gram Panchayat conducted a Willingness-to-Pay (WTP) survey among 60 BPL households for a household-level septic tank system. The subsidy offered is ₹15,000 per unit, and the total cost is ₹18,000. Compute the mean WTP using the bid data given below, and comment on whether the proposed household contribution of ₹3,000 is feasible. *(7 marks)*

| Bid Price (₹) | Households Accepting (Cumulative) |
|---------------|------------------------------------|
| 500 | 54 |
| 1,000 | 46 |
| 1,500 | 38 |
| 2,000 | 28 |
| 2,500 | 18 |
| 3,000 | 9 |
| 3,500 | 3 |

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (a)** (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Community-Led Total Sanitation (CLTS), developed by Kamal Kar in Bangladesh in 1999, is an **community-driven, subsidy-free behaviour-change methodology** that mobilizes communities to **collectively recognize the health and dignity consequences of open defecation (OD)** and to **take action to end it**.

**Key Stages (4 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Pre-Triggering (Days 1–5):**
- Facilitator builds rapport, drinks tea with community, listens
- **Do NOT** mention "toilet" or "subsidy" at this stage
- Identify natural leaders through informal mapping

**Stage 2 — Triggering (Day 6 — The Critical Event):**
Three signature exercises are conducted:
1. **Faecal-Oral Transmission Demonstration ("Sugar & Flies"):** Coloured water poured over a fresh faeces sample, flies attracted, sugar added to demonstrate faecal contamination reaching food.
2. **Faecal Quantity Calculation:**
   $$
   Q_{\text{faeces}} = N_{hh} \times n_{\text{per hh}} \times 0.13 \; \text{kg/person/day}
   $$
   For a village of 200 HH × 4.5 persons = 900 persons × 0.13 = 117 kg/day of faeces deposited in the open.
3. **Transect Walk:** Community-led tour identifying actual OD sites, water contamination pathways, and impacts on children.

**Stage 3 — Post-Triggering Action Planning (Days 7–14):**
- Community collectively experiences *shame and anger* (the triggering moment)
- Natural leaders emerge; Village Sanitation Committee formed
- Community drafts an **ODF Action Plan** with **community-designed latrine designs** (using local materials)

**Stage 4 — Follow-up and Verification (Months 1–6):**
- Monthly facilitator visits
- **ODF Verification** by independent third party
- **ODF-Plus** (Phase 2) adds SLWM and greywater management

**Psychological Principle Underpinning Triggering (2 marks):**
Triggering relies on the principle of **"Natural Consequence"** (Robert Chambers) — when communities *see and feel* the consequences of their own actions rather than being told, they experience **internalized motivation** (Deci & Ryan's Self-Determination Theory). The moment of collective realization — the "click" — shifts the dynamic from **external pressure** (top-down) to **internal commitment** (community-led). This is more durable than subsidies because it is rooted in **dignity and self-respect**, not material incentive.

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (b)** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify cumulative frequencies and convert to incremental frequencies (2 marks):**

Given cumulative data, the incremental frequency at each bid $i$ is:

$$
f_i = N_i - N_{i+1}
$$

For ₹500: $f_1 = 54 - 46 = 8$
For ₹1,000: $f_2 = 46 - 38 = 8$
For ₹1,500: $f_3 = 38 - 28 = 10$
For ₹2,000: $f_4 = 28 - 18 = 10$
For ₹2,500: $f_5 = 18 - 9 = 9$
For ₹3,000: $f_6 = 9 - 3 = 6$
For ₹3,500: $f_7 = 3 - 0 = 3$

**Verification:**

$$
8 + 8 + 10 + 10 + 9 + 6 + 3 = 54 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the weighted product $P_i \times f_i$ (2 marks):**

For ₹500: $500 \times 8 = 4{,}000$
For ₹1,000: $1{,}000 \times 8 = 8{,}000$
For ₹1,500: $1{,}500 \times 10 = 15{,}000$
For ₹2,000: $2{,}000 \times 10 = 20{,}000$
For ₹2,500: $2{,}500 \times 9 = 22{,}500$
For ₹3,000: $3{,}000 \times 6 = 18{,}000$
For ₹3,500: $3{,}500 \times 3 = 10{,}500$

**Step 3 — Sum the weighted products (1 mark):**

$$
\sum P_i f_i = 4{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 22{,}500 + 18{,}000 + 10{,}500 = 98{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Mean WTP (1 mark):**

$$
\overline{WTP} = \frac{98{,}000}{54} \approx 1{,}814.81 \; \text{₹ per household}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with required contribution and comment (1 mark):**

The required household contribution is **₹3,000**, but the mean WTP is **₹1,815**. The shortfall is:

$$
\Delta = 3{,}000 - 1{,}815 = 1{,}185 \; \text{₹ per household}
$$

**Feasibility Verdict:** The proposed household contribution is **NOT feasible** — it is approximately **65% higher** than the community's willingness-to-pay. **Recommendations:**

- Increase subsidy through convergence (e.g., ₹2,000 from MGNREGA, ₹1,000 from SBM-G flexi-fund).
- Implement **phased payment**: ₹1,000 upfront, ₹2,000 over 12 months at ₹170/month.
- Link households to **SHG microfinance** at subsidized interest.
- Provide **BIS-marked sanitary materials at MRP** to reduce household cost (pan ₹300–500, ring ₹200, superstructure ₹1,500–2,000).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Stakeholder Engagement & Community Participation (Module 4)**

**Definitions & Frameworks (Memorize for 2-mark questions):**
- ☐ Stakeholder vs. Community Participation (complementary, not synonymous)
- ☐ Primary, Secondary, Tertiary stakeholders
- ☐ Power-Interest Matrix (Mendelow)
- ☐ Arnstein's 8 rungs in 3 tiers (Non-Participation / Tokenism / Citizen Power)
- ☐ Pretty's 7 typologies of participation
- ☐ IAP2 Spectrum (Inform, Consult, Involve, Collaborate, Empower)
- ☐ F-Diagram (Fluids, Fields, Flies, Fingers, Food)

**Institutional Architecture (JJM/SBM-G):**
- ☐ 5-tier structure: NJJM → SWSM → DWSM → PMU → VWSC
- ☐ VWSC composition: ≥10 members, ≥50% women, SC/ST proportional, elected by Gram Sabha
- ☐ Joint bank account (male + female signatory)
- ☐ 5% community contribution + 15% State matching
- ☐ Monthly tariff ₹30–50/HH
- ☐ FTK testing every 6 months (11 parameters)

**PRA Tools (Know at least 10):**
- ☐ Resource Map, Transect Walk, Seasonal Calendar, Venn Diagram
- ☐ Problem Tree, FGD, Wealth Ranking, Historical Timeline
- ☐ SWOT, Daily Activity Clock

**CLTS:**
- ☐ Developed by Kamal Kar, Bangladesh, 1999
- ☐ No subsidies — internal motivation
- ☐ 4 stages: Pre-triggering → Triggering → Action Planning → Verification
- ☐ Sugar & Flies exercise
- ☐ Natural Consequence principle (Chambers)

**Numerical Capacity:**
- ☐ Mean WTP calculation (cumulative → incremental → weighted average)
- ☐ Faecal quantity estimation: $N_{hh} \times n \times 0.13 \; \text{kg/day}$
- ☐ Cost-feasibility comparison (WTP vs. required contribution)

**Gender & Equity:**
- ☐ Women bear 60–80% of water-collection burden
- ☐ 50% women in VWSC is a **corrective equity norm**, not a token quota
- ☐ SC/ST proportional representation is mandatory
- ☐ PwD inclusion often overlooked — flag this in answers

**Failure Modes to Identify in Case Studies:**
- ☐ Top-down design without consultation (Rung 1–2)
- ☐ "Constructed but unused" toilets (no BCC)
- ☐ Land-acquisition disputes (no farmer engagement)
- ☐ Single-gender VWSC (no women's voice)
- ☐ Lack of O&M handover (no asset register, no trained operator)
- ☐ Tariff non-collection leading to scheme collapse

**Policy Linkages (Always cite for 14-mark answers):**
- ☐ Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) — 2019
- ☐ Swachh Bharat Mission — Gramin (SBM-G) — 2014
- ☐ NRDWP (predecessor of JJM)
- ☐ MGNREGA convergence for material component
- ☐ Nirmal Gram Puraskar (SBM-G award)
- ☐ ODF-Plus verification protocol

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: National and International Policies on Water and Sanitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** National and International Policies on Water and Sanitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Water and Sanitation Policy** refers to a structured framework of principles, regulations, strategic guidelines, financial instruments, and institutional mechanisms formulated by governmental, inter-governmental, and non-governmental bodies to govern the planning, development, distribution, treatment, conservation, and management of water resources and sanitation services. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, this topic surveys the evolution, objectives, and implementation mechanisms of key Indian national policies (post-independence to contemporary era) alongside influential international declarations, conventions, and goals that shape global and national water and sanitation governance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECET867 - Module 4):**
> The module specifically emphasizes *Rural Water Supply and Onsite Sanitation Systems*. Therefore, all policies discussed must be critically examined through the lens of **rural implementation challenges**, **decentralized service delivery**, **community-led total sanitation (CLTS)**, and **technology choices appropriate for low-density, low-income settlements**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of national and international water-sanitation policies as a **"layered traffic rulebook for a city that never sleeps."**

- The **United Nations (UN)** acts like an international traffic authority that sets broad road safety rules (e.g., "every pedestrian deserves a safe crossing").
- Individual **national governments** translate these into country-specific speed limits, lane markings, and policing mechanisms (e.g., India deciding every village must have a paved road and a functional toilet).
- **State and local bodies (Panchayats, Municipalities)** enforce these rules on the ground, much like local traffic police.

Without this hierarchy of rules, each village would improvise its own water supply and sanitation system — leading to inefficiency, inequity, contamination, and disease.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **O&M** | Operation and Maintenance |
| **MDG** | Millennium Development Goals (2000–2015) |
| **SDG** | Sustainable Development Goals (2015–2030) |
| **JMP** | Joint Monitoring Programme (WHO/UNICEF) |
| **ODF** | Open Defecation Free |
| **FHTC** | Functional Household Tap Connection |
| **PWS** | Piped Water Supply |
| **WASH** | Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene |
| **JJM** | Jal Jeevan Mission |
| **SBM** | Swachh Bharat Mission |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition - WASH:**
> **WASH** is the collective term for **Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene** — the three interconnected pillars that underpin public health, gender equity, and human dignity. The WHO/UNICEF JMP uses WASH service *ladders* to benchmark country progress: **Surface water → Unimproved → Limited → Basic → Safely managed**.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

From a B.Tech Civil/Environmental Engineering perspective, understanding policy frameworks is essential because:

1. Every rural water supply or onsite sanitation project in India is **funded, designed, and benchmarked** against specific national missions.
2. International goals like **SDG 6** drive donor funding and technology choices (e.g., prefabricated toilets, Fecal Sludge Treatment Plants - FSTPs).
3. Engineers often work at the **intersection of policy and ground reality** — translating policy targets into engineering designs.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Policies

Policies can be classified along multiple axes:

### A. By Geographic Scope
- **International / Global** (UN, WHO, World Bank, World Water Council)
- **Regional** (SACOSAN for South Asia)
- **National** (India's NWP, SBM, JJM)
- **State-level** (e.g., Kerala's Jalanidhi, Suchitwa Mission)

### B. By Sectoral Focus
- **Water Supply Policies** (drinking water, irrigation, industrial)
- **Sanitation Policies** (toilets, sewage, solid waste, FSM)
- **Integrated / Cross-cutting** (Jal Shakti Abhiyan)

### C. By Approach
- **Supply-side augmentation** (building infrastructure)
- **Demand-side management** (pricing, awareness, CLTS)
- **Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM)**

## 2.2 Major National Policies (India)

### 2.2.1 National Water Policy (NWP)

- **First drafted: 1987** (under Rajiv Gandhi government)
- **Revised: 2002** (added ecological aspects)
- **Latest revision: 2012** (added climate change, privatization, PPP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key principles of NWP 2012:**
> 1. Water is a **public good**, not a private property.
> 2. Planning must be at the **river basin / sub-basin** level.
> 3. **Equity** between head-reach and tail-end users.
> 4. **Pricing** must reflect the *scarcity value* and *opportunity cost*.
> 5. Climate change adaptation must be embedded.
> 6. **Decentralized governance** through Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs).
> 7. Protection of water bodies and catchment areas.

### 2.2.2 National Sanitation Policy (2008) and NUSP (2012)

The **National Urban Sanitation Policy (NUSP), 2008** was one of the first comprehensive sanitation policies, followed by the **National Sanitation Policy, 2012 (drafted by MoDWS)** which extended the framework to rural areas emphasizing:

- **Open Defecation Free (ODF)** villages.
- **Solid and Liquid Waste Management (SLWM)**.
- **IEC (Information, Education, Communication)** campaigns.
- **Capacity building** of PRIs.

### 2.2.3 Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM)

| Parameter | SBM-G (Rural) | SBM-U (Urban) |
|---|---|---|
| **Launch Date** | 2 October 2014 | 2 October 2014 |
| **Lead Ministry** | Ministry of Jal Shakti (DDWS) | MoHUA |
| **Phase 1 End** | 2 October 2019 | March 2020 |
| **Phase 2 Start** | 2020 (ODF-Plus) | 2021 (ODF-Plus) |
| **Key Target** | 110 million toilets (Phase 1) | Universal sanitation |
| **Current Focus** | ODF-Plus (SLWM, Grey water mgmt) | Used water management |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SBM-G Phase 1 Achievement:**
> India declared itself **ODF on 2 October 2019**, with **110+ million toilets** built. However, KTU examiners often note that *toilet construction ≠ toilet usage* — hence the shift to **ODF-Plus** in Phase 2, which focuses on **sustainability** and **SLWM**.

### 2.2.4 Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) — The Flagship Rural Water Policy

Launched on **15 August 2019** by the Hon'ble Prime Minister, JJM is the most ambitious rural drinking water programme in independent India's history.

**Vision:** *"Har Ghar Jal"* — tap water to every rural household by **2024**.

**Core Objectives:**
1. Provide **Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC)** to every rural household.
2. Ensure **potable water** quality as per BIS 10500.
3. Promote **local water source sustainability** (groundwater recharge, rainwater harvesting).
4. Enable **community participation** through Village Water & Sanitation Committees (VWSC).
5. Implement **sustainable O&M** through tariff and contribution mechanisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition - FHTC:**
> A tap water connection provided to a rural household delivering a minimum of **55 litres per capita per day (lpcd)** as per the Indian standard, with water meeting BIS:10500 quality parameters, and the supply must be regular (ideally daily, or at minimum alternate days).

### 2.2.5 National Water Mission (NWM) under NAPCC

Formulated in **2008** as one of **eight missions** under the **National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC)**. The NWM has **five goals**:

1. Comprehensive water data collection.
2. Promoting citizen action.
3. Integrated water resources management.
4. Basin-level planning.
5. Increasing water use efficiency by **20%**.

### 2.2.6 Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation (AMRUT) and Smart Cities Mission

Both launched in **June 2015**, focusing on urban water supply, sewerage, and storm-water drainage. AMRUT covers **500 cities**, while Smart Cities Mission covers **100 cities**.

### 2.2.7 Namami Gange Programme

Launched in **June 2014** with a budget of **₹20,000 crore** to rejuvenate the Ganga river. Restructured in **2017** as the *Namami Gange - II* with an integrated *Ganga Action Plan*.

## 2.3 Major International Policies and Frameworks

### 2.3.1 Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) — 2000 to 2015

The MDGs were **8 goals** adopted by 189 UN member states in September 2000, with a 15-year horizon.

- **MDG 7 (Target 7.C):** *Halve, by 2015, the proportion of people without sustainable access to safe drinking water and basic sanitation.*
- **Outcome (by 2015):** Globally, the proportion using improved water sources rose from **76% to 91%**, and improved sanitation rose from **36% to 68%**. India achieved its drinking water target but **missed the sanitation target**.

### 2.3.2 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) — 2015 to 2030

Adopted at the **UN General Assembly in September 2015**, the SDGs consist of **17 goals** and **169 targets**.

**SDG 6: "Ensure availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all"** has **8 targets** (6.1 to 6.6, plus 6.a and 6.b).

| Target | Indicator | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **6.1** | 6.1.1 | By 2030, achieve universal and equitable access to safe and affordable drinking water |
| **6.2** | 6.2.1 | By 2030, achieve access to adequate and equitable sanitation and hygiene for all, end open defecation |
| **6.3** | 6.3.1, 6.3.2 | Improve water quality, reduce pollution, halve the proportion of untreated wastewater |
| **6.4** | 6.4.1, 6.4.2 | Increase water-use efficiency, address water scarcity |
| **6.5** | 6.5.1, 6.5.2 | Implement IWRM at all levels |
| **6.6** | 6.6.1 | Protect and restore water-related ecosystems |
| **6.a** | 6.a.1 | International cooperation and capacity-building |
| **6.b** | 6.b.1 | Strengthen local community participation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept - SDG 6.x Ladder:**
> The **JMP Service Ladders** classify WASH services into:
> - **Safely Managed** (highest — water on premises, available when needed, free of contamination)
> - **Basic**
> - **Limited**
> - **Unimproved**
> - **Surface Water** (lowest)
>
> Exam questions often ask students to **compare MDG vs SDG targets** — note that MDG spoke of "halving" while SDG speaks of **"universal access for all"** — a much more ambitious aspiration.

### 2.3.3 Dublin Principles (1992)

Formulated at the **International Conference on Water and the Environment** in Dublin, Ireland (January 1992). Four principles:

1. **Principle No. 1:** Freshwater is a finite and vulnerable resource, essential to sustain life, development, and the environment.
2. **Principle No. 2:** Water development and management should be based on a participatory approach, involving users, planners, and policymakers.
3. **Principle No. 3:** Women play a central part in providing, managing, and safeguarding water.
4. **Principle No. 4:** Water has an economic value in all its competing uses and should be recognized as an economic good.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question:**
> *"Enumerate the four Dublin Principles. Discuss their relevance to rural water supply in India."* — This question has appeared multiple times in KTU model papers.

### 2.3.4 Stockholm Declaration (1972) and Rio Declaration (1992)

- **Stockholm (June 1972):** First UN Conference on the Human Environment; established **UNEP**. Recognized that *"Man has the fundamental right to...an environment of a quality that permits a life of dignity and well-being."*
- **Rio (June 1992):** Earth Summit, led to **Agenda 21**, **UNFCCC**, and **CBD**. Chapter 18 of Agenda 21 deals with freshwater resources.

### 2.3.5 UN-Water and WHO/UNICEF JMP

- **UN-Water** (established 2003): Coordinates UN agencies on water and sanitation.
- **JMP (Joint Monitoring Programme):** Established in **1990**, it is the *official* UN mechanism to monitor global progress on WASH. Produces biennial reports (e.g., *Progress on Drinking Water, Sanitation and Hygiene 2000-2022*).

### 2.3.6 World Water Forum

Held every **3 years** since 1997 (Marrakesh). Organized by the **World Water Council** (headquarters: Marseille, France). It is the world's largest event on water, producing the **Ministerial Declarations** that guide global policy.

### 2.3.7 Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015-2030)

While not exclusively a water policy, it influences WASH resilience during disasters, emphasizing that resilient water and sanitation infrastructure is critical to disaster response.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Definition | Typical Value / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Q_{hh} = P \times q_{pc}$ | Household water demand = Population × per capita demand | $P$ = persons, $q_{pc}$ = lpcd | $q_{pc} = 55$ lpcd (rural JJM standard) |
| $V_{res} = Q_{d,max} \times t_{s}$ | Required reservoir volume | $m^3$ | $t_{s} = $ hours of storage |
| $N_{ODF} = \frac{N_{hh}^{toilet}}{N_{hh}^{total}}$ | ODF achievement ratio | Dimensionless | Must be **1.0** for ODF declaration |
| $C_{capi} = \frac{C_{tot}}{P \times L}$ | Per capita capital cost | $\text{Rs per capita per year}$ | Used for cost-benefit analysis of policies |
| $\eta_{supply} = \frac{Q_{delivered}}{Q_{produced}} \times 100\%$ | Supply efficiency | **$60 - 80\%$** typical for rural India | Low values indicate leakage/water losses |
| $\text{Lpcd}_{eff} = \frac{Q_{d}}{P}$ | Effective per capita supply | $\text{lpcd}$ | Compare to design $q_{pc}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Hint:** When asked to compute design flow or storage for a JJM scheme, use **$q_{pc} = 55$ lpcd**, peak factor = **2.7**, and fire demand as per IS 5332 (rural). Always show units in every step — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Policy-driven design choices:** JJM's FHTC mandate forces engineers to size distribution networks for *peak instantaneous* flow, not average flow.
- **Source sustainability assessments:** NWP 2012 mandates groundwater impact assessments for new schemes.
- **Tariff design:** Dublin Principle 4 (water as economic good) drives the **30% O&M recovery** norm adopted in JJM.
- **Sanitation technology selection:** SBM-ODF-Plus drives engineers toward **FSTPs (Fecal Sludge Treatment Plants)** rather than centralized STP models for rural areas.
