# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix: MDG vs SDG on Water & Sanitation

The transition from MDG to SDG is one of the most-examined themes. Below is a step-by-step comparative analysis.

### 3.1.1 Quantitative Comparison

**Step 1: Define MDG 7.C baseline (1990).**

Globally, in 1990 (MDG baseline year):
- 2.6 billion people lacked improved sanitation.
- 1.3 billion people lacked improved water supply.

**Step 2: Define MDG 7.C target (2015).**

The MDG target was to **halve** these proportions.

$$
\text{Target}_{water} = \frac{1.3}{2 \times \text{World Pop}_{1990}} = 0.5 \times 0.026 = 0.013 \times \text{World Pop}_{1990}
$$

Wait — let me express this correctly in terms of proportion:

$$
\text{Proportion}_{water,1990} = \frac{1.3 \times 10^9}{5.3 \times 10^9} = 24.5\%
$$

$$
\text{Target}_{water,2015} = \frac{24.5\%}{2} = 12.25\%
$$

This means by 2015, at most 12.25% of the global population should be unserved. Step 3: Actual outcome.

**Step 3: SDG 6.1 target (2030).**

$$
\text{Target}_{SDG\,6.1} = 0\% \text{ unserved (universal access)}
$$

**Step 4: Compare the ambition level.**

$$
\frac{\text{Target}_{SDG}}{\text{Target}_{MDG}} = \frac{0\%}{12.25\%} = 0
$$

This shows that SDG 6.1 is fundamentally more ambitious — it demands **zero** unserved, whereas MDG demanded only *halving*.

### 3.1.2 Qualitative Comparison Matrix

| Dimension | MDG 7.C | SDG 6 |
|---|---|---|
| **Adoption Year** | 2000 | 2015 |
| **Horizon** | 15 years | 15 years |
| **Coverage Ambition** | Halve the proportion | Universal access (100%) |
| **Equity Focus** | Aggregate national-level | Disaggregated by income, gender, location |
| **Service Definition** | "Improved" sources | "Safely managed" (higher bar) |
| **Cross-cutting** | Standalone Goal 7 | Integrated with Climate (SDG 13), Health (SDG 3), Gender (SDG 5) |
| **Monitoring** | JMP basic monitoring | JMP + UN-Water integrated monitoring |
| **Sanitation Aspiration** | Halve proportion | **End** open defecation |

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical: India's Progress Under MDG

Given: India's rural population in 1990 ≈ **630 million**. Rural population in 2011 ≈ **833 million**. Assume 1990 rural sanitation coverage ≈ **2%** (approximately, as per Census 1991).

**Step 1: Compute MDG target for India rural sanitation (2015).**

$$
\text{Target}_{coverage,2015} = \frac{2\%}{2} = 1\% \text{ uncovered (i.e., } 99\% \text{ covered)}
$$

Wait — that's incorrect because MDG aimed to *halve the unserved proportion*. Correct computation:

$$
\text{Unserved}_{1990} = 100\% - 2\% = 98\%
$$

$$
\text{Unserved}_{target,2015} = \frac{98\%}{2} = 49\%
$$

$$
\text{Coverage}_{target,2015} = 100\% - 49\% = 51\%
$$

**Step 2: Compare with actual 2015 rural coverage.**

Per NSSO and Census 2011, rural household toilet coverage in India was approximately **38.7%** by 2015 (after partial SBM effects).

$$
\text{Deficit}_{2015} = 51\% - 38.7\% = 12.3\%
$$

**Step 3: Conclusion.**

$$
\text{Result} = \text{India missed MDG sanitation target by } 12.3 \text{ percentage points}
$$

This is precisely why SDG 6's *universal access* mandate, combined with SBM-G and JJM, was launched — to bridge the unfinished MDG agenda.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: JJM Tap-Water Demand Calculation

**Problem:** A JJM scheme is to be designed for a Gram Panchayat with the following data:
- Number of households: **500**
- Average household size: **4.5**
- Per capita demand: **55 lpcd**
- Peak factor: **2.7**
- Hours of supply: **6 hours/day**
- Required storage: **8 hours** of average daily demand

**Step 1: Compute design population.**

$$
P = N_{hh} \times n = 500 \times 4.5 = 2250 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 2: Compute average daily demand (ADD).**

$$
Q_{d} = P \times q_{pc} = 2250 \times 55 = 123750 \text{ litres/day}
$$

Converting to cubic metres:

$$
Q_{d} = 123750 \div 1000 = 123.75 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 3: Compute peak demand.**

$$
Q_{peak} = Q_{d} \times \text{PF} = 123.75 \times 2.7 = 334.125 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 4: Compute peak flow rate (instantaneous design flow).**

Since supply is for **6 hours/day**, the pump must deliver the daily demand in 6 hours:

$$
Q_{pump} = \frac{Q_{d}}{t_{s}} = \frac{123.75 \text{ m}^3}{6 \text{ h}} = 20.625 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

Converting to litres per second:

$$
Q_{pump,L/s} = \frac{20.625 \times 1000}{3600} = 5.729 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Step 5: Compute storage reservoir capacity.**

$$
V_{res} = Q_{d} \times \frac{t_{st}}{24} = 123.75 \times \frac{8}{24} = 41.25 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 6: Apply peak factor to peak flow during transmission.**

$$
Q_{peak,L/s} = 5.729 \times 2.7 = 15.469 \text{ L/s}
$$

This is the **transmission main design flow**, used in the Hazen-Williams equation to size the rising main.

## 3.3 Analytical Framework: Policy Implementation Lifecycle

Every policy follows a **5-stage lifecycle**. KTU expects students to understand this for essay-type questions.

| Stage | Description | Example (JJM) |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Vision & Announcement** | PM's Independence Day 2019 speech | *"Har Ghar Jal"* |
| **2. Cabinet Approval** | Union Cabinet clears framework | 75% Centre + 25% State funding |
| **3. Institutional Architecture** | Creation of governing bodies | National Jal Jeevan Mission (NJJM), State WSSCs |
| **4. Implementation & Monitoring** | District Action Plans, village-level VWSCs | IMIS portal for daily progress |
| **5. Outcome Verification** | Independent third-party audits | NEERI, NABL-accredited labs |

## 3.4 Cost-Recovery Equation Under Dublin Principle 4

Per Dublin Principle 4 and NWP 2012, water should be priced. The JJM norm specifies **30% of O&M costs** to be recovered through user tariffs.

$$
R_{30\%} = 0.30 \times C_{O\&M}
$$

$$
\text{Tariff per household} = \frac{R_{30\%}}{N_{hh}}
$$

**Derivation:**

Let annual O&M cost for a JJM scheme = $C_{O\&M}$ (in Rs/year). Let number of households = $N_{hh}$.

Annual recovery target:

$$
T_{annual} = 0.30 \times C_{O\&M} = 0.30 \times C_{O\&M}
$$

Monthly recovery:

$$
T_{monthly} = \frac{T_{annual}}{12} = \frac{0.30 \times C_{O\&M}}{12} = 0.025 \times C_{O\&M}
$$

Per household monthly tariff:

$$
\text{Tariff}_{hh} = \frac{T_{monthly}}{N_{hh}} = \frac{0.025 \times C_{O\&M}}{N_{hh}}
$$

**Worked Example:**

Assume $C_{O\&M} = \text{Rs } 25{,}00{,}000$ per year for 500 households.

$$
T_{annual} = 0.30 \times 2500000 = \text{Rs } 750000
$$

$$
T_{monthly} = \frac{750000}{12} = \text{Rs } 62500
$$

$$
\text{Tariff}_{hh} = \frac{62500}{500} = \text{Rs } 125 \text{ per household per month}
$$

This tariff must be set at a level that **does not exclude vulnerable sections** — hence JJM allows for cross-subsidization between APL and BPL households.

## 3.5 Equity Assessment Under SDG 6.1

A key SDG 6.1 indicator is the **equity ratio**:

$$
E_{r} = \frac{C_{richest\,quintile} - C_{poorest\,quintile}}{C_{national\,average}} \times 100\%
$$

Where $C$ = coverage in each quintile. SDG target: $E_{r} \rightarrow 0$ (perfect equity).

This metric is often used in KTU questions to assess whether policies have *trickle-down* benefits.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC)" as per Jal Jeevan Mission guidelines. State the minimum service level of lpcd to be assured.

**Model Answer:**
A **Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC)** is defined under the Jal Jeevan Mission as a tap water connection provided to every rural household that delivers **potable water** in adequate quantity (a minimum of **55 litres per capita per day (lpcd)**, as per Indian standards), of prescribed quality (meeting **BIS:10500** parameters), on a regular and reliable basis (ideally **daily**, or at minimum on **alternate days**). The connection must be functional at the household level, not just at a public standpost, and must ensure equity, affordability, and sustainability. The Ministry of Jal Shakti monitors FHTC coverage through the IMIS portal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Students often forget the **BIS:10500** reference. Always cite the standard for any quality-related question.

### Question 2: Enumerate the four Dublin Principles (1992) and explain their relevance to water management.

**Model Answer:**
The **Dublin Principles** were formulated at the International Conference on Water and the Environment in Dublin, Ireland, in January 1992. The four principles are:

1. **Principle 1:** Fresh water is a finite and vulnerable resource, essential to sustain life, development, and the environment.
2. **Principle 2:** Water development and management should be based on a participatory approach, involving users, planners, and policymakers at all levels.
3. **Principle 3:** Women play a central part in the provision, management, and safeguarding of water.
4. **Principle 4:** Water has an economic value in all its competing uses and should be recognized as an economic good.

**Relevance:** These principles underpin India's National Water Policy 2012 and the Jal Jeevan Mission — particularly Principle 2 (VWSC participation) and Principle 4 (tariff for O&M recovery).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** with reference to its objectives, institutional architecture, funding pattern, and implementation challenges in rural India. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Millennium Development Goals (MDG) and Sustainable Development Goals (SDG)** framework as applied to water and sanitation, with reference to India's progress. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer to (a):

**Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) — Detailed Discussion**

**1. Genesis and Vision:**
JJM was launched on **15 August 2019** by the Hon'ble Prime Minister from the Red Fort. The vision is *"Har Ghar Jal"* — tap water to every rural household by 2024. The mission is implemented by the **Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation (DDWS), Ministry of Jal Shakti**.

**2. Objectives:**
- Provide **FHTC** to every rural household.
- Ensure **potable water quality** per BIS:10500.
- Promote **local source sustainability** through rainwater harvesting, groundwater recharge.
- Enable **community ownership** through Village Water & Sanitation Committees (VWSCs).
- Achieve **sustainable O&M** through a 30% cost-recovery norm via user tariffs.

**3. Institutional Architecture:**
- **National Level:** National Jal Jeevan Mission (NJJM) under DDWS.
- **State Level:** State Water & Sanitation Missions (SWSMs).
- **District Level:** District Water & Sanitation Missions (DWSMs).
- **Village Level:** VWSCs chaired by the Gram Pradhan.

**4. Funding Pattern:**
- **75%** by the Central Government.
- **25%** by the State Government.
- For **NE and Himalayan States**: 90:10 (Centre:State).
- For **UTs without legislature**: 100% Centre.

**5. Implementation Challenges:**
- **Source sustainability:** Over-extraction of groundwater.
- **Quality testing infrastructure** at village level.
- **Community participation** for long-term O&M.
- **Power supply reliability** for pumping.
- **Climate change** leading to drought and drying sources.
- **Slip-back** of FHTC functionality after initial provisioning.

#### Model Answer to (b):

**Comparison: MDG vs SDG on Water and Sanitation**

| Parameter | MDG 7.C | SDG 6 |
|---|---|---|
| **Adoption Year** | 2000 | 2015 |
| **Horizon** | 2000–2015 | 2015–2030 |
| **Quantitative Target** | Halve the unserved proportion | Universal access (100%) |
| **Quality Bar** | "Improved" sources | "Safely managed" (higher) |
| **Equity Lens** | National aggregate | Disaggregated by income, gender, geography |
| **Monitoring** | JMP biennial reports | JMP + UN-Water + national dashboards |
| **Hygiene Inclusion** | Limited | Explicit (handwashing with soap - 6.2.1a) |

**India's MDG Performance:**
- **Drinking water (MDG 7.C target):** India **achieved** the drinking water target — rural coverage rose from ~68% (1990) to ~93% (2015).
- **Sanitation (MDG 7.C target):** India **missed** the sanitation target — rural household toilet coverage rose from ~2% (1990) to ~38.7% (2015) versus a target of ~51%.

**India's SDG 6 Commitments:**
- Through **JJM**, India targets 100% rural tap water coverage by 2024 (i.e., ahead of the SDG 2030 deadline).
- Through **SBM-ODF-Plus**, India targets sustainability and SLWM.
- Through **Namami Gange**, river rejuvenation and STP coverage are targeted.

**Conclusion:** The shift from MDG to SDG represents a transition from **quantity-focused aggregate targets** to **quality-focused, equity-driven, integrated targets**. India's policy response — particularly JJM and SBM — is the operational translation of SDG 6 commitments.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss the major **National Policies on Water and Sanitation** in India, including the National Water Policy, SBM, JJM, and NWM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the role of **international agencies and frameworks** (UN, WHO, UNICEF, World Bank, Dublin Principles, World Water Forum) in shaping global and national water-sanitation policies. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer to (a):

**1. National Water Policy (NWP), 2012:**
The third revision of the NWP (after 1987 and 2002) emphasizes:
- Water as a **public good**, not private property.
- **River basin planning** at hydrological-unit scale.
- **Equity** between head-reach and tail-end users.
- **Pricing** to reflect scarcity and opportunity cost.
- **Decentralized governance** through PRIs.
- Climate change adaptation.

**2. Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM):**
- **SBM-G (Rural)** and **SBM-U (Urban)**, launched on **2 October 2014**.
- Phase 1 (2014–2019): 110+ million toilets built; India declared ODF on 2 October 2019.
- Phase 2 (2020–2025) — **ODF-Plus**: focus on **SLWM (Solid and Liquid Waste Management)**, grey water management, and FSTPs.
- Behaviour change through **IEC (Information, Education, Communication)** and **CLTS (Community-Led Total Sanitation)**.

**3. Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM):**
Already detailed in Question A(a). Key addition: 100% FHTC coverage by 2024.

**4. National Water Mission (NWM), 2008:**
One of **8 missions** under the **National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC)**. Targets:
- 20% increase in water use efficiency.
- Comprehensive water data.
- Basin-level master plans.
- Citizen and state action.
- Focus on vulnerable areas (over-exploited blocks).

**5. Other policies:**
- **National Urban Sanitation Policy (NUSP), 2008**.
- **AMRUT** (2015) — 500 cities for urban water supply.
- **Smart Cities Mission** (2015) — 100 cities, integrated water management.
- **Namami Gange** (2014, restructured 2017) — Ganga rejuvenation.
- **Atal Bhujal Yojana** (2019) — groundwater management in 7 states.

#### Model Answer to (b):

**1. United Nations System:**
- **UN-Water:** Coordinates 30+ UN agencies on water and sanitation. Produces the **World Water Development Report (WWDR)**.
- **WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP):** The *official* global WASH monitor. Produces biennial reports.

**2. World Bank:**
- Provides **concessional loans and grants** for water infrastructure (e.g., IDA, IBRD).
- Published the **Thirsty Energy** initiative, integrating water and energy planning.
- Hosts the **Water Global Practice** with $20+ billion active portfolio.

**3. Dublin Principles (1992):**
Already detailed in Part A Question 2. These principles shaped India's **National Water Policy 2012** (especially Principle 4 — water as economic good — and Principle 2 — participation).

**4. World Water Council and World Water Forum:**
- **World Water Council (WWC):** Founded 1996, HQ Marseille. Think tank and policy advocacy.
- **World Water Forum (WWF):** Held triennially since 1997 (Marrakesh). Largest global water event. Produces **Ministerial Declarations** that guide UN and national policy.

**5. Other international frameworks:**
- **Stockholm Declaration (1972):** First UN conference linking environment and development; led to UNEP.
- **Rio Declaration & Agenda 21 (1992):** Chapter 18 on freshwater.
- **Johannesburg Plan of Implementation (2002):** Linked water to poverty reduction.
- **Sendai Framework (2015):** WASH resilience in disaster response.
- **UN Climate Change Conferences (COPs):** Increasingly link water to climate finance.

**Impact on India:**
- The **Stockholm Conference (1972)** prompted India's first water pollution control laws.
- **Dublin Principles** are explicitly cited in the NWP 2012 preamble.
- **SDG 6** drives India's JJM, SBM-ODF-Plus, and Namami Gange programmes.
- **World Bank** funding has supported several state-level WSS projects (e.g., Karnataka, Kerala, Tamil Nadu).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Year mismatches:** Students often confuse MDG (2000) with SDG (2015) adoption years, or SBM (2014) with JJM (2019) launch dates. Get these right — examiners deduct **1 mark** per date error.
> 2. **Conflating "Improved" and "Safely Managed":** These are *not* the same. "Improved" = protected source. "Safely managed" = on premises + available + free of contamination. Conflating them costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting BIS 10500:** Always cite the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) 10500** when discussing drinking water quality. Omission costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting institutional hierarchy:** National → State → District → Block → Gram Panchayat is the correct governance chain. Listing only national-level bodies loses 2 marks in (a) sub-questions.
> 5. **MDG outcome errors:** India **achieved** the MDG drinking water target but **missed** the sanitation target. Mixing these is a common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — National and International Policies on Water and Sanitation**

### 🏛️ National Policies (India)
- **National Water Policy (NWP):** 1987 → 2002 → **2012** (current). Emphasizes basin planning, equity, pricing, climate change.
- **National Urban Sanitation Policy (NUSP):** 2008.
- **National Sanitation Policy:** 2012 (draft by MoDWS).
- **Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM):** 2 Oct 2014. **SBM-G (rural)** + **SBM-U (urban)**. **ODF declared: 2 Oct 2019**. **Phase 2: ODF-Plus** (SLWM focus).
- **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM):** 15 Aug 2019. **Har Ghar Jal** by 2024. **FHTC, 55 lpcd, BIS 10500**.
- **National Water Mission (NWM):** 2008 under NAPCC. **20% water-use efficiency** target.
- **AMRUT:** 2015, 500 cities, urban water supply.
- **Smart Cities Mission:** 2015, 100 cities.
- **Namami Gange:** 2014, ₹20,000 crore, Ganga rejuvenation.
- **Atal Bhujal Yojana:** 2019, groundwater in 7 states.

### 🌐 International Frameworks
- **Stockholm Conference:** 1972 (UNEP formed).
- **Dublin Principles:** 1992 (4 principles — finite resource, participation, women, economic good).
- **Rio / Agenda 21:** 1992 (Chapter 18 — freshwater).
- **MDG 7.C:** 2000–2015 — halve unserved proportion. India: ✓ water, ✗ sanitation.
- **SDG 6:** 2015–2030 — universal WASH access. 8 targets (6.1 to 6.6, 6.a, 6.b).
- **JMP:** WHO/UNICEF, since 1990, global WASH monitor.
- **UN-Water:** 2003, UN coordination.
- **World Water Forum:** Triennial since 1997 (Marrakesh).
- **Sendai Framework:** 2015–2030, disaster risk reduction.

### 📐 Key Numerical Standards
- **Rural water demand:** **55 lpcd** (JJM).
- **Urban water demand:** **135 lpcd** (CPHEEO).
- **Peak factor (rural):** **2.7**.
- **Hours of supply (rural JJM):** **6 hours/day** (minimum).
- **BIS 10500:** Drinking water quality standard.
- **Cost recovery norm:** **30% of O&M**.
- **Funding (JJM):** 75:25 (Centre:State); 90:10 for NE/Himalayan; 100% for UTs.

### 🎯 Likely 14-Mark Question Structures
- *"Discuss JJM: objectives, architecture, funding, challenges."*
- *"Compare MDG vs SDG with India case."*
- *"Discuss National Water Policy 2012 with rural relevance."*
- *"Explain SBM phases and ODF-Plus framework."*
- *"Dublin Principles and their application in Indian policies."*
- *"Role of international agencies in shaping national WASH policy."*

### 🔑 Terminology to Master
**WASH, FHTC, ODF, ODF-Plus, VWSC, DWSM, SWSM, IMIS, BIS 10500, IWRM, CLTS, SLWM, FSTP, lpcd, PF, RWHS, O&M, ODA, NAPCC, NDC, JMP, UN-Water.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regulatory Frameworks and Standards for Rural Water and Sanitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Regulatory Frameworks and Standards for Rural Water and Sanitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regulatory Framework** refers to the structured system of policies, codes, guidelines, statutory norms, and technical standards established by national and international agencies to govern the planning, design, operation, quality assurance, and service delivery of rural water supply and onsite sanitation systems.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, a **Standard** is a quantified, technically verified specification (numerical limit, dimensional requirement, or procedural protocol) issued by a recognized standards body — such as the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, **World Health Organization (WHO)**, or **Central Public Health and Environmental Engineering Organisation (CPHEEO)** — that must be mandatorily or voluntarily complied with during project execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:**
> - A *Policy* declares intent (e.g., National Water Policy 2012).
> - A *Regulation* enforces action (e.g., Water Act, 1974).
> - A *Standard* prescribes measurable limits (e.g., IS 10500:2012).
> - A *Code of Practice* gives design methodology (e.g., CPHEEO Manual).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Rulebook" for Water and Sanitation

Imagine a rural village installing a new piped water scheme. Without rules:
- One engineer might design a 50 lpcd (litres per capita per day) system; another 250 lpcd.
- One pipe could be of GI (galvanised iron); another of HDPE (high-density polyethylene) — different pressure ratings, different life spans.
- One panchayat may accept a 45 m³/day septic tank; another a 5 m³/day tank for the same population.

This chaos is prevented by the **regulatory framework** — it acts exactly like the **traffic rulebook of a city**:
- **Lane markings** = Pipe diameter and slope standards (NBC 2016).
- **Speed limits** = Permissible water quality limits (IS 10500:2012).
- **Traffic police** = KSPCB / PCB enforcement and monitoring.
- **Vehicle fitness certificates** = NABL-accredited lab testing reports.
- **Highway code** = CPHEEO design manuals.

Just as no one designs a city without a traffic rulebook, no rural water or sanitation scheme in India can be legitimately funded (by the Centre, State, or World Bank) without compliance with the documented regulatory architecture.

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Salient Quantities

- **Lpcd** — Litres per capita per day (per-person water demand). Rural India norm = **55 lpcd (minimum, Jal Jeevan Mission)**.
- **lpcd** for rural sanitation water usage ≈ **25–30 lpcd** for toilets alone.
- **Design period** for rural water supply = **30 years** (CPHEEO, 1999).
- **Population forecast horizon** = 30 years (rural schemes).
- **Service Level Benchmark (SLB)** for rural water supply, Jal Jeevan Mission target: **55 lpcd per capita supply with regular (long-term) service delivery**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Favourite Numbers (Memorise for KTU 2024):**
> - **55 lpcd** — JJM rural water supply target.
> - **25 lpcd** — toilet flushing + hand-wash minimum (IS 1172).
> - **30 years** — design period for rural piped water supply.
> - **pH 6.5–8.5** — acceptable drinking water range (IS 10500:2012).
> - **0 mg/L** — coliform in 100 mL (E. coli limit, IS 10500).
> - **45 m³/day** — typical septic tank design threshold above which Sewage Treatment Plant (STP) is mandated in Kerala (KSPCB).

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme

Module 4 shifts the student's perspective from *designing a system* to *designing it legally, ethically, and policy-correctly*. PECET867 explicitly positions the graduate to act as a **village-level consultant-cum-regulator** who can:
1. Justify technical choices with reference to BIS/CPHEEO clauses.
2. Diagnose non-compliance issues in existing schemes.
3. Interface with funding agencies (Jal Jeevan Mission, AMRUT, MGNREGA convergence).
4. Apply for statutory clearances from KSPCB / Local Self-Government.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of the Regulatory Framework

The Indian rural water and sanitation regulatory framework is layered into **four concentric tiers** (highest to lowest authority):

### Tier 1 — International / Multilateral Layer
- **World Health Organization (WHO)** — *Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality (4th Edition, 2017)*. Defines health-based targets.
- **United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF)** — co-implementer of WASH programmes.
- **World Bank / ADB** — funding conditionalities (safeguard policies).
- **Sustainable Development Goal 6 (SDG 6)** — "Ensure availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all" by **2030** (Target 6.1 = safely managed drinking water; 6.2 = safely managed sanitation).
- **ISO Standards** — ISO 24510 (drinking water service), ISO 24511 (wastewater service), ISO 24512 (drinking water — management of water utilities).

### Tier 2 — National Policy & Legal Layer
- **Constitution of India** — Article 243 (Panchayats — 73rd Amendment) places drinking water & sanitation in the **11th Schedule** (Entry 11: "Drinking water", Entry 17: "Drainage").
- **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** & **Water (Cess) Act, 1977**.
- **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** — umbrella legislation.
- **National Water Policy 2012** — most recent overarching policy document.
- **National Policy on Faecal Sludge and Septage Management (FSSM), 2017** — FSSM policy.
- **National Sanitation Policy (draft 2017, ratified 2019)**.
- **Public Health Engineering Act** (state-level, e.g., Kerala Public Health Act, 2023 notified sections).

### Tier 3 — National Standards & Codes Layer
- **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** — IS 10500:2012 (Drinking Water Specification), IS 1172:1993 (Basic Requirements for Water Supply, Drainage & Sanitation), IS 2470 (Septic Tank specifications — Part 1 & 2), IS 8419 (Biological Treatment — Part 1: trickling filters), IS 12288 (Low-cost sanitation), IS 14961 (Twin pit pour flush latrines).
- **CPHEEO Manuals** — *Manual on Water Supply and Treatment (1999, revised)*, *Manual on Sewerage and Sewage Treatment (2013, revised)*, *Manual on Operation & Maintenance of Water Supply System (2005)*.
- **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016) — Part 9: Plumbing Services**.
- **Service Level Benchmarks (SLBs)** for Water Supply (MoDWS, 2013).

### Tier 4 — Mission / Programme & Operational Layer
- **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** — launched **15 Aug 2019**, targets *Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC)* to every rural household by **2024** (now extended).
- **Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM) Grameen (Phase I: 2014–2019, Phase II: 2020–2025)** — Open Defecation Free (ODF), ODF Plus, GOBAR-DHAN.
- **National Rural Drinking Water Programme (NRDWP)** — restructured into JJM in 2019.
- **MGNREGA convergence** for construction of soak pits, household toilets.
- **State Schemes** — e.g., Kerala's *Haritha Keralam Mission*, *Suchitwa Mission*.

## 2.2 Key Drinking Water Quality Standards — IS 10500:2012 (Essential for KTU Exam)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 10500:2012** categorises parameters into:
> - **Organoleptic** (taste, odour, colour, turbidity) — *aesthetic* acceptability.
> - **Physico-chemical** (pH, hardness, TDS, chlorides, sulphates, iron, fluorides, nitrates).
> - **Microbiological** (E. coli, coliforms, viruses).
> - **Radioactive** (alpha and beta emitters).
> - **Heavy metals** (arsenic, lead, mercury, cadmium, chromium).

Each parameter has two limits: **"Desirable"** (acceptable for long-term use) and **"Maximum Permissible"** (in absence of alternative). When the maximum limit is exceeded, water is rejected for potable use.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates *all* quantitative formulas a KTU 2024 examinee can be tested on for Module 4. Memorise boundary conditions, not just symbols.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Limit | Unit | Governing Standard / Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rural water demand (per capita) | $D = 55$ | lpcd | JJM (2024 Scheme) |
| 2 | Total daily demand | $Q_{day} = P_n \times D + L + I$ | m³/day | CPHEEO Manual |
| 3 | Peak factor | $PF = 1.5$ to $2.5$ (rural); $2.5$ to $3.0$ (urban) | — | CPHEEO |
| 4 | Maximum daily demand | $Q_{max} = Q_{avg} \times PF$ | m³/day | CPHEEO |
| 5 | Hourly peak factor | $HPF = 1.5$ (rural); $3.0$ (urban) | — | CPHEEO |
| 6 | Fire demand (rural — usually ignored unless industrial cluster) | $Q_f$ as per NBC 2016 | L/min | NBC 2016 |
| 7 | Population forecast — Arithmetic | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x$ | persons | CPHEEO |
| 8 | Population forecast — Geometric | $P_n = P_0 \cdot (1 + r/100)^n$ | persons | CPHEEO |
| 9 | Incremental Increase (preferred for rural) | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot x + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot y$ | persons | CPHEEO |
| 10 | Septic tank liquid retention time | $t \geq 24$ (min); recommended $48$ | hours | IS 2470 (Part 1) |
| 11 | Septic tank capacity | $V = (0.7 \cdot N + 0.3 \cdot H) \cdot 1.0$ to $1.33$ (multiplier) | litres | IS 2470 (Part 1) |
| 12 | Per capita sewage (rural) | $0.80 \times$ water supplied | lpcd | CPHEEO |
| 13 | Twin-pit pour-flush latrine pit capacity (5 users) | $V_{pit} \geq 0.3$ m³ | m³ | IS 14961 |
| 14 | Soak-pit loading rate | $q \leq 20$ to $25$ | L/m²/day | CPHEEO FSSM |
| 15 | pH (drinking water) | $6.5$ to $8.5$ | — | IS 10500:2012 |
| 16 | Turbidity (max permissible) | $1 \text{ NTU (desirable)}; 5 \text{ NTU (max)}$ | NTU | IS 10500:2012 |
| 17 | Total Coliforms (per 100 mL) | Must be **0** in 95 % samples; **none** in two consecutive samples | MPN/100 mL | IS 10500:2012 |
| 18 | Nitrate (as $\text{NO}_3$) | $45$ (max permissible) | mg/L | IS 10500:2012 |
| 19 | Fluoride (as $\text{F}$) | $1.0$ (desirable); $1.5$ (max) | mg/L | IS 10500:2012 |
| 20 | Arsenic (as $\text{As}$) | $0.01$ (max permissible) | mg/L | IS 10500:2012 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Pipe-Symbol Rule Reminder:** All vertical bars / absolute value symbols in the formulas above (e.g., $\vert \cdot \vert$) must be rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` in any LaTeX/table context to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The regulatory framework is not an academic exercise. It governs:
- **Tender preparation** — every rural scheme tender must quote IS code numbers.
- **Statutory clearances** — KSPCB NoC requires conformance to CPCB norms.
- **Insurance & liability** — non-conformance nullifies insurance cover.
- **Dispute resolution** — courts use IS codes as "best engineering practice" benchmarks.
- **International funding** — World Bank/ADB require WHO + national standards compliance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Population Forecasting — The Three KTU-Approved Methods

### 3.1.1 Arithmetic Increase Method

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= P_0 + n \cdot x
\end{aligned}
$$

where $P_n$ = population after $n$ decades, $P_0$ = present population, $x$ = arithmetic increase per decade (assumed constant), $n$ = number of decades.

**Conversion Logic (Line by Line):**
- **Step 1:** Identify the present population $P_0$ from census data.
- **Step 2:** Calculate the decadal growth $x$ from the *last two census decadal differences* (assuming constant absolute growth).
- **Step 3:** Substitute $n$ (decades from base year to design year) and solve.

### 3.1.2 Geometric Increase Method

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= P_0 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $r$ = decadal growth rate (%), $n$ = number of decades.

**Conversion Logic:**
- **Step 1:** Compute $r$ from two census populations: $r = \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_{c2}}{P_{c1}}\right)^{1/d} - 1 \right] \cdot 100$, where $d$ = number of decades between censuses.
- **Step 2:** Apply the compound interest-style multiplier over the design horizon.
- **Step 3:** This method is preferred for *fast-growing* towns; for declining rural populations, results are unrealistic.

### 3.1.3 Incremental Increase Method (CPHEEO Recommended for Rural)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= P_0 + n \cdot x + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot y
\end{aligned}
$$

where $y$ = incremental increase per decade (assumed to grow linearly between decades).

**Conversion Logic:**
- **Step 1:** Compute $x$ from census data using arithmetic method.
- **Step 2:** Compute $y$ as the *change* in $x$ between two consecutive decade pairs.
- **Step 3:** Combine linear and second-order terms for the full forecast.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model for KTU Numerical Questions)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A rural panchayat has population **5,000 in 2024**. Decadal census data: 2004 — 3,500; 2014 — 4,200. Forecast population in **2054** using all three methods and the *Incremental Increase* method's projection for a 30-year design period of a piped water supply scheme. Compute the **average daily demand** in m³/day assuming JJM norm of 55 lpcd and 10 % system losses.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Compute decadal arithmetic increase $x$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= \frac{P_{2014} - P_{2004}}{1 \text{ decade}} = 4200 - 3500 = 700 \text{ persons/decade}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute incremental increase $y$ (using the previous decade's $x$ assumed ≈ 600 for 1994–2004):
$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= 700 - 600 = 100 \text{ persons/decade}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Assumed value for illustration; in real KTU problems, $y$ is given.)*

### Step 3 — Forecast $P_n$ for $n = 3$ decades (2024 → 2054):
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2054} &= 5000 + 3 \cdot 700 + \frac{3 \cdot 4}{2} \cdot 100 \\
&= 5000 + 2100 + 600 \\
&= 7700 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute average daily demand $Q_{avg}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{avg} &= \frac{P_n \times D}{1000} = \frac{7700 \times 55}{1000} = 423.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(This is the *net* demand.)*

### Step 5 — Add system losses (10 %):
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{gross} &= Q_{avg} \times 1.10 = 423.5 \times 1.10 = 465.85 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Maximum daily demand (peak factor 1.8, rural):
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{max} &= 423.5 \times 1.8 = 762.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always state the *units* explicitly when writing $Q_{max}$ — KTU valuators deduct ½ mark for ambiguous "demand" without unit specification.

## 3.3 Septic Tank Sizing Derivation (IS 2470: Part 1)

For a rural household cluster of $N$ users producing sludge of $L$ L/year each, the required septic tank volume $V$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{total}} &= V_{\text{liq}} + V_{\text{sludge}} \\
V_{\text{liq}} &= Q_{d} \cdot t_{r} \\
V_{\text{sludge}} &= N \cdot S \cdot r_{d} \\
V_{\text{total}} &= Q_{d} \cdot t_{r} + N \cdot S \cdot r_{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $Q_{d}$ = daily sewage flow (L/day), $t_{r}$ = retention time (days), $N$ = number of users, $S$ = sludge accumulation rate (L/person/year), $r_{d}$ = desludging interval (years).

**Conversion Logic (Step by Step):**
- **Step 1:** Compute $Q_{d} = P \times \text{lpcd} \times 0.8$ (rural sewage ratio).
- **Step 2:** Multiply by retention time ($1$ day min, $2$ days recommended by CPHEEO).
- **Step 3:** Estimate sludge volume using IS 2470 recommended $S = 30$ L/person/year (for septic tanks, unimproved sludge digestion).
- **Step 4:** Choose $r_{d} = 2$ to $3$ years (desludging cycle).
- **Step 5:** Sum and round up to next standard tank size.

## 3.4 Onsite Sanitation System — Decision Matrix

| Source / Context | Recommended Onsite System | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Rural household, water table > 2 m below pit base, low permeable soil | Twin-pit pour-flush latrine | IS 14961 |
| Rural household, high sub-soil water | Septic tank + soak pit | IS 2470 (Part 1 & 2) |
| Coastal / high water table, rocky / hard soil | Septic tank with raised soak-away | IS 2470 + KSPCB guidelines |
| Slum / dense rural cluster | Community Septic Tank + Decentralised STP | CPHEEO Manual (2013) |
| Floating population > 500/day | Community Bio-digester / DEWATS | National FSSM Policy 2017 |
| Areas with arsenic / fluoride contamination | Defluoridation / Arsenic Removal Unit (community scale) | IS 10500 + WHO |
| School / Anganwadi in rural area | Twin-pit pour-flush (separate for boys/girls/staff) | IS 14961 + SBM guidelines |

## 3.5 Quantitative Limit Matrix for Common Water Quality Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table** — KTU frequently frames Part A questions as "State the maximum permissible limit of X in drinking water as per IS 10500:2012."

| Parameter | Desirable Limit | Max Permissible Limit (in absence of alternative) | Unit | Health Effect on Exceedance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| pH | $6.5$–$8.5$ | No relaxation | — | Corrosion / scaling |
| Turbidity | $1$ | $5$ | NTU | Shielding of pathogens |
| Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) | $500$ | $2000$ | mg/L | Taste, laxative effect |
| Total Hardness (as $\text{CaCO}_3$) | $200$ | $600$ | mg/L | Scaling, soap consumption |
| Chloride (as $\text{Cl}$) | $250$ | $1000$ | mg/L | Saline taste |
| Fluoride (as $\text{F}$) | $1.0$ | $1.5$ | mg/L | Fluorosis (skeletal/dental) |
| Nitrate (as $\text{NO}_3$) | $45$ | No relaxation | mg/L | Blue-baby syndrome (methaemoglobinaemia) |
| Iron (as $\text{Fe}$) | $0.3$ | $1.0$ | mg/L | Staining, taste |
| Arsenic (as $\text{As}$) | — | $0.01$ | mg/L | Cancer, skin lesions |
| Lead (as $\text{Pb}$) | — | $0.01$ | mg/L | Neurological damage in children |
| E. coli (per 100 mL) | Must be **0** | Must be **0** | MPN/100 mL | Acute gastroenteritis |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Regulatory Framework Hierarchy — Mermaid Block Diagram

> The following Mermaid block renders the *four-tier regulatory hierarchy* for rural water supply and sanitation in India. The schematic maps **which body governs which layer**, **which body reports to whom**, and **which standards flow downward into engineering practice**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    n_intl["INTERNATIONAL LAYER\nWHO Guidelines for Drinking Water\nUNICEF WASH Programme\nWorld Bank Safeguard Policies\nISO 24510 - 24512 Standards\nUN SDG Goal 6 Targets"]
    n_natpol["NATIONAL POLICY LAYER\nConstitution 11th Schedule 73rd Amend\nWater Act 1974 and Cess Act 1977\nEnvironment Protection Act 1986\nNational Water Policy 2012\nFSSM Policy 2017"]
    n_std["NATIONAL STANDARDS LAYER\nBIS IS 10500 IS 1172 IS 2470 IS 14961\nCPHEEO Manual Water Supply 1999\nCPHEEO Manual Sewerage 2013\nNBC 2016 Part 9 Plumbing\nService Level Benchmarks MoDWS"]
    n_miss["MISSION AND PROGRAMME LAYER\nJal Jeevan Mission 2019\nSwachh Bharat Mission Phase 2 2020\nNRDWP restructured into JJM\nMGNREGA Convergence\nState Schemes Kerala Haritha Keralam"]
    n_state["STATE IMPLEMENTATION LAYER\nKerala Water Authority KWA\nKerala Suchitwa Mission\nKSPCB Enforcement Officer\nGrama Panchayat Engineering Wing\nDistrict Water and Sanitation Mission"]
    n_rur["RURAL HOUSEHOLD\nFunctional Household Tap Connection FHTC\nTwin Pit Pour Flush Latrine\nSeptic Tank with Soak Pit\nCommunity Biogas DEWATS System"]
    n_intl --> n_natpol
    n_natpol --> n_std
    n_std --> n_miss
    n_miss --> n_state
    n_state --> n_rur
    n_intl -. "Funding conditionalities" .-> n_miss
    n_std -. "Quality enforcement" .-> n_state
```

> **How to read the diagram:** Solid arrows show the *downward flow* of authority (international standards inform national policy, national policy informs national standards, and so on, reaching the rural household). Dashed arrows show the *cross-cutting influence* (e.g., WHO standards are not legally binding in India but are *enforced* indirectly via World Bank loan conditionalities and KSPCB clearances).

## 4.2 Onsite Sanitation Selection Logic — Mermaid Flowchart

> The following schematic guides the *decision-making process* of an engineer selecting an onsite sanitation system for a rural household or cluster, per CPHEEO FSSM policy and IS standards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n_a["START: Site Survey of Rural Household"]
    n_b["Is water table depth below 1.5 m from pit base?"]
    n_c{"Is soil permeability adequate for soakage?"}
    n_d{"Is space available for twin pits?"}
    n_e["OPTION A: Twin Pit Pour Flush Latrine\nStandard IS 14961\nLowest cost, desludge every 5 years"]
    n_f["OPTION B: Septic Tank with Soak Pit\nStandard IS 2470 Part 1 and Part 2\nRetention time 24 to 48 hours"]
    n_g["OPTION C: Septic Tank with Raised Soak Pit\nHigh water table area\nIS 2470 plus KSPCB norms"]
    n_h["OPTION D: Community Bio-digester or DEWATS\nFor cluster of 50 plus households\nFSSM Policy 2017"]
    n_a --> n_b
    n_b -- "Yes" --> n_c
    n_b -- "No" --> n_g
    n_c -- "Yes" --> n_d
    n_c -- "No" --> n_f
    n_d -- "Yes" --> n_e
    n_d -- "No" --> n_f
    n_f --> n_h
    n_h --> n_end["Submit Design to Grama Panchayat\nand KSPCB for Approval"]
    n_e --> n_end
    n_g --> n_end
```

## 4.3 Quality Monitoring & Surveillance Workflow — Mermaid Sequence

> This sequence diagram illustrates the *institutional chain of testing* drinking water quality in a rural panchayat under JJM surveillance protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n_src["Source: River, Borewell, Open Well"]
    n_5pot["5 Pot Sample Protocol\n1 Source + 4 Delivery Points"]
    n_pri["Panchayat Water Quality Testing Lab\nBasic 9 Parameters"]
    n_dist["District Water Quality Lab\n11 to 14 Parameters"]
    n_ref["State Reference Lab\nAll Parameters plus Heavy Metals"]
    n_nabl["NABL Accredited Private Lab\nFor Specialised Tests"]
    n_corr["Corrective Action if Non-compliant\nby KSPCB and KWA"]
    n_src --> n_5pot
    n_5pot --> n_pri
    n_pri -- "If Non-compliant" --> n_dist
    n_dist -- "Still Non-compliant" --> n_ref
    n_ref -- "Heavy Metal or Radioactive" --> n_nabl
    n_pri -- "Always report" --> n_corr
    n_dist -- "Always report" --> n_corr
    n_ref -- "Always report" --> n_corr
    n_nabl -- "Final report" --> n_corr
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the major categories of water quality parameters specified in IS 10500:2012. State the maximum permissible limits for two heavy metal contaminants in drinking water as per this standard.**

**Model Answer (Point-wise, 3 marks):**
- **Categories of parameters (2 marks):** (i) Organoleptic parameters (colour, odour, taste, turbidity); (ii) Physico-chemical parameters (pH, TDS, hardness, chlorides, sulphates, fluorides, nitrates, iron, residual chlorine); (iii) Microbiological parameters (E. coli, total coliforms); (iv) Heavy metals (arsenic, lead, mercury, cadmium, chromium); (v) Radioactive parameters (alpha and beta emitters). *(Mention any 4 categories clearly for full 2 marks.)*
- **Heavy metal limits (1 mark):** Arsenic (as As) = **0.01 mg/L** (max permissible); Lead (as Pb) = **0.01 mg/L** (max permissible). *(Either heavy metal pair with numerical limits and units is acceptable.)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a "Service Level Benchmark" (SLB) in rural water supply? Enumerate any four service level indicators defined by the Ministry of Drinking Water & Sanitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Service Level Benchmarks (SLBs) are *quantified minimum performance standards* for water supply and sanitation services against which utilities and rural schemes measure their service delivery. They are issued by the **Ministry of Drinking Water & Sanitation (MoDWS)** to enable objective performance assessment.
- **Four indicators (2 marks):** (i) Coverage of households with piped water supply (target: 100 % under JJM); (ii) Per capita availability of water in lpcd (target: 55 lpcd); (iii) Continuity of supply in hours per day (target: 24×7 in long-term); (iv) Quality of water — percentage of samples meeting IS 10500:2012 (target: 100 %); (v) Collection efficiency / NRW reduction; (vi) Cost recovery / O&M sustainability. *(Any four well-explained indicators fetch full 2 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Convention:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, the examinee answers **ONE** of two alternatives. Both alternatives are given below. Each question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question 3A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a) Explain the four-tier hierarchical regulatory framework governing rural water supply and onsite sanitation in India. (7 marks)**

**(b) A rural panchayat has the following census data: 1994 = 2,500; 2004 = 3,000; 2014 = 3,400. Forecast the population in 2054 using the (i) Arithmetic, (ii) Geometric, and (iii) Incremental Increase methods. Design the average daily water demand in m³/day using JJM norms. (7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question 3A

#### Part (a) Four-Tier Regulatory Framework (7 marks)

> **Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks per tier, 0.5 mark for the connecting narrative.

**Tier 1 — International / Multilateral Layer:**
- **WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality** (4th Edition, 2017) — provide *health-based* microbial and chemical targets.
- **United Nations SDG 6** — global target: safely managed drinking water and sanitation for all by 2030.
- **World Bank, ADB, UNICEF** — funding and technical support agencies whose loan conditionalities enforce downstream compliance.
- **ISO 24510 / 24511 / 24512** — performance metrics for water/wastewater utilities.

**Tier 2 — National Policy & Legal Layer:**
- **Constitution of India — 11th Schedule, 73rd Amendment** — places drinking water and sanitation under Panchayat jurisdiction.
- **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** and **Cess Act, 1977** — anti-pollution statutes.
- **Environment Protection Act, 1986** — umbrella legislation.
- **National Water Policy 2012** — overarching policy framework.
- **FSSM (Faecal Sludge and Septage Management) Policy 2017** — specifically governs rural onsite sanitation.

**Tier 3 — National Standards & Codes Layer:**
- **BIS standards** — IS 10500:2012 (drinking water), IS 1172:1993, IS 2470 (septic tanks), IS 14961 (twin-pit latrines).
- **CPHEEO Manuals** — design codes for water supply (1999), sewerage (2013), and O&M (2005).
- **National Building Code (NBC 2016)** — Part 9: Plumbing Services.
- **Service Level Benchmarks (SLBs)** — quantitative performance yardsticks.

**Tier 4 — Mission / Programme Layer:**
- **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) — 15 Aug 2019** — 100 % Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC) target.
- **Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM) Grameen Phase II (2020–2025)** — ODF Plus protocols, GOBAR-DHAN.
- **MGNREGA convergence** for construction of rural water and sanitation assets.
- **State schemes** (e.g., Kerala Haritha Keralam, Suchitwa Mission).

**Connecting Narrative (0.5 mark):** Higher tiers are *non-binding policy guidance*; lower tiers translate policy into *enforceable action*. The hierarchy ensures that rural engineering is not left to ad-hoc design but is anchored in globally accepted, nationally codified, and locally implemented standards.

#### Part (b) Population Forecast & Demand Calculation (7 marks)

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark each for the three forecasts, 2 marks for comparative analysis, 2 marks for demand calculation.

**Step 1 — Compute decadal arithmetic increases:**
- $x_1$ (1994–2004) = $3000 - 2500 = 500$ persons/decade
- $x_2$ (2004–2014) = $3400 - 3000 = 400$ persons/decade
- **$y$ (incremental increase)** = $400 - 500 = -100$ persons/decade²

**Step 2 — Forecast 2054 from 2014 base, $n = 4$ decades:**

**(i) Arithmetic Method:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2054} &= 3400 + 4 \cdot 400 = 3400 + 1600 = 5000 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Geometric Method:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \left[\left(\frac{3400}{2500}\right)^{1/2} - 1\right] \cdot 100 \\
&= \left[(1.36)^{0.5} - 1\right] \cdot 100 \\
&= [1.1662 - 1] \cdot 100 = 16.62\,\% \\
P_{2054} &= 3400 \cdot (1 + 0.1662)^4 = 3400 \cdot 1.84 = 6256 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Incremental Increase Method:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2054} &= 3400 + 4 \cdot 400 + \frac{4 \cdot 5}{2} \cdot (-100) \\
&= 3400 + 1600 - 1000 \\
&= 4000 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Adopt CPHEO-recommended Incremental Increase value = 4000 persons (4 marks total for 3 methods + 1 mark for selection rationale).**

**Step 5 — Average daily demand @ 55 lpcd (JJM):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{avg} &= \frac{4000 \times 55}{1000} = 220 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Gross demand (10 % losses, JJM norm):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{gross} &= 220 \times 1.10 = 242 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Maximum daily demand (PF = 1.8, rural):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{max} &= 220 \times 1.8 = 396 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Concluding Statement (0.5 mark):** The panchayat must design a scheme with **service reservoir capacity ≥ 242 m³** and source/pipe mains rated for **396 m³/day** to be future-proof.

---

### Question 3B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a) Describe the salient features of IS 10500:2012. What are the two-tier limit specifications (desirable and maximum permissible)? Explain with a numerical example for three key parameters. (7 marks)**

**(b) For a rural cluster of 80 households (5 persons each), design the size of a community septic tank as per IS 2470: Part 1. Assume sewage flow = 80 % of water supplied at 55 lpcd, retention time = 2 days, desludging interval = 3 years, sludge accumulation = 30 L/person/year. (7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question 3B

#### Part (a) IS 10500:2012 — Salient Features & Two-Tier Limits (7 marks)

> **Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for salient features, 3 marks for two-tier explanation + 3 example parameters, 2 marks for numerical comparison.

**Salient Features of IS 10500:2012:**
1. It is the **national standard for drinking water quality** in India, applicable to packaged drinking water and piped supplies.
2. It specifies **organoleptic, physico-chemical, microbiological, radioactive, and heavy metal** parameters.
3. It adopts a **two-tier limit system** — *Desirable* (no known adverse health effect over lifetime consumption) and *Maximum Permissible* (acceptable when no alternative source exists).
4. It provides **methodology of testing** (referring to IS methods).
5. It mandates **monitoring frequency** for utilities.

**Two-Tier Limit Specification (with examples):**

| Parameter | Desirable Limit | Maximum Permissible Limit |
|---|---|---|
| pH | $6.5$–$8.5$ | No relaxation |
| Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) | $500$ mg/L | $2000$ mg/L |
| Fluoride (as $\text{F}$) | $1.0$ mg/L | $1.5$ mg/L |
| Nitrate (as $\text{NO}_3$) | $45$ mg/L | No relaxation |
| Iron (as $\text{Fe}$) | $0.3$ mg/L | $1.0$ mg/L |

**Interpretation:** The *Desirable* limit is the **target** that all new schemes should achieve. The *Maximum Permissible* limit is a **temporary concession** when no alternative source is available — it triggers immediate action to either find an alternative source or install treatment.

**Numerical Example:** A rural hand-pump source has fluoride = **1.4 mg/L** and nitrate = **38 mg/L**. Fluoride is **above desirable** (1.0) but **below maximum permissible** (1.5) → *Acceptable for short-term use, but a defluoridation unit must be planned.* Nitrate is **below desirable** (45) → *Acceptable.* TDS at **1800 mg/L** is **above desirable** (500) but **below maximum** (2000) → *Acceptable; consumer should be informed about taste.*

#### Part (b) Community Septic Tank Sizing (7 marks)

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark setup, 2 marks liquid volume, 2 marks sludge volume, 1 mark summation, 1 mark design recommendation.

**Given:**
- Households $H = 80$, persons/household = 5 → $P = 400$ persons.
- Water supplied $D = 55$ lpcd; sewage = $0.80 \times 55 = 44$ lpcd.
- Retention time $t_r = 2$ days.
- Desludging interval $r_d = 3$ years.
- Sludge accumulation $S = 30$ L/person/year.

**Step 1 — Daily sewage flow $Q_d$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_d &= P \times 0.8 \times D = 400 \times 0.8 \times 55 = 17600 \text{ L/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Liquid volume $V_{\text{liq}}$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{liq}} &= Q_d \cdot t_r = 17600 \times 2 = 35200 \text{ L} = 35.2 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sludge volume $V_{\text{sludge}}$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{sludge}} &= P \times S \times r_d = 400 \times 30 \times 3 = 36000 \text{ L} = 36 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total tank volume $V_{\text{total}}$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{total}} &= V_{\text{liq}} + V_{\text{sludge}} = 35.2 + 36.0 = 71.2 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Adopt standard size:** Round up to next modular size. Recommend **two tanks of 40 m³ each (in parallel for maintenance continuity)** OR **one tank of 75 m³**. The first option is preferred for rural clusters as it allows one tank to be operational while the other is desludged.

**Final Recommendation:** Provide a community septic tank of total capacity **80 m³** (2 × 40 m³ in parallel), with **inlet T-pipe**, **baffle wall**, **outlet T-pipe**, **vent pipe ≥ 75 mm Ø**, and **access manholes** as per IS 2470 (Part 1).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing $L$ and $m^3$ mid-derivation causes a 2-mark penalty. Convert to $m^3$ at the *boundary* only, not during intermediate steps.
> 2. **Forgetting sludge volume:** Many students compute only $V_{\text{liq}}$ and ignore $V_{\text{sludge}}$ — this is the most common KTU mistake. Sludge volume is **roughly half** of the total tank volume in rural designs.
> 3. **No mention of IS code:** Always cite "IS 2470: Part 1" with the clause reference — ½ mark lost if omitted.
> 4. **Peak factor omission:** In design questions, the $Q_{max}$ or peak factor (1.8–2.5) must be **explicitly** applied when sizing pipes/pumps — even if not asked directly for tank sizing.
> 5. **No statement of assumption:** Always declare: *"Assuming JJM norm of 55 lpcd, 80 % sewage return, 10 % system losses, and a design period of 30 years."* Missing assumptions cost 1 mark in long answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Memorise Before KTU Exam Day.**

- ☐ **JJM (15 Aug 2019)** is the *current operative mission* for rural water supply — 55 lpcd with FHTC.
- ☐ **SBM Grameen Phase II (2020–2025)** governs rural sanitation; target = *ODF Plus* with safe sludge management.
- ☐ **IS 10500:2012** is the *only* national drinking water standard — remember the two-tier (desirable & max permissible) system.
- ☐ **CPHEEO Manual (1999, Water; 2013, Sewerage)** is the *design bible* for civil engineers — refer to it in any design question.
- ☐ **IS 2470 Part 1 (Septic Tanks)** & **IS 14961 (Twin-Pit Pour-Flush Latrines)** are the *two most-tested codes* for rural onsite sanitation.
- ☐ **WHO Guidelines (4th Ed., 2017)** define *health-based* targets — used when Indian data is unavailable.
- ☐ **SDG 6 (2015–2030)** is the *global* anchor — Target 6.1 (water) and 6.2 (sanitation).
- ☐ **Design period** for rural water supply = **30 years**.
- ☐ **Peak factor** for rural water = **1.5–2.5**; **Hourly peak factor** = **1.5–3.0**.
- ☐ **Population forecast** preferred method for rural = **Incremental Increase** (gives lower, more conservative values).
- ☐ **Sewage return ratio** (rural) = **80 %** of water supplied.
- ☐ **Per capita sewage** (rural) = **44 lpcd** (at 55 lpcd water supply).
- ☐ **Septic tank retention time** ≥ 24 hours; recommended = 48 hours.
- ☐ **pH 6.5–8.5**; **Turbidity ≤ 1 NTU desirable / 5 NTU max**; **TDS ≤ 500 / 2000 mg/L**; **Fluoride 1.0 / 1.5 mg/L**; **Nitrate 45 mg/L (no relaxation)**.
- ☐ **E. coli = 0 MPN per 100 mL** — *must* be zero; *no relaxation allowed*.
- ☐ **Arsenic ≤ 0.01 mg/L**; **Lead ≤ 0.01 mg/L** — both heavy metals, no desirable limit.
- ☐ **Five-Pot Sample Protocol** — 1 source + 4 delivery points; mandatory under JJM surveillance.
- ☐ **NABL accreditation** mandatory for any lab testing for regulatory submission.
- ☐ **KSPCB clearance** mandatory for septic tank/STP ≥ 45 m³/day (Kerala threshold).
- ☐ **73rd Constitutional Amendment, 11th Schedule, Entry 11 (Drinking Water) and 17 (Drainage)** — constitutional basis for Panchayat-led rural WASH.
- ☐ **FSSM Policy 2017** — National Faecal Sludge and Septage Management Policy — *current* operative policy for sludge management.
- ☐ **NBC 2016, Part 9** — National Building Code section governing plumbing services.
- ☐ **Service Level Benchmarks (SLBs)** — MoDWS-issued quantified KPIs for water supply.
- ☐ **MGNREGA convergence** — convergence permitted for water/sanitation asset creation in rural areas.
- ☐ **ODF, ODF Plus, ODF Plus Plus** — successive quality tiers of SBM verification.

> **End of Module 4 — Regulatory Frameworks and Standards for Rural Water and Sanitation Notes (PECET867, KTU 2024 Scheme).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Government, NGOs, and Private Sector in Rural Water and Sanitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET867 - RURAL WATER SUPPLY AND ONSITE SANITATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Role of Government, NGOs, and Private Sector in Rural Water and Sanitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Role of Government, NGOs, and Private Sector in Rural Water Supply and Sanitation (WSS)** refers to the *multi-stakeholder institutional framework* in which statutory bodies, non-profit organizations, and commercial enterprises collaborate with rural communities to plan, finance, implement, operate, and maintain water supply and onsite sanitation systems, ensuring service delivery that is **technically sound, financially sustainable, socially equitable, and institutionally accountable**.

The framework rests on **three functional pillars**:

| Stakeholder | Primary Mandate | Constitutional/Statutory Backing |
|---|---|---|
| **Government (Public Sector)** | Policy, financing, regulation, asset creation | Article 243 (73rd Amendment), Ministry of Jal Shakti |
| **NGOs (Civil Society)** | Awareness, capacity building, advocacy, social mobilization | Societies Registration Act XXI of 1860, FCRA 2010 |
| **Private Sector** | Technology, innovation, service delivery, O&M | Companies Act 2013, PPP Policy Guidelines |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Legged Stool of Rural WSS"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine rural water and sanitation as a **three-legged wooden stool (a "kambala")** in a Kerala village household.
> - **Leg 1 — Government:** Provides the *frame and the strongest wooden beam* (policy, capital subsidy, technical standards). Without it, the stool collapses under the weight of cost.
> - **Leg 2 — NGOs:** Acts as the *carpenter's apprentice* — trains the family (community) to use the stool, polishes it with awareness, and ensures no one trips (behavioral change).
> - **Leg 3 — Private Sector:** Is the *hardware supplier* — brings pre-fabricated, modern fittings (taps, pumps, septic tanks), and sometimes delivers the finished product through Public-Private Partnership (PPP).
> - **The Seat (Top):** The **rural community/user household** — the stool exists *only* for them. If the family refuses to sit on it (non-acceptance), all three legs fail.
>
> **Remove any single leg → the stool topples.** This is the essence of a *convergent, multi-stakeholder model*.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants)

The following **national benchmark metrics** govern India's rural WSS sector and must be memorized for KTU examinations:

- **40 litres per capita per day (lpcd)** — minimum service delivery for rural habitation (BIS 1172:2013 / NRDWP norm, now Jal Jeevan Mission).
- **100% functional Household Tap Connections (FHTC)** — Jal Jeevan Mission target by **2024**, extended mission period under Jal Jeevan Mission (Urban) 2.0 context.
- **55 litres per capita per day** — design standard for rural piped water supply schemes (CPHEEO manual).
- **Swachh Bharat Mission (Gramin)** goal: Open Defecation Free (ODF), ODF Plus, and ODF Plus Plus.
- **2.0 %** — typical annual water tariff escalation clause in O&M contracts.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECET867 Module 4):**
> This topic carries **direct 14-mark and 7-mark potential** in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE). It tests the student's ability to:
> 1. Recognize **institutional convergence** between schemes (JJM, SBM-G, MGNREGA, NRDWP, 15th Finance Commission tied grants).
> 2. Explain **governance tiers** — Central, State, District, Block, Gram Panchayat.
> 3. Justify **PPP models** in WSS (DBO, BOT, Management Contract, Lease).
> 4. Appraise **NGO interventions** with named case studies (e.g., Sulabh, WaterAid, ACF, BAIF).
>
> Examiners specifically look for the keyword **"convergence"** and **"decentralized governance"** in model answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Government (Public Sector) — Functions and Schemes

The Government operates on a **three-tier hierarchy**:

#### A. Central Government (Union List / Concurrent List entries)

**Primary Apex Body:** *Ministry of Jal Shakti* (formed in **May 2019** by merging Ministry of Water Resources with the Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Centrally Sponsored Schemes (CSS):**
> - **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** — launched **20 August 2019**; aims at providing **Functional Household Tap Connections (FHTC)** to every rural household by **2024**. Estimated outlay: **₹3.60 lakh crore** (Union Budget announcement). Funding pattern: **90:10 (Centre:State)** for NE/Himalayan states, **60:40** for others, **100%** for UTs.
> - **Swachh Bharat Mission – Gramin (SBM-G)** Phase II — focuses on **ODF Plus** (sustainability of toilets + solid/liquid waste management).
> - **National Rural Drinking Water Programme (NRDWP)** — predecessor of JJM, now subsumed.
> - **Jal Shakti Abhiyan** — catchment area treatment, water conservation.
> - **Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation (AMRUT)** — urban complement.

#### B. State Government

State Public Health Engineering Departments (PHED), now renamed Jal Nigams / Water & Sanitation Missions in many states (e.g., *Kerala Water Authority*, *Karnataka Rural Drinking Water & Sanitation Department*).

**State-level Functions:**
- Detailed Project Report (DPR) preparation.
- State-level scheme convergence with state finances.
- Quality monitoring labs.
- State Water & Sanitation Mission (SWSM) — apex body at state level.

#### C. Local Self-Government (Panchayati Raj Institutions — PRIs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constitutional Backing — 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992:**
> Entry **11 of the 11th Schedule** places *drinking water, sanitation, drainage* squarely in the **domain of Gram Panchayats**.
> **Three-tier PRI structure:**
> $$\text{Gram Panchayat (Village)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Panchayat Samiti / Block} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Zilla Parishad (District)}$$

**PRI Functions in Rural WSS:**
1. Beneficiary identification and prioritization.
2. Contribution of **5%–10%** of capital cost in many states (community contribution clause under JJM).
3. Operation & Maintenance (O&M) after defect-liability period.
4. Water User Charge (WUC) collection — typically **₹1 to ₹50 per household per month** depending on state notification.
5. Village Water & Sanitation Committee (VWSC) / Paani Samiti formation.

### 2.2 The NGO (Civil Society) — Functions and Models

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):**
> A Non-Governmental Organization (NGO) is a *legally constituted, non-profit, voluntary entity* independent of government control, engaged in advocacy, service delivery, capacity building, or community mobilization in the development sector.

**Functional Roles of NGOs in Rural WSS:**

| Role | Mechanism | Typical Activity |
|---|---|---|
| **IEC / BCC** | Information, Education, Communication / Behaviour Change Communication | Street plays, wall paintings, school sanitation clubs |
| **Capacity Building** | Training-of-Trainers (ToT) | Training masons on double-pit pour-flush toilets |
| **Social Mobilization** | Triggering & community-led total sanitation (CLTS) | Identifying "natural leaders," triggering disgust/empathy |
| **Advocacy** | Policy research, RTI activism | Submitting policy briefs to NITI Aayog |
| **Service Delivery** | Implementing partner (IP) for government | Sulabh running community toilet complexes |
| **Monitoring** | Third-party evaluation | Social audits, citizen report cards |

**Landmark NGOs in Rural WSS (India-focused):**
- **Sulabh International** — founder Dr. Bindeshwar Pathak; pioneer of *two-pit pour-flush toilets*; over **10 million toilets** constructed across India.
- **WaterAid India** — UK-origin; focus on *safely managed sanitation* SDG 6.2.
- **Action contre la Faim (ACF) / Action Against Hunger** — emergency WASH.
- **BAIF Development Research Foundation** — rural water conservation.
- **PRADAN** (Professional Assistance for Development Action) — women Self-Help Groups (SHGs) integrated with WSS.
- **Arghyam** — Bangalore-based; funds WSS research and pilots.
- **Gramalaya, Naandi Foundation, TERI-ASD** — southern India focus.

### 2.3 The Private Sector — Functions and PPP Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):**
> The Private Sector in rural WSS includes *for-profit firms, contractors, manufacturers, technology vendors, financial institutions, and corporate CSR arms* that deliver goods, services, or capital under market-based or hybrid (PPP) arrangements.

**Four Canonical PPP Models in Rural Water Supply:**

| Model | Acronym | Risk-Bearing Entity | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Design–Build–Operate** | **DBO** | Private consortium builds and operates for 5–10 years | Bulk water treatment plants |
| **Build–Operate–Transfer** | **BOT** | Private builds, operates 15–30 years, then transfers | Multi-village schemes |
| **Management Contract** | **MC** | Government owns; private manages for 3–5 years | Water distribution O&M |
| **Lease / Affermage** | **L** | Private leases infra from government; tariff-based revenue | Urban-fringe rural schemes |

**PPP Financial Principle — The "User-Pays" Tariff Equation:**

The long-run marginal cost of supplying water in PPP must satisfy:

$$
\sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{\text{Revenue}_t - \text{O\&M Cost}_t}{(1+r)^{t}} \;\geq\; \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{\text{Capital Investment}_t}{(1+r)^{t}}
$$

Where:
- $T$ = contract tenure (years).
- $r$ = social discount rate (typically **8%–12%** in Indian WSS appraisal by Ministry of Finance).
- $\text{Revenue}_t$ = tariff collection in year $t$.
- The LHS must exceed or equal the discounted capital cost for **commercial viability**.

### 2.4 Convergence Framework (Tri-Ministries)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Convergence:**
> *Convergence* means the **deliberate planning, budgeting, and execution** of multiple schemes to deliver integrated WSS outcomes in the same geography, avoiding duplication and maximizing rupee impact.

**Classic 5-Scheme Convergence Stack for Rural WSS:**

1. **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)** — piped water supply.
2. **Swachh Bharat Mission – Gramin (SBM-G)** — toilets + greywater management.
3. **MGNREGA** — unskilled labour for trenching, soak pits.
4. **15th Finance Commission** — tied grants for water & sanitation to Rural Local Bodies (RLBs).
5. **State Sector Schemes** — state supplements.

**Convergence Funding Mix — Illustrative Algebra:**

Let $C_{\text{total}}$ = total capital cost of a multi-village scheme.

$$
C_{\text{total}} \;=\; C_{\text{JJM}} \;+\; C_{\text{SBM-G}} \;+\; C_{\text{MGNREGA}} \;+\; C_{\text{State}} \;+\; C_{\text{Community}}
$$

Each component has its own sanction ceiling, beneficiary eligibility, and audit trail. **Mis-mapping causes disallowed expenditure** — a common audit objection.

### 2.5 The Community — The Often-Forgotten Fourth Pillar

> [!NOTE]
> Although the syllabus frames "three actors," the **rural community (households, women SHGs, VWSC)** is the *demand-side anchor*. KTU examiners expect students to mention community participation as a cross-cutting enabler. Phrases like **"community-led total sanitation (CLTS)"** and **"community ownership for O&M"** are high-yield keywords.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{lpcd} = \dfrac{V_{\text{daily}}}{N_{\text{users}}}$ | litres per capita per day | L/person/day | **40 (rural min.)**, 55 (CPHEEO design) |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{peak}} = 1.5 \cdot Q_{\text{avg}}$ | peak factor multiplier | dimensionless | 1.5 for rural |
| 3 | $r = \dfrac{C_{\text{community}}}{C_{\text{total}}}$ | community contribution ratio | % | 5%–10% (JJM norm) |
| 4 | $r_{\text{recovery}} = \dfrac{\text{WUC collected}}{\text{O\&M cost}}$ | O&M cost recovery ratio | % | $\geq 100\%$ for sustainability |
| 5 | $\text{NPV}_{\text{PPP}} = \sum_{t=0}^{T}\dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | net present value of PPP | ₹ Crore | $\geq 0$ for viable concession |
| 6 | $\text{BCR} = \dfrac{\sum \text{PV of benefits}}{\sum \text{PV of costs}}$ | benefit-cost ratio | dimensionless | $\geq 1.0$ (World Bank cutoff: 1.2) |
| 7 | $\text{Demand-side deficit} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{FHTCs delivered}}{\text{Total rural HHs}}$ | JJM gap metric | % | Targeting 0% by 2024 |
| 8 | $E_{\text{electricity}} = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{\eta_{\text{pump}} \cdot 75}$ | kWh/day for pumping | kWh | where $H$ = head (m), $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 9 | $\text{ODF coverage (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{HHs with toilets}}{\text{Total HHs}} \times 100$ | toilet coverage | % | SBM-G target: 100% |
| 10 | $\text{Capital cost per capita} = \dfrac{C_{\text{scheme}}}{P_{\text{design}}}$ | ₹/person | ₹ | ₹ 2,500–5,000 (rural India, indicative) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Strategy Note:** Memorize **Formula 4 (cost-recovery ratio)** and **Formula 7 (FHTC gap metric)**. These appear in almost every KTU ESE question on PPP and JJM.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

This multi-stakeholder framework is the **operational backbone** of:
- Project DPR preparation (Engineers India Limited, WAPCOS, Tata Consulting Engineers).
- Public Investment Board (PIB) appraisal of central schemes.
- World Bank / Asian Development Bank (ADB) loan covenants.
- State Sector Reform Cell assessments.

An M.Tech / M.Plan graduate working in a Jal Nigam, NGO, or consultancy *must* be able to draw the institutional stakeholder map of any rural WSS intervention.
